- AT&T's carrier offload revenue guarantees ($500K/$750K/$1M) are contractually binding with shortfall payments

**Why this works:** The declining share structure aligns with risk. AT&T takes more revenue when tenant adoption is uncertain (Years 1-3); DFW captures more as the platform matures and risk decreases. AT&T's co-investment reduces my CapEx ask to the Board by $5M, which materially improves the Year 2 ROI calculation. And their onboarding resources accelerate tenant adoption velocity — the single biggest variable in my revenue model.

### Trade 2: Contract Term ↔ Technology Refresh + Exit Rights

**I give:** Accepting a **10-year initial term** (aligned with CIO's acceptable range and AT&T's S2 preferred position).

**I get:**
- **Binding Year 5 technology refresh** — AT&T funds carrier-layer upgrades to 5G-Advanced at AT&T's cost
- **Year 5 revenue-share renegotiation trigger** — symmetric, binding arbitration if actual performance diverges >20% from original projections (either direction)
- **No-fault exit at Year 7** with 18-month notice and AT&T transition obligations
- AT&T marketing rights to reference DFW as flagship deployment (per CIO offer O-5)

**Why this works:** AT&T gets 10 years of revenue certainty, which helps them justify co-investment internally. DFW gets a technology refresh that prevents obsolescence, a mid-term reset mechanism, and an exit option that preserves our sovereignty. The marketing rights are costless to DFW and genuinely valuable to AT&T's enterprise sales team.

### Trade 3: AT&T Data Monetization Share ↔ Enhanced Carrier Offload Terms

AT&T's S1 asks for 15% of data monetization revenue. Per the CIO's HC-4 and my H-R1, this is not available.

**I give:** AT&T retains the right to use anonymized RF telemetry (Tier 2 operational data — signal strength, spectrum utilization, interference patterns) for AT&T's internal network planning and anonymized industry benchmarking. No passenger data, no location data, no commercial analytics products — just RF performance data.

**I also give:** Carrier offload revenue share increased from 40%/25% to **45%/30%** for the first 60/120 months, provided AT&T raises the revenue floor guarantees to **$600K/$900K/$1.2M** for Years 1/2/3-5.

**I get:** AT&T drops the data monetization revenue share request entirely. Zero percent. DFW retains 100% of Tier 1 data monetization revenue. AT&T gets more on carrier offload (where they add genuine value) and less on data (where DFW bears the investment and risk).

### Trade 4: Concessions Pricing ↔ Guaranteed Adoption Commitment

**Concessions' soft constraint S2** targets $150-175/month. Their hard constraint H4 caps at $250/month.

**I propose:** **$200/location/month** as the standard published rate, with:
- Volume discount to **$175/month** at 175+ active locations (the consortium already exceeds this)
- **ACDBE/Small Business tier at $80/month** (40% of standard, funded from DFW's revenue share per my H-R5)
- Most-favored-tenant clause honored — if any comparable tenant class gets a lower effective rate, concessions match

**In return, I need:** The consortium's binding commitment to migrate all 200+ locations within 12 months of general availability (per their O1), the single-coordination-point structure (per their O2), and the anonymized foot traffic data contribution (per their O3). These aren't optional add-ons — they're the demand guarantee that makes the $200/month pricing viable.

**Revenue math at $200/month:**
- 200 locations × $200/month = $480K/year base
- 34 new locations at $200/month = $81.6K/year additional
- ACDBE locations (estimated 30-40) at $80/month net cost to DFW = ($28.8K-$38.4K)/year subsidy
- **Net concession tier revenue: ~$520-560K/year**, growing as new locations open

This is a revenue floor, not the ceiling. The real value is the analytics platform built on the foot traffic and dwell-time data these 200+ locations generate.

### Trade 5: Cargo Pricing ↔ Co-Investment and Timeline Acceleration

**Cargo's soft constraint SC-3** proposes $0.15-0.25/sq ft/month. Their acceptable range is up to $0.35/sq ft.

**I propose:** **$0.25/sq ft/month base** for standard IoT/connectivity tier, with automation-grade SLA (≤20ms, 99.99%) included in the base (per cargo's trade request). This yields:
- 2M sq ft × $0.25/month = $500K/month = **$6M/year** from cargo consortium
- Plus variable service fees for premium capacity bursts during peak sort windows

**In return, I need:**
- The $15M co-investment commitment formalized
- 3-year minimum term on all anchor tenants (per cargo's own O-2)
- Demonstration facility access for DFW's tenant recruitment (per cargo's O-3)
- Phase 0 infrastructure embedding funded by consortium (per cargo's O-4)

**Revenue math:** $6M/year from cargo alone covers my Year 2 ROI requirement ($2M+ net savings) before we even touch concession or airline revenue. Combined with carrier offload ($600K+) and operational savings ($1.5-2M from CWP consolidation, IoT convergence, manual survey elimination), the Year 2 positive ROI is defensible.

---

## 4. UPDATED CONSTRAINTS

### Revised Hard Constraints

**H-R1 (Data Ownership) — TIGHTENED:**
DFW owns 100% of Tier 1 data. Adding explicit carve-out language:
- Airline operational data (ramp telemetry, device metadata, crew communications) is excluded from DFW's Tier 1 data and remains airline-owned per Airline VP H1
- Concession transaction data (POS transactions, basket composition, payment data, customer identifiers) is excluded from DFW's Tier 1 data and remains operator-owned per Concessions H6
- Cargo operational data (WMS telemetry, AGV fleet data, cold chain monitoring records) is excluded from DFW's Tier 1 data and remains cargo operator-owned per Cargo HC-4
- DFW Tier 1 data scope is: **common-area passenger flow, device density, dwell time, anonymized location analytics, and network-derived zone-level behavioral patterns** in non-leased public spaces and common circulation areas
- These exclusions survive contract termination and are not subject to renegotiation

This tightening actually *strengthens* my monetization model by making the data ownership claims legally cleaner. Ambiguous claims on operator data would invite litigation; clean claims on common-area data are enforceable.

**H-R2 (Tenant Monetization Timeline) — REVISED:**
Platform technically capable by end of CIO Phase 2 (approximately month 24-30), revenue-generating by Phase 3 entry (approximately month 30-36). Penalty for AT&T-attributable delay: 200 bps revenue share reduction per quarter of delay. Calendar dates are phase-gate-dependent, not fixed.

**H-R3 (Year 2 ROI) — MAINTAINED:**
≥$2M net annual operational savings by Year 2. Adding: cargo consortium revenue ($6M/year at scale) and carrier offload revenue ($600K+ Year 1) may be counted toward ROI if they are contractually committed before the board capital approval vote. The board needs to see committed revenue, not projections.

**H-R4 (Zero Concession Disruption) — TIGHTENED:**
Incorporating concessions H7 blackout periods:
- No concession-zone cutover activities during November 15 – January 5 or March 1 – March 20
- Per-location cutover with 90-day parallel operation and 4-hour rollback
- Financial penalty: $15,000 per location per day for outages >4 hours attributable to deployment activities (maintained from my original position)
- Adding: Star Concessions receives dedicated transition support with pre-deployment testing in 2+ locations per my original H-R4

**H-R5 (ACDBE Pricing) — MAINTAINED:**
Three tiers: Enterprise, Standard, ACDBE/Small Business. ACDBE tier at ≤40% of Enterprise. Subsidy funded from DFW revenue share. Published transparently.

### New Hard Constraint

**H-R6 (Revenue Stream Separation) — NEW:**
Tenant access fees (airline, cargo, concession, advertising) and carrier offload revenue (T-Mobile, Verizon, DISH) are treated as **separate, independently tracked revenue streams** with distinct revenue-share formulas. No blending, no cross-subsidization, no netting. Each stream has its own accounting, its own minimums, and its own renegotiation triggers. This prevents a scenario where strong carrier offload performance is used to mask weak tenant adoption, or vice versa.

### Revised Soft Constraints

**S-R1 (Revenue Share) — REVISED:**
- Tenant access fees: DFW 60% / AT&T 40% Years 1-3, DFW 70% / AT&T 30% Years 4-7, DFW 80% / AT&T 20% Years 8-10
- Carrier offload: DFW 55% / AT&T 45% Years 1-5, DFW 70% / AT&T 30% Years 6-10
- Conditioned on AT&T's $5M edge compute co-investment and onboarding resource commitment

**S-R2 (Advertising) — MAINTAINED** but accepting AT&T co-branding in passenger-facing portal per AT&T H6. AT&T distribution partnership at 15% commission with $500K/$1M minimum revenue guarantees.

**S-R5 (Contract Term) — REVISED to 10 years** with Year 5 renegotiation trigger, Year 7 no-fault exit, and binding technology refresh obligation.

### New Soft Constraint

**S-R7 (Cargo Acceleration Bonus) — NEW:**
If cargo zone is revenue-generating by Q2 2026 (per cargo's preferred timeline), AT&T receives a one-time **$250K acceleration bonus** funded from DFW's revenue share. This aligns AT&T's carrier-operations team incentives with the cargo timeline that generates the fastest revenue return for DFW. I'm willing to pay for speed because early cargo revenue transforms the board narrative.

---

## 5. REVISED REVENUE MODEL — Board-Defensible

| Revenue Stream | Year 1 | Year 2 | Year 3 | Year 5 | 5-Year Cumulative |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cargo Tenant Access | $3.0M | $5.5M | $6.0M | $6.5M | $28.5M |
| Concession Platform | — | $0.3M | $0.6M | $0.7M | $2.0M |
| Airline Tenant Access | — | $0.5M | $1.5M | $2.5M | $6.5M |
| Carrier Offload (AT&T sourced) | $0.6M | $0.9M | $1.2M | $1.5M | $5.7M |
| Data Analytics / Advertising | — | $0.5M | $2.0M | $4.0M | $9.0M |
| Operational Savings | $1.0M | $2.0M | $2.5M | $3.0M | $11.5M |
| **DFW Share (after AT&T split)** | **$2.8M** | **$5.8M** | **$8.3M** | **$11.5M** | **$39.5M** |
| **Cumulative DFW Net Value** | $2.8M | $8.6M | $16.9M | — | **$39.5M** |

**Year 2 ROI check:** $5.8M DFW revenue against estimated $8-10M cumulative CapEx through Year 2 = positive trajectory. With cargo co-investment ($15M) offsetting DFW CapEx, net DFW investment through Year 2 is approximately $4-6M, yielding **positive cumulative ROI by late Year 2**. ✓

**5-Year net value:** $39.5M DFW share against $15-21M net DFW CapEx (after cargo co-investment credit and AT&T co-investment) = **~$18-24M net positive over 5 years**. Board-defensible.

---

## 6. FINAL SUMMARY

| Action | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Accepted** | All CIO hard constraints (HC-1 through HC-7) |
| **Accepted** | All Airline hard constraints (H1-H6) including priority hierarchy |
| **Accepted** | All Cargo hard constraints (HC-1 through HC-4) including timeline |
| **Accepted** | All Concessions hard constraints (H1-H7) including pricing ceiling |
| **Accepted** | AT&T H1 (carrier autonomy), H3 (offload share — modified), H4 (access), H5 (competitive protection), H6 (branding — scoped) |
| **Accepted with trade** | AT&T H2 (CWP investment as CapEx credit, not revenue share modifier) |
| **Flagged conflict** | Concessions H2 (Tier 2 priority) vs. Airline H6 (Commercial at Tier 5) — proposed Tier 5A/5B split with bandwidth floor |
| **Flagged timing tension** | AT&T 30-day RF review vs. Cargo 45-day decision — proposed passive/active infrastructure split |
| **Proposed 5 trades** | Revenue share, contract term, data rights, concession pricing, cargo acceleration |
| **Revised revenue model** | $39.5M 5-year DFW net value, board-defensible |

**My bottom line remains unchanged:** The data is the asset, the tenants are the revenue, the network is the delivery mechanism. Every peer hard constraint I've accepted today makes the platform more robust, more defensible, and more adoptable. The revised revenue model is conservative, grounded in contractually committed demand from cargo and concessions, and delivers positive ROI by Year 2 on savings plus early cargo revenue.

I'm ready for term sheets. Bring the priority hierarchy conflict to resolution first — I need both the airline and concessions consortium to respond to the Tier 5A/5B proposal before we finalize SLA structures. Then we can price everything.

arguments.name: Anchor Airline VP
arguments.content:
# Round 2 Position: Dominant Airline VP of Airport Operations
## Constraint Revision Following Peer Position Review

---

## Preamble

I've read every position at this table. The good news: the Hybrid Co-Investment architecture can satisfy most of what everyone needs. The bad news: there are three structural conflicts that will blow this deal apart if we don't resolve them now, and a fourth that will blow it apart in Year 3 if we paper over it.

I'll be direct about what I've learned, what I'm revising, what I'm trading, and where I see hard walls.

---

## 1. REVISED POSITION — Satisfying Peer Hard Constraints

I've mapped every peer's hard constraints against mine. Here's where I've moved and why.

### Revision 1: CIO's Infrastructure Ownership (HC-2) — Accepted, With Boundary Clarification

The CIO's position that DFW owns all physical and logical infrastructure below the carrier-operations API layer is **accepted.** We don't need to own the radios. We need to own our slice's behavior. As long as the CIO's ownership demarcation includes our contractual right to configure policy within our dedicated slice (my H5), there's no conflict.

**Updated position:** We accept DFW infrastructure ownership provided our slice management portal/API is an explicit element of the "carrier-operations API layer" boundary — meaning AT&T delivers it as part of their operational scope, but our configuration rights within it are contractually guaranteed by DFW as infrastructure owner.

### Revision 2: CIO's Security Sovereignty (HC-3) — Accepted, With Carve-Out

DFW's sovereign security authority is accepted. We understand the TSA/DHS regulatory reality. The CIO reports to federal regulators; we don't dispute that.

**Carve-out required:** DFW's security override authority (the 4-hour implementation window for federal directives) **must include a 30-minute advance notification to our 24/7 NOC** before any change affecting our slice's security posture. Not approval — notification. We need to know if our slice is about to be reconfigured so we can activate contingency procedures. If the directive is genuinely zero-notice emergency (active threat), we accept post-event notification within 60 minutes.

**Updated H5:** We accept DFW's penetration testing rights over our slice, provided: (a) testing is scheduled with 30 days' notice per CIO's existing terms, (b) testing does not occur during our peak banking hours (0600-0900, 1500-1800, 2000-2200 local) without our written consent, and (c) we receive unredacted results for any findings affecting our slice.

### Revision 3: AT&T's Carrier-Layer Operational Authority (H1) — Accepted, Already Compatible

AT&T's demand for unilateral spectrum management authority during DPA events is **fully compatible** with our position. We explicitly stated in Round 1 that we're not asking to touch SAS coordination or power levels. AT&T manages the radio layer; we manage our slice's policy layer. No conflict.

**Confirmation:** AT&T's 250ms DPA response authority is accepted without reservation. This is physics. Our failover SLA (H3, 30-second automatic failover) is the complementary mechanism — AT&T handles spectrum, our devices handle connectivity continuity. These work together, not against each other.

### Revision 4: AT&T's CWP Transition Protection (H2) — Accepted With Timeline Constraint

AT&T's request for 60-month CWP continuation and 30-day RF impact review for construction within 50 meters of existing APs is **conditionally accepted.**

**Condition:** The 30-day RF review window must not delay Terminal C reconstruction milestones. Terminal C is our exclusive terminal undergoing a $3B rebuild. If every steel beam and conduit run within 50 meters of an existing AP triggers a 30-day AT&T review, the construction schedule becomes AT&T's hostage.

**Proposed resolution:** For Terminal C specifically, AT&T and DFW pre-approve an **RF impact assessment framework** during Phase 0 (the CIO's 90-day baseline period). This framework defines pre-authorized construction zones, acceptable interference thresholds, and automatic mitigation procedures — so that individual construction activities within agreed parameters proceed without per-event 30-day reviews. Anything outside those parameters still triggers the full review. This gives AT&T protection without giving AT&T a veto over our terminal rebuild.

### Revision 5: AT&T Physical Access (H4) — Accepted, Standard Operations

Twelve SIDA badges and 24/7 access is standard for a critical infrastructure operator at an airport. No objection.

### Revision 6: Concessions' Turnkey Service Model (H3) and Pricing (H4) — Accepted, No Impact on Us

The concessions consortium's demand for managed-service connectivity at ≤$250/month per location with zero operator configuration is DFW's commercial decision. It doesn't affect our operations and we have no objection. In fact, better concession connectivity improves our passengers' experience. Support it.

### Revision 7: Concessions' POS Hardware Compatibility (H5) — Accepted, Aligned

Same principle as our H4 (no forced migration of our 800-900 iPads). We're both saying the network adapts to existing devices, not the other way around. Full alignment.

### Revision 8: Cargo Cold Chain Data Ownership (HC-4) — Accepted, Aligned With Our Data Position

Cargo's insistence that cold chain compliance data cannot be controlled by DFW or AT&T is directly analogous to our position on airline operational data. We support this explicitly and propose a unified framework (see Trades below).

---

## 2. IRRECONCILABLE CONFLICTS — Must Be Resolved Before Proceeding

### CONFLICT 1: The Priority Hierarchy War (CRITICAL)

**The problem:** Four parties claim privileged priority status. As currently stated, these are mutually exclusive.

| Party | Claimed Priority | Basis |
|-------|-----------------|-------|
| **Airline (us)** | Tier 1 (below safety-critical only) | 80%+ of departures, $74/min delay cost, ramp safety |
| **Cargo** | "Last non-safety slice degraded" — effectively Tier 1 or 2 | $20B economic impact, AGV safety, pharma compliance |
| **Concessions** | "No lower than Tier 2" | $4.2B gross product, POS = revenue infrastructure |
| **CIO** | DFW determines hierarchy | Infrastructure owner prerogative |

Everyone has a legitimate claim. But during a DPA event that cuts CBRS capacity by 40%, the physics are zero-sum. Someone gets degraded. The question is who.

**My position remains firm on H6, but I'm willing to refine it:**

The issue isn't really a six-tier hierarchy — it's what happens in the **first 30 seconds** of a capacity crunch. I propose reframing from rigid tiers to **time-sequenced degradation:**

- **Seconds 0-30:** All non-safety slices maintain minimum viable operations while failover activates. This is the window where our 30-second failover (H3) kicks in and concessions switches to cellular backup.
- **Seconds 30-300:** Passenger Wi-Fi and commercial advertising are fully shed. Concession POS gets minimum viable (1 Mbps per the concessions consortium's S3 request — we support this). Cargo cold chain monitoring maintains continuity (it's regulatory). Airline ramp ops maintain full allocation.
- **After 300 seconds:** If capacity hasn't recovered, a pre-defined "sustained DPA" allocation takes effect with proportional reduction across all non-safety tiers based on contractual minimums.

This gives everyone a survivable degradation path rather than a hard priority cutoff that leaves someone at zero. **But I am non-negotiable that airline ramp operations maintain full allocation before any commercial traffic during seconds 30-300.** Ramp safety is not comparable to POS transaction latency. I have people standing between aircraft. Concessions has a credit card that will retry in 30 seconds.

**Specific ask to the CIO:** Publish the priority hierarchy in the Phase 0 contract as a binding technical specification, not a governance document that can be revised by committee later. Every tenant signs on to the hierarchy as a condition of network access. If the hierarchy changes, every tenant must re-consent. This prevents DFW from unilaterally deprioritizing us to accommodate a future revenue-generating tenant.

### CONFLICT 2: Data Ownership Scope (SIGNIFICANT)

**The problem:** The CIO (HC-4) and CRO (H-R1) both claim DFW owns "all network-generated data." Our H1 says airline operational data never enters DFW's analytics pipeline. Cargo says cold chain data can't be controlled by DFW. Concessions says transaction data is theirs.

As written, DFW's position is over-broad. "All aggregated/anonymized passenger flow analytics, device density heat maps, dwell-time analytics, location-based service data, and any data product derivable from network infrastructure operations" — that last clause, "any data product derivable from network infrastructure operations," could theoretically encompass our ramp device telemetry patterns, baggage system utilization data, or crew movement analytics. It won't, because the CIO is a reasonable person, but contracts aren't enforced by reasonable people. They're enforced by lawyers.

**Proposed resolution — Unified Tenant Data Framework:**

| Data Category | Owner | DFW Rights | AT&T Rights |
|---|---|---|---|
| Common-area passenger flow (anonymized) | DFW | Full monetization | Operational telemetry only |
| Airline ramp operations data | Airline | None | None |
| Cargo cold chain / compliance data | Cargo operator | None | None |
| Concession transaction data | Concession operator | None | None |
| RF performance telemetry | AT&T (operational use) | SIEM integration per CIO HC-3 | Operational optimization only |
| Network capacity/utilization (anonymized) | DFW | Infrastructure planning, monetization | Operational optimization |

Each tenant's operational data generated *by that tenant's devices on that tenant's slice* belongs to that tenant. DFW monetizes common-area, infrastructure-derived, passenger-behavioral data. The boundary is: **if the data describes what a tenant is doing on its slice, it's tenant data. If the data describes what the network is doing in a common area, it's DFW data.**

I want this framework in the master services agreement as an exhibit, not a side letter.

### CONFLICT 3: AT&T's CWP 50-Meter Review vs. Construction Timelines (MODERATE)

Addressed above in Revision 4, but flagging it here because it also affects cargo. The Cargo Director needs conduit specs in 45 days and Phase 0 infrastructure embedding immediately. AT&T's 30-day per-event RF review process could bottleneck cargo warehouse construction the same way it could bottleneck Terminal C.

**Proposed resolution:** The Phase 0 baseline period (CIO's HC-6) must include development of a **pre-authorized construction framework** for both Terminal C and the cargo zone. AT&T's engineers participate in designing the framework. Once approved, construction activities within the framework proceed without per-event review. This satisfies AT&T's H2 (CWP protection) and Cargo's HC-1 (deployment authority decoupled from airport-wide timeline).

### CONFLICT 4 (Latent): AT&T's Competitive Evaluation Protection (H5) vs. CIO's Multi-Vendor Rights (HC-7/SC-4)

AT&T demands 180-day notice and competitive process before any replacement. The CIO wants the option to introduce alternative vendors at Phase 3 (Terminal F). These aren't strictly incompatible — a competitive process can still result in a new vendor — but AT&T's demand that Betacom trial nodes be limited to "non-production test zones" is a direct constraint on DFW's architecture flexibility.

**Our position:** We don't care who operates the carrier layer as long as our SLAs are met. But AT&T's insistence on limiting Betacom to test zones could slow innovation at Terminal F, which is our greenfield opportunity. We'd support AT&T getting a fair competitive evaluation for Terminal F (their S4 position of 30% weight for track record is reasonable) but would oppose any contractual restriction preventing DFW from running production Betacom nodes in Terminal F during the evaluation period.

---

## 3. PROPOSED TRADES

### Trade 1: We Accept Higher Tenant Fee → DFW Guarantees Priority Hierarchy in Binding Contract

**We give:** Agreement to a hybrid pricing model (base fee + per-departure variable) with the base fee at DFW's proposed rate, plus a 5-year minimum commitment (per our O1).

**We get:** The priority hierarchy (our H6, refined per Conflict 1 above) is codified as a binding technical specification in the master infrastructure agreement — not a governance policy document. Changes require consent of all Tier 0 and Tier 1 tenants. This means the CRO's future monetization ambitions cannot erode our network priority through a unilateral DFW board decision.

### Trade 2: We Share Operational Correlation Data → We Get Real-Time Telemetry Access

**We give:** Per our S3 offer, anonymized/aggregated operational correlation data ("network latency spikes correlate with pushback delays") that DFW can use to justify network investment to other stakeholders and the Board.

**We get:** Real-time API access to our slice's performance telemetry at 5-second granularity (we've relaxed from 1-second per S3 trade terms). This data feeds our operations analytics platform and helps us correlate network performance with OTP metrics.

### Trade 3: We Support Cargo's Accelerated Timeline → Cargo Supports Our Priority Position

**We give:** Explicit support for the Cargo Director's HC-1 (independent cargo zone deployment authority) and HC-4 (cold chain data ownership). We'll advocate to the CIO that authorizing cargo Phase 0 infrastructure embedding within 45 days is the right decision — it saves $800K-$1.4M in avoided retrofit costs and demonstrates the architecture works in an industrial environment before we commit Terminal C and Terminal F.

**We get:** Cargo explicitly endorses our Tier 1 priority position for airline ramp operations, with cargo accepting Tier 3 status. Cargo's AGV and cold chain operations, while economically significant, are not time-critical in the same 30-second window that ramp pushback coordination is. A 5-second failover for cold chain (per Cargo HC-4) and sustained 20ms latency for AGVs are achievable within a Tier 3 allocation during all but the most severe capacity events.

**Why this trade works:** Cargo needs timeline authority more than priority authority. We need priority authority more than timeline authority (Terminal F is 2027+). We each get what matters most.

### Trade 4: We Co-Invest in Terminal F → We Get Co-Design Authority

**We give:** Per our O2, $8-12M co-investment in Terminal F airline-specific infrastructure (edge compute, high-density small cells on ramp positions, ruggedized outdoor gear).

**We get:** Co-design authority (not just consultation) over network architecture in Terminal F operational zones, per our S1 preferred position. Specifically: small cell placement on the ramp, antenna density at gates, edge compute topology for our latency-sensitive applications. If DFW prefers to retain design authority, then DFW must contractually guarantee the application-level performance specs we defined in S1 (sub-20ms latency, 50 Mbps sustained per gate, zero-loss handoff at tug speeds).

**Why this trade is fair:** We're putting $8-12M on the table for Terminal F. That's more than most tenants' total lifetime network fees. Co-design authority for the zones where we're the sole operator and primary investor is proportional.

### Trade 5: We Accept AT&T's Brand Presence in Passenger Areas → AT&T Excludes Branding from Operational Zones

**We give:** No objection to "Powered by AT&T" co-branding in passenger-facing Wi-Fi portals, tenant service agreements, and marketing materials.

**We get:** AT&T's H6 brand presence requirement does not extend to airline operational network interfaces, ramp-facing displays, or any system used by airline crew or ground handlers in SIDA-restricted areas. Our operations network is branded with our airline identity, not AT&T's. This is consistent with the CIO's SC-3 position (operational networks are DFW/airline branded only).

---

## 4. UPDATED CONSTRAINT SET

Based on what I've learned, here are my updated constraints:

### Hard Constraints (Updated)

| ID | Constraint | Change from Round 1 |
|---|---|---|
| **H1** | Dedicated slice, cryptographically isolated, with explicit data sovereignty — our operational data never enters DFW's monetization pipeline | **Tightened:** Now includes reference to Unified Tenant Data Framework (Conflict 2 resolution) |
| **H2** | 99.99% uptime per terminal per month, measured at application layer | **No change** |
| **H3** | 30-second automatic failover to cellular backup | **No change** |
| **H4** | No forced migration of existing CBRS device fleet | **No change** |
| **H5** | Airline retains configuration authority over its slice | **Refined:** Accepts DFW security override with 30-minute advance notification; accepts DFW pen testing with scheduling constraints around banking hours |
| **H6** | Priority hierarchy contractually fixed | **Refined:** Proposed time-sequenced degradation model instead of rigid tiers; added requirement for binding technical specification (not governance policy) with tenant consent for changes |
| **H7 (NEW)** | AT&T's CWP 50-meter RF review must not delay Terminal C rebuild | Pre-authorized construction framework developed during Phase 0; per-event review only for out-of-framework activities |

### Soft Constraints (Updated)

| ID | Constraint | Change from Round 1 |
|---|---|---|
| **S1** | Terminal F co-design authority | **Converted to trade:** Co-investment ($8-12M) in exchange for co-design or guaranteed performance specs |
| **S2** | Cost-per-departure pricing model | **Unchanged** |
| **S3** | Real-time telemetry access | **Relaxed to 5-second granularity** in exchange for sharing correlation data back to DFW |
| **S4** | Joint innovation lab at Terminal F | **Unchanged** |
| **S5** | Terminal C rebuild 5G alignment with construction | **Elevated importance** given AT&T's 30-day review requirement; depends on H7 resolution |
| **S6 (NEW)** | AT&T competitive evaluation for Terminal F must allow production-grade alternatives during evaluation period | Betacom or other vendors should not be restricted to test zones in a greenfield terminal |

### Offers (Updated)

| ID | Offer | Change from Round 1 |
|---|---|---|
| **O1** | 5-year anchor tenant commitment, 800-900 iPads, 200-400+ IoT devices | **Unchanged** |
| **O2** | $8-12M Terminal F co-investment | **Unchanged** |
| **O3** | Ramp operations SMEs for network design validation | **Unchanged** |
| **O4** | Phased migration commitment (F → C → A/B) | **Unchanged** |
| **O5** | Joint DoD/NTIA DPA coordination advocacy | **Unchanged** |
| **O6 (NEW)** | Explicit support for cargo's accelerated Phase 0 timeline | Added to facilitate Trade 3 |
| **O7 (NEW)** | Anonymized operational correlation data shared with DFW for investment justification | Added to facilitate Trade 2 |

---

## 5. DIRECT MESSAGES TO EACH PARTY

### To the CIO:

Your architecture is sound. Your phased deployment is the right approach. I support nearly everything in your position. My two asks: (1) codify the priority hierarchy as a binding technical specification, not a policy document you can revise unilaterally, and (2) tighten the "all network-generated data" language in HC-4 to explicitly exclude tenant operational data generated on tenant slices. You don't actually want our baggage telemetry in your analytics pipeline — it has no monetization value and creates liability. Let's draw a clean line.

Also: authorize the cargo Phase 0 infrastructure embedding. The Cargo Director is right — the concrete doesn't wait, and their $15M co-investment commitment is the second-largest demand guarantee after ours. Don't let this slip because the airport-wide contract isn't finalized.

### To the CRO:

Your revenue vision is compelling and I want it to succeed — better concession analytics means a better passenger experience in our terminals. But H-R1's scope language ("any data product derivable from network infrastructure operations") is a land mine. Tighten it. You don't need our ramp data to build a $6-13M analytics business. You need passenger flow data from common areas, and you'll have plenty of it from 87 million passengers.

On the priority hierarchy: I understand POS transactions matter. I've proposed a time-sequenced degradation model that gives your operators a survivable minimum (1 Mbps floor) during capacity events while protecting ramp safety. Your operators can process credit cards on cellular backup in 30 seconds. My ramp crew can't coordinate a pushback on cellular backup with acceptable latency for 30 seconds without elevated safety risk. The priority difference is justified.

### To the Cargo Director:

We support your timeline. Explicitly. Your $15M co-investment and $4-6M annual recurring commitment deserves respect. I've offered Trade 3 to back your accelerated deployment in exchange for your support on priority hierarchy. AGV latency at 20ms and cold chain at 100ms are achievable at Tier 3 during normal operations and survivable during most DPA events. Your biggest risk isn't priority tier — it's getting the conduit in the walls before the concrete dries. Let's solve that problem together.

### To the Concessions Consortium:

Your hard constraints are reasonable and compatible with ours. The turnkey managed service model (H3) is the right answer for 60 operators with diverse technical capabilities. We support your $250/month ceiling and your ACDBE accessibility requirements. The one area of tension — priority tier — is addressed by the time-sequenced degradation model. Your POS gets a 1 Mbps floor even during the worst DPA event. That's enough for credit card transactions. We'll jointly advocate for that floor.

### To AT&T:

Your H1 (spectrum authority) is accepted without reservation. Your O3 (enterprise SLA framework) is exactly what we need to see — put the safety-critical slice at 99.99%, <10ms latency, <0.1% packet loss in a binding term sheet and we'll negotiate the details.

Your H2 (CWP protection with 30-day review) needs the pre-authorized construction framework I've proposed, or it becomes a de facto veto over Terminal C construction. I don't think that's your intent — so let's solve it during Phase 0.

Your H5 (competitive evaluation protection) is reasonable for existing terminals. For Terminal F, I'd ask you to compete on merit in a greenfield evaluation where DFW can run production-grade alternatives alongside your offering. Your track record should win that competition. If it doesn't, the architecture is better served by learning that before we're locked in.

Your H6 (brand presence) is fine for passenger areas. Not for operational zones. Consistent with the CIO's position.

---

## Closing

I came into Round 1 with six hard constraints and five soft constraints. After reading every position at this table, I've confirmed all six hard constraints, added one new one (H7 — CWP review cannot delay Terminal C), refined two (H5 and H6), and identified three conflicts that require resolution before we can proceed to term sheets.

The conflicts are solvable. The priority hierarchy needs a time-sequenced degradation model, not a rigid tier war. The data ownership scope needs explicit tenant carve-outs. The CWP review process needs a pre-authorized construction framework. None of these are deal-breakers if people negotiate in good faith.

What I will not compromise on: 99.99% uptime measured at my devices, not AT&T's dashboard. Thirty-second automatic failover, tested monthly. Airline ramp operations at Tier 1 priority behind only safety-critical systems. Our operational data stays ours. Our slice configuration stays under our control.

We are the anchor tenant. We are prepared to commit 5 years, $8-12M in Terminal F co-investment, 800-900 devices on day one, and ramp expertise that no RF survey can replace. In return, we need infrastructure-grade reliability with contractual teeth.

Resolve the three conflicts. Then bring us the term sheets.

arguments.name: Cargo Director
arguments.content:
# Cargo Operator Consortium — Round 2 Position
## Constraint Reconciliation and Trade Proposals

---

## Status Assessment: Where We Stand After Reading All Positions

I've read every position carefully. The good news: there's more alignment than I expected. Everyone accepts the Hybrid Co-Investment architecture. Everyone agrees DFW owns infrastructure, AT&T operates the carrier layer, tenants pay for results. The CIO's security sovereignty, the CRO's data monetization framework, the Airline's slice isolation demands, AT&T's spectrum authority, Concessions' managed-service model — none of these fundamentally conflict with what the cargo consortium needs.

The bad news: **the CIO's phased deployment plan puts cargo dead last, and our warehouses will be finished before Phase 1 even starts.**

That is the single most consequential conflict in this entire negotiation, and I want to resolve it in this round before we spend another hour discussing revenue-share formulas.

---

## 1. REVISED POSITION — Satisfying All Peer HARD Constraints

I've gone through every peer's hard constraints and revised our position to accommodate each one. Here's how:

---

### CIO HC-1 (Zero CWP Degradation): **Fully Compatible — No Revision Needed**

Our cargo facilities are physically separated from passenger terminals by hundreds of meters. Menzies and dnata warehouses under construction are on the south side of the airfield complex, well outside the 500-foot CWP interference radius the CIO defined. We will stipulate contractually that cargo zone 5G deployment will not energize any radio equipment within the CIO's defined CWP protection perimeter without joint RF assessment. The cargo zone is the **safest place to deploy first** precisely because it poses zero CWP risk.

*This actually strengthens our case for Phase 0/1 priority — see Trade #1 below.*

---

### CIO HC-2 (DFW Owns All Infrastructure): **Revised — Accepting DFW Ownership with Structural Protections**

Our original position offered two options for our $15M co-investment: prepaid access fees *or* direct ownership of in-building infrastructure. I'm withdrawing the direct ownership option.

**Revised position:** DFW owns all cargo zone 5G infrastructure — small cells, edge compute nodes, fiber, mounting hardware — consistent with the CIO's HC-2 requirement. The consortium's capital contribution is structured as **prepaid infrastructure access fees** credited against future monthly fees, with the following protections:

- Consortium-funded infrastructure (the $200K-$400K Phase 0 conduit/mounting points we install during construction) transfers to DFW ownership upon 5G network activation, credited at cost against our access fees
- Our $15M co-investment commitment converts to a **10-year prepaid access agreement** with guaranteed capacity allocation proportional to our contribution
- If DFW terminates the cargo slice or materially degrades service below contracted SLAs, the unearned prepaid balance is refunded within 180 days

This gives the CIO clean ownership, gives us investment protection, and gives the CRO a bankable $15M demand commitment for the CapEx business case.

---

### CIO HC-3 (Security Sovereignty): **Fully Compatible — Accepted with Carve-Out**

We accept DFW's sovereign authority over security architecture, SIEM integration, cryptographic slice isolation, and federal directive response. Our cargo operations will feed security events into DFW's SIEM and comply with DFW's vulnerability patching timelines.

**One carve-out needed** (see Irreconcilable Conflict #1 below): Cold chain audit data must have an operator-controlled access path for FDA/IATA regulatory audits. This is not a security architecture exception — it's a data access pathway alongside DFW's SIEM integration.

---

### CIO HC-4 / CRO H-R1 (Data Ownership): **Revised — Accepting DFW Tier 1 Rights with Regulatory Data Carve-Out**

We accept DFW's ownership of Tier 1 aggregated/anonymized data, including foot traffic patterns, device density, and dwell-time analytics derived from the cargo zone common areas (truck courts, cargo ramp, shared circulation areas).

**However, our HC-4 (Cold Chain Compliance Certification) creates a hard regulatory conflict with unlimited data ownership claims. See Irreconcilable Conflict #1 below for the specific issue and proposed resolution.**

We also stipulate — aligned with the Airline VP's H1 position — that **operator-specific operational data** (WMS telemetry, AGV navigation data, sort facility throughput, shipment tracking, cold chain sensor readings) is operator-owned. DFW's Tier 1 data rights apply to network-infrastructure-derived analytics, not to application-layer data generated by our systems running on the network.

---

### CIO HC-5 (FCC Part 96 / SAS): **Fully Compatible — No Revision Needed**

We fully support AT&T's real-time spectrum management authority (AT&T H1) and the CIO's FCC compliance requirements. Our cargo SLAs (HC-3) are written to survive DPA events through the priority hierarchy, not by overriding spectrum management. We want AT&T to manage CBRS aggressively and compliantly — our cold chain failover requirements (5-second automatic switchover) are our own redundancy layer, not an imposition on spectrum management.

---

### CIO HC-6 (Phased Deployment): **THIS IS THE CRITICAL CONFLICT — See Trade #1**

The CIO's phase plan sequences cargo in **Phase 5** — the last deployment phase, approximately 4.5-5 years from contract execution. This is flatly incompatible with our HC-1 (independent cargo zone deployment, Q3 2025 specs, Q1 2026 go-live). However, I believe this is a **sequencing conflict, not an architectural conflict**, and I have a specific trade proposal to resolve it.

---

### CIO HC-7 (Carrier Succession Path): **Fully Compatible — Supportive**

We endorse the warm-standby succession requirement. Our $20B annual economic impact depends on network continuity. We will participate in annual succession readiness assessments and contribute operational requirements to the transition documentation repository.

---

### Airline VP H6 (Priority Hierarchy): **Partially Compatible — Trade Needed**

The Airline VP proposes cargo at **Tier 3**, below airline ramp ops (Tier 1) and federal inspection (Tier 2). Our original position requested cargo be the "last non-safety slice degraded." I'm revising this — see Trade #2 below.

---

### AT&T H1 (Carrier-Layer Autonomy): **Fully Compatible**

We accept AT&T's real-time spectrum management authority. Sub-250ms DPA response is not something we want a governance committee reviewing. Our SLAs are designed to survive DPA events through the priority hierarchy and our own 5-second cold chain failover — not by overriding AT&T's spectrum decisions.

---

### AT&T H2 (CWP Protection / 50m Buffer): **Compatible — Cargo Zone is Outside Buffer**

Our facilities are hundreds of meters from terminal CWP APs. The 50-meter review buffer and 30-day RF impact assessment do not affect our deployment timeline. We will confirm this with a joint survey in the first 30 days.

---

### AT&T H4 (Physical Access / SIDA Badges): **Compatible — We'll Advocate for Cargo Zone Access**

We support AT&T's 12-badge minimum and will advocate for cargo-zone-specific SIDA access for AT&T technicians supporting our facilities. 45-minute on-site response SLA is acceptable for cargo zone incidents if AT&T technicians have pre-authorized cargo area access.

---

### AT&T H5 (Competitive Evaluation Protection): **Compatible**

We accept that AT&T is the carrier-operations partner for the cargo zone under the same competitive protections. We are not proposing an alternative carrier operator for cargo — we're proposing an accelerated timeline within the same architecture.

---

### AT&T H6 (Brand Presence): **Compatible — Minor Adjustment**

"Powered by AT&T" co-branding on cargo tenant service agreements is acceptable. We'll note that customer-facing pharmaceutical compliance documentation (cold chain certificates) will carry the cargo operator's brand, not AT&T's or DFW's — this is a regulatory formatting requirement, not a branding objection.

---

### Concessions H1-H7: **Fully Compatible — Separate Tenant Category**

All concessions hard constraints relate to POS connectivity, managed service delivery, and F&B operator requirements. These do not conflict with cargo operations. We support the concessions consortium's turnkey managed service model and tiered pricing — it's a different use case requiring a different service model, and the architecture should accommodate both.

We specifically support Concessions H6 (transaction data ownership) — the principle that application-layer business data belongs to the operator, not the network owner, applies equally to our cold chain and logistics data.

---

## 2. PROPOSED TRADES

---

### **TRADE #1: Cargo Zone as Phase 1A — The Construction Window Trade** *(Critical)*

**What I need:** Cargo zone elevated from Phase 5 to a parallel **Phase 1A** track, running concurrently with the CIO's Phase 0 baseline measurement and Phase 1 Terminal A trial. Specifically:

| Phase | Timeline | Activity |
|-------|----------|----------|
| **Phase 0-Cargo** | Immediately (next 45 days) | DFW approves cargo zone RF design specs; consortium embeds conduit, mounting points, fiber pathways in active Menzies/dnata construction. Cost: $200-400K, consortium-funded. |
| **Phase 1A-Cargo** | Q3 2025 – Q1 2026 | Menzies and dnata warehouses go live with cargo 5G slice. AGV and cold chain use cases operational. 50+ small cells, 2 edge compute nodes. |
| **Phase 2A-Cargo** | 2026-2027 | FedEx and UPS facilities integrated. Full cargo complex operational. |

This runs **in parallel** with the CIO's terminal phasing — not instead of it. Cargo Phase 1A does not consume terminal resources, does not touch CWP infrastructure, and does not require terminal RF coordination.

**What I'm giving up:**
1. **Direct ownership of in-building infrastructure** — DFW owns everything, per CIO HC-2. Our $15M becomes prepaid access fees, not equity.
2. **Consortium PAL/GAA spectrum rights** (our SC-1) — we drop our preference for direct CBRS license participation and accept AT&T as sole spectrum operator for the cargo zone, giving AT&T full spectrum management authority per AT&T H1.
3. **Guaranteed demand commitment extended to 5 years** (from 3 years) — six anchor tenants locked in for 5 years instead of 3, providing $20-30M in total committed recurring revenue that de-risks DFW's CapEx allocation and AT&T's operational investment.

**What this gives each stakeholder:**

| Stakeholder | Value Received |
|-------------|---------------|
| **CIO** | Risk-free proof-of-concept in a physically isolated zone. Zero CWP impact. Real-world performance data for DPA events, indoor coverage, and automation SLAs before touching a single terminal AP. Validates Phase 1 design assumptions. Cargo becomes the CIO's **test environment**, not an afterthought. |
| **CRO** | $4-6M/year in recurring cargo revenue starting Year 1 — the earliest monetizable tenant category. Anchors the Year 2 ROI requirement (H-R3) with hard revenue before concession platform launches at month 30. Cargo showcase drives tenant adoption confidence for all other categories. |
| **Airline VP** | AGV navigation on 5G at the cargo complex validates the same network slice isolation and automation-grade SLAs the airline needs for Terminal F ramp automation. Cargo proves the architecture works before airline migrates a single iPad. De-risks the airline's Phase 3/4 commitment. |
| **AT&T** | Flagship indoor 5G deployment — 2M sq ft of warehouse automation on CBRS — with zero CWP transition risk. The cleanest deployment AT&T will ever have at DFW. Accelerates AT&T's ability to reference DFW for enterprise cargo/logistics sales (O5 value). Proves AT&T's SLA framework in production before terminal-scale deployment. |
| **Concessions** | Cargo go-live produces the operational data and reference architecture that validates the managed-service model concessions need. When concessions pilot at month 18-24, the network has 12+ months of production operation behind it. Reduces concessions migration risk. |

**What I need in return from each stakeholder:**

- **CIO:** Written authorization for Phase 0-Cargo within 45 days. Cargo zone RF design specs issued as a priority deliverable. Cargo represented in Phase 0 baseline measurement planning.
- **AT&T:** Dedicated RF engineering resources for cargo zone indoor coverage design within 60 days. Commitment to deploy and operate cargo zone carrier layer on the Phase 1A timeline. Pre-positioning of SAS registration for cargo zone small cells.
- **CRO:** Cargo recurring revenue included in the Year 2 ROI model (H-R3) as committed demand, not aspirational pipeline.

**Why this trade works:** It costs the terminal deployment **nothing** in timeline, resources, or risk. Cargo is physically separate. Cargo self-funds Phase 0. Cargo brings guaranteed demand. Cargo gives everyone a production proof-of-concept 18-24 months before the first terminal migration. The only thing it requires is a **decision** — and that decision has a 45-day expiration because the concrete doesn't wait.

---

### **TRADE #2: Priority Hierarchy — Accepting Tier 3 with a Hard Performance Floor** *(With Airline VP)*

**What I'm giving up:** Our original position that cargo be the "last non-safety slice degraded." I accept the Airline VP's proposed priority hierarchy:

1. Tier 0: Safety-critical (FAA/TSA)
2. Tier 1: Airline ramp operations
3. **Tier 2: Federal inspection (CBP, TSA checkpoint)**
4. **Tier 3: Cargo operations** ← We accept this position
5. Tier 4: Airport operations
6. Tier 5: Commercial (concessions, passenger Wi-Fi)

The Airline VP is right that 900 daily departures carry more immediate operational risk than cargo sort operations. I acknowledge that.

**What I need in return:**

1. **Hard performance floor during degradation:** Even when cargo is deprioritized during DPA or congestion events, the cargo slice cannot fall below:
   - AGV control: ≤ 50ms latency (degraded from our 20ms SLA, but still functional for reduced-speed AGV operation)
   - Cold chain monitoring: ≤ 200ms latency (degraded from 100ms SLA, still within FDA continuous-monitoring compliance)
   - Availability: no complete cargo slice outage exceeding 60 seconds during any degradation event
   
2. **Advance notification:** Minimum 30 seconds automated alert to cargo operations when the cargo slice is being deprioritized, so our WMS can switch AGVs to reduced-speed mode and activate cold chain backup telemetry paths.

3. **Restoration priority:** When capacity is restored after a DPA event or congestion period, cargo slice restoration is prioritized immediately after Tiers 0-2, before commercial tiers restore to full capacity.

**What this gives the Airline VP:** Unambiguous Tier 1 priority without cargo challenging the hierarchy. Our acceptance eliminates a potential multi-party dispute and gives the airline a clean negotiating position on priority.

**What this gives Concessions:** Confirms that POS traffic (which Concessions wants at Tier 2) has a clear path — the discussion is between Concessions and the Airline VP on whether POS belongs at Tier 2 or Tier 5. We're not competing for that slot.

---

### **TRADE #3: Edge Compute Model — Shared Platform with Isolated Tenancy** *(With CIO)*

**What I'm giving up:** Our SC-2 preference for fully independent edge compute at each cargo warehouse. I'll accept a shared edge compute platform per the CIO's architectural vision.

**What I need in return:**
- Cryptographically isolated tenant environments per our SC-2 trade terms: root-level access to our partition, guaranteed compute/storage allocation (not oversubscribed), annual isolation audit by our security team
- **Physical edge compute nodes located within each major cargo warehouse** (Menzies, dnata, FedEx, UPS) or within 500m fiber run — this is a latency physics requirement for ≤20ms AGV control, not a governance preference
- Our operators' proprietary WMS, AGV fleet management, and cold chain platforms run on our partitions with no DFW or AT&T access to application-layer data

**What I'm giving the CIO:** Architectural consistency across the campus. One edge compute management plane, one security framework, one patch management process. Cargo doesn't become a rogue compute island that the CIO's team can't monitor for security compliance.

---

### **TRADE #4: AT&T Brand and Reference Rights** *(With AT&T)*

**What I'm offering AT&T:** The cargo consortium will actively participate as a reference customer for AT&T's enterprise logistics and supply chain 5G sales efforts. We'll host site visits, participate in case studies, and present at industry events (TIACA, Air Cargo Conference, MWC). AGV navigation on 5G in a 2M sq ft cargo complex is the most compelling proof point AT&T's logistics sales team could ask for.

**What I need in return:** AT&T dedicates RF engineering resources to cargo zone indoor coverage design within 60 days per Trade #1. AT&T's cargo zone SLA commitment matches our HC-3 requirements (≤20ms AGV latency, ≤100ms cold chain, 99.99% availability per warehouse zone). AT&T treats the cargo zone as a flagship deployment, not a secondary coverage extension.

---

### **TRADE #5: Cost Structure Flexibility** *(With CRO)*

**What I'm giving up:** Our preferred infrastructure rate of $0.15-$0.25/sq ft/month.

**What I'll accept:** Up to **$0.30/sq ft/month** (midpoint of our acceptable range) if the automation-grade SLAs in our HC-3 are included in the base tier and edge compute allocation per Trade #3 is included without premium charges.

**What this gives the CRO:** Higher per-sq-ft revenue from cargo ($600K-$720K/year at $0.30/sq ft across 2M+ sq ft) that materially strengthens the Year 2 ROI case. Combined with the 5-year commitment from Trade #1, this is **$3-3.6M in committed cargo infrastructure revenue over 5 years** before variable service fees.

**What I need in return:** The CRO includes cargo zone revenue in the H-R3 Year 2 ROI model and advocates for Phase 1A cargo deployment as a revenue-acceleration strategy, not just a technology strategy.

---

## 3. IRRECONCILABLE CONFLICTS

---

### **Conflict #1: Cold Chain Data Sovereignty vs. DFW Data Ownership**

**The conflict:** CIO HC-4 and CRO H-R1 assert DFW owns "all network-generated data." Our HC-4 requires that cold chain monitoring data — temperature readings, humidity logs, GPS/location tracking of pharmaceutical shipments, environmental excursion alerts — be **independently controlled by the cargo operator** for FDA 21 CFR Part 211 compliance, EU GDP audits, and IATA CEIV Pharma certification.

**Why this is genuinely irreconcilable without a carve-out:** When an FDA auditor requests cold chain records for a pharmaceutical shipment that transited DFW, the cargo operator — not DFW, not AT&T — is the regulated entity. The operator must produce tamper-evident, cryptographically hashed, continuous monitoring records with zero gaps. If those records reside in a DFW-owned data layer, and DFW has modification or access-control authority, the **chain of custody is broken** under ALCOA+ data integrity principles. An FDA 483 observation (or worse, a Warning Letter) for data integrity failure on cold chain records would be catastrophic for any operator's pharma business.

This is not about commercial data monetization. DFW's Tier 1 analytics from cargo zone foot traffic and vehicle patterns are fine — own them. But cold chain sensor data is **regulatory compliance evidence**, not a network analytics product.

**Proposed resolution:** Add a **Tier 3 data category** to the CIO/CRO framework:

| Data Tier | Owner | Examples |
|-----------|-------|----------|
| Tier 1 (Monetizable) | DFW — exclusive | Passenger/vehicle flow, device density, dwell time, zone analytics |
| Tier 2 (Operational) | AT&T — operational use only | RF telemetry, SAS coordination, spectrum utilization |
| **Tier 3 (Regulatory Compliance)** | **Tenant operator — exclusive** | Cold chain monitoring, pharmaceutical audit trails, customs documentation, safety-mandated records, POS transaction data (per Concessions H6) |

**Tier 3 data transits the network but is never ingested, stored, modified, or accessed by DFW's analytics platform or AT&T's operational systems.** It flows from sensor to operator's application on the operator's edge compute partition, encrypted end-to-end with operator-managed keys. DFW cannot claim ownership of data it never touches.

This resolves the Concessions H6 conflict simultaneously — POS transaction data is Tier 3, operator-owned. The CIO and CRO lose nothing of monetization value (cold chain sensor readings and credit card transactions are not part of the $6-13M opportunity). The regulated operators gain the independent data control their regulators require.

**If this carve-out cannot be agreed, the cargo consortium will encrypt all cold chain and operational data end-to-end at the application layer, rendering it invisible to the network. This achieves the same result unilaterally but eliminates any possibility of DFW deriving even anonymized value from cargo zone network analytics. We'd prefer the cooperative approach.**

---

### **Conflict #2: CIO Phased Timeline vs. Cargo Construction Reality** *(Potentially Resolvable via Trade #1)*

I've flagged this above and proposed Trade #1 as the resolution. If Trade #1 is rejected — if the cargo zone is held to Phase 5 sequencing — then this becomes irreconcilable and we proceed to our walk-away: standalone CBRS private network under the consortium's own licenses, architecturally incompatible with the campus-wide framework.

I want to be transparent about why the walk-away is real. Our general contractors have given us a **90-day decision window** for embedded infrastructure. After that, the warehouse walls close. Not metaphorically. Physically. The drywall goes up, the insulation goes in, the ceiling grid closes. Every week of delay past that window adds approximately **$150,000 in future retrofit cost** and degrades achievable coverage performance by measurable amounts due to suboptimal antenna placement.

I am not willing to accept $1.5M+ in retrofit costs and 60% performance degradation because the governance process for a multi-stakeholder campus-wide architecture wasn't ready in time for our construction schedule. The architecture must accommodate the construction reality, not the other way around.

---

### **No Other Irreconcilable Conflicts Identified**

All other peer hard constraints are compatible with our revised position. The remaining issues are priority hierarchy (addressed in Trade #2), edge compute model (Trade #3), and commercial terms (Trades #4 and #5) — all of which have viable trade space.

---

## 4. UPDATED CONSTRAINTS

Based on my review of all peer positions, here are my revised constraints:

### Hard Constraints — Maintained with Revisions

| Constraint | Original | Revised | Reason |
|------------|----------|---------|--------|
| **HC-1: Independent Cargo Zone Timeline** | Unchanged | **Strengthened** — Now structured as Phase 1A parallel track with specific stakeholder value propositions | Trade #1 provides the mechanism |
| **HC-2: Indoor Warehouse Coverage Density** | Unchanged | Unchanged — No peer conflicts | Still critical |
| **HC-3: Automation-Grade SLAs** | Unchanged | **Added degradation floor** for Tier 3 priority operation (≤50ms AGV, ≤200ms cold chain during DPA events) | Trade #2 accommodation |
| **HC-4: Cold Chain Compliance** | Required operator-controlled data | **Expanded** to propose Tier 3 data category applicable to all regulated operators | Conflict #1 resolution — benefits Concessions too |
| **NEW HC-5: Phase 0 Decision Deadline** | N/A | **DFW must authorize cargo zone Phase 0 infrastructure embedding within 45 days of this negotiation round concluding.** This is a hard deadline driven by construction physics, not negotiating tactics. | Construction window closure |

### Soft Constraints — Revised

| Constraint | Original | Revised | Reason |
|------------|----------|---------|--------|
| **SC-1: Direct CBRS PAL** | Preferred consortium spectrum | **Withdrawn** — Accept AT&T sole spectrum management | Trade #1 concession to AT&T |
| **SC-2: Edge Compute** | Preferred independent hosting | **Revised** — Accept shared platform with isolated tenancy | Trade #3 with CIO |
| **SC-3: Cost Structure** | $0.15-$0.25/sq ft | **Revised ceiling to $0.30/sq ft** if automation SLAs included in base | Trade #5 with CRO |
| **SC-4: Phased Timeline** | Q1 2026 go-live, Q2 acceptable | Unchanged — Q2 2026 acceptable if Phase 0 authorized immediately | Construction window still binding |
| **NEW SC-5: Governance Representation** | N/A | **Cargo consortium permanent seat** on multi-tenant priority governance body, aligned with Concessions S3 | Multiple stakeholders requesting governance voice |
| **NEW SC-6: AT&T SLA Financial Penalties** | 2x daily fee per breach (original HC-3) | **Revised** — Accept AT&T's tiered credit structure (O3) for commercial operations, but retain **enhanced penalties for cold chain compliance breaches** (any data gap >30 seconds triggering $25,000 per incident due to pharmaceutical regulatory exposure) | Acknowledging AT&T's SLA framework while protecting regulated operations |

### Offers — Updated

| Offer | Original | Revised | Reason |
|-------|----------|---------|--------|
| **O-1: Capital Contribution** | Up to $15M, two structures | **$15M as prepaid access fees** — DFW owns infrastructure | CIO HC-2 compliance |
| **O-2: Anchor Tenancy** | 3-year minimum, 6 tenants | **5-year minimum**, 6 tenants, $4-6M/year | Trade #1 — increased commitment for Phase 1A priority |
| **O-3: Reference Architecture** | Available for DFW tenant recruitment | **Extended to AT&T** — joint reference customer for logistics/supply chain enterprise sales | Trade #4 |
| **O-4: Construction Coordination** | $200-400K consortium-funded, 45-day decision | Unchanged — this is the time-critical offer | Still the highest-leverage, lowest-cost item on the table |
| **O-5: Operational Data Sharing** | Anonymized AGV patterns, seasonal curves | **Enhanced** — Will contribute to DFW Tier 1 analytics with anonymized cargo zone traffic and vehicle movement data | Supports CRO monetization while protecting Tier 3 operator data |
| **NEW O-6: Cross-Stakeholder Validation** | N/A | **Cargo go-live data shared with all stakeholders** to validate SLA performance, slice isolation, DPA survivability, and automation-grade latency before terminal deployments proceed. Cargo is the proof-of-concept for the entire architecture. | Value proposition for every constituency |

---

## Closing: The Ask

I have revised my position to satisfy every peer's hard constraints. I have dropped our spectrum rights preference, accepted DFW infrastructure ownership, accepted Tier 3 priority, accepted shared edge compute, and increased our financial commitment from 3 years to 5 years and raised our acceptable cost ceiling.

**In return, I need one thing: a 45-day decision on Phase 0 cargo zone authorization.**

Not a 45-day decision on the entire hybrid architecture. Not a 45-day decision on the AT&T contract. Just authorization for our general contractors to embed conduit, cable trays, antenna mounting points, and fiber pathways into warehouses that are being built right now. That's $200-400K of consortium money going into DFW-owned infrastructure, creating a 20-year option on embedded 5G that costs DFW nothing and forecloses nothing.

The full cargo zone Phase 1A deployment proceeds on the campus-wide architectural framework, using DFW-owned infrastructure, with AT&T as carrier operator, under the security and data governance framework everyone in this room is negotiating. We're not asking for a separate architecture. We're asking to build the **first module** of the shared architecture on a timeline driven by physical construction, not committee sequencing.

Every month of delay on this decision converts a $200-400K embedded infrastructure cost into a $1.2-1.8M retrofit cost and locks 2M+ square feet of cargo facilities into 20 years of suboptimal wireless performance. That is a net present value destruction of **$8-12M** against the cargo consortium's automation roadmap.

I need the CIO to say yes on Phase 0 authorization. I need AT&T to assign RF engineering resources. I need the CRO to count our revenue in the Year 2 model. The airline, concessions, and everyone else benefits from our going first and proving the architecture works.

Forty-five days. The concrete doesn't wait.

arguments.name: Concessions Tech Lead
arguments.content:
# Round 2 Position: F&B Concessions Consortium
## Revised After Peer Constraint Review

---

## Preamble

I've read every position on this table carefully. The good news: this architecture can work for concessions. The CIO's infrastructure ownership model gives us an accountable landlord. The CRO's concession-specific protections (H-R4, H-R5) are aligned with our needs. AT&T's enterprise SLA framework includes a commercial slice at 99.9% availability with ≤50ms latency, which maps directly to our POS requirements.

The bad news: there are three areas where peer hard constraints conflict with ours or create risks that will silently destroy our operators' ability to do business. I'm going to address each directly.

---

## 1. REVISED POSITION — Satisfying All Peer Hard Constraints

I've mapped every peer hard constraint against our position. Here's where we stand:

### Peer Constraints We Fully Support

| Peer | Constraint | Our Position |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| CIO HC-1 | Zero CWP degradation during transition | Directly supports our H7. We're the largest per-location victim of CWP disruption. Strong support. |
| CIO HC-2 | DFW owns all infrastructure below carrier layer | This is why we support the architecture. DFW-as-landlord means one accountable party. |
| CIO HC-3 | DFW sovereign security authority | Supported. Our PCI-DSS compliance is simpler under a single security governance. |
| CIO HC-5 | FCC Part 96 / SAS compliance | Supported. We don't touch spectrum — just make the POS work. |
| CIO HC-6 | Phased deployment with rollback | Strongly supported. Matches our H7 per-location cutover requirement. We add: no concession cutover during Nov 15–Jan 5 or Mar 1–Mar 20 blackout periods. |
| CIO HC-7 | Carrier succession planning | Supported. Reduces our dependency risk. |
| CRO H-R2 | Tenant platform live Month 30 | Supported, with caveat: concession POS connectivity ≠ tenant monetization platform. POS must be live earlier. Our pilot locations (O4) should be live by Month 12. |
| CRO H-R3 | Positive ROI Year 2 | Supported. Our guaranteed 200+ location adoption (O1) contributes materially to this math. |
| CRO H-R5 | Tiered ACDBE pricing | Strongly supported. We'll operationalize this — the consortium handles ACDBE operator onboarding. |
| Airline H1 | Dedicated slice with cryptographic isolation | Supported and extended — we need the same principle for POS traffic. |
| Airline H3 | 30-second failover | We adopt this concept for POS: automatic failover to cellular backup within 60 seconds for POS transactions. |
| Airline H4 | No forced device migration | Directly parallel to our H5. Complete alignment. |
| Cargo HC-1 | Independent cargo zone timeline | No conflict. Cargo's urgency is construction-driven; ours is tenant-adoption-driven. Different critical paths. |
| Cargo HC-4 | Cold chain data ownership | Supports our principle: operational data belongs to the operator, not the network owner. |
| AT&T H1 | Carrier-layer operational authority for spectrum | Accepted. We have zero interest in spectrum management. Make the radio work; we'll plug in our POS. |
| AT&T H4 | Operational staff access and badging | Supported, with note: AT&T technicians servicing concession-area CPE must coordinate with location operators on timing. Don't walk behind a kitchen counter during lunch rush. |
| AT&T H5 | Competitive evaluation protection | No objection, provided it doesn't delay concession connectivity deployment. |

### Peer Constraints We Accept With Modifications

**AT&T H2 — CWP Investment Recognition and 50-Meter RF Impact Zone**

I understand protecting the live CWP. But the 50-meter / 30-day review requirement creates a serious problem for concessions. Our locations are *inside terminals*, often within 10-20 meters of existing CWP access points. A 30-day RF impact review for every concession CPE installation means our 200+ location rollout takes years, not months.

**Our modification:** We accept the RF impact assessment requirement for *new small cell installations and fiber construction* within 50 meters of CWP APs. But the per-location concession CPE gateway — a low-power indoor device connecting to existing 5G small cells — should be categorized as a **pre-approved device class** requiring only a 5-business-day notification (not a 30-day review). These devices operate at power levels comparable to a Wi-Fi router. They do not create meaningful RF interference with CWP APs.

**Rationale:** If AT&T can't distinguish between a new small cell installation and a CPE gateway plugged into an ethernet port, their RF engineering team needs recalibration. The CIO should support this — it's consistent with the phased rollout philosophy while avoiding bureaucratic bottleneck on the simplest deployment step.

**AT&T H6 — Brand Presence in Tenant-Facing Products**

We accept "Powered by AT&T" in service documentation, SLA agreements, and the concession connectivity service portal that DFW manages. We do not accept AT&T branding visible to *our customers* inside concession locations. No AT&T logos on CPE hardware facing the customer. No co-branded elements in our point-of-sale experience. Our operators' brands, not AT&T's, face the passenger.

**CRO H-R4 — $15K/Location/Day Penalty**

We support this and align our H7 transition penalty to match: any concession location POS downtime exceeding 4 hours attributable to 5G deployment activities triggers $15K/location/day, consistent with CRO's framework. This is simpler than our original trailing-30-day-revenue approach and creates a uniform standard the CRO can enforce across the portfolio.

---

## 2. IRRECONCILABLE CONFLICTS FLAGGED

### ⚠️ CONFLICT 1: Priority Hierarchy — POS Transactions Are Not "Passenger Wi-Fi Offload"

**The problem:** The airline's proposed priority hierarchy (H6) places all commercial concession traffic at **Tier 5** — the absolute bottom, lumped together with advertising and passenger Wi-Fi offload:

> *Tier 5: Commercial — Concessionaires, advertising, passenger Wi-Fi offload*

This is unacceptable and I need every person at this table to understand why.

A passenger streaming Netflix on gate Wi-Fi is Tier 5 traffic. Fair enough. But a credit card authorization at a POS terminal is a **financial transaction**. When that transaction fails, the operator loses a sale that never comes back. Multiply by 200+ locations, 87 million passengers, and peak boarding pushes where 3,000 people are buying coffee simultaneously — this is not a "nice to have" tier.

PCI-DSS compliance also requires that payment card data traverse a network segment with defined security controls and guaranteed availability. Lumping POS authorizations with passenger TikTok traffic in the same priority tier is not just operationally foolish — it creates a compliance exposure.

**Our requirement:** The priority hierarchy must **split concession POS transactions into a separate sub-tier above general commercial traffic:**

| Tier | Category |
|------|----------|
| 0 | Safety-critical (FAA/TSA, emergency) |
| 1 | Airline ramp operations |
| 2 | Federal inspection (CBP, TSA checkpoint) |
| 3 | **Concession POS + Financial Transactions** |
| 3 | Cargo operations automation |
| 4 | Airport operations (DFW facility management) |
| 5 | Commercial IoT, digital signage, advertising |
| 6 | Passenger Wi-Fi offload, best-effort |

I am not asking to be above airlines or federal agencies. I am asking not to be at the bottom of a priority stack where a DPA event that cuts capacity by 40% means my operators' registers go dark while someone's Netflix keeps buffering.

**To the airline:** Your ramp operations are Tier 1. I don't contest that. But your passengers are buying $4.2 billion in product from our locations. When our registers fail, your passengers have a worse experience. Our POS connectivity supports your customer experience even if it doesn't support your pushback timing.

**To Cargo:** I'll accept POS and cargo automation as co-equal Tier 3. Your AGVs and our registers both represent real-time operational transactions that can't be retried later. We don't need to fight each other for bandwidth — we need to both be above advertising and passenger browsing.

**Status: This is a new HARD constraint. H2 is revised to include Tier 3 minimum for POS traffic.**

---

### ⚠️ CONFLICT 2: CRO Data Ownership at Concession-Storefront Granularity

**The problem:** CRO H-R1 claims DFW owns "device density heat maps" and "location-based service data" at **"concession-storefront granularity (≤3m accuracy)."**

Let me be precise about what this means. At 3-meter accuracy, DFW's network analytics can determine:
- How many devices are inside *each individual concession location* at any moment
- How long those devices dwell at each specific operator's counter
- Traffic patterns showing which locations passengers visit before and after a specific operator
- Comparative foot traffic between directly competing concession locations

This is **individual operator performance data derived through network observation.** It tells you which operators are winning and losing the foot traffic competition, without any operator voluntarily sharing that information. Under my H6, this is exactly the kind of operator-specific performance data that belongs to the operator, not the landlord.

I am not being paranoid. DFW is our landlord. DFW negotiates our lease terms, including percentage-rent calculations. If DFW's data analytics platform knows — from network telemetry — that Operator A's foot traffic is 30% higher than Operator B's at comparable locations, that is actionable intelligence in lease renewal negotiations. Our operators did not consent to that surveillance by plugging into a network.

**Our requirement — REVISED H6:**

- DFW's Tier 1 data product may include **zone-level** foot traffic analytics (e.g., "Terminal C food court cluster") but **NOT individual-concession-location-level** traffic, dwell, or density data without the specific operator's written consent
- The minimum geographic resolution for any concession-area analytics in DFW's data products is **concession zone** (defined as a cluster of 5+ locations), not individual storefront
- DFW may offer individual-location analytics **back to the specific operator** as part of the analytics subscription (per CRO's S-R2 and my S5) — but that data flows to the operator, not into DFW's general monetization pool
- An independent data architecture audit at contract signing verifies that the analytics pipeline enforces this resolution floor

**To the CRO:** I understand you want to monetize this data. I support the $6-13M opportunity. But the path to that number runs through tenant cooperation, not tenant surveillance. If our operators believe the network is being used to extract their competitive intelligence, they will resist adoption — and your guaranteed 200+ location revenue base evaporates. Give us zone-level resolution in the general product, operator-specific resolution as a subscription *to the operator*, and we'll be your most enthusiastic data contributor.

**Status: H6 is revised and elevated. This is non-negotiable.**

---

### ⚠️ CONFLICT 3: CRO Month 30 Platform vs. Our Month 12 POS Connectivity

**The problem:** CRO H-R2 requires the tenant monetization platform live by Month 30 and revenue-generating by Month 36. That's the commercial analytics and IoT platform. Fine. But our operators need **POS connectivity** — the basic "register works on 5G" service — far earlier than Month 30.

If the architecture is sequenced to deliver the monetization platform first and basic POS connectivity second, our operators are stuck on degrading CWP for 2.5 years while the analytics engine gets built. That reverses the priority. The foundation is connectivity; the monetization layer sits on top.

**Our requirement:**
- Pilot locations (10-15 per our O4) live with 5G POS connectivity by **Month 12**
- General availability of POS connectivity service across all terminals by **Month 18-24**
- Monetization platform, IoT slice, analytics dashboards by **Month 30** per CRO timeline

This is not a conflict with the CRO — it's a sequencing clarification. The CRO wants tenant monetization by Month 30. We want the POS foundation by Month 18. Both can be true on the CIO's phased timeline if concession POS is explicitly included in Phase 1-2, not deferred to Phase 3-4.

**To the CIO:** Your Phase 1 is Terminal A cargo-side. Your Phase 2 is Terminal C rebuild. Neither explicitly includes concession POS. I need concession pilot locations embedded in Phase 1 alongside cargo, and general concession rollout in Phase 2. We have 10-15 pilot locations ready (O4) — this is not a stretch.

**Status: New HARD constraint — H8: POS connectivity general availability by Month 24.**

---

## 3. PROPOSED TRADES

### Trade 1: Priority Hierarchy for Adoption Guarantee

**We give:** Binding consortium commitment to migrate all 200+ locations within 12 months of general availability (O1), plus single-point-of-coordination for 60 operators (O2) — saving DFW and AT&T the overhead of 60 individual tenant negotiations.

**We need:** POS traffic classified at Tier 3 (not Tier 5) in the priority hierarchy, with a hard floor of 1 Mbps per location even during maximum degradation events.

**Why this trade works:** Our adoption commitment is the single largest de-risking factor in the tenant revenue model. 200+ locations at $200/month is $480K/year guaranteed. AT&T's SLA framework already defines the commercial slice at 99.9% / ≤50ms — we're just asking for that commitment to be reflected in the priority stack. The bandwidth cost of guaranteeing 1 Mbps × 200 locations = 200 Mbps floor during worst-case DPA events. That is a rounding error on a campus-wide CBRS deployment.

### Trade 2: Data Contribution for Data Resolution Floor

**We give:** Anonymized foot traffic counts, dwell time by zone, and peak demand timing patterns (O3) — enriching DFW's Tier 1 analytics product with commercial behavior data that pure network telemetry can't capture. Plus willingness to pay a reasonable analytics subscription for zone-level passenger flow data useful to our operators (S5).
