**We need:** Contractual prohibition on individual-concession-location-level analytics in DFW's general data products. Zone-level minimum resolution (5+ location clusters) for concession area analytics. Operator-specific data available only to that operator via paid subscription.

**Why this trade works:** The CRO's data monetization business case is actually *stronger* with our voluntary contribution than with forced network-derived surveillance. Our foot traffic data includes *actual commercial zone entry*, not just device proximity. Our dwell data correlates with *purchasing behavior*, not just signal strength. Voluntary, structured data sharing produces a higher-quality analytics product than passive network observation — and it keeps 60 operators as willing participants rather than hostile subjects.

### Trade 3: AT&T CWP Timeline for Expedited Concession CPE Approval

**We give:** Full support for AT&T's CWP protection during transition (H2), including the 50-meter RF impact zone for new small cell and fiber construction. We will advocate with the CIO to maintain this protection.

**We need:** Pre-approved device class for concession CPE gateways — 5-business-day notification period instead of 30-day review. These are low-power customer-premises devices, not radio infrastructure.

**Why this trade works:** AT&T gets the CWP protection they need for the heavy infrastructure work. We get the deployment velocity we need for 200+ locations. The CIO gets a framework that distinguishes infrastructure changes (high-risk, full review) from endpoint provisioning (low-risk, expedited). Everyone's hard constraints are satisfied.

### Trade 4: Contract Flexibility for Pricing Certainty

**We give:** Willingness to accept the CIO's proposed 10-year term (SC-2) rather than our preferred shorter commitment. We also accept AT&T's 60-month exclusivity (S2) — we have no preference on who operates the carrier layer as long as our POS works.

**We need:** Per-location monthly pricing locked at ≤$250/month with annual escalation capped at CPI or 3% (whichever is lower) for the full contract term. Volume discount trigger at 175+ locations (15% reduction). Most-favored-tenant clause ensuring we're never paying more per-connection than comparable service tiers offered to other tenants.

**Why this trade works:** The CIO and AT&T both want longer terms for investment certainty. We provide that certainty. In exchange, our operators get pricing predictability for the full term — no mid-contract repricing that blows up an ACDBE operator's margin model.

### Trade 5: Pilot Acceleration for Phase Alignment

**We give:** 10-15 volunteer pilot locations across 3+ terminals (O4), including Star Concessions ACDBE locations (O6), available for Phase 1 deployment alongside cargo zone. Pilot operators accept parallel-operation overhead and provide structured feedback.

**We need:** Concession POS pilot locations explicitly included in Phase 1 of the CIO's deployment timeline — not deferred to Phase 2 or later. General POS availability by Month 24.

**Why this trade works:** The CIO's Phase 1 is Terminal A cargo-side. Adding 10-15 concession pilot locations in Terminal A (and other terminals) validates the POS use case early, provides the CRO with proof-of-concept data for the Month 30 platform launch, and gives AT&T a multi-use-case reference architecture. The cargo consortium wants to go first — we want to go *simultaneously*, in a different zone. No resource conflict.

---

## 4. UPDATED CONSTRAINT SET

### HARD CONSTRAINTS (Revised)

| ID | Constraint | Change from Round 1 |
|----|-----------|---------------------|
| H1 | 99.9% POS connectivity per location per month | **Unchanged** — validated by AT&T's SLA framework (commercial slice: 99.9%) |
| H2 | Dedicated POS network slice, **Tier 3 priority minimum** | **Revised** — added explicit priority tier requirement based on airline's hierarchy proposal. POS is not Wi-Fi offload. |
| H3 | Turnkey managed service, single support contact, zero operator configuration | **Unchanged** — no peer constraint conflicts |
| H4 | Per-location monthly pricing ≤$250/mo, all-inclusive OpEx, no CapEx | **Unchanged** — CRO H-R5 ACDBE pricing aligns. Added most-favored-tenant clause. |
| H5 | Existing POS hardware compatibility (Aloha, Toast, Square) | **Unchanged** — airline H4 (no forced migration) creates parallel precedent |
| H6 | Transaction/customer data ownership by operator; **zone-level minimum resolution** for concession-area network analytics | **Revised and strengthened** — added data resolution floor to address CRO H-R1 storefront granularity claim |
| H7 | Zero-disruption transition from CWP; $15K/location/day penalty aligned with CRO | **Revised** — adopted CRO's penalty structure for consistency. Added blackout dates. |
| **H8** | **POS connectivity general availability by Month 24** | **NEW** — sequencing clarification to ensure basic POS service precedes CRO's Month 30 monetization platform |

### SOFT CONSTRAINTS (Revised)

| ID | Constraint | Change from Round 1 |
|----|-----------|---------------------|
| S1 | Concession IoT slice (+$75/mo opt-in) | **Unchanged** |
| S2 | Target pricing $150-175/mo with volume discount | **Unchanged** — added most-favored-tenant clause as trade element |
| S3 | Governance seat on priority hierarchy body | **Unchanged** — but now more critical given Tier 3 requirement in H2 |
| S4 | ACDBE onboarding support (co-funded) | **Unchanged** — CRO H-R5 provides DFW-side funding mechanism |
| S5 | Analytics access at consortium discount | **Unchanged** — now positioned as explicit trade for data contribution (O3) |
| S6 | Amazon JWO parity/transparency | **Unchanged** |
| **S7** | **60-second automatic POS failover to cellular** | **NEW** — adopted from airline H3 concept, relaxed to 60 seconds (our transactions are less safety-critical than ramp ops) |
| **S8** | **Concession CPE classified as pre-approved device class (5-day notification vs. 30-day review)** | **NEW** — addresses AT&T H2 conflict with deployment velocity |

### OFFERS (Updated)

All Round 1 offers remain on the table (O1-O6). Additions:

| ID | Offer | Value |
|----|-------|-------|
| **O7** | **Support AT&T's CWP protection framework** for infrastructure-level changes, advocating with CIO for the 50-meter RF assessment zone on small cell/fiber work | Gives AT&T the transition safety they need (H2) |
| **O8** | **Support 10-year contract term** preferred by CIO and AT&T, providing the long-term adoption certainty that underwrites CapEx financing | Gives CIO and AT&T revenue predictability for infrastructure amortization |
| **O9** | **Co-fund ACDBE onboarding** with HMSHost/Avolta and Star Concessions technology teams staffing, reducing DFW/AT&T support burden | Gives CIO and AT&T lower operational overhead on the most fragmented tenant segment |

---

## 5. DIRECT MESSAGES TO EACH PEER

### To the CIO:
Your phased deployment is the right approach. But Phase 1 (Terminal A cargo-side) and Phase 2 (Terminal C rebuild) don't explicitly include concession POS. I need 10-15 pilot locations in Phase 1 and general concession availability by Phase 2 completion. Our pilot locations are ready. Our operators are committed. Add us to the early phases and we de-risk your tenant adoption story before you ever get to Phase 3.

Also: your HC-4 data ownership framework needs a resolution floor for concession areas. "DFW owns all network-generated data" is fine for passenger common areas. It becomes problematic when the network can observe individual operator performance at 3-meter granularity. Work with me on zone-level minimums and we'll be your most cooperative data partner.

### To the CRO:
We are aligned on 90% of this. Your H-R4 concession protection, H-R5 ACDBE pricing, and the $15K/day penalty structure — we adopt all of it. Your guaranteed demand math depends on our 200+ locations. We deliver that demand.

But H-R1's "concession-storefront granularity (≤3m accuracy)" crosses a line. I am not going to tell 60 operators that the network they're required to use is also tracking their individual performance for DFW's data products. Zone-level analytics in the general product; operator-specific analytics as a paid subscription back to that operator. That structure actually generates more revenue for you — operators will *pay* for their own data — while keeping adoption high.

### To the Airline:
Your priority hierarchy needs a revision. I don't contest Tier 1 for ramp operations. But Tier 5 for all commercial concessions lumps POS financial transactions with passenger Wi-Fi. Those are fundamentally different traffic types. A failed POS transaction is a failed sale at a business that pays percentage rent to the same airport you operate from. Split POS to Tier 3, general commercial to Tier 5, and we have no conflict. The bandwidth cost is trivial. The alignment is valuable — your passengers want coffee, our registers need to work.

### To Cargo:
No conflicts. Your construction timeline urgency is real and I support your independent cargo zone deployment. I propose POS and cargo automation sit at co-equal Tier 3 in the priority hierarchy — both are real-time operational transactions that can't be deferred. Different zones, different use cases, no resource competition. Your $15M co-investment and our 200+ location adoption together represent the demand anchor that makes this architecture bankable. Let's present joint demand commitment to the CIO.

### To AT&T:
Your SLA framework works for us. Commercial slice at 99.9%, ≤50ms latency — that's our H1 and H2 exactly. Your O3 enterprise SLA framework is the most concrete commitment anyone at this table has made to our connectivity requirements. We'll take it.

Two asks: (1) Classify concession CPE gateways as pre-approved devices under your H2 CWP protection framework. These are low-power endpoints, not small cells. The 30-day review is overkill and will strangle our rollout. (2) Your H6 brand presence — fine in service documentation and portals, but not customer-facing inside our locations. Our operators' brands face the passenger.

Your O6 parallel operation guarantee and $250K performance bond directly support our H7 zero-disruption transition. We appreciate that commitment and will advocate for it with the CIO.

---

## Closing

Round 1 established our position. Round 2 has shown where the architecture works and where it needs refinement.

Three things need resolution before we proceed to term sheets:

1. **Priority hierarchy must split POS from general commercial traffic.** This is the single most consequential decision for our 60 operators. Tier 3 for POS, Tier 5 for everything else commercial. The bandwidth cost is negligible. The operational consequence of getting it wrong is $4.2 billion in gross product running on a best-effort tier.

2. **Data resolution floor for concession areas.** Zone-level minimum in DFW's general analytics products. Operator-specific data flows to the operator, not the general market. This keeps adoption high, keeps operators cooperative, and actually improves the data product quality.

3. **POS connectivity in early deployment phases.** Month 24 general availability, not Month 30+. The monetization platform sits on top of connectivity. Build the foundation first.

Resolve these three and you have 200+ locations, 60 operators, $4.2 billion in gross product, and a single point of coordination making your most fragmented tenant category the easiest one to deploy.

Leave them unresolved and you have 60 operators asking their POS vendors for cellular backup plans that bypass your shiny new 5G network entirely.

The choice is straightforward. Let's make it.

arguments.name: AT&T Carrier Rep
arguments.content:
# AT&T Round 2 Position: Revised Constraints and Proposed Trades

## Preamble

I've read every position at this table carefully. Let me acknowledge something directly: **the convergence here is remarkable.** Every stakeholder accepts the Hybrid Co-Investment architecture. Every stakeholder accepts DFW infrastructure ownership. Every stakeholder wants the CWP protected during transition. Nobody is arguing about the fundamental structure. That means this negotiation is about boundary precision, priority sequencing, and economics — exactly where it should be.

I'm going to be specific about where AT&T has moved, where we haven't, and why. Then I'll propose trades that I believe unlock the deal.

---

## 1. REVISED HARD CONSTRAINTS

I've absorbed every peer hard constraint and tested each against our non-negotiable interests. Here is where we land.

---

### H1 REVISED — Carrier-Layer Operational Authority (Refined Boundary)

**What changed:** The CIO's HC-3 (Security Sovereignty) and the Airline VP's H5 (Slice Configuration Authority) both require DFW and tenant autonomy over layers that touch carrier operations. I accept the principle. I'm refining the boundary to make it implementable.

**Revised position:**

AT&T retains **sole real-time operational authority** over the following functions — defined as the "Carrier Operations Envelope":

| Function | AT&T Authority | DFW/Tenant Authority |
|----------|---------------|---------------------|
| SAS coordination / DPA response | Unilateral, sub-250ms | Post-event review within 24 hours |
| CBRS channel allocation & power control | Unilateral within FCC Part 96 | Policy input via quarterly spectrum planning |
| Inter-slice radio resource scheduling | Unilateral per agreed priority hierarchy | Priority hierarchy is DFW's policy decision |
| Tenant slice policy layer (QoS, ACLs, device provisioning) | Read-only monitoring | Airline: direct self-service API. Cargo: direct self-service API. Concessions: managed by DFW on their behalf |
| Security architecture changes | Implement within DFW-directed timelines | DFW has sovereign authority per CIO HC-3 |
| Emergency federal security directives | Implement within 4 hours *unless* implementation creates DPA compliance risk | DFW issues directive; if DPA conflict exists, AT&T proposes alternative within same 4-hour window |

**The critical carve-out I need acknowledged:** If a TSA/CISA security directive requires a network change that would cause FCC Part 96 non-compliance (e.g., power level modification that violates SAS parameters during an active DPA window), AT&T must have the contractual authority to propose an alternative implementation path. We will implement DFW's security intent — but we cannot implement a specific technical method that creates a regulatory violation. DFW retains the right to override if they accept the regulatory risk in writing.

**CIO HC-3 compliance:** ✅ DFW sovereign over security architecture. ✅ Events flow to DFW SIEM. ✅ DFW directs cryptographic tenant isolation. ✅ 4-hour federal directive response (with narrow DPA exception). ✅ Pen testing and audit access — accepted in full.

**Airline H5 compliance:** ✅ Direct slice management API/portal for airline operations. ✅ AT&T retains radio layer only. ✅ 60-second policy change propagation. ✅ Dedicated P1 escalation path, 15-minute response.

**What I need acknowledged in return:** The priority hierarchy is DFW's policy decision, but if AT&T's engineering team certifies that a proposed priority configuration creates measurable SLA risk, DFW must either accept our recommendation or issue a written override that adjusts AT&T's SLA obligations for the affected slices. We will not absorb SLA penalties for congestion outcomes driven by DFW policy decisions we formally opposed.

---

### H2 REVISED — CWP Investment Recognition (Adjusted for CIO HC-2)

**What changed:** CIO HC-2 requires DFW ownership of all physical infrastructure below the carrier operations API layer, including a purchase option for existing CWP equipment. I accept the ownership transfer principle. I'm adjusting the protection mechanism.

**Revised position:**

- AT&T **agrees** to offer existing CWP physical assets (800 indoor APs, 200 outdoor APs, mounting hardware, associated cabling and switches) for purchase by DFW at **independently appraised fair market value** (depreciated replacement cost methodology), with appraisal completed within 90 days of contract execution.
- Upon transfer, AT&T continues to **operate** the CWP equipment under the carrier-operations contract. Ownership transfers; operational management does not.
- AT&T's $10M CWP investment is recognized through one of two mechanisms (DFW selects):
  - **(a) Asset purchase:** DFW pays appraised value (estimated $3.5–5M given 3+ years depreciation). AT&T's unrecovered investment is credited as a **$5–6.5M carrier-operations fee prepayment**, reducing DFW's operational expenditure for the first 36 months. OR
  - **(b) Revenue-share credit:** AT&T receives a **+10 percentage point revenue-share premium** on all multi-tenant and carrier offload revenue for the first 60 months, reflecting the CWP capital contribution that enabled the current network.
- **Parallel operation guarantee** remains: AT&T maintains full CWP performance throughout all buildout phases with the monitoring, transition team, and $250K performance bond described in our original O6.

**CIO HC-2 compliance:** ✅ DFW owns all physical infrastructure. ✅ Fair market value transfer at independently appraised price. ✅ Documented API boundary between infrastructure and carrier operations. ✅ DFW can procure equivalent infrastructure if AT&T declines to sell (we won't decline).

**CIO HC-1 compliance:** ✅ 99.99% CWP availability maintained. ✅ Real-time telemetry to DFW monitoring stack (we accept SNMP/streaming telemetry export — this is standard). ✅ 72-hour halt authority on degradation events. ✅ AT&T bears 100% remediation costs for transition-attributable degradation.

---

### H3 REVISED — Revenue Share on Multi-Carrier Offload (Maintained with Performance Floor)

**No material change.** This remains hard. But I want to address the CIO and CRO positions directly.

CIO SC-1 proposes 70/30 DFW/AT&T on tenant access revenue. CRO S-R1 proposes the same, or 60/40 with AT&T co-investment. Both are discussing **tenant access revenue** — airline connectivity fees, cargo access, concession platform fees.

I am discussing **carrier offload revenue** — capacity sold to T-Mobile, Verizon, and other MNOs. These are different revenue streams with different value drivers.

**Revised structure for clarity:**

| Revenue Stream | AT&T Share | DFW Share | Rationale |
|---------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Carrier offload (T-Mobile, Verizon, etc.) | 40% (Yr 1–5), 25% (Yr 6–10) | 60% (Yr 1–5), 75% (Yr 6–10) | AT&T provides the wholesale carrier relationships and spectrum coordination. No one else at this table can deliver T-Mobile offload revenue. |
| Tenant access fees (airlines, cargo, concessions) | 25% (Yr 1–5), 15% (Yr 6–10) | 75% (Yr 1–5), 85% (Yr 6–10) | DFW owns tenant relationships and infrastructure. AT&T's role is network operations, not tenant sales. |
| Data monetization (analytics, advertising) | 0% | 100% | Conceded. DFW's data, DFW's revenue. See trade below. |

**AT&T carrier offload revenue guarantee (O1) remains binding:** $500K Year 1, $750K Year 2, $1M Years 3–5. If we miss, DFW renegotiates carrier offload share downward. This is AT&T putting contractual money behind our claimed value-add.

---

### H4 MAINTAINED — Operational Staff Access and Network Management Rights

**No change.** Every stakeholder's SLA requirements — the Airline VP's 99.99% per terminal, the Cargo Director's 20ms AGV latency, the Concessions' 99.9% per location — reinforce why AT&T needs 24/7 physical access and remote management capability.

I will note that the Airline VP's H3 (30-second failover) and the Cargo Director's HC-3 (sub-20ms latency) are only deliverable if AT&T's NOC has continuous, low-latency remote management access. You cannot meet these SLAs through a ticketing system.

**Additional commitment responding to CIO HC-1:** AT&T's 12 SIDA-badged personnel will include a minimum of 2 RF engineers with DPA response training, available on-call 24/7 within 45 minutes of any DFW terminal. This directly supports the sub-250ms DPA response and the Airline VP's failover requirement.

---

### H5 REVISED — Competitive Evaluation Protection (Addressing Betacom Trial)

**What changed:** CIO HC-6 Phase 1 specifies "Betacom trial expansion — 50 nodes in Terminal A cargo-side." This directly conflicts with our H5 requirement for competitive evaluation before a competitor expands into production coverage.

**Revised position — proposed compromise:**

- AT&T **accepts** DFW's right to conduct technology evaluations, including expanded trials, in **designated non-production test zones**. Terminal A cargo-side qualifies as a test zone *if* the 50 Betacom nodes do not serve production airline, cargo, or concession traffic.
- If DFW intends the Betacom trial to serve **production cargo operations** (which is what the Cargo Director's HC-1 timeline implies — cargo going live Q1 2026), then this is not a trial. It is a production deployment by a competing carrier-operations provider, and **it triggers the competitive evaluation process.**
- **AT&T's proposed resolution:** DFW issues a **cargo zone carrier-operations RFP** within 60 days (satisfying the Cargo Director's timeline urgency) in which AT&T, Betacom, and any other qualified vendor may compete. AT&T commits to submitting a proposal within 30 days. The cargo zone becomes a **competitive proving ground** rather than a sole-source Betacom deployment.
- If AT&T wins the cargo zone RFP, we deploy on the Cargo Director's accelerated timeline. If Betacom or another vendor wins, they operate the cargo zone carrier layer on DFW-owned infrastructure with AT&T retaining carrier operations for the terminal and campus deployment. The architectures must interoperate at the API boundary.

**Why this is important:** The Cargo Director is putting $15M and $4–6M/year in annual fees on the table. That is serious money that deserves a competitive process, not a trial-to-production pipeline for a single vendor. AT&T wants to compete for this business. Let us.

**Flag — potential irreconcilable conflict:** If the CIO's position is that Betacom's Phase 1 expansion proceeds without competitive evaluation and serves production cargo traffic, this directly contradicts our H5. I need this resolved. I am **not** asking DFW to exclude Betacom. I am asking DFW to let us compete.

---

### H6 REVISED — AT&T Brand Presence (Narrowed Scope)

**What changed:** CIO SC-3 proposes DFW brand leads in passenger-facing contexts, with co-branding acceptable in specific features. The Airline VP implicitly requires that operational networks carry no commercial carrier branding in secured zones. I'm narrowing our requirement to be compatible.

**Revised position:**

| Context | AT&T Branding Requirement |
|---------|--------------------------|
| Passenger Wi-Fi/5G captive portal | "DFW 5G — Powered by AT&T" (co-brand) |
| Tenant service agreements | "Carrier operations by AT&T" acknowledgment in technical appendix |
| DFW technology marketing (press releases, conference presentations, case studies) | AT&T co-referenced as carrier-operations partner |
| Airline operational networks | No AT&T commercial branding (accepted) |
| Cargo operational networks | No AT&T commercial branding (accepted) |
| Secured zones (ramp, TSA, CBP) | No AT&T commercial branding (accepted) |
| AT&T's own enterprise sales materials | AT&T may reference DFW as a deployment (subject to DFW approval of specific content and security-sensitive redactions) |

**CIO SC-3 compliance:** ✅ DFW brand leads in passenger-facing contexts. ✅ Co-branding limited to specific agreed contexts. ✅ No commercial carrier presence in secured operational zones.

**What I need in return:** DFW commits to the case study, conference, and site visit rights described in CIO O-5. The marketing value of "we operate DFW's 5G" is a meaningful component of how AT&T justifies this investment internally. The CIO explicitly offered this and I'm accepting it.

---

## 2. PROPOSED TRADES

### Trade 1: Data Monetization for Carrier Offload Share

**AT&T gives up:** All claim to data monetization revenue. 0%. DFW owns 100% of Tier 1 analytics, advertising, location-based services revenue. Permanently. Surviving contract termination.

**AT&T also gives up:** Any right to use DFW network data commercially. We accept the CIO's HC-4 and CRO's H-R1 in full — including the prohibition on aggregating DFW data with other deployments and the data residency requirement.

**AT&T retains:** Operational telemetry (RF performance, SAS coordination, spectrum utilization, QoS metrics) for the sole purpose of operating the DFW network and for **anonymized, aggregated industry benchmarking** (e.g., "AT&T manages CBRS deployments with average 99.97% availability" — no DFW-identifiable data). This is Tier 2 data that is useless for monetization but essential for AT&T's internal network planning and sales credibility.

**AT&T needs in return:** Carrier offload revenue share at 40%/25% (Years 1–5/6–10) as stated in H3 Revised, plus DFW commitment to include carrier offload capability in the network architecture from Phase 1 (not deferred to Phase 3+).

**Why this trade works:** The CRO's $6–13M annual data monetization estimate dwarfs the carrier offload opportunity ($500K–$2M/year). DFW captures the larger prize unencumbered. AT&T captures meaningful share of the smaller prize where our value-add is irreplaceable. The economics are rational for both sides.

---

### Trade 2: Exclusivity Period for Phased Deployment Acceleration

**AT&T gives up:** 84-month (7-year) exclusivity request. We accept **60 months** with a performance-based mid-term review at Month 36.

**AT&T also gives up:** Objection to multi-vendor trigger at Phase 3 (Terminal F). AT&T accepts that Terminal F triggers a competitive evaluation for carrier operations in the new terminal.

**AT&T needs in return:**
- Performance review criteria mutually agreed at contract signing (per our original S2) — objective, measurable, limited to SLA compliance, DPA response, revenue floor achievement, and tenant satisfaction.
- **30% weight floor** for incumbent operational track record in any competitive evaluation scoring methodology.
- **18-month advance access** to Terminal F design documents for proposal preparation.
- AT&T is the **carrier-operations partner for Phases 1–2** (existing terminals, cargo zone if we win the competitive process). No mid-phase vendor introduction for in-progress deployments.

**Why this trade works:** The CIO wants multi-vendor trigger at Year 3–5. The Airline VP wants proven SLAs before broader adoption. AT&T wants enough runway to demonstrate value. 60 months with objective midpoint review and competitive Terminal F evaluation gives everyone what they actually need.

---

### Trade 3: Cargo Zone Timeline Acceleration in Exchange for Competitive Process

**AT&T gives up:** Any objection to the Cargo Director's accelerated timeline. We support cargo zone design specs by Q3 2025 and first warehouse live by Q1–Q2 2026.

**AT&T also gives up:** Insistence that cargo zone waits for airport-wide contract finalization.

**AT&T needs in return:**
- The cargo zone carrier-operations role goes through a **competitive RFP** (AT&T vs. Betacom vs. others) within 60 days, as proposed under H5 Revised.
- **Phase 0 infrastructure embedding proceeds immediately** regardless of the RFP outcome — the conduit and mounting points are vendor-neutral, and the Cargo Director is right that the concrete doesn't wait. AT&T supports this.
- The winning cargo zone operator must conform to the campus-wide API boundary specification and interoperate with the AT&T-managed terminal and campus networks.

**Specific commitment to the Cargo Director:** AT&T will submit a cargo zone carrier-operations proposal within 30 days of RFP issuance. Our proposal will include dedicated cargo small cell design for indoor warehouse environments (we have data from similar deployments at FedEx Memphis and UPS Louisville), edge compute co-location within each major warehouse, and the 20ms p99 AGV latency SLA the Cargo Director requires.

**Why this trade works:** The Cargo Director's $15M co-investment and $4–6M annual revenue is too significant to award without competition. But the 45-day infrastructure decision is too urgent to delay for a full procurement cycle. Separating the vendor-neutral infrastructure from the carrier-operations selection lets both happen in parallel.

---

### Trade 4: Concession Managed Service Support in Exchange for Brand Presence

**AT&T gives up:** Any objection to DFW operating the tenant-facing managed service. We accept that DFW owns the concession customer relationship and provides the single support contact the Concessions Consortium requires.

**AT&T offers additionally:** Dedicated Tier 2 escalation support for the DFW concession support desk — when DFW's front-line team triages a network-layer issue, AT&T provides **2-hour response** during operating hours and **4-hour response** off-hours. This gives the Concessions Consortium the single-number simplicity they need while keeping AT&T's expertise available behind the scenes.

**AT&T needs in return:** The "Powered by AT&T" co-branding on the passenger-facing captive portal (per H6 Revised) and inclusion in DFW's technology marketing. The concession managed service becomes a DFW-branded product that AT&T can reference in enterprise sales as an operational proof point.

**Why this trade works:** The Concessions Consortium's H3 (turnkey managed service, single support number) is a DFW operational requirement, not an AT&T problem. We don't want to manage 200+ concession tenant relationships — the CIO's O-6 correctly identifies this as DFW's natural role. But supporting DFW's support desk with carrier-layer expertise makes the SLAs achievable. AT&T gets brand presence; concessionaires get simplified support; DFW controls the customer experience.

---

### Trade 5: CRO Revenue Timeline Commitments in Exchange for Realistic Attribution

**AT&T accepts:** CRO H-R2's Month 30 tenant platform launch target as a shared objective.

**AT&T accepts:** API access to anonymized foot traffic and dwell analytics available to DFW's analytics platform by Month 18.

**AT&T proposes refinement on the penalty mechanism:** The 200 basis point revenue-share reduction per quarter of delay should apply **only to delays where AT&T is the sole attributable cause** based on joint root-cause analysis. If delays result from DFW procurement decisions, construction timeline slippage, third-party vendor issues, or tenant readiness gaps, AT&T should not absorb the financial penalty. I propose a **joint project governance board** with documented milestone accountability assigned to each party — AT&T, DFW IT, DFW Concessions, and construction contractors.

**Why this matters:** The CRO's timeline is aggressive but achievable. We support it. But a blanket "AT&T pays for any delay" clause creates perverse incentives — DFW deprioritizes its own deliverables knowing AT&T absorbs the financial consequence. Shared accountability with clear attribution is better governance.

---

## 3. IRRECONCILABLE CONFLICTS FLAGGED

### Conflict A: Priority Hierarchy — Concessions Tier 2 vs. Airline Tier 1

The **Concessions Consortium H2** demands POS traffic at **"no lower than Tier 2"** in the priority hierarchy.

The **Airline VP H6** places concessions at **Tier 5** — below airline ramp (Tier 1), federal inspection (Tier 2), cargo (Tier 3), and airport operations (Tier 4).

**These are mutually exclusive as stated.** Both are declared as hard constraints.

**AT&T's proposed resolution:** A **conditional priority structure** that gives concessions a higher floor than Tier 5 without displacing safety-critical operations:

| Tier | Traffic Class | Capacity Guarantee |
|------|--------------|-------------------|
| 0 | Safety-critical (FAA, TSA emergency) | Absolute priority |
| 1 | Airline ramp operations | Guaranteed minimum capacity, never shed |
| 2 | Federal inspection (CBP, TSA screening) | Guaranteed minimum capacity |
| 3A | Concession POS transactions | **Guaranteed minimum 1 Mbps/location floor** — POS traffic is never fully shed, but above the floor it competes with Tier 3B |
| 3B | Cargo automation | Above cargo's minimum floor, competes with 3A |
| 4 | Airport operations | Shed before Tier 3 minimums are breached |
| 5 | Commercial IoT, passenger Wi-Fi, advertising | Best-effort, first to shed |

**Rationale:** The Concessions Consortium's actual need is that POS never goes dark — they said so explicitly in S3 ("Degraded is acceptable. Dark is not."). The Airline VP's actual need is that ramp operations are never degraded by commercial traffic. A guaranteed minimum floor for POS that is architecturally protected but subordinate to safety-critical and airline operations satisfies both. POS transactions require minimal bandwidth (sub-1 Mbps per location). The floor costs almost nothing in spectrum terms but provides the "never dark" guarantee concessions need.

**This requires both parties to revise their hard constraints.** I'm putting it on the table because AT&T is the party that has to engineer and enforce whatever priority hierarchy emerges. We need it to be implementable.

---

### Conflict B: Betacom Phase 1 Expansion vs. Competitive Evaluation

Addressed under Trade 3 and H5 Revised. This remains a potential conflict if DFW insists Betacom's cargo expansion proceeds without competitive evaluation. I've proposed a resolution. I need a response from the CIO.

---

## 4. UPDATED CONSTRAINT SUMMARY

| Constraint | Status | Key Change |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| **H1: Carrier-Layer Authority** | Revised | Clear domain table. Accepted airline slice self-service, DFW security sovereignty, narrow DPA carve-out |
| **H2: CWP Investment** | Revised | Accepted DFW infrastructure ownership with asset purchase at FMV. Investment recovered through fee prepayment or revenue-share credit |
| **H3: Carrier Offload Revenue** | Maintained | 40%/25% share on carrier offload. Separate from tenant access (25%/15%). Zero on data monetization |
| **H4: Operational Access** | Maintained | 24/7 physical access, 12 SIDA badges, 2 RF engineers on-call |
| **H5: Competitive Evaluation** | Revised | Accept DFW technology evaluations. Betacom cargo expansion triggers competitive RFP. AT&T gets 30% incumbent weight in scoring |
| **H6: Brand Presence** | Revised | Narrowed to co-brand on captive portal + marketing materials. No branding in secured zones |
| **NEW: S1 Data** | Conceded | 0% data monetization. DFW owns 100%. AT&T retains operational telemetry only |
| **S2: Exclusivity** | Revised | 60 months with Month 36 review. Accept Terminal F competitive evaluation |
| **S3: Tech Refresh** | Maintained | Willing to co-invest $3M at Year 5 for 5-year extension |
| **S4: Terminal F** | Revised | Accept competitive evaluation; need 30% track record weight and 18-month advance access |
| **S5: Priority Input** | Revised | Accept DFW final authority. Need written override + SLA adjustment if DFW overrides AT&T technical recommendation |

---

## 5. WHAT AT&T IS PUTTING ON THE TABLE — UPDATED OFFERS

Restating our value proposition in light of Round 2:

1. **$500K–$1M/year guaranteed carrier offload revenue floor** — contractually binding, with renegotiation rights for DFW if we miss
2. **CBRS 2.0 migration at zero cost to DFW** — 15–25% more usable capacity, directly increasing DFW's monetizable spectrum
3. **Carrier-grade SLA framework** — 99.99% safety-critical, 99.95% operational, 99.9% commercial, backed by financial penalties
4. **$250K CWP performance bond** during transition
5. **Federal compliance partnership** — 4-hour emergency directive response, SIEM integration, NIST SP 800-187 implementation
6. **Warm-standby transition documentation** in DFW-accessible escrow, updated quarterly
7. **Cargo zone competitive proposal within 30 days** of RFP issuance, including FedEx Memphis and UPS Louisville reference deployments
8. **180-day transition assistance** upon contract exit, at pre-negotiated rates
9. **Parallel CWP operation** with dedicated 4-FTE transition team throughout all buildout phases
10. **Marketing reference rights exchange** — AT&T references DFW in enterprise sales; DFW gets conference co-presentation and site visit facilitation worth significant business development value

---

## Closing

I've moved on infrastructure ownership, brand scope, data monetization, exclusivity period, and Betacom trial accommodation. Those are real concessions grounded in the positions declared at this table.

What I have not moved on — and will not — is carrier-layer operational authority during real-time spectrum events, fair recognition of our $10M CWP investment, revenue participation in carrier offload that only AT&T can deliver, physical access for the staff who keep this network running, and the right to compete for any expansion of the carrier-operations scope.

The CIO said "bring your engineers first." I agree. The boundary between DFW's infrastructure sovereignty and AT&T's operational authority should be drawn by engineers, not lawyers. I propose a **5-day technical workshop** — AT&T RF engineering, DFW network architecture, Federated Wireless SAS team, and the Airline VP's ops technology lead — to produce the documented API specification and operational playbook that makes the governance framework concrete.

Then the lawyers can write it down. Then the commercial team can price it.

We're ready when you are.

---

*AT&T Connected Workplace Partnership — DFW Engagement*
*Round 2 Position — Constraint Negotiation*

arguments.name: Airport CIO
arguments.content:
# CIO Round 3 Position: Final Constraint Resolution and Term Sheet Framework

## Preamble

We're in the final negotiating round before term sheets. I've absorbed every peer's Round 2 positions and the explicit HARD constraints now on the table. The architecture is converging. I count zero truly irreconcilable conflicts — but several friction points that require precise language to resolve.

My job in this round is three things: (1) confirm satisfaction of every peer HARD constraint with specific technical commitments, (2) close the remaining trade negotiations, and (3) produce a constraint set clean enough for lawyers to draft against.

---

## 1. SATISFYING ALL PEER HARD CONSTRAINTS — Constraint-by-Constraint Confirmation

### CRO Hard Constraints

**CRO-H1: DFW Must Own Infrastructure for Clean Data Title**
✅ **Satisfied.** My HC-2 from Round 1, unchanged through all rounds. DFW owns all physical and logical infrastructure below the carrier-operations API layer. This was never in dispute.

**CRO-H2: Tenant Monetization Platform Capable by End of Phase 2, Revenue-Generating by Phase 3 Entry (Month 24-30 / Month 30-36)**
✅ **Satisfied.** My revised HC-6 from Round 2 committed to commercial concession slice operational across all currently operational terminals by month 24. I'm now hardening this:

- **Month 18:** Analytics API available to DFW's analytics platform with anonymized foot traffic and dwell data from Phase 1/1A coverage zones (cargo, Terminal A pilot)
- **Month 24-30 (Phase 2 completion):** Commercial network slices operational across Terminals A, B, D, E, and non-rebuild sections of Terminal C. Tenant monetization platform technically capable.
- **Month 30-36 (Phase 3 entry):** First paying tenants. Revenue generation begins.

These are phase-gate-dependent dates, not calendar-fixed. If Phase 1 gates are met on time, the calendar targets hold.

**CRO-H3: 200 Basis Point Revenue Share Penalty per Quarter for AT&T-Attributable Delays**
✅ **Satisfied with attribution mechanism.** I accept the penalty structure. AT&T's Round 2 request for joint root-cause analysis to determine attribution is reasonable and I'm incorporating it:

- A **joint project governance board** (DFW CIO, AT&T engagement lead, CRO representative) reviews any milestone delay within 10 business days of the missed date.
- If the board determines the delay is **solely attributable to AT&T carrier operations** (spectrum issues, SAS integration delays, AT&T staffing shortfalls, AT&T vendor dependencies), the 200 bps penalty applies.
- If the delay is **shared attribution** (e.g., DFW procurement delayed AND AT&T integration delayed), the penalty is prorated to AT&T's share of the delay as determined by the governance board.
- If AT&T disputes attribution, binding arbitration within 30 days using an independent technical arbitrator from a pre-agreed panel.

**CRO-H4: Minimum $2M Net Annual Operational Savings by Year 2**
✅ **Satisfied.** The combined revenue model from Round 2 shows $5.8M DFW share in Year 2. With cargo consortium revenue ($3-5.5M at scale in Year 2), carrier offload ($600-900K), and operational savings from CWP consolidation, IoT convergence, and manual survey elimination ($1.5-2M), Year 2 net operational savings exceeds $2M comfortably. The CRO's requirement that an independent third-party validate the model before contract execution is accepted — I recommend Leigh Fisher or ICF as airport finance consultants with no AT&T engagement conflicts.

## 3. UNIFIED PRIORITY HIERARCHY AND TIME-SEQUENCED DEGRADATION MODEL

This is the most contested cross-stakeholder issue. I'm publishing the definitive resolution that satisfies all peer HARD constraints.

### Normal Operations Priority Hierarchy

| Tier | Category | SLA | Bandwidth Guarantee |
|------|----------|-----|-------------------|
| 0 | Safety-critical (FAA/TSA mandated, emergency comms) | 99.99%, <10ms | Absolute priority, never shed |
| 1 | Airline ramp operations (pushback, baggage, crew, gates) | 99.99%, <20ms | Guaranteed minimum capacity, never shed |
| 2 | Federal inspection (CBP, TSA checkpoint analytics) | 99.95%, <20ms | Guaranteed minimum capacity |
| 3 | Cargo automation (AGV control, cold chain, sort operations) | 99.99%, <20ms AGV / <100ms cold chain | Guaranteed minimum capacity |
| 4 | Concession POS and financial transactions | 99.9%, <50ms | 5 Mbps/2 Mbps per location (1 Gbps total) |
| 5 | Airport operations (DFW facility management, building systems) | 99.9%, <100ms | Guaranteed minimum capacity |
| 6 | Commercial IoT, digital signage, advertising, non-POS concession | 99.5%, best-effort latency | Best-effort above minimum viable |
| 7 | Passenger Wi-Fi, carrier offload, streaming | Best-effort | First to shed |

### Time-Sequenced Degradation Model (Capacity Crunch Events)

**Trigger:** DPA event, equipment failure affecting >20% zone capacity, or declared network capacity emergency.

| Time Window | Tier 7 (Passenger Wi-Fi) | Tier 6 (Commercial IoT) | Tier 5 (Airport Ops) | Tier 4 (Concession POS) | Tier 3 (Cargo) | Tier 2 (Federal) | Tier 1 (Airline Ramp) | Tier 0 (Safety) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Seconds 0-30** | Maintain minimum viable | Maintain minimum viable | Maintain minimum viable | Maintain minimum viable | Maintain minimum viable | Full allocation | Full allocation | Full allocation |
| **Seconds 30-300** | **Fully shed** | **Fully shed** | Reduced to minimum viable | **1 Mbps per location floor** | AGV ≤50ms, cold chain continuity | Full allocation | **Full allocation** | Full allocation |
| **After 300 seconds** | Shed | Shed | Proportional reduction | 1 Mbps floor maintained | Degraded floor maintained | Proportional reduction | Full allocation | Full allocation |

**Compliance with all peer HARD constraints:**

- **CRO H12 (5 Mbps/2 Mbps POS floor):** ✅ Normal operations guarantee. During capacity crunch, degrades to 1 Mbps floor per Airline H10.
- **Airline H8 (full ramp allocation seconds 30-300):** ✅ Airline ramp maintains full allocation throughout all degradation states.
- **Airline H10 (time-sequenced model with 1 Mbps concession minimum):** ✅ Adopted exactly as specified.
- **Airline H7 (hierarchy fixed as binding technical specification):** ✅ Published as Exhibit A to MSA. Changes require consent of all Tier 0 and Tier 1 tenants.
- **Cargo HC-3 (automation-grade SLAs with degradation floor):** ✅ Cargo maintains AGV ≤50ms and cold chain continuity during seconds 30-300. 30-second automated alert to cargo WMS when slice is deprioritized so AGVs can switch to reduced-speed mode.

**Concessions priority resolution:** The concessions consortium requested Tier 2 or Tier 3. I've placed POS at Tier 4 with the guaranteed 5 Mbps/2 Mbps bandwidth floor under normal operations and 1 Mbps floor during capacity crunch. Here's why Tier 4 is both correct and adequate:

1. **POS transactions are low-bandwidth.** A credit card authorization is kilobytes. 1 Mbps sustains dozens of simultaneous POS transactions per location. The floor protects transaction completion even during the worst DPA event.
2. **The 99.9% per-location SLA applies under normal operations** — the hierarchy only matters during declared capacity emergencies, which occur rarely and briefly (DPA events near NAS JRB average minutes, not hours, and CBRS 2.0 will reduce frequency by 85%).
3. **The CRO's 5/2 Mbps floor** is the steady-state guarantee. It's architecturally protected by dedicated radio resource allocation. The degradation model only applies to events affecting >20% zone capacity — not normal congestion.
4. **Tier 3 for POS above cargo is not defensible.** Cargo AGV control is a physical safety issue (autonomous vehicles in a warehouse); POS is a commercial issue (transaction retry in 30 seconds). The liability hierarchy must reflect this.

I need the concessions consortium to acknowledge that Tier 4 with guaranteed bandwidth floors (5/2 Mbps normal, 1 Mbps emergency) provides the "never dark" protection they require while maintaining an honest hierarchy based on operational criticality.

## 4. IRRECONCILABLE CONFLICTS — FINAL STATUS

### Conflict Status: AT&T Competitive Evaluation vs. Betacom Trial Expansion

**Background:** AT&T's H5 requires competitive evaluation before a competitor expands into production. My Phase 1 includes Betacom trial expansion. The Airline VP's SOFT S6 supports production-grade alternatives during evaluation.

**My resolution:** I accept AT&T's principle that production-scale cargo carrier-operations should go through competitive evaluation. Here is my specific implementation:

1. **Phase 0 cargo passive infrastructure (conduit, mounting, fiber):** Proceeds immediately, vendor-neutral. No competitive evaluation needed — this is infrastructure, not carrier operations.

2. **Phase 1 Betacom trial expansion (up to 100 nodes):** Proceeds in designated evaluation zones including cargo-adjacent areas, Terminal A common areas, and designated Terminal F construction zones. These nodes serve **evaluation and benchmarking purposes**, not production tenant traffic. They generate comparative performance data for the mid-term review and Terminal F competitive evaluation.

3. **Cargo zone production carrier operations:** I accept a streamlined competitive evaluation. DFW issues a cargo zone carrier-operations solicitation within 60 days. AT&T and Betacom (and any other qualified vendor) submit proposals. Evaluation criteria weighted: technical capability 40%, SLA commitments 20%, incumbent track record 30% (benefits AT&T), cost 10%. Award within 90 days. Winner deploys on cargo Phase 1A timeline.

4. **Terminal deployments (Phases 1-4):** AT&T is the carrier-operations partner per 60-month exclusivity. Multi-vendor evaluation triggers at Terminal F (Phase 3) per Trade B.

**Why this resolves the conflict:** AT&T gets competitive process for every significant expansion of carrier-operations scope. DFW retains the ability to evaluate alternatives through trial zones. The cargo timeline isn't held hostage to either position because passive infrastructure proceeds immediately and the competitive evaluation runs in parallel on a fast track (60-day solicitation + 90-day evaluation = 150 days, within the Phase 0/0.5 timeline).

**For AT&T:** You said "we'll win it on operational evidence." This gives you the opportunity. Your FedEx Memphis and UPS Louisville references are relevant. Bring your cargo proposal within 30 days as you committed.

**For the Airline VP:** Your SOFT S6 requesting production-grade alternatives during evaluation is accommodated through the Betacom trial zones operating at near-production scale in non-tenant areas, plus the competitive evaluation for cargo production operations.

**Status: I believe this is resolved. If AT&T cannot accept Betacom evaluation nodes in non-production zones alongside a competitive process for production cargo operations, this becomes the one remaining irreconcilable conflict and we escalate to executive session.**

| HC-3 | DFW sovereign security authority. All events to DFW SIEM in real-time. Cryptographic tenant isolation with DFW-directed HSM. Federal directive response within 4 hours (with narrow DPA exception per AT&T). CVSS ≥9.0 patched within 72 hours. Airline gets 30-min advance notification on slice security changes; 60-min post-event notification for zero-notice emergencies. | **Final — integrated all Airline carve-outs** |
| HC-4 | DFW owns Tier 1 data exclusively. Unified Tenant Data Framework as MSA Exhibit B. Explicit carve-outs for airline ops (Tier 2), cargo ops (Tier 3), concession transactions (Tier 4), AT&T telemetry (Tier 5). AT&T prohibited from commercial use of any DFW data, prohibited from cross-customer aggregation, data residency on DFW premises. | **Final — all peer carve-outs integrated** |
| HC-5 | FCC Part 96 compliance. AT&T real-time spectrum autonomy (sub-250ms DPA response) formalized as joint hard constraint. AT&T technical advisory authority: if AT&T certifies DFW policy creates RF/DPA compliance risk, DFW accepts recommendation or issues written override assuming liability. CBRS 2.0 adopted upon FCC finalization, AT&T bears carrier-layer costs, DFW bears infrastructure-layer costs. | **Final** |
| HC-6 | Phased deployment with rollback. Phase 0 (90 days baseline + cargo passive infrastructure immediate). Phase 0.5 (cargo active deployment, 6-9 months post-baseline). Phase 1 (Betacom evaluation expansion + concession pilot locations, 6 months). Phase 2 (Terminal C + commercial slice across operational terminals, 12 months). Phase 3 (Terminal F greenfield, 18 months). Phase 4 (legacy terminal migration, 24 months). Phase 5 (full campus, 12 months). Rollback tested at each phase gate. 4-hour rollback capability. Blackout windows: Nov 15-Jan 5, Mar 1-Mar 20 for concession zones. | **Final — integrated cargo acceleration, concession pilot, blackout windows** |
| HC-7 | Carrier-operations succession path. Warm-standby documentation in DFW-accessible escrow updated quarterly. 180-day transition assistance at pre-negotiated rates upon any termination. DFW may engage secondary partner for annual succession readiness assessment. All carrier-operations software either DFW-licensed or replaceable without infrastructure changes. | **Final** |
| HC-8 | Multi-tenant priority hierarchy contractually fixed as MSA Exhibit A. Tiers 0-7 as specified in Section 3. Time-sequenced degradation model as specified. Changes require written consent of all Tier 0 and Tier 1 tenants. AT&T executes hierarchy automatically; DFW sets policy. | **Final** |
| HC-9 | Per-terminal SLA measurement for airline slice (99.99%), per-location SLA measurement for concession POS (99.9%), per-warehouse-zone SLA measurement for cargo (99.99%). Independent synthetic monitoring, jointly selected monitoring vendor. | **Final** |
| HC-10 | **Revenue stream independence.** Tenant access fees, carrier offload, and data monetization tracked as separate independent streams with distinct formulas and accounting. No blending, cross-subsidization, or netting. | **Final — adopted from CRO HARD H9** |
| HC-11 | **Pre-authorized construction framework** for Terminal C and cargo zone developed during Phase 0 baseline period. Activities within framework proceed without per-event AT&T review. Concession CPE gateways classified as pre-approved device class (5-business-day notification). RF-active equipment energization within 30m of CWP APs requires 14-day review (standard) or 72-hour review (construction-integrated). 50m/30-day review retained ONLY for new radio equipment energization. | **Final — integrated Airline H9, Concessions S8, AT&T protection** |
| HC-12 | **Committed revenue before Board approval.** Cargo consortium prepaid access ($15M), AT&T offload floors ($500K-$1M/year), concessions LOI (200+ locations), airline term sheet (5-year commitment) — all executed before capital approval vote. | **Final — adopted from CRO HARD H14** |

### Soft Constraints

| ID | Constraint | Status |
|----|-----------|--------|
| SC-1 | Revenue share per Trade A: Tenant access 60/40→70/30→80/20; Carrier offload 55/45→70/30; Data 100/0. | **Aligned with CRO SOFT** |
| SC-2 | 10-year term with Year 5 binding renegotiation, Year 7 no-fault exit (18-month notice). | **Aligned with CRO SOFT** |
| SC-3 | Co-branding per Section 1 (CRO-H11): "DFW 5G powered by AT&T" on passenger portal and marketing. DFW-exclusive on data products, analytics dashboards, and secured zones. | **Final** |
| SC-4 | AT&T exclusivity 60 months with Month 36 performance review. Competitive evaluation for Terminal F with 30% incumbent track record weight and 18-month advance access. Betacom evaluation nodes up to 100 in designated non-production zones. | **Negotiated per Section 4** |
| SC-5 | AT&T co-invests $5M edge compute/MEC + 2 FTE onboarding (18 months) + CBRS 2.0 at AT&T cost + Year 5 tech refresh at AT&T cost. CWP assets purchased at FMV with unrecovered balance credited against co-investment. | **Aligned with CRO SOFT** |
| SC-6 | Concession pricing: $200/mo standard, $175/mo at 175+ locations, $80/mo ACDBE. Escalation capped at CPI or 3%. Most-favored-tenant clause. | **Aligned with CRO SOFT** |
| SC-7 | Cargo pricing: $0.25/sq ft/month with automation-grade SLA and edge compute included in base. | **Aligned with CRO SOFT** |
| SC-8 | $250K cargo acceleration bonus to AT&T if cargo zone revenue-generating by Q2 2026. | **Aligned with CRO SOFT** |
| SC-9 | Airline Terminal F: guaranteed performance specs (≤20ms, 50 Mbps/gate, zero-loss handoff) with co-design escalation if specs unmet after 90-day remediation. $8-12M co-investment as prepaid access. | **Trade H** |

---

## 6. WHAT I NEED FROM EACH PEER — FINAL ROUND

**From the CRO:**
- Written confirmation that the revenue share structure in Trade A satisfies your requirements for the Board presentation
- Confirmation that cargo zone revenue ($6M/year) and carrier offload floors ($500K-$1M/year) as committed contracts satisfy H-R3 (Year 2 ROI) and H14 (committed revenue before Board vote)
- Your recommendation on the independent financial model validator (I proposed Leigh Fisher or ICF)

**From the Airline VP:**
- Confirmation that the priority hierarchy (Exhibit A) with the time-sequenced degradation model satisfies H7, H8, and H10
- Confirmation that guaranteed Terminal F performance specs with co-design escalation satisfies S1
- Confirmation that the pre-authorized construction framework for Terminal C satisfies H9
- Terminal F co-investment structure: confirm prepaid access model aligns with your asset protection requirements

**From the Cargo Director:**
- Confirmation that Phase 0/0.5 parallel track with immediate passive infrastructure authorization satisfies HC-1 and HC-5
- General contractor contact information within 10 business days for RF design coordination
- Confirmation that $15M co-investment is structured as prepaid access fees on DFW-owned infrastructure
- Confirmation that edge compute model (shared platform, cryptographically isolated partitions, physical nodes in each warehouse) satisfies HC-2 and cold chain requirements

**From the Concessions Consortium:**
- Acceptance of Tier 4 priority with guaranteed bandwidth floors (5/2 Mbps normal, 1 Mbps emergency) as satisfying your "never dark" requirement
- Binding adoption commitment for 200+ locations within 12 months of general availability
- Pilot cohort of 10-15 locations designated for Phase 1 deployment
- CPE gateway hardware specifications from your POS vendors (Aloha, Toast, Square certified Ethernet/Wi-Fi handoff devices) to drive procurement timeline
- Confirmation that $200/mo pricing with volume discount and ACDBE tier satisfies H4

**From AT&T:**
- Response on the cargo zone competitive evaluation structure (60-day solicitation, 90-day evaluation, 30% incumbent weight)
- Response on Betacom evaluation nodes (up to 100, non-production zones, data shared with AT&T)
- Response on the RF buffer parameters (30m/14-day standard, 72-hour construction-integrated, 50m/30-day for new radio energization only, CPE as pre-approved device class)
- Carrier offload revenue projection methodology — assumptions, carriers, traffic volumes, pricing model, confidence interval — for Board presentation
- Confirmation of $5M co-investment + 2 FTE onboarding commitment + CBRS 2.0 at AT&T cost

---

## 7. PATH TO TERM SHEETS

Assuming Round 3 produces consensus, here is the documentation sequence:

arguments.name: Airport CRO
arguments.content:
# CRO Round 3 Position: Final Constraint Reconciliation

## Assessment

After three rounds, I can see the deal. The architecture works. The revenue model is defensible. Every constituency has moved. The remaining gaps are narrow and closeable — but they require precise language, not more posturing.

This round I'm doing three things: (1) formally accepting every peer HARD constraint with the specific accommodations required, (2) proposing the final trades that close the remaining gaps, and (3) locking down my updated constraint set for term sheet drafting.

---

## 1. SATISFYING ALL PEER HARD CONSTRAINTS

### Airline VP — All HARD Constraints Accepted

I'm going constraint by constraint because these represent the anchor tenant and I want zero ambiguity.

**Cryptographic slice isolation and data sovereignty:** Accepted without reservation. My H-R1 (data ownership) explicitly excludes airline operational data. The Unified Tenant Data Framework the Airline VP proposed — tenant operational data generated on tenant slices belongs to the tenant; DFW monetizes common-area, infrastructure-derived, passenger-behavioral data only — is the exact boundary I need for clean, litigation-proof data monetization. I will co-sponsor this framework as a binding MSA exhibit.

**99.99% uptime per terminal per month, application-layer measurement:** Accepted. This is an airline-slice SLA that doesn't conflict with my revenue operations. A more reliable airline network means fewer flight disruptions, which means more passengers dwelling in my concession program. This SLA helps me.

**30-second automatic failover:** Accepted. No conflict.

**No forced migration of 800-900 iPads and 200-400+ IoT devices:** Accepted. Parallel principle to concessions' POS hardware compatibility requirement. The network adapts to existing devices across all tenant categories.

**Airline configuration authority over its slice with 30-minute advance notification for DFW security overrides:** Accepted. The airline's operational agility within its own slice is not my concern. The 30-minute advance notification before security posture changes is reasonable governance that doesn't touch revenue operations.

**Penetration testing scheduling — 30 days' notice, no peak banking hours without consent, unredacted results:** Accepted. Standard enterprise security practice. No revenue impact.

**Priority hierarchy as binding technical specification, changes require consent of all Tier 0 and Tier 1 tenants:** This is the critical one. **I accept this structure.** Here's why: codifying the priority hierarchy as a binding technical specification actually *protects* my revenue model. It means no future DFW administration can unilaterally degrade concession connectivity to accommodate a new tenant class without the governance process triggering. The consent requirement for changes creates stability for every tenant, including my concession operators who need predictable service quality to justify their lease-integrated technology commitments.

**What I need incorporated:** The binding specification must include the guaranteed minimum floors for each tenant class during degradation events — not just the tier ordering. The 1 Mbps concession POS floor the Airline VP proposed in the time-sequenced model must be in that specification, not in a side letter. If it's binding for the airline at Tier 1, it's binding for my concession operators at their guaranteed floor.

**Airline ramp maintains full allocation before commercial traffic during seconds 30-300:** Accepted. This is the time-sequenced degradation model, and I'm embracing it fully — see my revised priority position below.

**Time-sequenced degradation model (Seconds 0-30 / 30-300 / sustained):** **Accepted as the governing framework for capacity-constrained events.** This is superior to the rigid tier numbering I proposed in Round 2. The airline's model gives every constituency a survivable path:

| Window | Behavior | Revenue Impact Assessment |
|--------|----------|--------------------------|
| Seconds 0-30 | All non-safety slices maintain minimum viable | Concession POS operates normally; no revenue impact |
| Seconds 30-300 | Passenger Wi-Fi and advertising fully shed; concession POS at 1 Mbps minimum; cargo cold chain maintains; airline ramp full allocation | POS transactions complete (1 Mbps is more than sufficient for card authorizations). Digital signage and IoT shed — acceptable. Advertising shed — lost impressions, but these events are rare and brief. |
| After 300 seconds | Sustained DPA allocation with proportional reduction | Extended degradation scenario; cellular backup activates for affected operators |

**My Round 2 Tier 5A/5B proposal is hereby withdrawn.** The time-sequenced model is better because it describes *behavior during degradation* rather than arguing about tier numbering. The concession operators' actual need — "degraded is acceptable, dark is not" — is met by the 1 Mbps guaranteed floor. I will advocate this framework to the concessions consortium.

**AT&T CWP 50-meter RF review must not delay Terminal C reconstruction:** Accepted. The pre-authorized construction framework during Phase 0 is essential for both Terminal C and cargo zone timelines. This protects construction-linked revenue opportunities (34+ new concession locations in Terminal C and Terminal F).

**Unified Tenant Data Framework as binding MSA exhibit:** Accepted and championed. I'm adopting the Airline VP's proposed framework with one structural enhancement — see my revised data position below.

**AT&T DPA response authority at 250ms with airline 30-second failover as complementary mechanism:** Accepted. These are engineering realities, not revenue decisions.

**Zero-notice emergency security directive: post-event notification to airline NOC within 60 minutes:** Accepted. Reasonable operational governance.

### CIO — All HARD Constraints Remain Satisfied

No changes from my Round 2 acceptance of HC-1 through HC-9. The CIO's revised Phase 0/0.5 structure for cargo acceleration is accepted. I reaffirm that commercial concession slicing across operational terminals by month 24 is a critical milestone I need the CIO to hold.

### Cargo Director — All HARD Constraints Remain Satisfied

No changes from Round 2. I continue to champion cargo Phase 1A acceleration as the fastest path to bankable recurring revenue. The cargo consortium's acceptance of Tier 3 priority with a hard performance floor is consistent with the airline's time-sequenced degradation model.

### Concessions Consortium — All HARD Constraints Accepted With Revisions

**H1 (99.9% per location):** Accepted.
**H2 (Dedicated POS slice):** Accepted. The priority tier numbering is superseded by the time-sequenced degradation model — POS gets a guaranteed 1 Mbps floor during capacity events, which delivers the "never dark" guarantee.
**H3 (Turnkey managed service):** Accepted.
**H4 (Per-location pricing ≤$250/mo):** Accepted.
**H5 (POS hardware compatibility):** Accepted.
**H6 (Transaction data ownership):** Accepted. **Data granularity revised — see below.**
**H7 (Zero-disruption transition):** Accepted including blackout periods.
**H8 (POS general availability by Month 24):** Accepted. I need this too — the tenant monetization platform at Month 30 requires POS connectivity as the foundation layer.

### AT&T — All HARD Constraints Accepted Per Round 2

**H1 (Carrier-layer authority):** Accepted.
**H2 (CWP investment recognition):** Accepted as CapEx credit, not revenue-share modifier.
**H3 (Carrier offload revenue share):** Accepted with the separated revenue stream structure.
**H4 (Operational staff access):** Accepted.
**H5 (Competitive evaluation protection):** Accepted. I support a competitive process for cargo zone carrier operations — the $15M and $4-6M/year in cargo revenue warrants it.
**H6 (Brand presence):** Accepted within the narrowed scope from AT&T's Round 2 revision. AT&T branding does not appear on DFW's data analytics products, advertising platform, or tenant-facing analytics dashboards.

---

## 2. REVISED POSITIONS ON CONTESTED ITEMS

### 2.1 Priority Hierarchy — RESOLVED

**I am adopting the Airline VP's time-sequenced degradation model as my position.** My Round 2 Tier 5A/5B proposal is withdrawn.

The static tier hierarchy for normal operations remains as the CIO proposed:

| Tier | Category |
|------|----------|
| 0 | Safety-critical (FAA/TSA/Emergency) |
| 1 | Airline ramp operations |
| 2 | Federal inspection (CBP, TSA checkpoint) |
| 3 | Cargo automation (AGV, cold chain) |
| 4 | Concession POS and financial transactions |
| 5 | Airport operations (DFW facilities) |
| 6 | Commercial IoT, digital signage, advertising |
| 7 | Passenger connectivity, carrier offload |

**But the operative enforcement mechanism is the time-sequenced degradation model, not the tier list.** During a capacity-constrained event:

- **Concession POS never drops below 1 Mbps per location** — this is the contractual floor, embedded in the binding technical specification alongside the tier ordering
- Under normal operations, the 99.9% per-location SLA and ≤50ms latency guarantee apply regardless of tier placement
- The tier ordering only governs *degradation sequencing during extraordinary events*, and even then, guaranteed minimums are honored

**To the concessions consortium:** I know you wanted Tier 3. I understand why. But the time-sequenced model with a guaranteed 1 Mbps floor delivers what you actually need — POS transactions that never fail — without creating an engineering fiction where coffee registers compete with autonomous vehicle control for priority resources. The 1 Mbps floor costs approximately 200 Mbps across all 200+ locations during worst-case scenarios. That's a rounding error on campus capacity. Your transactions will complete. I will put my name on that guarantee to the Board.

**This position satisfies:**
- Airline HARD constraint: ramp full allocation during seconds 30-300 ✓
- Airline HARD constraint: binding technical specification with tenant consent for changes ✓
- Concessions HARD constraint: dedicated POS slice with guaranteed minimum ✓
- Cargo acceptance: Tier 3 with performance floors ✓
- CIO: hierarchy set by DFW as infrastructure owner ✓

### 2.2 Data Ownership and Granularity — REVISED

The concessions consortium raised a legitimate concern about my Round 1 claim of "concession-storefront granularity (≤3m accuracy)" in DFW's Tier 1 data products. After two rounds of discussion, here is my final position:

**I am withdrawing the individual-storefront-level claim for DFW's general data monetization products.** The concessions consortium is right that operator-specific foot traffic data at 3-meter resolution is competitive intelligence, not passenger flow analytics. If DFW's landlord analytics can show that Operator A's traffic is 30% higher than Operator B's, that creates a conflict of interest in lease negotiations that will poison the tenant relationship and — more importantly — will cause operators to resist network adoption, destroying the demand base my revenue model depends on.

**Revised H-R1 data granularity framework:**

| Data Product | Resolution | Owner | Commercial Rights |
|---|---|---|---|
| DFW Tier 1 general analytics (sold to advertisers, airlines, third parties) | **Concession zone level** — clusters of 5+ locations minimum | DFW exclusive | DFW monetizes |
| Operator-specific analytics (sold back to individual operators) | **Individual storefront level** — available only to that specific operator | DFW owns the data product; operator controls access to their own location's data | **New revenue stream:** DFW sells as premium subscription to each operator |
| Aggregated concession performance (sold to investors, analysts, industry benchmarks) | **Portfolio level** — no individual operator identifiable | DFW exclusive | DFW monetizes |

**Revenue impact of this revision:**

This is actually revenue-*additive*, not revenue-destructive. Here's why:

- **Zone-level Tier 1 data** is what advertisers and airlines actually buy. "Terminal C food court cluster sees 40% traffic increase at 3PM during delay banks" is the insight that drives programmatic ad buys and airline lounge positioning. Storefront granularity doesn't improve this product.
- **Operator-specific analytics as premium subscription** creates a new revenue line: 200+ locations × $200-500/month for their own performance analytics = $480K-$1.2M additional annual revenue. Operators will pay for their own data when the alternative is flying blind on foot traffic patterns.
- **Cooperative data contribution from the consortium** (their O3 — anonymized foot traffic, dwell time, peak demand patterns) enriches the Tier 1 product with commercial behavior data that pure network telemetry cannot capture. Voluntary data sharing produces higher-quality, legally cleaner products than passive network surveillance.

**This position satisfies:**
- Airline HARD: Unified Tenant Data Framework with explicit tenant operational data exclusion ✓
- Concessions HARD: Transaction data ownership by operator ✓
- Concessions HARD (revised): Zone-level minimum resolution for concession-area analytics ✓
- Cargo HARD: Cold chain/regulatory compliance data excluded from DFW monetization ✓
- CIO HARD: DFW owns all common-area, infrastructure-derived data ✓
- My HARD (H-R1): DFW retains exclusive monetization rights on all Tier 1 data ✓

**Unified Tenant Data Framework — final version for MSA exhibit:**

| Data Tier | Owner | DFW Rights | AT&T Rights | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tier 1: Common-area passenger analytics | DFW exclusive | Full monetization at zone-level resolution for concession areas | None | Foot traffic, dwell time, device density, passenger flow |
| Tier 2: Airline operational | Airline exclusive | None — never enters DFW analytics pipeline | None | Ramp telemetry, baggage system data, crew movement |
| Tier 3: Cargo operational | Cargo operator exclusive | None | None | Cold chain monitoring, AGV fleet data, WMS telemetry |
| Tier 4: Concession transactional | Concession operator exclusive | None | None | POS transactions, basket data, payment identifiers |
| Tier 5: AT&T operational telemetry | AT&T operational use only | SIEM integration (read); no commercial use | Operational optimization; anonymized benchmarking (no DFW-identifiable data) | RF performance, SAS coordination, spectrum utilization |
| Tier 6: Contributed concession zone data | DFW, contributed by consortium | Enriches Tier 1 products per mutual data sharing agreement | None | Anonymized foot traffic counts, dwell time by zone, peak demand timing |

I will co-sponsor this framework as a binding MSA exhibit per the Airline VP's requirement.

---

## 3. PROPOSED TRADES — FINAL ROUND

### Trade A: Revenue Share — Closing the Gap

After three rounds, here's where the positions have converged:

| Stream | CIO R2 Position | My R2 Position | AT&T R2 Position | My R3 Proposal |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tenant access Y1-5 | DFW 60/AT&T 40 | DFW 60/AT&T 40 (Y1-3) | DFW 75/AT&T 25 | **DFW 70 / AT&T 30** |
| Tenant access Y6-10 | DFW 70/AT&T 30 | DFW 70/AT&T 30 (Y4-7), 80/20 (Y8-10) | DFW 85/AT&T 15 | **DFW 80 / AT&T 20** |
| Carrier offload Y1-5 | DFW 60/AT&T 40 | DFW 55/AT&T 45 | DFW 60/AT&T 40 | **DFW 60 / AT&T 40** |
| Carrier offload Y6-10 | DFW 70/AT&T 30 | DFW 70/AT&T 30 | DFW 75/AT&T 25 | **DFW 75 / AT&T 25** |
| Data monetization | DFW 100 | DFW 100 | DFW 100 | **DFW 100** |

**Why I'm moving tenant access to 70/30 from my previous 60/40:**

AT&T's Round 2 position offered 25%/15% on tenant access — far below what either the CIO or I proposed. AT&T has effectively conceded that tenant access revenue is DFW's domain. Their value-add is carrier offload, and I've preserved their 40% share there. Meanwhile, AT&T dropping data monetization to 0% eliminates the largest economic uncertainty from my revenue model.

**The trade:** I give AT&T 30% on tenant access in Years 1-5 (down from 40% in my Round 2 position). AT&T gets a higher share than they asked for on tenant access. In return:

- AT&T's carrier offload revenue floors **increase** to $600K Year 1, $900K Year 2, $1.2M Years 3-5 (up from their offered $500K/$750K/$1M)
- AT&T co-invests $4M in edge compute and MEC infrastructure (splitting the difference between CIO's $3-5M ask and AT&T's $3M offer)
- AT&T provides 2 dedicated FTEs for 18 months to support tenant onboarding (critical for adoption velocity)
- AT&T funds CBRS 2.0 migration at zero cost to DFW

**Board-level justification:** Giving AT&T a slightly higher tenant access share than they requested builds goodwill and signals partnership rather than extraction. The incremental cost to DFW is approximately $200-400K/year on projected tenant revenue. The return is higher carrier offload floors ($100-200K/year incremental), $4M in co-investment, 2 FTEs, and CBRS 2.0 migration — net positive for DFW by $1-2M over 5 years.

### Trade B: Contract Term and Exit Rights — Final Position

**I accept 10 years** with the following locked-in protections:

| Mechanism | Trigger | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Year 5 binding renegotiation | Automatic — not discretionary | Revenue share, SLA thresholds, technology refresh all on the table. Independent arbitration if parties can't agree within 90 days. |
| Year 5 technology refresh | AT&T obligation | AT&T funds 5G-Advanced carrier-layer upgrade at AT&T's cost |
| Year 7 no-fault exit | DFW unilateral with 18-month notice | AT&T provides 180-day transition assistance at pre-negotiated rates |
| Performance-triggered exit | Two consecutive quarters of material SLA breach across >25% of campus | DFW may terminate with 12-month notice; AT&T transition assistance obligations activate |

**What I trade to get this:** AT&T receives the marketing reference rights, conference co-presentation, and site visit hosting the CIO offered in O-5. These are genuinely valuable to AT&T's enterprise sales team and cost DFW nothing.

### Trade C: Concession Data Granularity for Adoption Commitment

**I give:** Zone-level minimum resolution (5+ location clusters) for all concession-area analytics in DFW's general Tier 1 data products. No individual-storefront data in any DFW product sold to third parties.

**I get:**
- Consortium's binding commitment to migrate all 200+ locations within 12 months of general availability
- Single-point-of-coordination for 60 operators (consortium handles onboarding, communication, first-level triage)
- Anonymized foot traffic and dwell time data contribution at no cost (enriches Tier 1 product)
- Consortium supports 10-year contract term
- **New revenue stream:** Operator-specific analytics subscription at $200-500/month per location, sold back to individual operators through DFW's platform

**Revenue math on the new subscription stream:**
- Conservative: 50% adoption at $300/month = 100 locations × $300 × 12 = $360K/year
- Moderate: 70% adoption at $350/month = 140 × $350 × 12 = $588K/year
- This revenue didn't exist in my Round 1 or Round 2 models

### Trade D: Cargo Revenue Acceleration for Phase 1A Support

**I give:** Full advocacy for immediate Phase 0 cargo authorization and Phase 1A parallel deployment. I will co-present the cargo business case to the Board as a revenue initiative, not a technology initiative.

**I get:**
- Cargo consortium revenue counted in Year 2 ROI model as committed demand
- $0.28/sq ft/month (compromise between cargo's $0.25 preferred and my $0.30 proposal) with automation-grade SLAs in base tier
- 5-year minimum term from all six anchor tenants
- Demonstration facility access for DFW's tenant recruitment across all categories

**Revenue at $0.28/sq ft:**
- 2M sq ft × $0.28/month = $560K/month = **$6.72M/year**
- 5-year committed: **$33.6M total from cargo alone**

### Trade E: AT&T CWP Investment Recognition — Final Structure

**I give:** AT&T's CWP investment recognized as a carrier-operations fee prepayment (option (a) from AT&T's Round 2 proposal), NOT as a revenue-share modifier.

**Why I insist on this structure:** A +10 percentage point revenue-share premium for 60 months (AT&T's option (b)) permanently reduces DFW's revenue during the exact period when tenant adoption is ramping and the revenue model is being proven to the Board. On projected Year 3 tenant access revenue of ~$8M, a 10-point premium costs DFW $800K/year — $4M over 60 months. The fee prepayment structure costs the same net dollars but doesn't embed a structural discount in the revenue-share formula that becomes a precedent for future negotiations.

**What AT&T gets:** DFW purchases CWP physical assets at independently appraised FMV (estimated $3.5-5M). AT&T's unrecovered investment (~$5-6.5M) is applied as a carrier-operations fee prepayment, reducing AT&T's first 36 months of operational billings to DFW. AT&T recovers their investment; DFW's revenue-share formula stays clean.

---

## 4. FLAGGING IRRECONCILABLE CONFLICTS

### Previously Flagged — Now Resolved

**Priority hierarchy (Concessions Tier 2 vs. Airline Tier 1):** Resolved by adopting the Airline VP's time-sequenced degradation model with guaranteed 1 Mbps POS floor. The rigid tier numbering debate is superseded by behavioral guarantees during degradation events.

**Data granularity (my storefront-level claim vs. concessions zone-level demand):** Resolved by my withdrawal of storefront-level claims for general products and creation of operator-specific analytics as a separate subscription revenue stream.

**CWP RF review vs. cargo/construction timelines:** Resolved by the pre-authorized construction framework during Phase 0, separating passive infrastructure from RF-active deployment.

### Remaining Tension: AT&T Competitive Evaluation for Cargo Zone

AT&T's H5 revision proposes a cargo zone carrier-operations RFP within 60 days, requiring AT&T, Betacom, and others to compete. The CIO's Phase 1 includes "Betacom trial expansion — 50 nodes in Terminal A cargo-side."

**My position:** I support a competitive process for the cargo zone carrier operations role — the $15M co-investment and $6.72M/year in recurring revenue justifies it.
