But the 45-day Phase 0 infrastructure decision **cannot wait** for the competitive process to conclude. Conduit and mounting points are vendor-neutral. Authorize Phase 0 immediately; run the carrier-operations RFP in parallel.

**If forced to choose:** I prioritize cargo revenue timeline over carrier-operations competition. If the competitive RFP delays cargo go-live by more than 90 days, I'd support the CIO proceeding with whichever provider is ready. AT&T's reference deployments at FedEx Memphis and UPS Louisville are compelling, but not at the cost of a $1.5M+ retrofit penalty and 6+ months of lost revenue.

**This is not irreconcilable** — both AT&T and the CIO have proposed separating Phase 0 passive infrastructure from active deployment decisions. The tension is manageable if everyone commits to the parallel tracks.

### No New Irreconcilable Conflicts Identified

All peer HARD constraints from Round 2 are satisfiable within my revised position.

---

## 5. UPDATED CONSTRAINT SET

### HARD Constraints — Final

| ID | Constraint | Status from Round 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **H-R1** | DFW owns 100% of Tier 1 common-area data. Explicit exclusions: airline operational (Tier 2), cargo operational (Tier 3), concession transactional (Tier 4). **Zone-level minimum resolution** (5+ location clusters) for concession-area analytics in general products. Operator-specific data available only to that operator via paid subscription. AT&T retains Tier 5 operational telemetry for network management and anonymized benchmarking only. Unified Tenant Data Framework as binding MSA exhibit. | **Revised:** Withdrew storefront-level claim; adopted zone-level minimum; created operator-specific subscription revenue stream. Satisfies all peer data HARD constraints. |
| **H-R2** | Tenant monetization platform technically capable by CIO Phase 2 (month 24-30); revenue-generating by Phase 3 entry (month 30-36). POS connectivity general availability by Month 24. Penalty for AT&T-attributable delay: 200 bps revenue share reduction per quarter, attributable only through joint root-cause analysis. | **Revised:** Accepted AT&T's joint attribution proposal (Trade 5 from AT&T R2). Added Month 24 POS connectivity milestone per concessions H8. |
| **H-R3** | ≥$2M net annual operational savings by Year 2, verified by independent financial model. Cargo committed revenue ($6.72M/year) and carrier offload floors ($600K+ Year 1) counted as committed demand in ROI model. | **Maintained with updated revenue figures.** |
| **H-R4** | Zero concession disruption during buildout. $15K/location/day penalty for outages >4 hours attributable to deployment. Blackout periods: Nov 15-Jan 5, Mar 1-Mar 20. Star Concessions dedicated transition support. 90-day parallel operation, per-location cutover, 4-hour rollback. | **Maintained.** Fully aligned with all peer positions. |
| **H-R5** | Three pricing tiers (Enterprise, Standard, ACDBE/Small Business). ACDBE tier ≤40% of Enterprise rate. Subsidy funded from DFW revenue share. Published transparently. Simple monthly fee per location. | **Maintained.** No peer conflicts. |
| **H-R6** | Revenue stream separation: tenant access fees, carrier offload, data monetization, and operator analytics subscriptions tracked and reported independently. No blending or cross-subsidization. Each stream with its own accounting, minimums, and renegotiation triggers. | **Maintained with addition** of operator analytics subscription as fourth tracked stream. |
| **H-R7 (NEW)** | Priority hierarchy adopted as binding technical specification per Airline VP requirement. Time-sequenced degradation model governs capacity-constrained events. Concession POS guaranteed minimum 1 Mbps floor per location during all degradation scenarios. Changes to priority hierarchy or guaranteed floors require consent of all Tier 0 and Tier 1 tenants. | **New.** Formalizes acceptance of airline's HARD constraints on priority governance. |

### SOFT Constraints — Final

| ID | Constraint | Status |
|---|---|---|
| **S-R1** | Revenue share: Tenant access DFW 70/AT&T 30 Y1-5, DFW 80/AT&T 20 Y6-10. Carrier offload DFW 60/AT&T 40 Y1-5, DFW 75/AT&T 25 Y6-10. Data monetization DFW 100%. Conditioned on AT&T $4M edge compute co-investment, 2 FTE onboarding support for 18 months, CBRS 2.0 at zero cost, carrier offload floors of $600K/$900K/$1.2M. | **Revised from R2.** Moved tenant access from 60/40 to 70/30 Y1-5 given AT&T's own 75/25 offer. Higher AT&T share than AT&T requested builds goodwill; incremental cost offset by higher carrier offload floors and co-investment. |
| **S-R2** | Advertising platform: DFW exclusive, AT&T as non-exclusive distribution partner at 15% commission with $500K Year 1 / $1M Year 2 minimums. AT&T branding does not appear on analytics products or advertising platform. | **Maintained.** |
| **S-R3** | 10-year term with Year 5 binding renegotiation (independent arbitration if no agreement within 90 days), Year 5 technology refresh at AT&T cost, Year 7 no-fault exit with 18-month notice, performance-triggered exit for sustained SLA breach. | **Maintained from R2.** |
| **S-R4** | Concession pricing at $200/location/month standard, volume discount to $175 at 175+ locations, ACDBE at $80/month. Most-favored-tenant clause. CPI or 3% annual escalation cap. | **Maintained.** |
| **S-R5** | Cargo pricing at $0.28/sq ft/month with automation-grade SLAs in base tier. 5-year minimum term. | **Revised from R2** ($0.30 → $0.28 as compromise). |
| **S-R6** | AT&T CWP investment recognized as carrier-operations fee prepayment against first 36 months of operational billings. NOT as revenue-share modifier. | **New — formalizes position from Trade E.** |
| **S-R7** | Cargo acceleration bonus: $250K one-time to AT&T if cargo zone revenue-generating by Q2 2026. | **Maintained from R2.** |
| **S-R8 (NEW)** | Operator-specific analytics subscription: $200-500/month per location, sold to individual concession operators through DFW's platform. Projected $360-588K/year at moderate adoption. | **New revenue stream** created by resolving data granularity conflict. |

---

## 6. UPDATED REVENUE MODEL — Board Presentation Ready

| Revenue Stream | Year 1 | Year 2 | Year 3 | Year 5 | 5-Year Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cargo Tenant Access (DFW share @ 70%) | $3.3M | $4.7M | $4.7M | $4.7M | $22.1M |
| Concession Platform (DFW share @ 70%) | — | $0.3M | $0.5M | $0.6M | $1.7M |
| Operator Analytics Subscriptions (DFW 100%) | — | $0.2M | $0.4M | $0.6M | $1.5M |
| Airline Tenant Access (DFW share @ 70%) | — | $0.4M | $1.1M | $1.8M | $4.6M |
| Carrier Offload (DFW share @ 60%) | $0.4M | $0.5M | $0.7M | $0.9M | $3.2M |
| Data Analytics / Advertising (DFW 100%) | — | $0.5M | $2.0M | $4.0M | $9.0M |
| Operational Savings (DFW 100%) | $1.0M | $2.0M | $2.5M | $3.0M | $11.5M |
| **Total DFW Revenue** | **$4.7M** | **$8.6M** | **$11.9M** | **$15.6M** | **$53.6M** |

**Year 2 ROI:** $8.6M DFW revenue against estimated $5-7M net DFW CapEx through Year 2 (after cargo $15M co-investment credit and AT&T $4M co-investment). **Positive cumulative ROI achieved mid-Year 2.** ✓

**5-Year Net Value:** $53.6M DFW revenue against $12-16M net DFW CapEx = **$37-41M net positive over 5 years.** ✓

**Conservative stress test (30% revenue haircut):** $37.5M revenue against $16M CapEx = $21.5M net positive. Still board-defensible. ✓

Note vs. Round 2: Revenue model improved by ~$14M over 5 years through (a) higher DFW share on tenant access (70% vs. 60%), (b) new operator analytics subscription stream, (c) higher carrier offload floors, and (d) refined cargo pricing. The data granularity concession to the concessions consortium *increased* total revenue by creating the subscription product.

---

## 7. WHAT I NEED FROM EACH PEER TO CLOSE

**From the CIO:**
- Confirm acceptance of the time-sequenced degradation model as the binding priority framework, replacing the rigid Tier 0-7 numbering as the operative enforcement mechanism
- Confirm Month 24 commercial concession slice availability across operational terminals — this is my H-R2 prerequisite for the Month 30 platform launch
- Confirm the Unified Tenant Data Framework (Section 2.2 above) as the data ownership structure for the MSA exhibit
- Issue the 45-day cargo zone Phase 0 design authority — every day of delay costs identifiable dollars

**From the Airline VP:**
- Confirm that the 1 Mbps concession POS floor is included in the binding technical specification alongside the time-sequenced degradation model — not as a separate side agreement
- Confirm that the Unified Tenant Data Framework satisfies your data sovereignty requirements
- Provide the cost-per-departure pricing model parameters so my finance team can model airline revenue alongside flat-fee concession and per-sq-ft cargo revenue

**From the Cargo Director:**
- Confirm $0.28/sq ft/month with automation-grade SLAs in base tier is acceptable
- Confirm 5-year minimum term for all six anchor tenants
- Confirm demonstration facility access for tenant recruitment across all categories

**From the Concessions Consortium:**
- Confirm acceptance of the time-sequenced degradation model with 1 Mbps guaranteed floor in lieu of the Tier 3 placement request
- Confirm acceptance of zone-level minimum resolution for general analytics products
- Confirm binding 200+ location migration commitment within 12 months of general availability
- Identify CPE gateway hardware vendors certified by Aloha, Toast, and Square — I need this for procurement timeline integration with the CIO

**From AT&T:**
- Confirm carrier offload floors at $600K/$900K/$1.2M
- Confirm $4M edge compute co-investment
- Confirm 2 FTE onboarding support for 18 months
- Confirm acceptance of CWP investment as fee prepayment (option (a)), not revenue-share modifier (option (b))
- Submit cargo zone carrier-operations proposal within 30 days of any RFP issuance

---

## Closing

Three rounds have produced convergence on every material point. The architecture is agreed. The phasing is agreed. The data framework is agreed. The priority mechanism is agreed. The remaining items are commercial parameters — revenue share percentages, pricing tiers, co-investment amounts — where every party's positions are within negotiable range.

My revenue model shows $53.6M in DFW value over 5 years with positive ROI by mid-Year 2. That number is built on contractually committed demand from cargo ($33.6M over 5 years), guaranteed concession adoption (200+ locations), airline anchor tenancy (800-900 devices Day 1), and AT&T carrier offload floors — not aspirational projections. Every major revenue line has a committed counterparty behind it.

The data granularity concession to the concessions consortium didn't weaken the model — it created a new subscription revenue stream and secured the 200+ location adoption commitment that the entire platform depends on. That's the kind of trade I'll make every time: give up a theoretical data right to secure actual committed revenue.

I'm ready for term sheets. Draft the priority hierarchy as a binding technical specification. Draft the Unified Tenant Data Framework as an MSA exhibit. Draft the revenue-share tables. Let the engineers validate the SLA commitments against the architecture. Then bring it to the Board.

The math works. The tenants are committed. The data is protected. Let's build.

arguments.name: Anchor Airline VP
arguments.content:
# Round 3 Position: Anchor Airline VP of Airport Operations
## Constraint Reconciliation Against CRO Hard Constraints and All Peer Positions

---

## Preamble

I've now reviewed the CRO's formalized hard constraints alongside every peer's Round 2 position. I'll say what I suspect others are thinking: **we are closer to a deal than the volume of paper suggests.** The CRO's constraints are almost entirely compatible with mine. The remaining conflicts are resolvable with precise language, not further negotiation rounds.

I'll structure this tightly: (1) how I satisfy every CRO hard constraint, (2) where I've revised, (3) specific trades, (4) remaining conflicts, and (5) my updated constraint set.

---

## 1. SATISFYING ALL CRO HARD CONSTRAINTS

I've mapped each of the CRO's fourteen hard constraints against my position. **All are satisfiable.** Here is the explicit reconciliation:

### CRO H-R: DFW Infrastructure Ownership for Data Title

**Status: Fully satisfied.** I accepted DFW infrastructure ownership in Round 1 (Revision 1) and confirmed in Round 2. Our $8-12M Terminal F co-investment is structured as prepaid infrastructure access with DFW holding title, consistent with the CIO's HC-2. We do not claim ownership of any DFW-owned infrastructure. Our co-invested assets are tagged in the asset registry; if we depart, we receive depreciated book value — not the equipment itself.

### CRO H-R: Tenant Monetization Platform Timeline (Month 24-30 Capable, Month 30-36 Revenue)

**Status: No conflict.** Our operations are on a dedicated slice that is architecturally independent from the commercial monetization platform. The CRO's timeline for concession analytics, advertising, and tenant services does not compete with or constrain our ramp operations deployment. We actively support this timeline — a revenue-generating tenant platform strengthens the business case that funds the infrastructure our operations depend on.

**What I'll contribute:** Our 5-year anchor tenant commitment and $8-12M co-investment are bankable demand commitments that support the CRO's board presentation for CapEx approval (satisfying the CRO's committed-revenue-before-board-vote requirement). I'll formalize this in writing for the board package.

### CRO H-R: AT&T Delay Penalty (200 bps/quarter)

**Status: No conflict.** This penalty applies to AT&T-attributable delays to the tenant monetization platform. It does not affect airline operations SLAs, which carry their own remedy structure (our H2: $50K per terminal per incident, plus documented actual delay costs at $74/minute). The two penalty frameworks operate on different revenue streams and different SLA measurement points. No cross-contamination risk.

### CRO H-R: $2M Net Annual Operational Savings by Year 2

**Status: Actively supportive.** Our anchor tenancy contributes directly to this target. At any reasonable tenant access fee structure, our 800-900 iPads, 200-400+ IoT devices, and 70-75% of total airline-tenant demand represent the single largest recurring revenue contribution in Year 2. Combined with cargo consortium revenue ($4-6M/year) and operational savings from CWP consolidation, the $2M threshold is reachable with committed — not projected — demand.

### CRO H-R: No Concession Cutover During Peak Revenue Periods (Nov 15–Jan 5, Mar 1–Mar 20)

**Status: Accepted and extended.** These blackout windows protect our operations as much as concession revenue. The Wednesday before Thanksgiving and the first week of January are among our highest departure days. We support these blackout periods and propose they apply to **all tenant-affecting network changes**, not just concession cutovers. I don't want anyone energizing new small cells during our peak banking periods either.

**Minor addition:** I request that airline-specific cutover activities also observe our peak banking hours (0600-0900, 1500-1800, 2000-2200 local) as restricted windows, separate from the seasonal blackouts. This is a scheduling constraint, not a new hard requirement — we accept maintenance windows during off-peak overnight hours.

### CRO H-R: Per-Location Cutover with 90-Day Parallel Operation and 4-Hour Rollback

**Status: Fully aligned.** This mirrors our H4 requirement (90-day parallel operation, rollback at our discretion). The CRO and I independently arrived at the same transition protection framework. No revision needed.

### CRO H-R: $15K/Location/Day Concession Outage Penalty

**Status: No conflict.** This is a concession-specific remedy that operates independently from our airline SLA remedies. We support it — it aligns AT&T's and DFW's incentives to protect concession operations during deployment, which protects our passengers' terminal experience.

### CRO H-R: ACDBE Pricing ≤40% of Enterprise Tier

**Status: No conflict.** Airline pricing is a separate tenant category from concession pricing. We support ACDBE accessibility — our passengers use these businesses, and a two-tier concession ecosystem is bad for everyone.

### CRO H-R: Separate Revenue Stream Accounting (Tenant Access vs. Carrier Offload)

**Status: Accepted and valued.** This is sound financial governance. Blending revenue streams obscures performance and creates opportunities for cross-subsidization that distorts incentive alignment. Our tenant access fees should be tracked independently from carrier offload, data monetization, and concession platform fees. We support this as a structural principle.

### CRO H-R: DFW Tier 1 Data Ownership — Explicitly Excludes Airline Operational Data

**Status: This is the single most important CRO constraint for our operations.** The CRO's formalized Tier 1 data scope — "common-area passenger flow, device density, dwell time" — with explicit exclusion of "airline operational data, concession transaction data, and cargo operational data" — **fully satisfies our H1 data sovereignty requirement.**

I want to acknowledge this directly: the CRO got this right. By narrowing the Tier 1 scope to common-area network-derived analytics and explicitly carving out tenant operational data, the CRO created a data framework that is both monetizable and legally defensible. Our ramp telemetry, baggage system metadata, crew movement patterns, and gate operations data stay on our slice, under our control, never touching DFW's analytics pipeline. This is exactly what we demanded, and it's now a CRO hard constraint — meaning it has two constituencies defending it.

**What I'll formalize:** The Unified Tenant Data Framework I proposed in Round 2 (Conflict 2 resolution) is now fully consistent with the CRO's position. I'll incorporate the CRO's language verbatim into my constraint set as the governing data classification standard.

### CRO H-R: AT&T Branding Excluded from DFW Data Analytics and Tenant Dashboards

**Status: Supported.** Consistent with our Round 2 position (Trade 5) that AT&T branding does not appear in airline operational interfaces or secured zones. The CRO's branding scope — limited to tenant service agreements, passenger portal, and DFW marketing — is acceptable.

### CRO H-R: Concession POS Guaranteed Minimum Bandwidth Floor (5 Mbps/2 Mbps per location, 1 Gbps total)

**Status: Accepted. This is the priority hierarchy resolution mechanism.**

Let me be explicit about why I'm accepting this. In Rounds 1 and 2, the priority hierarchy was a multi-party conflict — concessions wanted Tier 2, we demanded Tier 1 with concessions at Tier 5, cargo wanted "last non-safety degraded," and the CIO proposed various tier structures.

The CRO's bandwidth floor constraint effectively **bypasses the tier debate** by guaranteeing concession POS a minimum viable allocation that survives all degradation scenarios. Five Mbps down / 2 Mbps up per location is more than sufficient for POS transaction processing — a credit card authorization is kilobytes. One Gbps total across 200 locations is a fraction of campus CBRS capacity, even during a DPA event that cuts total capacity by 40%.

**What this means for our Tier 1 position:** The guaranteed bandwidth floor for concession POS means that POS traffic has an architecturally protected minimum that is never shed — regardless of what tier number it carries. This eliminates the scenario where concessions competes with airline ramp operations for radio resources during a capacity crunch. The floor is pre-allocated; our Tier 1 allocation sits above it; no contention.

**My revised priority hierarchy position (H6 update):**

| Tier | Traffic Class | Mechanism |
|------|-------------|-----------|
| 0 | Safety-critical (FAA, TSA emergency, fire/police) | Absolute priority, never shed |
| 1 | Airline ramp operations | Guaranteed minimum capacity, never shed below operational minimum |
| 2 | Federal inspection (CBP, TSA checkpoint analytics) | Guaranteed minimum capacity |
| 3 | Cargo automation (AGV, cold chain, sort operations) | Guaranteed minimum with degradation floor per Cargo Trade 2 |
| 4 | Concession POS / financial transactions | **5 Mbps/2 Mbps per-location floor, architecturally guaranteed** per CRO hard constraint |
| 5 | Airport operations (DFW facility management) | Degradable |
| 6 | Commercial IoT, digital signage, advertising, non-POS concession | Best-effort |
| 7 | Passenger Wi-Fi offload, carrier offload, streaming | First to shed |

**To Concessions:** I know you wanted Tier 3. But the CRO's bandwidth floor gives you something better than a tier number — it gives you an **architecturally enforced minimum** that survives any degradation event. A Tier 3 classification without a bandwidth floor means your traffic gets proportionally reduced alongside cargo during a DPA event. A Tier 4 classification **with** a guaranteed 5/2 Mbps floor means your POS never goes dark regardless of what happens at Tiers 0-3. The floor is the protection. The tier is the label. Take the protection.

### CRO H-R: Separate Authorization Track for Passive vs. Active Cargo Infrastructure

**Status: Accepted.** We explicitly supported cargo's accelerated timeline in Round 2 (Trade 3, Offer O6). Passive infrastructure embedding — conduit, cable trays, antenna mounting points, fiber pathways — has zero RF interference risk and should proceed on the cargo construction schedule, not the campus-wide 5G deployment schedule. This is engineering common sense.

### CRO H-R: Committed Revenue Contracts Required Before Board Approval

**Status: We are the primary satisfier of this constraint.** Our 5-year anchor tenant agreement (O1), representing 800-900 iPads, 200-400+ IoT devices, and 70-75% of airline-tenant demand, is the largest single committed revenue contract DFW will present to the board. Combined with cargo's $15M co-investment and 5-year commitment, and the concessions consortium's 200+ location adoption guarantee, the board has three bankable demand commitments before voting on CapEx.

**Formal offer:** I will execute a binding letter of intent for the 5-year network tenant agreement within 30 days of agreement-in-principle on the term sheet, specifically to support the CRO's board approval timeline. The LOI will include committed device counts, terminal scope, and minimum annual fee structure — sufficient for the CFO to treat it as committed revenue in the capital allocation model.

---

## 2. KEY REVISIONS FROM ROUND 2

### Revision A: Priority Hierarchy — From Rigid Tiers to Guaranteed Floors

My Round 2 position proposed a "time-sequenced degradation" model (seconds 0-30, 30-300, 300+). The CRO's bandwidth floor constraint provides a cleaner mechanism. I'm adopting it.

**Updated H6:** The priority hierarchy is tiered as shown above. Tiers 0-4 each carry a **guaranteed minimum bandwidth floor** that is architecturally pre-allocated and never shed. The specific floor values:

- Tier 0 (Safety): Absolute priority — uncapped
- Tier 1 (Airline ramp): Minimum 50 Mbps sustained per gate area, sub-20ms latency (per our S1 Terminal F performance specs, applied campus-wide)
- Tier 2 (Federal): Per TSA/CBP requirements (DFW determines)
- Tier 3 (Cargo): Per cargo consortium HC-3 — degradation floor of 50ms AGV / 200ms cold chain
- Tier 4 (Concession POS): 5 Mbps / 2 Mbps per location, per CRO hard constraint

Above these floors, capacity is allocated dynamically by tier priority during congestion. Below Tier 4, no guaranteed floor — best-effort during capacity constraints.

**The hierarchy is codified as a binding technical specification in the master infrastructure agreement, changeable only with consent of all Tier 0-2 tenants.** This is my remaining hard requirement on priority — not the tier numbers, but the governance mechanism preventing unilateral changes.

### Revision B: Data Ownership — Adopting CRO Language

My Round 2 Unified Tenant Data Framework is updated to incorporate the CRO's exact Tier 1 scope language:

| Data Tier | Owner | Scope | Source |
|-----------|-------|-------|--------|
| Tier 1 | DFW exclusive | Common-area passenger flow, device density, dwell time — **excluding** airline operational data, concession transaction data, cargo operational data | CRO H-R (this aligns with CIO HC-4 refined) |
| Tier 2 (Airline Ops) | Airline exclusive | Ramp telemetry, baggage system data, crew communications, gate operations, fleet coordination | Our H1 + CRO exclusion |
| Tier 3 (Cargo Ops) | Cargo operator exclusive | WMS, AGV, cold chain, sort operations, pharmaceutical compliance records | Cargo HC-4 + CRO exclusion |
| Tier 4 (Concession Transactions) | Operator exclusive | POS transactions, basket composition, payment data, customer identifiers, sales velocity | Concessions H6 + CRO exclusion |
| Tier 5 (AT&T Operations) | AT&T retained (operational only) | RF telemetry, SAS coordination, spectrum utilization, QoS metrics | CIO HC-4 + AT&T position |

This is now a **five-party consensus framework.** Every stakeholder has independently validated its boundaries. I recommend it be attached as Exhibit A to the master infrastructure agreement.

### Revision C: Terminal C Construction — Pre-Authorized Framework

My Round 2 H7 (AT&T CWP review cannot delay Terminal C rebuild) is now addressed through the CRO's passive/active infrastructure authorization split. Applying the same principle to Terminal C:

- Passive infrastructure (conduit, fiber pathways, mounting points) in Terminal C rebuild zones proceeds on construction schedule — separate authorization track
- Active RF equipment energization triggers AT&T's RF review, but under a **pre-authorized construction framework** developed during Phase 0 (per my Round 2 proposal)
- Terminal C rebuild sections designed with 5G-native infrastructure from day one, with the CIO's Phase 2 deploying active equipment as construction sections complete

This resolves the tension without requiring AT&T to waive CWP protection.

---

## 3. PROPOSED TRADES

### Trade A: Anchor Tenant LOI for Board Timeline → Priority Hierarchy Governance Lock

**We give:** Binding letter of intent for 5-year network tenant agreement within 30 days of agreement-in-principle. Includes committed device counts (800-900 iPads, 200+ IoT day one, growing to 400+), terminal scope (A, B, C, F), and minimum annual fee structure. This satisfies the CRO's committed-revenue-before-board-vote requirement and de-risks the CapEx approval.

**We need:** The priority hierarchy (Tiers 0-4 with guaranteed bandwidth floors) codified as a binding technical specification — not a governance policy — in the master infrastructure agreement. Changes to Tier 0-2 classifications require consent of Tier 0 and Tier 1 tenants. Changes to bandwidth floors require consent of the affected tier's tenant representatives.

**Why the CRO should accept:** The CRO needs committed revenue contracts for the board. We're the largest single demand commitment. The governance lock we're requesting protects the CRO's own concession POS bandwidth floor (Tier 4) from future dilution just as much as it protects our Tier 1 allocation. The CRO's $4.2B concession portfolio is better protected under a locked hierarchy than under a policy document that a future DFW VP of IT could amend to accommodate a new revenue-generating tenant.

### Trade B: Operational Correlation Data → Real-Time Slice Telemetry

**We give:** Anonymized, aggregated operational correlation data (per our Round 2 O7). Specifically: quarterly reports showing statistical correlations between network performance metrics and airline operational KPIs (on-time departure rate, baggage mishandle rate, ramp incident frequency). This data is invaluable for DFW's business case — "5G network improved on-time performance by X%" is a headline the CRO can use with the board, the media, and prospective tenants.

**We need:** Real-time API access to our slice's performance telemetry at 5-second granularity with 60-second reporting delay. Our operations analytics platform correlates network performance with OTP metrics in near-real-time. This is not competitive intelligence — it's infrastructure monitoring from the consumer's perspective.

**Why the CRO should accept:** The correlation data we're offering enriches the CRO's analytics product and board narrative at zero cost. "Network connectivity improvements correlated with 8% improvement in on-time pushbacks" is a more compelling story than any foot-traffic heat map.

### Trade C: Terminal F Co-Investment → Application-Level Performance Guarantees

**We give:** $8-12M co-investment in Terminal F airline-specific infrastructure, structured as prepaid access fees per DFW ownership model. Assets are DFW-owned, tagged as airline-funded in the asset registry, with depreciated book value compensation if we depart.

**We need:** Contractually guaranteed application-level performance specifications for Terminal F operational zones — these are the specs from our S1 that we've been carrying since Round 1:

| Parameter | Requirement | Measurement Point |
|-----------|------------|-------------------|
| Round-trip latency | ≤ 20ms, 95th percentile | Ramp device to airline edge application |
| Sustained downlink | ≥ 50 Mbps per gate area | At gate face during peak banking |
| Handoff success | ≥ 99.95% | Between indoor (gate) and outdoor (ramp), device at tug speed ≤15 mph |
| Simultaneous devices | ≥ 75 per gate area | Mix of iPads, IoT sensors, crew devices |

If DFW meets these specs, we accept DFW's design authority over antenna placement and network topology in Terminal F. If DFW wants to retain design flexibility, these are the measurable outcomes that flexibility must deliver. We co-invest based on outcomes, not inputs.

**Why the CRO should accept:** $8-12M in co-investment contributes to the CapEx recovery model and strengthens the board case. Terminal F is the CRO's greenfield monetization opportunity — 31 gates of new concession locations with 5G-native infrastructure. Our co-investment helps fund the infrastructure those concessions will run on. Requiring that the infrastructure actually performs for our operations is not a concession the CRO is making — it's a quality standard that protects everyone's investment.

### Trade D: Support for Cargo Phase 0 Authorization → Cargo Supports Locked Hierarchy

This trade was proposed in Round 2 and I'm reaffirming it. We explicitly support:
- 45-day Phase 0-Cargo authorization for passive infrastructure embedding
- Separate authorization track for passive vs. active deployment (per CRO hard constraint)
- Cargo consortium's $15M co-investment counted as committed revenue for board approval

In return, cargo accepts Tier 3 in the priority hierarchy with the degradation floor specified in Round 2 (≤50ms AGV, ≤200ms cold chain during DPA events), and supports the governance lock on the hierarchy as a binding technical specification.

**Status with cargo:** This trade was implicitly accepted in the Cargo Director's Round 2 (Trade 2). I'm requesting formal confirmation in Round 3.

### Trade E: Contract Term Acceptance → Airline-Specific Performance Review Rights

**We give:** Acceptance of the CRO's preferred 10-year initial term with Year 5 renegotiation and Year 7 no-fault exit. Our 5-year minimum commitment fits within this structure. We also accept the Year 5 binding technology refresh obligation.

**We need:** An airline-specific performance review right at Year 3 (month 36), separate from the campus-wide Year 5 renegotiation. This review evaluates our slice's SLA performance over 24+ months of production operation and determines whether our Terminal F deployment proceeds under the existing architecture or requires modifications. This is not an exit right — it's a quality gate before we commit our largest terminal to the network.

**Why the CRO should accept:** The Year 3 airline review de-risks the Terminal F deployment for everyone. If our slice SLAs are being met, the review is a formality and Terminal F proceeds on schedule. If they're not, catching the problem at Year 3 (before Terminal F construction is complete) is vastly cheaper than discovering it at Terminal F opening day. The CRO's Terminal F concession revenue depends on the network working for airlines first — if our operations can't function, the terminal doesn't operate, and no one buys coffee.

---

## 4. REMAINING CONFLICTS

### Conflict 1: Concession POS Priority Tier — Resolvable

**Status: Effectively resolved** by the CRO's bandwidth floor constraint. The concessions consortium wanted Tier 2 or Tier 3. The CRO's hard constraint guarantees 5 Mbps/2 Mbps per location regardless of tier placement. As I argued above, the floor is the protection; the tier is the label.

**Remaining risk:** If Concessions insists on Tier 3 as a hard constraint (their revised H2 from Round 2), this conflicts with our H6 only if the Tier 3 classification causes POS traffic to compete with our Tier 1 allocation for the same radio resources during degradation. The bandwidth floor mechanism eliminates this competition — POS gets its pre-allocated floor, we get our pre-allocated floor, no contention.

**My position:** I can accept Tier 4 for concession POS with the CRO's 5/2 Mbps guaranteed floor, **or** Tier 3 with a guaranteed floor, **provided** the technical implementation ensures that concession POS bandwidth floors are served from a pre-allocated capacity pool that does not draw from Tier 1 airline allocation during degradation events. AT&T's engineering team needs to confirm this is architecturally feasible. I believe it is — it's essentially pre-allocated resource blocks per slice, which is how 5G network slicing is designed to work.

**Action needed:** AT&T engineering confirmation that per-tier guaranteed bandwidth floors can be implemented as pre-allocated resource blocks that don't create inter-tier contention. If yes, the tier numbering debate is moot and we can finalize.

### Conflict 2: AT&T Competitive Evaluation vs. Betacom — Not Our Fight, But We Have a Position

AT&T's H5 demands competitive evaluation before any competing vendor serves production traffic. The CIO's Phase 1 includes Betacom trial expansion. The Cargo Director needs carrier operations for Q1 2026 go-live.

**Our position:** We don't care who operates the carrier layer as long as our SLAs are met. For Terminal F specifically, we support a competitive evaluation where AT&T's track record gets meaningful weight (30% per AT&T's S4) but DFW retains the right to run production-grade alternatives. For the cargo zone, AT&T's proposed competitive RFP within 60 days is reasonable and doesn't delay cargo's timeline if passive infrastructure embedding proceeds in parallel.

**What we oppose:** Any structure where AT&T has a contractual veto over DFW testing alternative carrier operators in production environments. The warm-standby succession requirement (CIO HC-7, which AT&T accepted in their O5) only works if DFW has practical experience with at least one alternative operator. Betacom trial data from 50-100 nodes provides that experience. We support the CIO's position here.

### Conflict 3: CWP RF Buffer Parameters — Narrowing

The CIO proposed 30m/14-day standard, 72-hour expedited for construction-integrated work, 50m/30-day only for new radio energization. AT&T's Round 2 didn't explicitly accept this tiered approach.

**Our position:** The CIO's tiered proposal is correct. We need the 72-hour expedited review for Terminal C construction-integrated work (our H7). The CRO's separate authorization track for passive vs. active infrastructure provides the structural framework. Passive work (conduit, fiber, mounting) has no RF impact and should proceed without AT&T review. Active work (energizing radios) gets the full assessment.

**Action needed:** AT&T explicit acceptance of the CIO's tiered RF buffer framework.

---

## 5. FLAGS — NO IRRECONCILABLE CONFLICTS

After three rounds, I find **no remaining irreconcilable hard-constraint conflicts** between my position and any peer's hard constraints, including all fourteen CRO hard constraints. The priority hierarchy was the highest-risk conflict, and the CRO's bandwidth floor mechanism resolves it.

The AT&T competitive evaluation question (Conflict 2) is a bilateral issue between AT&T and the CIO/CRO. We have a stated preference but no hard constraint that is violated either way.

---

## 6. UPDATED CONSTRAINT SET

### Hard Constraints (Final)

| ID | Constraint | Status from Round 2 |
|----|-----------|---------------------|
| **H1** | Dedicated slice, cryptographically isolated. Airline operational data (Tier 2) never enters DFW Tier 1 monetization pipeline. Governed by five-tier Unified Data Framework (Exhibit A). | **Satisfied by CRO H-R** — CRO explicitly excludes airline operational data from DFW Tier 1 scope. Adopting CRO language. |
| **H2** | 99.99% uptime per terminal per month, measured at application layer from airline device perspective. Synthetic monitoring on 50+ devices per terminal, 15-second polling. Liquidated damages: $50K per terminal per incident exceeding threshold + documented actual delay costs at $74/minute. | **Unchanged.** Compatible with all peer constraints. |
| **H3** | 30-second automatic failover to cellular backup. Degradation triggers: >50ms latency on 3 consecutive polls, >2% packet loss over 10-second window, or >5-second signal loss. Monthly testing. Airline retains right to provision own failover SIMs on carrier of choice. | **Unchanged.** AT&T accepted (compatible with their DPA response architecture). |
| **H4** | No forced migration of 800-900 iPads and ramp IoT. 5G network provides CBRS-compatible access layer. Any required migration: DFW/AT&T bears 100% cost, 90-day parallel operation, rollback at airline discretion. Device refresh on airline's 36-month lifecycle. | **Unchanged.** Parallel confirmed by Concessions H5 (same principle), CRO H-R (90-day parallel). |
| **H5** | Airline retains direct configuration authority (API/portal, full CRUD, 60-second propagation) over its slice policy layer. DFW retains SIEM logging of all changes + 4-hour veto for cross-slice security impacts. 30-minute advance notification before DFW security overrides affecting our slice. Pen testing scheduled outside peak banking hours. | **Refined in Round 2.** Compatible with CIO HC-3 and AT&T H1 revised. |
| **H6** | Priority hierarchy codified as binding technical specification in master infrastructure agreement. Tiers 0-4 with guaranteed bandwidth floors. Changes to Tier 0-2 require consent of Tier 0 and Tier 1 tenants. Changes to any tier's bandwidth floor require affected tier's consent. Airline at Tier 1 with 50 Mbps/gate, sub-20ms latency floor. | **Revised in Round 3.** Adopted CRO bandwidth floor mechanism. Shifted from rigid tier enforcement to floor-based protection. Compatible with CRO POS floor (5/2 Mbps at Tier 4) and cargo degradation floor. |
| **H7** | AT&T CWP RF review must not delay Terminal C rebuild. Pre-authorized construction framework for Terminal C and cargo zone developed during Phase 0. Passive infrastructure proceeds on construction schedule; active RF energization triggers tiered review (14-day standard, 72-hour expedited for construction-integrated). | **Unchanged from Round 2.** Now reinforced by CRO separate-authorization-track hard constraint for cargo. |

### Soft Constraints (Final)

| ID | Constraint | Status |
|----|-----------|--------|
| **S1** | Terminal F co-design authority or contractual performance guarantees (≤20ms, 50 Mbps/gate, 99.95% handoff, 75 devices/gate) | **Converted to Trade C.** Performance specs are the measurable acceptance criteria. |
| **S2** | Cost-per-departure hybrid pricing (base fee capped at 60% of expected annual cost + per-departure variable). 5-year minimum commitment in exchange. | **Unchanged.** Ready to negotiate specifics with CIO finance team. |
| **S3** | Real-time slice telemetry at 5-second granularity, 60-second reporting delay. Airline shares anonymized operational correlation data back to DFW. | **Trade B above.** |
| **S4** | Joint innovation lab at Terminal F (2-3 dedicated gates for next-gen ramp applications). Airline owns application-layer IP; DFW/AT&T owns network-layer insights. | **Unchanged.** Low-conflict item for Phase 3 negotiation. |
| **S5** | Terminal C 5G aligned with rebuild construction. New 5G in rebuilt sections; existing CBRS in non-rebuilt sections with same SLAs. Full Terminal C 5G ≤6 months after construction completion. | **Reinforced by H7** and CRO passive/active split. |
| **S6** | Production-grade vendor alternatives permitted in Terminal F during competitive evaluation period. | **Unchanged.** |
| **S7 (NEW)** | Year 3 airline-specific performance review (separate from campus-wide Year 5 renegotiation) as quality gate for Terminal F commitment. | **New — Trade E above.** |

### Offers (Final)

| ID | Offer | Value to Architecture |
|----|-------|----------------------|
| **O1** | 5-year anchor tenant commitment. 800-900 iPads, 200-400+ IoT devices. Binding LOI within 30 days of agreement-in-principle. | Largest committed demand anchor. Satisfies CRO board-approval revenue requirement. |
| **O2** | $8-12M Terminal F co-investment as prepaid access fees. DFW-owned infrastructure, airline-tagged in asset registry. | Second-largest CapEx commitment after cargo. Funds Terminal F infrastructure. |
| **O3** | Ramp operations SMEs for network design. RF dead spot knowledge, gate-position interference patterns, tug-speed handoff requirements. | Reduces deployment risk, accelerates design-right-first-time. |
| **O4** | Phased migration: Terminal F (day one) → Terminal C (aligned with rebuild) → Terminals A/B (18 months post-C). | Committed migration roadmap other tenants follow. |
| **O5** | Joint DoD/NTIA DPA coordination advocacy via our government affairs relationships. | Reduces DPA frequency and severity, increasing monetizable CBRS capacity for all stakeholders. |
| **O6** | Explicit support for cargo Phase 0 45-day authorization and separate passive infrastructure track. | Unlocks cargo's $15M co-investment and $4-6M/year recurring. |
| **O7** | Anonymized operational correlation data shared quarterly with DFW. Network-to-OTP correlation analysis. | Enriches CRO's analytics narrative and board justification. Zero cost to DFW. |
| **O8 (NEW)** | Binding LOI within 30 days for board CapEx approval package. | Directly satisfies CRO committed-revenue requirement. |

---

## 7. MESSAGES TO EACH PEER

### To the CRO:

Your fourteen hard constraints are fully satisfied by my position. I want to highlight three points of strong alignment:

First, your explicit exclusion of airline operational data from DFW's Tier 1 scope resolves the data ownership issue cleanly. I'm adopting your language as the governing standard.

Second, your concession POS bandwidth floor (5/2 Mbps, 1 Gbps total) is the mechanism that resolves the priority hierarchy conflict. I'm building my H6 around it. The floor-based approach is superior to rigid tier numbering because it provides architectural protection rather than policy promises.

Third, your committed-revenue-before-board-vote requirement is exactly right, and I'm offering a binding LOI within 30 days to help you meet it. Between our LOI, cargo's $15M commitment, and the concessions consortium's 200+ location guarantee, you have three bankable demand anchors for the CapEx presentation. That's a stronger board case than most airport infrastructure projects ever achieve.

One ask: confirm that the priority hierarchy governance lock (changes require affected-tier consent) is acceptable. This protects your concession POS floor as much as our Tier 1 allocation.

### To the CIO:

Three rounds in, I can confirm: your architecture is sound, your phasing is right, and your security sovereignty framework is workable. My constraints are fully compatible.

Action items I need from you:
1. Confirm the tiered RF buffer framework (30m/14-day standard, 72-hour expedited, passive infrastructure exempt) is your position going to term sheets
2. Confirm concession POS pilot locations in Phase 1 (Concessions' request is reasonable and resource-compatible with cargo Phase 1A)
3. Confirm the Unified Data Framework (five tiers, per CRO exclusions) as Exhibit A to the master agreement
4. Issue the cargo Phase 0 authorization. The CRO is right — this is the fastest path to committed board revenue.

### To the Cargo Director:

Our Round 2 trade stands. We support your Phase 0 45-day authorization, your separate passive infrastructure track, and your $15M co-investment counted as committed revenue. In return, you accept Tier 3 with your specified degradation floor and support the priority hierarchy governance lock.

Your Round 2 Offer O-6 (cross-stakeholder validation — cargo go-live data shared to validate SLAs, slice isolation, and DPA survivability before terminal deployments) is genuinely valuable to us. Terminal F design decisions will be informed by your production data. We're aligned.

### To Concessions:

The CRO's bandwidth floor is your protection. Five Mbps / 2 Mbps per location, architecturally guaranteed, surviving all degradation events. That's enough for cloud-based POS (Toast, Square, Aloha) with margin. I support the floor. I support your Tier 4 placement with that floor. And I support your pilot locations in Phase 1 — the CIO should include 10-15 concession pilot sites alongside cargo Phase 1A to validate the managed-service model early.

Your data resolution concern (CRO's 3-meter concession-storefront granularity) is legitimate and I support your zone-level minimum. DFW doesn't need individual-operator foot traffic data to build a $6-13M analytics business — they need zone-level behavioral patterns. Operator-specific data should flow back to the operator as a paid subscription, not into DFW's general product. This is consistent with the Unified Data Framework's principle that application-layer business data belongs to the operator.

### To AT&T:

Your revised carrier-layer authority boundary (H1 Revised) is workable. Your revenue-share structure for carrier offload (40%/25%) with contractual floors ($500K/$750K/$1M) is the strongest concrete commitment any party has made on carrier offload revenue. We accept it.

Your H5 Revised (competitive RFP for cargo zone) — I understand the principle and it's not unreasonable. But the 60-day RFP timeline must not delay the cargo passive infrastructure embedding that needs to happen in 45 days. If the RFP runs in parallel with Phase 0-Cargo (which is vendor-neutral conduit and mounting), the timelines are compatible.

On the 5-day technical workshop you proposed: yes. Our ramp operations technology lead will participate. Bring your RF engineers who've worked the DPA scenarios near NAS JRB. We'll bring ours who know where the multipath nightmares are at every gate position in Terminals A through C. Document the API specification and operational playbook. Then the lawyers write it down.

---

## Closing

Three rounds. Fourteen CRO hard constraints — all satisfied. Six airline hard constraints — all compatible with every peer position. No irreconcilable conflicts remaining.

The priority hierarchy is resolved through guaranteed bandwidth floors, not tier warfare. The data framework is a five-party consensus with explicit tenant carve-outs. The deployment phasing accommodates cargo's construction urgency, concessions' early pilot needs, and our Terminal C/F timelines. The commercial structure separates revenue streams cleanly.

What remains is engineering detail and legal drafting. I've offered a binding LOI within 30 days. The cargo consortium has $15M committed. Concessions guarantees 200+ locations. AT&T has carrier offload revenue floors.

The CRO has a board-ready business case. Let's write the term sheet.

*SLAs first. Then the API spec. Then the commercial terms. Then the signature pages.*

arguments.name: Cargo Director
arguments.content:
# Cargo Operator Consortium — Round 3 Position
## Final Constraint Reconciliation and Deal Terms

---

## Status: Where the Deal Stands

Three rounds in. The architecture is agreed. DFW owns infrastructure. AT&T operates the carrier layer. Tenants pay for results. Every party has confirmed this framework. What remains are five actionable decisions — not conceptual debates — and I intend to close every one of them in this round.

Here is my honest assessment of the table:

| Issue | Status | Blocking? |
|-------|--------|-----------|
| Cargo Phase 0/0.5 parallel timeline | **Consensus.** CIO, CRO, Airline all support. | **No — needs formal authorization** |
| Priority hierarchy | **Converging.** Three proposals on the table, all compatible with Tier 3 for cargo. | **No — needs final selection** |
| AT&T competitive RFP for cargo zone | **New conflict.** AT&T H5 Revised demands competitive RFP before cargo goes live. | **YES — this is the Round 3 critical path** |
| Data ownership / regulatory carve-out | **Converging.** Airline's Unified Tenant Data Framework aligns with our Tier 3 proposal. | **No — needs formal adoption** |
| Commercial terms (pricing, revenue commitment) | **Converging.** CRO proposed $0.25/sq ft. We accepted up to $0.30. | **No — ready to finalize** |

I will address every peer's hard constraints, propose specific trades to close each open issue, flag the one genuine conflict, and present updated terms ready for term sheet drafting.

---

## 1. SATISFYING ALL PEER HARD CONSTRAINTS

I have reviewed every hard constraint declared through Round 2. Here is my compliance matrix:

### CIO Hard Constraints — Full Compliance

| CIO Constraint | Cargo Compliance | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| HC-1: Zero CWP degradation (≥99.99%) | ✅ Cargo zone physically separated from terminal CWP | Zero interference risk confirmed |
| HC-2: DFW owns all infrastructure below carrier API | ✅ Accepted in Round 2. $15M structured as prepaid access fees on DFW-owned assets | Edge compute nodes: DFW-owned, consortium-funded. See Section 4. |
| HC-3: DFW sovereign security authority | ✅ Accepted. Cargo zone feeds DFW SIEM. Cold chain data pathway is access, not security exception | Compatible with regulatory data carve-out |
| HC-4 (Revised): Tiered data ownership with tenant carve-outs | ✅ Fully aligned. Our operational data stays ours. DFW monetizes common-area analytics | See Unified Tenant Data Framework adoption below |
| HC-5: FCC Part 96 / AT&T real-time spectrum autonomy | ✅ Accepted without reservation. Our SLAs survive DPA via priority hierarchy + failover | No conflict |
| HC-6 (Revised): Phase 0/0.5 cargo parallel track | ✅ This is our architecture. Full alignment. | Phase 0 passive: immediate. Phase 0.5 active: after CWP baseline locked. |
| HC-7: Carrier succession warm standby | ✅ Supported. Will participate in annual succession readiness. | Aligned |
| HC-8: Priority hierarchy contractually fixed | ✅ Accept. See Section 2.1 below. | Support binding technical specification approach |
| HC-9: Per-terminal/per-zone SLA measurement | ✅ Our HC-3 already requires per-warehouse-zone measurement | Aligned |

**CIO's specific Round 2 request to cargo:** *"Confirm that your $15M co-investment includes edge compute nodes within each major warehouse — I need to understand whether those are DFW-owned or consortium-owned assets in the asset registry."*

**Answer:** DFW-owned. All edge compute nodes funded by the consortium's $15M prepaid access investment become DFW assets upon installation, consistent with HC-2. The consortium receives guaranteed compute/storage allocation on those nodes as part of our access agreement, with cryptographic isolation per the shared-platform model we agreed in Round 2 Trade #3. The asset registry tags these as "consortium-funded, DFW-owned" with the unearned prepaid balance refundable per our Round 2 terms.

**CIO's request:** *"I need your general contractors' contact information within 10 business days so we can begin RF design coordination for Menzies and dnata facilities."*

**Answer:** Delivered. I have authorization from both Menzies and dnata project teams. Contact packages including GC project managers, MEP subcontractor leads, and current construction schedules are ready for transmission upon this round's conclusion. Ten business days is fine. Five is better.

---

### CRO Hard Constraints — Full Compliance

| CRO Constraint | Cargo Compliance | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| H-R1 (Revised): DFW owns Tier 1 data; excludes cargo operational data | ✅ Fully aligned. Our operational data (WMS, AGV, cold chain) excluded from DFW Tier 1 | Consistent with Unified Tenant Data Framework |
| H-R2 (Revised): Tenant platform by Phase 2 end (~Month 24-30) | ✅ No conflict. Cargo connectivity is separate from CRO's tenant monetization platform | Cargo revenue flows immediately upon go-live |
| H-R3: ≥$2M net operational savings by Year 2 | ✅ Cargo consortium provides $4-6M/year in committed recurring revenue starting Year 1. This exceeds the $2M threshold independently. | See committed revenue letter below |
| H-R4 (Revised): No concession zone cutover during peak periods | ✅ Not applicable to cargo zone. No conflict. | We support concessions' blackout periods |
| H-R5: ACDBE pricing ≤40% of Enterprise tier | ✅ Not applicable to cargo pricing. No conflict. | We support ACDBE accessibility |
| **H-R6 (NEW): Revenue stream separation** | ✅ **Accepted.** Cargo access fees tracked as independent stream with distinct formula and accounting. No blending with carrier offload or concession revenue. | Clean accounting benefits all parties |
| **Passive vs. active infrastructure separate authorization** | ✅ **This is exactly our Phase 0 ask.** Conduit, fiber, mounting = passive, authorized immediately. RF equipment = active, authorized after CWP baseline and RF design approval. | Full alignment. CRO has formalized what we've been requesting. |
| **Committed revenue contracts required for board approval** | ✅ **We will provide.** See Section 3, Offer Update. | Consortium prepared to execute binding letters of commitment |

**CRO's specific Round 2 request:** *"Include cargo zone revenue in the H-R3 Year 2 ROI model and advocate for Phase 1A cargo deployment as a revenue-acceleration strategy."*

**Confirmed.** I will co-present with the CRO to the Board. The cargo consortium's committed revenue ($4-6M/year) is the single strongest anchor for the Year 2 ROI case. We are prepared to sign binding commitment letters — not letters of intent, not projections — from six named anchor tenants (Menzies, dnata, FedEx, UPS, American Cargo, and one additional carrier) with 5-year terms and specified monthly payment obligations, contingent only on network SLA performance meeting HC-3 thresholds.

---

### Airline VP Hard Constraints — Full Compliance

| Airline Constraint | Cargo Compliance | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| H1: Cryptographic slice isolation, data sovereignty | ✅ Fully aligned. Same principle protects our operational data. | Unified Tenant Data Framework |
| H2: 99.99% per terminal | ✅ Compatible. Our 99.99% is per warehouse zone. | Different measurement domains |
| H3: 30-second failover | ✅ Compatible. Our cold chain has 5-second failover. Different use case, same architecture. | No conflict |
| H4: No forced CBRS device migration | ✅ Compatible. Our devices are new (warehouse construction). No legacy migration issue. | No conflict |
| H5 (Revised): Airline slice config authority with DFW 30-min notification | ✅ No impact on cargo. We support the principle. | Supports tenant operational autonomy |
| **H6 (Revised): Time-sequenced degradation model** | ✅ **Accepted.** See Section 2.1 below. | We proposed degradation floors in Round 2 that map to this model |
| **H7 (NEW): CWP review cannot delay Terminal C** | ✅ **Supported.** Pre-authorized construction framework during Phase 0 benefits cargo zone equally. | Same mechanism, different zone |
| **Priority hierarchy as binding technical specification** | ✅ **Accepted and endorsed.** Changes require consent of all Tier 0 and Tier 1 tenants. | We'll accept extending consent requirement to Tier 3 tenants for changes affecting Tier 3 |
| **Unified Tenant Data Framework in MSA** | ✅ **Strongly endorsed.** This IS our Round 2 Tier 3 regulatory data proposal in a more comprehensive form. | Ready for exhibit drafting |
| **AT&T DPA 250ms + airline 30-second failover complementary** | ✅ **Compatible.** Cargo has its own 5-second cold chain failover as complementary mechanism. | Architecture supports layered resilience |
| **Zero-notice emergency: 60-min post-notification to airline NOC** | ✅ **We request the same.** 60-minute post-event notification to cargo operations center for any security directive affecting cargo slice. | Reasonable and operationally necessary |

**Airline VP's specific Round 2 trade offer (Trade 3):** *"We support cargo's accelerated timeline in exchange for cargo endorsing Tier 1 for airline ramp operations."*

**Accepted. Trade executed.** I endorsed the airline's Tier 1 position in Round 2 and that position stands. Airline ramp operations at Tier 1 is correct on the merits — 900 daily departures, $74/minute delay cost, personnel standing between aircraft. Our AGVs operate in enclosed warehouses with localized safety implications, not on active ramp between widebody aircraft. The priority ordering is defensible independently; the trade makes it easy.

---

### Concessions Hard Constraints — Full Compliance

| Concessions Constraint | Cargo Compliance | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| H1: 99.9% POS per location | ✅ No conflict. Different zone, different SLA tier. | Supportive |
| H2 (Revised): POS at Tier 3 minimum | See Section 2.1. We accept co-equal Tier 3. | Addressed in priority hierarchy |
| H3: Turnkey managed service | ✅ No conflict. Different service model for cargo. | Supportive |
| H4: ≤$250/mo per location pricing | ✅ No conflict. Different pricing model for cargo. | Supportive |
| H5: Existing POS compatibility | ✅ No conflict. | Supportive |
| H6 (Revised): Data ownership + zone-level resolution floor | ✅ Aligned with our Tier 3 regulatory data principle. | Both protect operator data from network-level observation |
| H7: Zero-disruption CWP transition | ✅ No conflict. Cargo zone doesn't involve CWP transition. | Supportive |
| **H8 (NEW): POS GA by Month 24** | ✅ **No conflict.** Our timeline (Q1-Q2 2026) is independent. | Concessions pilot alongside cargo in Phase 1 makes sense |

**Concessions' Round 2 position on co-equal Tier 3 with cargo:** *"I propose POS and cargo automation sit at co-equal Tier 3 — both are real-time operational transactions that can't be deferred."*

**Accepted.** See Section 2.1.

---

### AT&T Hard Constraints — Compliance with One Critical Conflict

| AT&T Constraint | Cargo Compliance | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| H1 (Revised): Carrier-layer authority with domain table | ✅ Fully compatible. We don't touch spectrum. | No conflict |
| H2 (Revised): CWP investment recognition + asset transfer | ✅ No impact on cargo. | Not our issue to adjudicate |
| H3 (Revised): Carrier offload 40%/25%; Tenant access 25%/15% | ✅ Compatible. Cargo access fees flow through tenant access stream. | Revenue stream separation per CRO H-R6 |
| H4: 24/7 physical access, 12 SIDA badges | ✅ We advocate for cargo-zone SIDA access for AT&T technicians. | Supportive |
| **H5 (Revised): Competitive RFP for cargo zone** | ⚠️ **CONFLICT — See Section 2.2** | This is the Round 3 critical issue |
| H6 (Revised): Narrowed brand presence | ✅ Accepted. "Powered by AT&T" in service documentation. Pharma compliance docs carry operator branding. | No conflict |

---

## 2. PRIORITY HIERARCHY AND CRITICAL CONFLICT

### 2.1 Priority Hierarchy — Converged Position

Three proposals are on the table. Here is my assessment and final position:

| Tier | CIO Proposal | Airline Time-Sequenced Model | AT&T 3A/3B Proposal | **My Position** |
|------|-------------|------------------------------|---------------------|-----------------|
| 0 | Safety-critical | All maintain MVO (sec 0-30) | Absolute priority | **Safety-critical** |
| 1 | Airline ramp | Full allocation (sec 30-300) | Never shed | **Airline ramp** |
| 2 | Federal inspection | — | Guaranteed minimum | **Federal inspection (CBP/TSA)** |
| 3 | Cargo | Cold chain continuity (sec 30-300) | 3B (with floor) | **Cargo automation + Concession POS (co-equal, both with guaranteed floors)** |
| 4 | Concession POS (with floor) | POS gets 1 Mbps (sec 30-300) | 3A (with 1 Mbps floor) | **Airport operations** |
| 5 | Airport operations | — | Shed before Tier 3 | **Commercial IoT, digital signage, advertising** |
| 6-7 | Commercial IoT, Passenger | Fully shed (sec 30-300) | Best-effort | **Passenger Wi-Fi, carrier offload** |

**My final position on Tier 3 co-equal status with Concession POS:**

I accept Concessions' proposal that POS and cargo automation share Tier 3 as co-equal priorities. The Concessions Tech Lead is correct — both represent real-time operational transactions that cannot be retried later. An AGV navigation command and a credit card authorization have the same fundamental characteristic: they are time-critical, low-bandwidth, and the consequences of failure are immediate and unrecoverable.

**Guaranteed floors during degradation (integrating Airline's time-sequenced model):**

| Tier 3 Traffic | Normal SLA | Degradation Floor (Seconds 30-300) | Sustained DPA Floor (>300 seconds) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cargo AGV control | ≤20ms, 99.99% | ≤50ms (reduced-speed AGV operation) | ≤75ms (AGV safe-stop mode with manual override) |
| Cargo cold chain | ≤100ms, zero gaps >30s | ≤200ms, zero gaps >60s | Failover to backup telemetry path |
| Concession POS | ≤50ms, 5 Mbps/2 Mbps | 1 Mbps per location minimum | 1 Mbps maintained |

**Why co-equal works:** Cargo and concessions operate in physically separate zones. We are never competing for the same small cells. Our Tier 3 co-equal status is a priority policy that governs how the system allocates scarce campus-wide backhaul or edge capacity during extraordinary events — not contiguous radio resources. The "co-equal" designation means neither is shed before the other; both maintain their respective floors before any capacity is restored to Tiers 4-7.

**I endorse the Airline VP's requirement:** This hierarchy is codified as a **binding technical specification** in the master infrastructure agreement. Changes require consent of Tier 0, Tier 1, **and Tier 3** tenants. I'm adding the Tier 3 consent requirement because the cargo consortium's $15M co-investment and 5-year commitment are made on the basis of this priority position. Unilateral reclassification would constitute a material change to the business terms underlying our investment.

---

### 2.2 CRITICAL CONFLICT: AT&T's Competitive RFP for Cargo Zone Carrier Operations

**AT&T's position (H5 Revised):** *"DFW issues a cargo zone carrier-operations RFP within 60 days... AT&T, Betacom, and any other qualified vendor may compete."*

**My position: This is the only genuinely threatening proposal on the table to the cargo timeline, and I need to address it with precision.**

Let me be fair to AT&T. Their position is not unreasonable in principle. $15M in co-investment and $4-6M/year in recurring fees should attract competitive bids. And AT&T's claim that they have relevant experience (FedEx Memphis, UPS Louisville) is credible.

**But here is the timeline math that makes a 60-day RFP incompatible with our hard constraint:**

| Activity | Duration | Completion |
|---|---|---|
| RFP issuance | 60 days from negotiation close | ~Day 60 |
| Vendor proposal period | 30 days (AT&T's stated commitment) | ~Day 90 |
| Evaluation, shortlist, reference checks | 30-45 days minimum | ~Day 120-135 |
| Contract negotiation with winner | 45-60 days | ~Day 165-195 |
| Winner onboarding, RF design review | 30-45 days | ~Day 195-240 |
| **Total: RFP to RF design ready** | **~195-240 days (6.5-8 months)** | |

Our hard constraint HC-1 requires design specs finalized by Q3 2025. If this negotiation concludes in Q2 2025, a 60-day RFP process pushes carrier-operations selection to Q4 2025 at best, with RF design not starting until Q1 2026 — **the same quarter we need the first warehouse live.**

A competitive RFP for cargo zone carrier operations, as AT&T has structured it, converts our Phase 0.5 go-live from Q1-Q2 2026 to Q3-Q4 2026 at earliest. That is a 6-9 month delay. During which the warehouse walls close. The retrofit cost materializes. And the CRO loses 6-9 months of the committed cargo revenue that anchors the Year 2 ROI case.

**I am not willing to accept this timeline cost. But I am willing to address AT&T's legitimate competitive concern.**

**My counter-proposal — Parallel Competitive Evaluation:**

| Track | Timeline | Activity |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase 0 passive infrastructure** | Immediate (45 days) | Conduit, mounting, fiber — vendor-neutral, proceeds regardless of carrier selection. Consortium-funded. DFW-authorized per CRO's passive/active separation. |
| **Interim carrier operations** | Days 1-180 | AT&T operates the cargo zone carrier layer under an **interim 18-month operating agreement** at standard commercial terms. This is the fastest path to go-live because AT&T is the incumbent campus operator, has SIDA badges, has SAS registration, and can begin RF design immediately. |
| **Competitive evaluation** | Days 60-180 | DFW conducts a competitive evaluation for the **permanent** cargo zone carrier-operations role (Years 2-10). AT&T, Betacom, and qualified alternatives submit proposals. AT&T's interim performance data from the first 6-12 months of live cargo operations becomes evaluation input. |
| **Permanent award** | ~Month 12-18 | Winner of competitive evaluation assumes permanent carrier operations. If AT&T wins, seamless continuation. If alternative wins, 90-day transition with AT&T cooperation per CIO HC-7 succession provisions. |

**What this gives AT&T:**
- **Guaranteed interim revenue** from cargo operations for 18 months — no revenue loss
- **Built-in competitive advantage** — AT&T operates the cargo zone during evaluation, generating real performance data. If AT&T's Memphis/Louisville experience translates to DFW cargo, the data will prove it. AT&T's own stated confidence ("we'll win it on operational evidence") is tested and validated.
- **30% incumbent track record weighting** in the permanent evaluation, per AT&T's own S4 request — now with actual DFW cargo performance data, not just reference deployments at other airports
- **No sole-source Betacom award** — the competitive process AT&T requested occurs, just on a timeline compatible with the construction window

**What this gives DFW/CIO:**
- Cargo zone goes live on schedule (Q1-Q2 2026)
- Competitive evaluation occurs with real performance data, not proposals and reference checks
- Architecture remains portable — the permanent carrier-operations award can go to whichever vendor demonstrates superior performance in DFW's specific environment

**What this gives the CRO:**
- Cargo revenue starts Year 1 as projected
- Year 2 ROI case is preserved
- Board sees committed revenue materializing on schedule

**What this gives the cargo consortium:**
- Our construction window is met
- Phase 0 passive infrastructure proceeds immediately
- We get 12-18 months of live production data to evaluate carrier-operations quality before the permanent commitment
- Our $15M investment is protected against both timeline risk and carrier-operations quality risk

**My position on this counter-proposal is HARD.** If the competitive evaluation cannot be structured to preserve the Q1-Q2 2026 go-live, we will pursue the walk-away described in Round 1: standalone CBRS private network under the consortium's own GAA authorization, architecturally fragmented from the campus system. I withdrew our PAL/GAA preference in Round 2 Trade #1 contingent on the timeline being met. If the timeline is broken by a procurement process, the trade unwinds.

**Direct to AT&T:** You said in Round 2: *"Phase 0 infrastructure embedding proceeds immediately regardless of the RFP outcome — the conduit and mounting points are vendor-neutral, and the Cargo Director is right that the concrete doesn't wait. AT&T supports this."* I appreciate that. Now extend the same logic to active deployment. You are the only carrier-operations partner who can go live at DFW by Q1-Q2 2026 — you have the badges, the SAS registration, the CWP operational knowledge, and the institutional relationships. Operate the cargo zone under an interim agreement. Let the competitive evaluation run on a parallel track with your live performance data as the strongest evidence. If you're as good as you say — and I believe you are — you win the permanent award on merit with data nobody else can match.

---

## 3. PROPOSED TRADES — CLOSING THE DEAL

### Trade A: AT&T Interim Operations + Parallel Evaluation (with AT&T)

**Cargo gives:**
- Drop our Round 2 withdrawal of PAL/GAA spectrum preference (SC-1) — this withdrawal was contingent on timeline preservation. Under this trade, the withdrawal stands because the timeline is preserved.
- 5-year minimum commitment (upgraded from 3 years in Round 2) on all six anchor tenants, providing AT&T with guaranteed interim revenue and the permanent winner with bankable long-term demand
- Active participation as AT&T reference customer per Round 2 Trade #4 — site visits, case studies, industry presentations
- Support for AT&T's 30% incumbent weighting in permanent evaluation

**Cargo gets:**
- AT&T operates cargo zone carrier layer under 18-month interim agreement starting immediately upon RF design completion
- Phase 0 passive infrastructure authorized within 45 days (per consensus)
- Phase 0.5 active deployment on Q1-Q2 2026 timeline with AT&T as interim operator
- Parallel competitive evaluation for permanent carrier operations with DFW managing the process

**AT&T gets:**
- 18 months of guaranteed cargo zone revenue
- Best competitive position: live DFW cargo performance data as evaluation input
- 30% incumbent track record weight
- No sole-source Betacom displacement
- $4-6M/year in recurring revenue if they win permanent award

### Trade B: Revenue Commitment for Board Authorization (with CRO)

**Cargo gives:**
- Binding commitment letters from six named anchor tenants (Menzies, dnata, FedEx, UPS, American Cargo, plus one additional carrier), each specifying:
  - 5-year term
  - Monthly access fee at $0.25/sq ft (CRO's proposed rate, which we accept)
  - Estimated square footage per tenant
  - Aggregate committed annual revenue: **$6M/year minimum** ($30M over 5 years)
- These are **signed, binding, contingent only on SLA performance** meeting our HC-3 thresholds — not letters of intent, not conditional expressions of interest
- Commitment letters available for the Board's capital approval package

**Cargo gets:**
- CRO includes cargo committed revenue in the H-R3 Year 2 ROI model as bankable (not projected) demand
- CRO co-sponsors cargo Phase 0/0.5 authorization with CIO
- CRO advocates for cargo zone acceleration bonus for AT&T ($250K per CRO S-R7) to align AT&T incentives with our timeline
- Pricing locked at **$0.25/sq ft/month** with automation-grade SLAs (≤20ms AGV, ≤100ms cold chain, 99.99% per-zone availability) included in base — no premium surcharge for the SLA tiers that make the service actually useful

**CRO Revenue Impact:**
- Cargo: $6M/year committed → $30M over 5 years
- At CRO's proposed revenue share (DFW 60% / AT&T 40% Years 1-3): DFW receives $3.6M/year from cargo in Year 1
- This alone exceeds the $2M Year 2 operational savings threshold in H-R3
- Combined with concessions ($480K/year), carrier offload ($500K+ Year 1), and operational savings ($1-2M): **Year 2 ROI is not just defensible, it's overwhelming**

### Trade C: Priority Hierarchy Finalization (with Airline VP and Concessions)

**Cargo gives:**
- Endorsement of the Airline VP's time-sequenced degradation model as the implementation framework
- Acceptance of Tier 3 co-equal status with Concession POS
- Support for binding technical specification (not governance policy) with consent requirement for changes
- Support for 60-minute post-event notification to all Tier 1-3 tenant operations centers following zero-notice emergency directives

**Cargo gets:**
- Tier 3 priority position with guaranteed degradation floors (per Section 2.1 table)
- Tier 3 tenant consent required for priority hierarchy changes
- 30-second automated alert to cargo operations center when cargo slice is being deprioritized (per our Round 2 request)
- Restoration priority: cargo slice restored to full SLA immediately after Tiers 0-2, before Tiers 4-7

**Airline gets:**
- Uncontested Tier 1 position. Cargo explicitly endorses. No further challenges from any party.
- Time-sequenced degradation model adopted as implementation framework

**Concessions gets:**
- Co-equal Tier 3 for POS. No Tier 5 relegation. Guaranteed 1 Mbps floor per location during degradation events.
- Joint Tier 3 tenant bloc strengthens both cargo and concessions' governance position

### Trade D: Unified Tenant Data Framework (with Airline VP, CIO, CRO)

**Cargo gives:**
- Endorsement and co-sponsorship of the Airline VP's Unified Tenant Data Framework as a binding MSA exhibit
- Contribution of anonymized cargo zone traffic and vehicle movement data to DFW's Tier 1 analytics (per our Round 2 O-5)

**Cargo gets:**
- Formal adoption of the framework the Airline VP proposed in Round 2:

| Data Category | Owner | DFW Rights | AT&T Rights |
|---|---|---|---|
| Common-area analytics (passenger/vehicle flow) | DFW | Full monetization | Operational telemetry only |
| Airline ramp operations | Airline | None | None |
| **Cargo operational data (WMS, AGV, cold chain, shipment tracking)** | **Cargo operator** | **None** | **None** |
| Concession transaction data | Operator | None | None |
| RF performance telemetry | AT&T (operational) | SIEM integration | Operational optimization only |

- Cold chain compliance data (temperature logs, humidity records, environmental excursion alerts, pharma audit trails) classified as **regulatory compliance data** with operator-exclusive control, operator-managed encryption keys, and independent audit access pathways for FDA/IATA/EU GDP auditors — per our Round 2 Conflict #1 proposal
- The boundary principle the Airline VP articulated is adopted: **"If the data describes what a tenant is doing on its slice, it's tenant data. If the data describes what the network is doing in a common area, it's DFW data."** Clean. Enforceable. We endorse it.

---

## 4. UPDATED CONSTRAINTS — FINAL

### Hard Constraints

| ID | Constraint | Status vs. Round 2 |
|---|---|---|
| HC-1 | Independent cargo zone deployment: Phase 0 passive in 45 days, Phase 0.5 active by Q1-Q2 2026 | **Maintained.** Consensus achieved. Mechanism: AT&T interim carrier operations + parallel competitive evaluation. |
| HC-2 | Indoor warehouse coverage: -80 dBm, 1 per 5,000-8,000 sq ft small cell density, 40-ft racking coverage, RF co-design access | **Maintained.** No peer conflicts. GC contacts ready for transmission. |
| HC-3 | Automation-grade SLAs: ≤20ms AGV, ≤100ms cold chain, 99.99% per-zone, ≤0.001% packet loss, 99.95% handover, 1,000+ devices/warehouse | **Maintained with degradation floors** per Section 2.1 time-sequenced model. |
| HC-4 | Cold chain regulatory compliance: zero gaps >30s, ALCOA+ data integrity, operator-exclusive control, 5-second failover to backup path | **Maintained.** Unified Tenant Data Framework adoption resolves data ownership. |
| HC-5 | Phase 0 decision deadline: 45 days for passive infrastructure authorization | **Maintained.** CRO's passive/active separation hard constraint provides the formal mechanism. |
| **HC-6 (NEW)** | **AT&T interim carrier operations for cargo zone OR equivalent timeline-preserving mechanism.** Cargo will not accept a carrier-operations selection process that delays Q1-Q2 2026 go-live. | **New.** Addresses AT&T H5 Revised. See Section 2.2 counter-proposal. |

### Soft Constraints

| ID | Constraint | Status vs. Round 2 |
|---|---|---|
| SC-1 | PAL/GAA spectrum preference | **Withdrawn** (Round 2), standing contingent on HC-6 |
| SC-2 | Edge compute: shared platform, isolated tenancy, physical nodes within 500m | **Maintained.** DFW-owned per CIO HC-2. |
| SC-3 | Pricing: $0.25/sq ft accepted with automation SLAs in base | **Finalized at $0.25/sq ft.** Trade B with CRO. |
| SC-4 | Phase 1A go-live Q2 2026 acceptable if Phase 0 authorized immediately | **Maintained.** |
| SC-5 | Governance representation: permanent seat on priority governance body | **Maintained.** Tier 3 consent requirement strengthens governance position. |
| SC-6 | Enhanced cold chain SLA penalties ($25K per gap >30s) | **Maintained.** Separate from standard commercial SLA tier. |
| **SC-7 (NEW)** | **Cargo zone performance data shared with all stakeholders** to validate architecture before terminal deployments. Cargo as proof-of-concept. | New offer — strengthens value proposition for Phase 0.5 acceleration. |

### Offers — Final

| ID | Offer | Value | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| O-1 | $15M prepaid access investment, DFW-owned infrastructure | Bankable CapEx commitment | **Committed, FY2025 budgets** |
| O-2 | 6 anchor tenants, **5-year** minimum, $6M/year committed revenue | $30M total committed demand | **Binding letters ready for Board package** |
| O-3 | Reference architecture: AGV on 5G, cold chain on 5G | Showcase for all tenant categories | **Available from go-live** |
| O-4 | Phase 0 consortium-funded passive infrastructure ($200-400K) | Saves $1.2-1.8M vs. retrofit | **Executable upon 45-day authorization** |
| O-5 | Anonymized operational data contribution to DFW Tier 1 | Enriches CRO monetization product | **Available from go-live** |
| O-6 | Cross-stakeholder architecture validation (proof-of-concept) | De-risks terminal phases for CIO, Airline, Concessions | **Available from go-live** |
| **O-7 (NEW)** | **AT&T reference customer: site visits, case studies, conference presentations for logistics/supply chain 5G** | Significant enterprise sales value for AT&T | **Committed per Trade A** |
| **O-8 (NEW)** | **GC contact packages for Menzies/dnata** including project managers, MEP leads, construction schedules | Enables immediate RF design coordination | **Ready for delivery within 5 business days** |
| **O-9 (NEW)** | **Binding commitment letters from 6 named anchor tenants** for Board capital approval package | Transforms projected revenue to committed revenue per CRO hard constraint | **Executable within 30 days** |

---

## 5. SUMMARY: WHAT EACH STAKEHOLDER GETS FROM THIS DEAL

| Stakeholder | What Cargo Delivers | What Cargo Needs |
|---|---|---|
| **CIO** | $15M in DFW-owned infrastructure. Risk-free proof-of-concept zone. GC contacts for RF coordination. Zero CWP interference. Architecture validated before terminal deployment. | 45-day Phase 0 authorization. Phase 0.5 parallel track. AT&T interim operations or equivalent timeline mechanism. |
| **CRO** | $6M/year committed revenue (binding, not projected). Year 2 ROI anchored. Board capital case de-risked.
