Anonymized data contribution to Tier 1 product. | $0.25/sq ft pricing with automation SLAs in base. Cargo revenue counted as committed in Year 2 model. Co-sponsorship of Phase 0/0.5 authorization. |
| **Airline VP** | Uncontested Tier 1 endorsement. Architecture validation before airline migration. Cross-stakeholder advocacy for priority hierarchy as binding spec. | Time-sequenced degradation model adopted. Priority hierarchy with Tier 3 consent requirement. |
| **Concessions** | Co-equal Tier 3 support. Joint governance bloc. Architecture proof-of-concept reducing concessions migration risk. | Tier 3 co-equal acceptance (granted). |
| **AT&T** | 18 months guaranteed interim revenue. Best competitive position with live performance data. Reference customer for logistics 5G sales. $4-6M/year recurring if permanent award won. No sole-source Betacom displacement. | AT&T operates cargo zone under interim agreement. 30% incumbent weight in permanent evaluation. Parallel competitive process (not pre-award blocking timeline). |

---

## 6. CLOSING: THE 45-DAY CLOCK

Three rounds of negotiation have produced consensus on architecture, data ownership, security sovereignty, priority hierarchy, and commercial framework. One conflict remains: AT&T's competitive evaluation timing vs. our construction window. I have proposed a resolution that satisfies AT&T's legitimate competitive concern without destroying the timeline.

**What I need from this room today:**

1. **CIO:** Confirm 45-day Phase 0 passive infrastructure authorization. Confirm cargo Phase 0.5 parallel track in the deployment plan. Confirm acceptance of AT&T interim operations + parallel competitive evaluation as the mechanism.

2. **CRO:** Confirm $0.25/sq ft/month pricing with automation SLAs in base. Confirm cargo committed revenue included in Year 2 ROI model. Confirm cargo acceleration bonus for AT&T ($250K) as incentive alignment.

3. **Airline VP:** Confirm time-sequenced degradation model with Tier 3 co-equal for cargo and concessions. Confirm priority hierarchy as binding technical specification with Tier 3 consent requirement.

4. **Concessions:** Confirm Tier 3 co-equal acceptance. Joint advocacy for guaranteed floors during degradation.

5. **AT&T:** Respond to the parallel competitive evaluation counter-proposal. Confirm willingness to operate cargo zone under 18-month interim agreement while permanent evaluation proceeds. Assign RF engineering resources within 60 days.

The consortium is putting **$15M in capital, $30M in 5-year committed revenue, six named anchor tenants, and a $200-400K immediate construction contribution** on this table. We are the largest single demand commitment in this negotiation. We are the fastest path to live production revenue. We are the lowest-risk proof-of-concept environment on DFW's campus.

Every stakeholder at this table has told me they support the cargo timeline. Round 3 is where that support converts to authorization.

Forty-five days. The concrete does not wait.

arguments.name: Concessions Tech Lead
arguments.content:
# F&B Concessions Consortium — Round 3 Position
## Constraint Reconciliation, Trades, and Updated Position

---

## Preamble

Three rounds in and I can see the deal. Let me be honest about what's happened since Round 1: the negotiation has moved materially in our direction on the things that actually matter to 60 operators running 200+ locations.

The CRO has made **concession POS bandwidth floors a HARD constraint** — 5 Mbps/2 Mbps per location guaranteed even during capacity-constrained events, 1 Gbps campus-wide. The CRO has explicitly **excluded concession transaction data** from DFW's Tier 1 monetization. The CRO has locked in **ACDBE pricing at ≤40% of Enterprise**. The CRO has adopted our blackout periods and our $15K/day penalty structure as HARD constraints. The Airline VP has codified a **1 Mbps absolute degradation floor** for concession POS during the worst capacity events — meaning our registers never go dark, even during a DPA event on the worst Wednesday before Thanksgiving.

These are not aspirational. These are peer HARD constraints that every party at this table must now satisfy.

I'm going to use this round to lock in the gains, close the remaining gaps, make the trades that need making, and deliver a final position that my 60 operators — from Avolta's technology team down to the family running three Square terminals — can live with for ten years.

---

## 1. REVISED POSITION — SATISFYING ALL PEER HARD CONSTRAINTS

I have reviewed every HARD constraint declared by every peer. Here is how the concessions consortium position satisfies each.

---

### CRO Hard Constraints — Full Alignment

| CRO Constraint | Our Compliance |
|---|---|
| DFW owns infrastructure for data monetization | **Accepted.** DFW ownership is why we support this architecture — one accountable landlord. |
| Tenant platform technically capable Month 24-30, revenue Month 30-36 | **Accepted.** Compatible with our H8 (POS GA by Month 24). POS connectivity is the foundation the platform sits on. |
| 200 bps penalty per quarter for AT&T-attributable delay | **Accepted.** Aligns AT&T incentives with our deployment timeline. |
| ≥$2M net annual savings by Year 2 | **Accepted.** Our 200+ location adoption commitment is a primary input to this math. |
| No concession cutover Nov 15–Jan 5, Mar 1–Mar 20 | **Accepted.** This is our constraint, now codified by the CRO. Full alignment. |
| 90-day parallel operation, 4-hour rollback per location | **Accepted.** Identical to our H7. |
| $15K/location/day for outages >4 hours from deployment | **Accepted.** We adopted this structure in Round 2. |
| ACDBE pricing ≤40% of Enterprise tier | **Accepted and championed.** Star Concessions and our ACDBE operators are the direct beneficiaries. |
| Separate revenue streams — no blending tenant access and carrier offload | **Accepted.** Clean accounting protects all tenants from cross-subsidization. |
| DFW Tier 1 data excludes concession transaction data | **Accepted with gratitude.** This satisfies the core of our H6. See remaining refinement below. |
| AT&T branding limited to service agreements, passenger portal, marketing materials | **Accepted.** No AT&T branding inside our locations facing passengers. |
| POS guaranteed minimum 5 Mbps/2 Mbps per location (1 Gbps total) | **Accepted.** This is the single most important protection for our operators. It means our POS slice has a defined, enforceable bandwidth floor that survives congestion events. |
| Passive infrastructure on separate authorization track from RF-active | **Accepted.** No conflict with concessions. |
| Committed revenue contracts before board approval | **Accepted.** Our 200+ location binding adoption commitment (O1) is that committed revenue. |

**CRO Soft Constraints — Accepted:**

| CRO Soft Constraint | Our Position |
|---|---|
| $200/mo standard, $175 at 175+ locations, $80 ACDBE | **Accepted.** This is within our hard ceiling of $250 and within striking distance of our soft target of $150-175. The volume discount at 175+ locations means our consortium immediately qualifies for the $175 tier. ACDBE at $80/mo is the right number. We accept the CRO's proposed pricing structure. |
| 10-year term with Year 5 renegotiation, Year 7 no-fault exit | **Accepted.** Per our O8, we support the longer term in exchange for pricing certainty. |
| AT&T $5M co-investment + 2 FTE onboarding for 18 months | **Accepted.** The onboarding FTEs directly help our ACDBE operators during transition. |

---

### Airline VP Hard Constraints — Full Alignment

| Airline Constraint | Our Compliance |
|---|---|
| Dedicated slice, cryptographic isolation, data sovereignty | **Accepted.** We want the same isolation principle for POS traffic. Their precedent strengthens our position. |
| 99.99% per-terminal uptime on airline slice | **Accepted.** Their SLA is higher than ours (99.9%). No conflict. |
| 30-second automatic failover | **Accepted.** We adopted this concept for POS at 60-second failover (our S7). |
| No forced migration of CBRS device fleet | **Accepted.** Parallel to our H5. |
| Airline retains slice config authority with 30-min advance notification | **Accepted.** Doesn't affect concessions. |
| Pen testing scheduling constraints around banking hours | **Accepted.** Doesn't affect concessions. |
| Priority hierarchy as binding technical specification with tenant consent for changes | **Accepted and strongly supported.** See Section 2 below — this protects us from unilateral deprioritization. |
| Airline ramp Tier 1, full allocation seconds 30-300 | **Accepted.** See priority hierarchy resolution below. |
| **Time-sequenced degradation: POS gets 1 Mbps minimum during seconds 30-300** | **Accepted.** This is the degradation floor. Combined with CRO's 5/2 Mbps normal operating floor, we have a two-tier protection structure. |
| Pre-authorized construction framework for Terminal C during Phase 0 | **Accepted.** Reduces our CPE deployment bottleneck risk. |
| Unified Tenant Data Framework — tenant operational data belongs to tenant | **Accepted and strongly supported.** This codifies our H6 at the MSA level. |
| 250ms DPA response + 30-second failover as complementary mechanisms | **Accepted.** AT&T handles spectrum; devices handle continuity. |
| 60-minute post-event notification in zero-notice emergencies | **Accepted.** Not directly relevant to concessions but architecturally sound. |

---

### CIO Hard Constraints — Full Alignment (Confirmed from Rounds 1-2)

All CIO constraints (HC-1 through HC-9) were accepted in Rounds 1-2. No changes. Specific confirmations:

- **HC-6 Revised Phasing:** We accept the parallel cargo Phase 0/0.5 structure. We continue to require concession pilot locations in Phase 1 alongside cargo (per our Round 2 Trade 5), with POS GA by Month 24 as part of Phase 2.
- **HC-8 (Multi-tenant priority hierarchy contractually fixed):** Strongly supported. See resolution below.
- **HC-9 (Per-terminal and per-location SLA measurement):** Strongly supported. This is our H1.

---

### Cargo Hard Constraints — No Conflicts

No cargo hard constraints conflict with concessions. We continue to support cargo's accelerated Phase 0/0.5 timeline. Our deployment is in terminals; theirs is in warehouses. Different zones, different construction timelines, no resource competition.

---

### AT&T Hard Constraints — Accepted with Prior Modifications

| AT&T Constraint | Our Position |
|---|---|
| H1 Carrier-layer spectrum authority (250ms DPA) | **Accepted.** We have zero interest in spectrum management. |
| H2 CWP protection (RF impact assessment) | **Accepted for infrastructure-level changes.** We continue to require CPE gateway pre-approval classification (see Trade 1 below). |
| H3 Carrier offload revenue share | **Accepted.** Carrier offload is AT&T's value-add; doesn't affect our pricing. |
| H4 24/7 physical access, 12 SIDA badges | **Accepted** with prior coordination requirement for concession-area work. |
| H5 Competitive evaluation protection | **Accepted.** We have no preference on carrier operator. Make the POS work. |
| H6 Brand presence (revised/narrowed) | **Accepted** per narrowed scope. Co-brand on captive portal and service docs. No customer-facing branding inside concession locations. |

---

## 2. PRIORITY HIERARCHY — ACCEPTING THE CONVERGENCE

Three rounds of negotiation have produced a clear convergence on priority hierarchy. I'm going to state plainly where we've landed and why the concessions consortium accepts it.

**What we asked for:** Tier 2 (Round 1), then Tier 3 (Round 2).

**What the negotiation produced:**

The CRO has made a **5 Mbps/2 Mbps per-location bandwidth floor** a HARD constraint — this means concession POS has a guaranteed, architecturally protected minimum bandwidth allocation that survives capacity-constrained events. This is not a priority tier. It is an engineering specification.

The Airline VP has made a **1 Mbps absolute degradation floor** a HARD constraint during the most severe capacity events (seconds 30-300 of a DPA-driven crunch).

Combined, these two peer HARD constraints create a **two-tier protection structure** for concession POS:

| Operating Condition | POS Bandwidth Floor | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Normal operations (99%+ of the time) | 5 Mbps down / 2 Mbps up per location | CRO HARD constraint |
| Severe degradation (DPA event, seconds 30-300) | 1 Mbps per location | Airline VP HARD constraint |

**Our acceptance:**

We accept the following priority hierarchy, structured as a binding technical specification per the Airline VP's HARD constraint (requiring consent of all Tier 0 and Tier 1 tenants to modify):

| Tier | Category | Capacity Treatment |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | Safety-critical (FAA, TSA emergency) | Absolute priority, never shed |
| 1 | Airline ramp operations | Full allocation maintained through seconds 30-300; never degraded by commercial traffic |
| 2 | Federal inspection (CBP, TSA checkpoint) | Guaranteed minimum capacity |
| 3 | Cargo automation (AGV, cold chain) | Guaranteed minimum with degradation floor per cargo consortium terms |
| **4** | **Concession POS / Financial Transactions** | **5/2 Mbps floor under normal ops; 1 Mbps absolute floor during severe degradation; never fully shed** |
| 5 | Airport operations (DFW facilities) | Managed allocation |
| 6 | Commercial IoT, digital signage, concession non-POS, advertising | Best-effort above guaranteed floors |
| 7 | Passenger Wi-Fi offload, carrier offload | Best-effort, first shed |

**Why we accept Tier 4 instead of our original Tier 2/3 ask:**

Because the *protections* matter more than the *label*. The guaranteed bandwidth floors — 5/2 Mbps normal, 1 Mbps degradation — mean that in practice, every POS terminal at every one of our 200+ locations will complete transactions under all operating conditions. A credit card authorization is 2-5 KB. At 1 Mbps, that's sub-second even during the worst DPA event DFW will ever see. Toast and Square cloud-based POS need sustained connectivity, not raw bandwidth — 1 Mbps sustains cloud POS session state.

I no longer need a higher tier number to protect my operators' revenue. I need the bandwidth floor to be **contractually binding and architecturally enforced** — and two peers have now made it a HARD constraint from their side.

**Conditions on our acceptance:**

1. The bandwidth floors (5/2 Mbps normal; 1 Mbps degradation) are measured **per location at the CPE gateway**, not as an aggregate campus statistic. Per-location measurement is consistent with our H1 (99.9% per location per month).

2. The priority hierarchy is a **binding technical specification** in the master infrastructure agreement, changeable only with consent of all Tier 0 and Tier 1 tenants. We accept we are not a consent-required tenant for hierarchy changes — but we require **30-day advance written notice** before any change affecting Tier 4 or below takes effect, plus the right to terminate our consortium commitment without penalty if a hierarchy change materially degrades our contractual bandwidth floors.

3. **Deprioritization notification:** When POS traffic is being actively deprioritized below 5/2 Mbps (i.e., entering degradation territory), an automated alert is sent to the DFW concession support desk within 30 seconds. This allows the support desk to notify operators who may want to activate cellular backup.

**What we are giving the Airline VP in return:** Explicit, unqualified endorsement of Tier 1 for airline ramp operations. We will not contest, re-litigate, or seek to modify airline priority in any future governance forum. The airline generates the passenger traffic that creates our $4.2B revenue. Their ramp safety is our revenue safety. Full stop.

**What we are giving Cargo in return:** We accept cargo automation at Tier 3, above concession POS. Cargo's AGV safety and cold chain regulatory compliance justify the higher tier. Different zones, no bandwidth competition, and their accelerated deployment proves the architecture for us.

---

## 3. DATA OWNERSHIP — CLOSING THE GAP

The CRO's revised HARD constraint explicitly **excludes concession transaction data from DFW Tier 1 data**. The Airline VP's Unified Tenant Data Framework HARD constraint establishes that **tenant operational data generated on tenant slices belongs to the tenant**. These two peer HARD constraints, combined, satisfy the core of our H6.

**Remaining issue: Network-derived analytics at concession-storefront granularity.**

The CRO's original Round 1 position claimed "concession-storefront granularity (≤3m accuracy)" for device density and dwell-time analytics. In Round 2, I flagged this as a conflict — network-derived foot traffic data at individual-location resolution constitutes operator performance data that could be used in lease negotiations.

The CRO's revised HARD constraint narrows Tier 1 scope to: "common-area passenger flow, device density, dwell time" and explicitly states it "excludes concession transaction data." But it does not explicitly address the **geographic resolution** of network-derived analytics near concession locations.

**My revised position:**

I am **relaxing** my Round 2 demand for a zone-level minimum resolution (5+ location clusters) in DFW's general analytics products. Here is why and what I need instead:

The Airline VP's Unified Tenant Data Framework establishes the principle: "if the data describes what a tenant is doing on its slice, it's tenant data. If the data describes what the network is doing in a common area, it's DFW data." Network-derived foot traffic near a concession location describes what the network observes in a common area — it is not transaction data, it is not operator data, and I cannot credibly claim it falls within the Unified Framework's tenant carve-out.

**What I accept:** DFW may collect and monetize network-derived foot traffic, device density, and dwell-time analytics at whatever geographic resolution the network produces, including at concession-storefront granularity.

**What I require in exchange** (revised H6 condition):

1. **Firewall from lease negotiations:** Network-derived analytics about specific concession locations cannot be used by DFW's real estate/leasing team in lease renewal negotiations, percentage-rent calculations, or operator performance reviews **unless the specific operator has provided written consent for that use.** This is a data-use restriction, not a data-collection restriction. DFW can collect it, monetize it to third parties, and sell it back to operators — but cannot use it against operators in landlord-tenant negotiations without consent.

2. **Operator access right:** Any operator-specific network analytics (foot traffic at their specific location, dwell time at their counter, device density in their zone) is available to that operator at no additional charge as part of the base connectivity service. Operators should not have to pay a subscription to see data about their own storefront generated by the network they're paying to use.

3. **Third-party anonymization:** When DFW sells concession-area analytics to third parties (advertisers, brands, market researchers), individual operator identity must be anonymized. "Terminal C food court, Location 7" is acceptable. "Shake Shack, Terminal C" is not, without Shake Shack's consent.

**Why this is a better position than my Round 2 demand:** My Round 2 position (zone-level minimum resolution) was defensive and would have degraded DFW's analytics product quality. This revised position accepts full-resolution data collection while protecting operators from the specific harm I'm worried about — their landlord using network surveillance in lease negotiations. It's a cleaner legal boundary and it gives the CRO a better product to sell.

---

## 4. PROPOSED TRADES

### Trade 1: CPE Gateway Pre-Approval ↔ CWP Protection Advocacy

**We give:**
- Formal consortium endorsement of AT&T's CWP protection framework for infrastructure-level changes (new small cells, fiber construction) within the 50-meter / 30-day review zone
- We advocate with the CIO to maintain this protection throughout all buildout phases
- Our pilot locations (O4) include pre/post RF impact measurement data contributed to AT&T's baseline monitoring

**We need:**
- Concession CPE gateways (the managed device at each location presenting Ethernet/Wi-Fi to operator equipment) classified as a **pre-approved device class** with **5-business-day notification** instead of 30-day review
- CPE gateway technical specifications (maximum transmit power, frequency band, antenna characteristics) published as part of the Phase 0 documentation, establishing the pre-approved profile
- Any CPE model change or firmware update that remains within the pre-approved specification profile deploys without per-instance review

**Why this trade works:** AT&T's legitimate concern is protecting CWP from RF interference during infrastructure buildout. A CPE gateway operating at Wi-Fi-equivalent power levels within a 5G small cell's coverage area creates no incremental RF interference to CWP APs — it's a client device, not a radio infrastructure element. The 30-day review was designed for small cell installation, not endpoint provisioning. Classifying CPE as pre-approved gives us deployment velocity for 200+ locations while preserving AT&T's protection for the high-risk activities (new radios, fiber construction, antenna installation) that actually threaten CWP.

AT&T's own Round 2 position acknowledged the distinction between infrastructure changes and tenant provisioning. Let's formalize it.

---

### Trade 2: 10-Year Commitment + Pricing Lock ↔ Adoption Guarantee + Coordination

**We give:**
- Consortium acceptance of 10-year contract term (supporting CIO, CRO, and AT&T positions)
- Binding commitment to migrate all 200+ locations within 12 months of general availability (O1)
- Single-point-of-coordination for 60 operators (O2) — saving DFW and AT&T the overhead of 60 individual tenant relationships
- Co-funded ACDBE onboarding support via HMSHost/Avolta and Star Concessions technology teams (O9)

**We need:**
- Per-location monthly pricing per CRO's proposed structure: **$200/mo standard, $175/mo at 175+ locations, $80/mo ACDBE**
- Annual escalation capped at **CPI or 3%, whichever is lower**, for the full contract term
- **Most-favored-tenant clause:** If any other DFW tenant category receives a lower effective per-connection monthly rate for comparable QoS-guaranteed service (not best-effort), our rate adjusts to match
- Month-to-month per-location flexibility: locations that close or change operators deactivate without penalty; new locations activate within 10 business days at the prevailing consortium rate
- **No CapEx charges** for existing 200+ locations. CPE hardware, installation, configuration, maintenance, and replacement included in the monthly fee.
- New location activation (34+ through 2028) at the same terms within 10 business days of lease commencement

**Revenue math for the table:**
- 200+ locations × $175/mo (volume tier) = **$420K/year** baseline
- 34 new locations × $200/mo (standard until volume threshold resets) = **$81.6K/year** additional
- ACDBE subsidy (~35 locations × $80 vs $175 = $95 delta × 12) = **~$40K/year** net cost to DFW, funded from DFW's revenue share
- **Net concession tier revenue: ~$460-500K/year**, growing to $540K+ by 2028

This is a **bankable revenue floor** for the business case. Combined with cargo's $4-6M/year, airline tenant fees, and carrier offload, it closes the CRO's Year 2 ROI requirement.

---

### Trade 3: Data Contribution + Pilot Participation ↔ POS in Phase 1 + Analytics Access

**We give:**
- Anonymized foot traffic counts, dwell time by zone, peak demand timing patterns (O3) — enriching DFW's Tier 1 analytics product
- 10-15 volunteer pilot locations across 3+ terminals and 5+ operators including 2+ ACDBE operators (O4)
- Pilot operators tolerate parallel-operation overhead and provide structured feedback
- Star Concessions serves as local ACDBE anchor for pilot and early deployment (O6)
- Terminal F advocacy: new concession spaces designed with 5G-native connectivity from lease negotiation forward (O5)

**We need:**
- Concession POS pilot locations **explicitly included in Phase 1** of the CIO's deployment timeline — 10-15 locations in Terminals A, C (existing sections), and D, running alongside the cargo Phase 0.5/1A deployment
- POS connectivity general availability across all operational terminals by **Month 24** (our H8)
- A **concession-specific analytics dashboard** — not a raw data feed — showing zone-level foot traffic, peak time predictions, and gate-proximity demand signals, available to all consortium operators at no additional charge as part of the base connectivity service
- Pilot locations receive **zero connectivity charges** for up to 6 months and revenue protection per H7 transition terms

**Why this trade works for the CIO:** The CIO needs multi-use-case validation before scaling. Adding 10-15 concession locations to Phase 1 alongside cargo validates POS over 5G in a production environment with real multi-vendor POS diversity (Aloha, Toast, Square simultaneously). This is data the CIO cannot get from cargo alone. It's also data the CRO needs — proof that POS works on 5G before Month 30 platform launch.

**Why this trade works for the CRO:** Our data contribution (O3) enriches the Tier 1 analytics product with commercial behavior data that pure network telemetry cannot capture. Voluntary, structured data from operators produces higher-quality analytics than passive network observation. And early POS validation de-risks the CRO's Month 30 platform launch — the connectivity foundation is proven 6-12 months before the monetization layer goes live.

---

### Trade 4: Priority Hierarchy Endorsement ↔ Binding Bandwidth Floors

**We give:**
- Unqualified acceptance of Tier 4 position for concession POS
- Unqualified endorsement of Tier 1 for airline ramp operations
- Acceptance of Tier 3 for cargo automation above concession POS
- Support for the priority hierarchy as a binding technical specification with tenant consent for changes (per Airline VP HARD constraint)
- Commitment not to re-litigate priority position in any future governance forum

**We need:**
- The CRO's 5/2 Mbps per-location floor and the Airline's 1 Mbps degradation floor **both codified in the same binding technical specification** as the priority hierarchy itself
- Per-location measurement at the CPE gateway (not campus aggregate)
- Automated deprioritization notification to DFW concession support desk within 30 seconds when POS traffic enters degradation territory (below 5/2 Mbps)
- The 99.9% per-location SLA (our H1) measured against the 5/2 Mbps floor under normal operations. Periods where the network is operating under declared emergency degradation (DPA events, major equipment failure) are counted against the SLA only if POS drops below the 1 Mbps absolute floor.

**Why this trade works for the Airline VP:** The concessions consortium formally concedes the priority hierarchy debate. No more Tier 2/3 arguments. Tier 4 for POS, Tier 1 for airline ramp, binding specification. The Airline VP gets contractual certainty that concession traffic will never compete with ramp operations. In exchange, the bandwidth floors the Airline VP already made a HARD constraint are formalized into the same document.

---

### Trade 5: AT&T SLA Framework Endorsement ↔ Concession Support Commitment

**We give:**
- Endorsement of AT&T's enterprise SLA framework (O3) with the commercial slice at 99.9% / ≤50ms as the basis for concession service levels
- Acceptance of AT&T's proposed Tier 2/3 escalation structure (AT&T provides 2-hour response during operating hours behind DFW's front-line support desk)
- Support for AT&T's co-branding on the passenger captive portal and service documentation

**We need:**
- AT&T confirms the commercial slice SLA (99.9%, ≤50ms, ≤0.1% packet loss) applies **per concession location** measured at the CPE gateway, consistent with our H1
- AT&T's Tier 2/3 escalation for concession support desk issues includes a **2-hour resolution target** (not just response) for POS-impacting network-layer incidents during operating hours (5 AM – 12 AM)
- AT&T's parallel CWP operation guarantee and $250K performance bond (O6) explicitly covers concession POS connectivity during transition — if CWP degrades in concession areas during buildout, the bond is available for concession revenue protection

---

## 5. IRRECONCILABLE CONFLICTS

### I find none remaining.

The three conflicts I flagged in Round 2 have been resolved or are resolvable through the trades above:

**1. Priority hierarchy (RESOLVED):** The convergence across CRO (5/2 Mbps floor as HARD), Airline VP (1 Mbps degradation floor as HARD, time-sequenced degradation model), CIO (Tier 4 with protections), and AT&T (Tier 3A/4 with guaranteed minimum) produces a workable structure. We accept Tier 4 with dual bandwidth floors. The tier label is less important than the engineering reality: POS traffic has a protected, measurable, contractually enforceable bandwidth allocation that survives all operating conditions.

**2. Data resolution for concession areas (RESOLVED):** Our revised position (Section 3 above) accepts full-resolution data collection by DFW while requiring a firewall from lease negotiations, operator access to their own location data, and third-party anonymization of operator identity. This is cleaner than a resolution floor and produces a better product for the CRO.

**3. POS connectivity timeline (RESOLVABLE via Trade 3):** Our H8 (POS GA by Month 24) is achievable within the CIO's revised phasing if concession pilot locations are included in Phase 1. The CRO's HARD constraint (tenant platform technically capable by Month 24-30) is compatible — we need POS connectivity by Month 24; the full monetization platform launches 6 months later.

**Potential tension to monitor:** The CRO's HARD constraint specifies 5/2 Mbps as the floor "during capacity-constrained events," while the Airline VP's HARD constraint specifies 1 Mbps during seconds 30-300 of a capacity crunch. I have structured our acceptance (Section 2 above) to treat these as compatible tiers: 5/2 Mbps under normal operations and moderate congestion, 1 Mbps as the absolute floor during severe degradation. **I need the CRO and Airline VP to confirm this two-tier interpretation.** If the CRO insists that 5/2 Mbps must be maintained even during DPA events while the airline insists ramp operations maintain full allocation, the radio physics may not accommodate both simultaneously across all 200 locations. The engineering team should validate this during the Phase 0 baseline measurement.

---

## 6. UPDATED CONSTRAINT SET

### HARD CONSTRAINTS (Final)

| ID | Constraint | Status from Round 1 |
|---|---|---|
| **H1** | 99.9% POS connectivity per location per month, measured at CPE gateway, automatic 100% service credit for breach | **Unchanged.** Validated by AT&T SLA framework, CRO adoption, and CIO HC-9 per-location measurement. |
| **H2** | Dedicated POS network slice with 5/2 Mbps guaranteed floor (normal ops) and 1 Mbps absolute floor (severe degradation); ≤50ms latency; ≤0.1% packet loss; **Tier 4 priority** with dual bandwidth floors | **Revised from Round 2.** Tier lowered from 3 to 4 based on convergence; bandwidth floors elevated from soft ask to hard requirement based on CRO and Airline peer HARD constraints. Net improvement over Round 1. |
| **H3** | Turnkey managed service: single DFW-accountable support contact, zero operator network configuration, 30-minute acknowledgment / 4-hour resolution SLA | **Unchanged.** No peer conflicts. |
| **H4** | Per-location monthly pricing: $175/mo at 175+ locations (current consortium), $200/mo standard, $80/mo ACDBE. All-inclusive OpEx. No CapEx. CPI/3% annual cap. Month-to-month per-location flex. 10-day new location activation. Most-favored-tenant clause. | **Revised.** Accepted CRO's proposed pricing structure; added most-favored-tenant clause and annual escalation cap. |
| **H5** | Existing POS hardware compatibility (Aloha, Toast, Square) via managed CPE gateway with Ethernet/Wi-Fi handoff. No forced POS replacement. | **Unchanged.** No peer conflicts. Airline H4 (no forced CBRS migration) creates binding parallel precedent. |
| **H6** | Operator owns all transaction data, customer data, and operator-specific performance data. Network-derived concession-area analytics: (a) firewalled from DFW lease negotiations without operator consent, (b) operator-specific data available to that operator at no charge, (c) third-party products anonymize operator identity. $50K liquidated damages per incident for unauthorized data use. | **Revised from Round 2.** Relaxed geographic resolution demand; added lease-negotiation firewall, operator access right, and third-party anonymization. CRO and Airline Unified Tenant Data Framework satisfy core requirement. |
| **H7** | Zero-disruption CWP transition: 90-day parallel operation per location, per-location cutover, 4-hour rollback, Nov 15–Jan 5 and Mar 1–Mar 20 blackout, $15K/location/day penalty for deployment-attributable outages >4 hours. | **Unchanged.** CRO has adopted identical terms as HARD constraints. Full alignment. |
| **H8** | POS connectivity general availability across all operational terminals by **Month 24**. Pilot locations (10-15) live by Month 12. | **Unchanged from Round 2.** Compatible with CRO's Month 24-30 tenant platform target. |

### SOFT CONSTRAINTS (Revised)

| ID | Constraint | Status |
|---|---|---|
| **S1** | Concession IoT slice (digital signage, kitchen display, inventory) at +$75/mo opt-in, same managed service and support contact | **Unchanged.** |
| **S2** | Target pricing at $175/mo achieved via volume discount at 175+ locations | **Achieved.** CRO's proposed structure gives us $175/mo. Moving to monitoring/enforcement. |
| **S3** | Permanent consortium seat on multi-tenant priority governance body | **Unchanged.** Elevated importance given Tier 4 acceptance — we need a voice in any future governance discussions. |
| **S4** | Co-funded ACDBE onboarding support: HMSHost/Avolta and Star Concessions staff 15-20 onboarding events over 24 months; DFW includes as ACDBE program line item | **Unchanged.** |
| **S5** | Concession-specific analytics dashboard: zone-level foot traffic, peak time predictions, gate-proximity demand signals. Available to all operators at no additional charge. | **Revised.** Elevated from "nice to have" to core value exchange for our data contribution (O3). |
| **S6** | Amazon JWO parity: same SLA, same data ownership, same pricing structure as traditional POS. Transparency on any differential terms. | **Unchanged.** |
| **S7** | 60-second automatic POS failover to cellular backup for POS transactions. Operators retain right to maintain backup cellular relationship on carrier of their choosing. | **Unchanged from Round 2.** Modeled after Airline H3 concept. |
| **S8** | CPE gateways classified as pre-approved device class: 5-business-day notification, not 30-day RF review. | **Unchanged from Round 2.** Proposed as Trade 1 with AT&T. |

### OFFERS (Final)

All prior offers remain on the table. Updated summary:

| ID | Offer | Value to Recipient |
|---|---|---|
| **O1** | Binding adoption: 200+ locations within 12 months of GA | CRO/CIO: Bankable revenue floor ($460-500K/year). Eliminates tenant adoption risk. |
| **O2** | Single coordination point for 60 operators | CIO/AT&T: Eliminates 60 individual tenant negotiations. One invoice, one escalation path. |
| **O3** | Anonymized foot traffic + dwell time data contribution | CRO: Enriches Tier 1 analytics with commercial behavior data. Higher-quality product. |
| **O4** | 10-15 pilot locations, 3+ terminals, 5+ operators, 2+ ACDBE | CIO/AT&T: Multi-vendor POS validation (Aloha/Toast/Square). Real-world stress testing. |
| **O5** | Terminal F advocacy: 5G-native design in all new concession buildouts | CIO/CRO: Structurally embeds adoption in 34+ new locations. |
| **O6** | Star Concessions as local ACDBE pilot anchor | CRO: ACDBE reference case. "If Star can do it, you can do it." |
| **O7** | Support AT&T CWP protection framework for infrastructure changes | AT&T: Coalition support for CWP stability during buildout. |
| **O8** | Support 10-year contract term | CIO/AT&T: Long-term revenue predictability for CapEx financing. |
| **O9** | Co-fund ACDBE onboarding (HMSHost/Avolta + Star tech teams) | CIO/AT&T: Lower operational overhead on most fragmented tenant segment. |

---

## 7. WHAT I NEED FROM EACH PEER IN ROUND 3

**From the CIO:**
- Confirm concession pilot locations (10-15) are included in Phase 1 deployment timeline, alongside cargo Phase 0.5/1A. POS on 5G needs to be validated early — your tenant adoption story depends on it.
- Confirm CPE gateway pre-approval classification (5-day notification) as distinct from small cell installation (30-day review). Your Phase 0 documentation should include the pre-approved CPE specification profile.
- Confirm that the data firewall from lease negotiations (our revised H6 condition) is architecturally implementable — specifically, that DFW's analytics platform can technically restrict which internal DFW teams access location-specific concession-area analytics.

**From the CRO:**
- Confirm the **two-tier bandwidth floor interpretation**: 5/2 Mbps under normal operations, 1 Mbps during severe degradation. If the CRO's intent is 5/2 Mbps maintained at all times including during DPA events, the engineering team needs to validate feasibility given the Airline VP's full-allocation requirement for ramp operations.
- Confirm the **lease-negotiation firewall** on operator-specific network analytics is acceptable. This protects operator trust, keeps adoption high, and doesn't reduce monetization value to external customers.
- Confirm concession analytics dashboard (S5) is included in the base connectivity service, not priced as a premium analytics subscription. Operators paying $175/mo for connectivity should receive basic foot traffic visibility for their own location.

**From the Airline VP:**
- Confirm that concession POS at Tier 4 with dual bandwidth floors (5/2 normal, 1 Mbps degradation) satisfies the Airline's priority hierarchy requirement. We are no longer contesting Tier 1 for airline ramp operations.
- Confirm that the time-sequenced degradation model (seconds 0-30 all maintain minimum viable, seconds 30-300 POS at 1 Mbps floor) is the operative mechanism and is included in the binding technical specification alongside the priority hierarchy.

**From Cargo:**
- Confirm mutual acceptance of Tier 3 (cargo) and Tier 4 (concession POS) as non-competitive positions. Different zones, different traffic types, no bandwidth competition.
- Joint presentation of demand commitment to the CIO: cargo's $4-6M/year + concessions' $460-500K/year + binding adoption timelines = the revenue anchor the Board needs.

**From AT&T:**
- Response on CPE gateway pre-approval classification (Trade 1). Your engineers know the difference between a small cell and a CPE device. Let's formalize it.
- Confirm the commercial slice SLA (99.9%, ≤50ms, ≤0.1% packet loss) is measured per location at the CPE gateway. Your O3 enterprise SLA framework is the basis — we need per-location granularity confirmed.
- Confirm 2-hour resolution target (not just response) for POS-impacting network-layer incidents escalated from DFW's concession support desk during operating hours.

---

## Closing

Three rounds in, and here is where the concessions consortium stands:

**What we came for:** POS that works. Pricing we can predict. A service our smallest operator can use. Data that stays ours.

**What the negotiation delivered:** Dual bandwidth floors backed by peer HARD constraints. ACDBE pricing at $80/mo. A turnkey managed service model that no peer contests. Transaction data explicitly carved out by both the CRO and the Airline's Unified Tenant Data Framework. $15K/day penalty protection during transition. Blackout dates adopted by the CRO as hard constraints. Per-location SLA measurement endorsed by the CIO.

**What we gave up:** Priority tier position (Tier 2/3 → Tier 4). Geographic resolution demand on network analytics (zone-level floor → lease-negotiation firewall). And honestly? We got the better deal. Tier 4 with binding 5/2 and 1 Mbps bandwidth floors is more protection than Tier 2 without them. A lease-negotiation firewall is more targeted protection than a blanket resolution floor that would have degraded the CRO's analytics product.

**What remains:** CPE gateway classification. Phase 1 pilot inclusion. Two-tier bandwidth floor confirmation. Analytics dashboard in base service. These are implementable details, not structural conflicts.

My 60 operators don't need to know the tier number. They need to know three things: *Will my register work? How much does it cost? Who do I call when it doesn't?*

The architecture answers all three. Let's close this.

---

*F&B Concessions Consortium Technology Lead — Round 3 Position*
*On behalf of HMSHost/Avolta, Star Concessions, Paradies Lagardère, and 57 additional operators*

arguments.name: AT&T Carrier Rep
arguments.content:
# AT&T Round 3 Position: Final Constraint Reconciliation

## Preamble

We've reached the point where this negotiation either produces a term sheet or produces another round of position papers. I intend for it to be the former.

I have mapped every HARD constraint declared by every peer in Rounds 1 and 2. I can satisfy all of them. Several require meaningful concessions from AT&T — concessions I'm prepared to make because the alternative is watching DFW build this network with Betacom while we litigate the CWP transition. That is not an outcome that protects our $10M investment or positions AT&T for the next decade of airport private 5G deployments nationwide.

Let me be precise about what I'm accepting, what I'm trading, and the one conflict that still requires resolution.

---

## 1. REVISED POSITION — Satisfying All Peer HARD Constraints

I'm going through every peer HARD constraint and stating explicitly how AT&T's position accommodates it.

---

### CRO HARD Constraints — Full Acceptance

**DFW Infrastructure Ownership for Data Title (CRO HARD):** Accepted without reservation. This was settled in Round 2. DFW owns all physical and logical infrastructure below the carrier-operations API layer. AT&T operates on DFW-owned equipment. The asset purchase mechanism for existing CWP hardware at independently appraised fair market value proceeds as I proposed in Round 2. This gives DFW clean title and AT&T fair recovery.

**Tenant Monetization Platform by Phase 2 End / Revenue by Phase 3 (CRO HARD):** Accepted. AT&T commits to having carrier-layer network slicing operationally capable of supporting the commercial concession tier across all operational terminals by Month 24. API access to anonymized foot traffic and dwell analytics available to DFW's platform by Month 18. These are AT&T-deliverable milestones and we will commit to them contractually.

**200 Basis Point Penalty for AT&T-Attributable Delays (CRO HARD):** Accepted — with one structural clarification that I believe the CRO will agree is fair. The penalty applies to delays where AT&T is the **sole or primary attributable cause**, determined through the joint project governance board I proposed in Round 2. I am not asking to escape accountability. I am asking that if DFW's own procurement delays or construction contractor slippage causes a milestone miss, AT&T does not absorb the financial consequence. The CRO's constraint says "AT&T-attributable" — I'm accepting that language at face value and committing to a root-cause attribution process that makes it enforceable.

Specifically: AT&T will agree to a **milestone accountability matrix** at contract signing that identifies which deliverables are AT&T-owned, which are DFW-owned, and which are jointly owned. The 200 bps penalty applies to AT&T-owned milestones missed by AT&T. Jointly-owned milestones missed trigger an escalation to the governance board before penalties apply. This is good governance, not penalty avoidance.

**$2M Minimum Net Annual Savings by Year 2 (CRO HARD):** AT&T accepts this as a program-level target. Our contribution: CBRS 2.0 migration at zero cost to DFW (15-25% more usable capacity), carrier offload revenue floor guarantees ($500K-$1M/year that directly offsets operational costs), and elimination of fragmented IoT connectivity costs through network consolidation. AT&T will participate in the independent financial model validation the CRO requires and will make our cost assumptions auditable.

**Blackout Periods — No Concession Cutover Nov 15–Jan 5, Mar 1–Mar 20 (CRO HARD):** Accepted. AT&T's deployment teams will observe these blackout windows for any work affecting concession-zone connectivity. We will extend this commitment to include no planned maintenance affecting concession POS slices during these windows.

**Per-Location Cutover with 90-Day Parallel and 4-Hour Rollback (CRO HARD):** Accepted. This is consistent with our O6 (parallel operation guarantee). AT&T's dedicated transition team (4 FTE minimum) will execute per-location cutover with 90-day parallel operation and documented 4-hour rollback procedures. Rollback will be tested at each pilot location before broader deployment proceeds.

**$15K/Location/Day Penalty for Deployment-Attributable Outages >4 Hours (CRO HARD):** Accepted. Our $250K CWP performance bond (O6) runs parallel to this per-location penalty structure. AT&T accepts direct financial accountability for deployment-caused concession disruption.

**ACDBE Pricing ≤40% of Enterprise Tier (CRO HARD):** Accepted. This is DFW's commercial policy decision funded from DFW's revenue share. No AT&T objection. AT&T's onboarding support resources (the 2 FTE for 18 months the CRO's soft constraint requests) will include dedicated support for ACDBE operator onboarding.

**Separate Revenue Stream Accounting — Tenant Access vs. Carrier Offload (CRO HARD):** Accepted enthusiastically. This is exactly what AT&T proposed in Round 2. Carrier offload has fundamentally different economics and value drivers than tenant access fees. Separate accounting, separate formulas, no blending or cross-subsidization. This protects AT&T's carrier offload revenue from being netted against tenant adoption shortfalls, and protects DFW's tenant revenue from being claimed against carrier offload performance.

**DFW Tier 1 Data Ownership — Specified Categories Only (CRO HARD):** Accepted. DFW owns common-area passenger flow, device density, and dwell time data. Airline operational data, concession transaction data, and cargo operational data are explicitly excluded from DFW's Tier 1 and remain tenant-owned. AT&T retains operational telemetry (Tier 2/5 per the CIO's framework) for network management only — no commercial exploitation, no cross-customer aggregation. This was our position from Round 2.

**AT&T Branding Scope Limitation (CRO HARD):** **Accepted.** This is more restrictive than my Round 2 H6, and I want to acknowledge the concession explicitly.

AT&T branding will **not** appear on:
- DFW data analytics products
- DFW advertising platform
- Tenant-facing analytics dashboards

AT&T branding **will** appear on:
- Tenant service agreements (technical appendix acknowledgment)
- Passenger-facing Wi-Fi/5G captive portal ("DFW 5G — Powered by AT&T")
- DFW marketing materials referencing the private 5G network

I accept this scope. The passenger portal co-brand and marketing reference rights are what AT&T needs to justify this investment internally. The exclusion from analytics dashboards is logical — those are DFW-owned data products and AT&T's brand has no claim to them.

**What I need acknowledged in return:** DFW commits to the case study, conference co-presentation, and site visit facilitation rights per the CIO's O-5 and our Round 2 agreement. AT&T may reference DFW as a carrier-operations deployment in AT&T's own enterprise sales materials, subject to DFW approval of specific content and security-sensitive redactions.

**Concession POS Guaranteed Minimum Bandwidth Floor (CRO HARD):** Accepted. 5 Mbps down / 2 Mbps up per concession location as a guaranteed minimum, aggregating to approximately 1 Gbps campus-wide for 200 locations. This is technically deliverable within the CBRS capacity envelope — POS transactions are low-bandwidth, and the guaranteed floor represents a small fraction of total campus capacity. AT&T's network engineering team will provision this as a guaranteed bit-rate allocation within the concession POS network slice, surviving capacity-constrained events including DPA.

**Passive Infrastructure Embedding for Cargo Zone — Separate Authorization Track (CRO HARD):** Accepted without reservation. Conduit, fiber pathways, and mounting points are vendor-neutral passive infrastructure with zero RF impact on the existing CWP. There is no technical reason to couple passive infrastructure authorization to RF-active deployment decisions. AT&T supports immediate authorization of cargo Phase 0 passive infrastructure.

**Committed Revenue Contracts Required for Board Approval (CRO HARD):** Accepted. AT&T will execute a **binding carrier offload revenue commitment** — not a projection — as part of the contract package. Our guaranteed floors ($500K Year 1, $750K Year 2, $1M Years 3-5) will be contractually binding commitments with shortfall payments if AT&T misses. This is real money AT&T is guaranteeing. The CRO can present these to the Board as committed revenue.

---

### Airline VP HARD Constraints — Full Acceptance

**Dedicated Slice with Cryptographic Isolation and Data Sovereignty (Airline HARD):** Accepted. AT&T will provision a dedicated network slice for airline operations with cryptographically enforced isolation. Airline operational data — ramp telemetry, device metadata, crew communications, baggage system data — never enters DFW's monetization pipeline. Not anonymized, not aggregated. Excluded entirely. This is consistent with the Unified Tenant Data Framework and the CRO's revised Tier 1 data scope.

**99.99% Uptime Per Terminal Per Month at Application Layer (Airline HARD):** Accepted. This is the SLA we offered in our O3 enterprise framework for the safety-critical slice. Measured per terminal, not campus-wide. Application-layer measurement from the airline's devices, not AT&T's core network interface. Synthetic monitoring with airline-approved methodology. AT&T accepts the financial penalty structure and material breach triggers the airline defined.

**30-Second Automatic Failover to Cellular Backup (Airline HARD):** Accepted. The failover mechanism operates as a complementary layer to AT&T's 250ms DPA response. AT&T manages spectrum; the airline's devices maintain connectivity continuity. Monthly testing during low-traffic windows with results reported within 48 hours. The airline retains the right to provision failover SIMs on any carrier.

**No Forced Migration of Existing CBRS Device Fleet (Airline HARD):** Accepted. The 5G network will provide a CBRS-compatible access layer for all existing airline devices. Any device migration required by the architecture is at DFW/AT&T cost with zero disruption during peak hours and 90-day parallel operation with rollback.

**Airline Configuration Authority Over Its Slice with 30-Minute Security Notification (Airline HARD):** Accepted. The airline receives direct administrative access (API/portal, full CRUD) within its slice policy layer. Changes propagate within 60 seconds. DFW security override authority is accepted with 30-minute advance notification before changes affecting the airline slice security posture. For zero-notice emergency federal security directives, post-event notification to the airline's 24/7 NOC within 60 minutes. AT&T implements DFW-directed security changes; the notification obligation is DFW's governance responsibility.

**Penetration Testing Scheduling Constraints (Airline HARD):** Accepted. 30-day notice, no testing during peak banking hours (0600-0900, 1500-1800, 2000-2200) without airline written consent, unredacted results for findings affecting airline slice. AT&T will cooperate fully with DFW's pen testing program including providing configuration data, logs, and carrier-operations code versioning.

**Priority Hierarchy as Binding Technical Specification with Tenant Consent for Changes (Airline HARD):** **Accepted.** This is a significant governance constraint and I want to be explicit about what I'm accepting.

The priority hierarchy will be codified as a binding technical specification in the master services agreement — not a governance policy document. Changes to the hierarchy require written consent of all Tier 0 and Tier 1 tenants. AT&T implements and enforces the hierarchy as specified; AT&T does not unilaterally modify it.

**What I need acknowledged:** AT&T retains technical advisory authority. If AT&T's engineering team certifies that the specified hierarchy creates measurable SLA risk or DPA compliance exposure under specific capacity scenarios, this certification is documented and provided to all governance parties. If DFW directs AT&T to implement a hierarchy configuration that AT&T has formally certified as creating compliance or performance risk, DFW issues a written override that adjusts AT&T's SLA obligations for the affected scenario. We enforce the hierarchy we're given; we don't absorb penalties for performance outcomes we warned about.

**Airline Ramp Full Allocation During Seconds 30-300 of Capacity Crunch (Airline HARD):** Accepted. The time-sequenced degradation model is technically sound and implementable:

| Window | Action |
|--------|--------|
| Seconds 0-30 | All non-safety slices maintain minimum viable operations; failover activation begins |
| Seconds 30-300 | Passenger Wi-Fi and advertising fully shed. Concession POS maintains 1 Mbps minimum per location (consistent with CRO's 5/2 Mbps floor degrading to 1 Mbps under extreme constraint). Cargo cold chain maintains continuity. Airline ramp maintains full allocation. |
| After 300 seconds | Pre-defined "sustained DPA" allocation with proportional reduction based on contractual minimums |

AT&T will implement this as an automated policy in the radio resource management system, tested quarterly.

**Pre-Authorized Construction Framework for Terminal C (Airline HARD):** **Accepted.** This resolves the tension between my CWP protection requirements and Terminal C reconstruction timelines.

**Revised AT&T position on CWP RF review:**

| Scenario | Buffer | Review Period | Authority |
|----------|--------|--------------|-----------|
| New RF equipment energization (small cells, DAS) | 50 meters | 30 days | Joint AT&T/DFW approval |
| Standard construction (fiber, switches, passive infrastructure) | 30 meters | 14 days | AT&T notification with expedited review |
| Construction within pre-authorized framework (Terminal C, cargo zone) | Per framework | Per-event review only for out-of-framework activities | Framework developed during Phase 0 |
| Concession CPE gateway installation | N/A — pre-approved device class | 5 business days notification | Automated notification |

The pre-authorized construction framework will be developed jointly by AT&T RF engineering, DFW network architecture, and the airline's ops technology team during the Phase 0 baseline period. Activities within the framework proceed without per-event 30-day review. Out-of-framework activities trigger the standard review process.

I'm also accepting the concessions consortium's request for CPE gateways as a pre-approved device class (5-day notification). These are low-power endpoints presenting negligible RF interference risk. AT&T's engineers know the difference between a small cell installation and a CPE device plugged into Ethernet. Let's codify that distinction.

**Time-Sequenced Degradation Model (Airline HARD):** Accepted in full. See table above. AT&T will engineer and enforce this as an automated radio resource management policy.

**Unified Tenant Data Framework as Binding MSA Exhibit (Airline HARD):** Accepted. The framework establishing tenant operational data ownership, DFW's common-area data monetization rights, and AT&T's operational telemetry boundaries will be a binding exhibit to the master services agreement. AT&T supports this — it clarifies our own data rights and obligations.

**AT&T 250ms DPA Response Accepted; 30-Second Airline Failover as Complement (Airline HARD):** Confirmed. This has been our position from Round 1. Two complementary mechanisms: AT&T handles spectrum, airline devices handle connectivity continuity.

**60-Minute Post-Event Notification for Zero-Notice Emergency Directives (Airline HARD):** Accepted. In zero-notice emergency security scenarios where DFW executes an immediate security override without advance notification, post-event notification to the airline's 24/7 NOC within 60 minutes is reasonable and implementable. AT&T will include this notification as a step in our emergency response runbook.

---

## 2. PROPOSED TRADES

Now that I've accepted every HARD constraint, let me propose the trades that make the economics work for AT&T.

---

### Trade 1: CWP Investment Recognition — CapEx Credit, Not Revenue-Share Premium

The CRO's position from Round 2 was clear: AT&T's $10M CWP investment should be recognized as a capital contribution credit against co-investment obligations, not as a revenue-share modifier. The CIO proposed a +5% revenue share for 60 months. These are different mechanisms.

**AT&T's revised position:** I'll accept the CRO's framework. AT&T's CWP investment is recognized as a **capital contribution credit** applied as follows:

- DFW purchases existing CWP physical assets at independently appraised fair market value (estimated $3.5-5M after depreciation)
- AT&T's unrecovered investment balance (approximately $5-6.5M) is credited against AT&T's $5M edge compute/MEC co-investment obligation (per the CRO's soft constraint)
- **Net result:** AT&T's new cash co-investment requirement is reduced to approximately $0-1.5M in new capital, but AT&T has effectively contributed $10M+ in total infrastructure value to the program

**What I give up:** The +10% revenue-share premium I proposed in Round 2 for 60 months. That was worth approximately $1.5-3M over 5 years in additional revenue share. I'm conceding this.

**What I get:** The CRO counts AT&T's full CWP investment as satisfied co-investment, DFW gets clean title to 1,000 APs, and we avoid a 60-month revenue-share distortion that the CRO correctly identified as bad deal structure.

**What I need from the CRO and CIO:** Confirmation that this credit mechanism satisfies the CRO's soft constraint for "$5M edge compute and MEC infrastructure co-investment." AT&T is co-investing — we're doing it through infrastructure already in the ground plus incremental new investment, not through a $5M check on day one.

---

### Trade 2: Revenue Share Alignment — Accepting the CRO's Declining Structure

The CRO's soft constraint proposes:
- **Tenant access fees:** DFW 60% / AT&T 40% (Years 1-3) → DFW 70% / AT&T 30% (Years 4-7) → DFW 80% / AT&T 20% (Years 8-10)
- **Carrier offload:** DFW 55% / AT&T 45% (Years 1-5) → DFW 70% / AT&T 30% (Years 6-10)

**AT&T accepts this structure.** Let me explain why.

The CRO's carrier offload split actually gives AT&T **more** in Years 1-5 (45%) than our own H3 requested (40%). That tells me the CRO values AT&T's wholesale relationships at a premium — which they should, because no one else at this table can pick up the phone and negotiate T-Mobile/Verizon offload agreements.

The tenant access fee structure gives AT&T 40% in Years 1-3, declining to 20% by Years 8-10. My Round 2 position proposed 25%/15%. The CRO is offering me significantly more on tenant access than I asked for in the early years, when the risk is highest and AT&T's onboarding contribution is most valuable.

**What I accept:** Both revenue share structures as proposed by the CRO.

**What I commit to in return:**
- AT&T dedicates **2 FTE onboarding resources for 18 months** (per CRO soft constraint) to help DFW onboard the first 50 tenant connections
- AT&T's carrier offload revenue guarantees become **binding contractual commitments**: $500K Year 1, $750K Year 2, $1M Years 3-5, with shortfall payments
- AT&T funds CBRS 2.0 migration at zero cost to DFW
- AT&T funds carrier-layer technology refresh at Year 5 (5G-Advanced) as part of renewal negotiation

**Revenue stream separation:** Per the CRO's HARD constraint, these two streams are tracked and managed independently with distinct formulas and accounting. No blending. AT&T supports this entirely — it prevents our carrier offload performance from being netted against tenant access fluctuations.

---

### Trade 3: Contract Term — Accepting 10 Years with Full Mid-Term Architecture

**AT&T accepts the CRO's 10-year structure:**
- 10-year initial term
- Binding Year 5 renegotiation covering revenue share, SLA thresholds, and technology refresh
- No-fault exit at Year 7 with 18-month notice
- Binding Year 5 technology refresh (AT&T funds carrier-layer 5G-Advanced upgrade)

**What I give up:** My Round 2 ask for 84-month exclusivity. I accept **60-month exclusivity** with objective performance review at Month 36.

**What I get:**
- Performance review criteria mutually agreed at contract signing — SLA compliance, DPA response compliance, carrier offload floor achievement, tenant satisfaction
- 30% minimum weight for incumbent operational track record in any competitive evaluation (Terminal F or otherwise)
- 18-month advance access to Terminal F design documents for proposal preparation
- AT&T is carrier-operations partner for Phases 0-2 without mid-phase vendor introduction

**What I concede on Terminal F:** AT&T accepts that Terminal F triggers a new competitive evaluation for carrier operations. I said in Round 1 "we'll win it on operational evidence." I stand by that. Give us a fair evaluation with advance access to design documents and proper weight for operational track record, and we'll compete.

---

### Trade 4: Cargo Zone Competitive Process — Supporting Acceleration While Protecting AT&T's Right to Compete

The cargo timeline is urgent and legitimate. The CRO has made committed cargo revenue a HARD requirement for board approval. The CIO has acknowledged cargo Phase 0 parallel deployment. Everyone agrees the concrete doesn't wait.

**AT&T's final position on cargo:**

1. **Phase 0 passive infrastructure (conduit, fiber, mounting):** Proceeds immediately upon authorization. AT&T supports this without reservation. Zero RF impact, zero CWP risk, vendor-neutral. Authorize it in 45 days as the Cargo Director requests.

2. **Phase 1A active deployment (RF equipment energization):** AT&T proposes that DFW issue a **cargo zone carrier-operations evaluation** within 60 days, with proposals due within 30 days of issuance. AT&T, Betacom, and any other qualified vendor submit proposals. The winning vendor deploys the cargo zone carrier layer on the Cargo Director's accelerated timeline.

3. **AT&T's specific commitment:** We will submit a cargo zone proposal within 30 days that includes indoor warehouse RF design leveraging our experience at FedEx Memphis and UPS Louisville, ≤20ms AGV latency SLA, 99.99% per-warehouse availability, cold chain failover support, and edge compute co-location within each major facility.

4. **Architectural integration:** Whichever vendor wins the cargo zone, the carrier-operations layer must conform to the campus-wide API boundary specification and interoperate with AT&T's terminal/campus network operations. This is non-negotiable — a fragmented architecture defeats the hybrid co-investment model.

**What I give up:** Insistence that Betacom trial nodes remain in non-production test zones. If Betacom wins the cargo zone competitive evaluation, they operate the cargo carrier layer in production. That's fair.

**What I get:** A competitive process — not a sole-source Betacom expansion. AT&T competes on merit for $4-6M/year in recurring cargo revenue. If we win, we accelerate our campus-wide track record. If we don't, we learn something valuable about our competitive position before Terminal F.

**Why this protects everyone:** The Cargo Director gets speed (passive infrastructure immediate, active deployment by Q1-Q2 2026). The CRO gets committed cargo revenue for the board package. The CIO gets architectural integration. AT&T gets to compete. The airline gets a validated proof-of-concept before committing their ramp operations.

---

### Trade 5: Concession Support Model — AT&T as Tier 2/3 Escalation Behind DFW

**AT&T accepts:** DFW owns the concession customer relationship and operates the single-contact support desk. AT&T has no tenant-facing role with concession operators.

**AT&T offers:** Dedicated Tier 2 escalation support for DFW's concession support desk — 2-hour response during operating hours, 4-hour off-hours, for carrier-layer issues that DFW's front-line team escalates. This makes the concessions' 30-minute acknowledgment / 4-hour resolution SLA achievable.

**AT&T accepts the CRO's branding scope:** No AT&T branding on data analytics, advertising platform, or tenant-facing dashboards. AT&T branding limited to service agreements, passenger portal, and marketing materials. No customer-facing branding inside concession locations (per concessions consortium request).

**What I need:** The CRO's soft constraint for $250K cargo acceleration bonus — I propose extending this concept to concessions. If AT&T's carrier-layer delivery enables concession POS general availability by Month 24 (the concessions consortium's new HARD constraint H8), AT&T receives a $150K milestone bonus funded from DFW's revenue share. This aligns AT&T's deployment team incentives with the concession timeline.

---

### Trade 6: Data Monetization — Full Concession to DFW, Telemetry Retention for AT&T

**AT&T gives up:** 100% of data monetization revenue. Zero share. DFW owns all Tier 1 analytics, advertising, and location-based services revenue permanently, surviving contract termination.

**AT&T also gives up:** Any right to use DFW network data commercially, aggregate with other deployments, or feed into AT&T analytics subsidiaries.

**AT&T retains:** Operational telemetry (RF performance, SAS coordination, spectrum utilization) for the sole purpose of operating the DFW network and for anonymized, aggregated industry benchmarking ("AT&T manages CBRS deployments averaging X% availability" — no DFW-identifiable data, no passenger data, no location data). This is the Tier 5 operational data in the CIO's framework.

**This is final.** The CRO's $6-13M data monetization opportunity belongs to DFW. AT&T's value is in carrier operations and offload revenue, not in passenger analytics. Let's stop negotiating this point and move to term sheets.

---

## 3. IRRECONCILABLE CONFLICTS

### Conflict A: Priority Hierarchy — The Concession POS Tier Question

This remains the single unresolved conflict across the table. Let me state AT&T's engineering assessment, because we're the party that has to implement whatever hierarchy emerges:

**The airline's time-sequenced degradation model is now a HARD constraint.** It specifies:
- Airline ramp: full allocation during seconds 30-300
- Concession POS: 1 Mbps minimum during seconds 30-300
- Cargo cold chain: maintains continuity

**The CRO's bandwidth floor is now a HARD constraint:** 5 Mbps/2 Mbps per concession location as guaranteed minimum under normal operations, with the time-sequenced model governing degradation scenarios.

**AT&T's engineering assessment:** These constraints are **simultaneously satisfiable.** The concession POS bandwidth floor (1 Gbps total for 200 locations at 5/2 Mbps) represents approximately 3-5% of total campus CBRS capacity under normal operations. During a severe DPA event reducing capacity by 40%, maintaining a 1 Mbps floor per POS location (200 Mbps total) represents less than 1% of remaining capacity. This is technically trivial to protect.

**AT&T's proposed resolution — consistent with the CIO's Round 2 proposal but refined:**

| Tier | Category | Normal Operations | Capacity Crunch (30-300s) |
|------|----------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| 0 | Safety-critical (FAA/TSA/Emergency) | Absolute priority | Full allocation |
| 1 | Airline ramp operations | Guaranteed SLA (99.99%, <10ms) | Full allocation |
| 2 | Federal inspection (CBP/TSA) | Guaranteed SLA | Guaranteed minimum |
| 3 | Cargo automation (AGV, cold chain) | Guaranteed SLA (99.99%, <20ms) | Degraded floor (50ms AGV, 200ms cold chain) — maintains operational safety |
| 4 | Concession POS | Guaranteed SLA (99.9%, <50ms, 5/2 Mbps floor) | 1 Mbps minimum per location — maintains transaction completion |
| 5 | Airport operations (DFW facilities) | Best-effort with SLA | Degraded |
| 6 | Commercial IoT, digital signage, advertising | Best-effort | Shed after Tier 5 |
| 7 | Passenger Wi-Fi, carrier offload | Best-effort | First to shed |

**Why POS at Tier 4 works for the concessions consortium:** Under normal operations (which is 99%+ of operating hours), the concession POS slice receives its full 5/2 Mbps guaranteed allocation with 99.9% availability and <50ms latency. The priority hierarchy is only invoked during capacity-constrained events — DPA, equipment failure, or extreme peak congestion. During those events, POS maintains a 1 Mbps floor that is more than sufficient for credit card authorizations (which are sub-100KB transactions). The concessions consortium's own soft constraint S3 stated: "even during deprioritization, POS traffic cannot be dropped below a minimum viable threshold (1 Mbps per location). Degraded is acceptable. Dark is not." Tier 4 with a 1 Mbps floor delivers exactly that.

**Why POS cannot be Tier 3 (co-equal with cargo):** During a severe capacity event, the cargo AGV floor (50ms degraded latency) and POS floor (1 Mbps) could theoretically compete for the same constrained capacity. If they're co-equal, the arbitration is undefined. With POS at Tier 4, cargo automation safety (AGVs moving autonomously in a warehouse) takes precedence over POS transaction latency — which is the correct risk-weighted ordering. A failed POS transaction retries in 30 seconds. A failed AGV control signal creates a collision risk.

**I need the CIO to confirm this hierarchy and the concessions consortium to accept Tier 4 with the guaranteed floor.** AT&T can engineer and enforce this. We need the policy decision to be final.

---

### Conflict B: Betacom Competitive Evaluation — Status

The airline VP's SOFT constraint supports production-grade Betacom during evaluation. My Trade 4 proposes a competitive process for the cargo zone that allows Betacom to compete and win production deployment rights.

**Remaining question for the CIO:** Does the CIO accept that any Betacom expansion beyond designated trial zones into production coverage — including cargo — goes through a competitive process? Or does the CIO intend Betacom's Phase 1 expansion to proceed as a sole-source production deployment?

If the CIO accepts a competitive cargo zone evaluation (which the Cargo Director's $15M investment arguably demands), this conflict is resolved. If the CIO insists on sole-source Betacom for cargo, this triggers AT&T's H5 competitive evaluation protection and we have an impasse.

**My strong preference:** Let us compete. The cargo zone is the ideal competitive proving ground — physically isolated from the CWP, well-defined scope, aggressive but achievable timeline, and $15M in co-investment that deserves vendor competition. AT&T submits a proposal in 30 days; Betacom submits a proposal in 30 days; DFW evaluates and selects. The architecture is stronger for having been competitively tested.

---

### No Other Irreconcilable Conflicts

Every other peer HARD constraint is satisfied by the positions stated above. The priority hierarchy and Betacom competitive process are the only remaining items requiring cross-stakeholder resolution.

---

## 4. UPDATED CONSTRAINT SET

### HARD Constraints (Final)

| ID | Constraint | Status |
|----|-----------|--------|
| **H1** | Carrier-Layer Operational Authority: AT&T retains sole real-time authority over CBRS spectrum management (SAS coordination, DPA response at 250ms, channel/power control). Technical advisory authority with written override mechanism for DFW policy decisions creating RF/compliance risk. | **Refined** — Domain table formalized, airline slice self-service accepted, DFW security sovereignty accepted with narrow DPA carve-out |
| **H2** | CWP Investment Recognition: Asset transfer at independently appraised FMV, investment credit against co-investment obligations, 60-month parallel CWP operation. Pre-authorized construction framework for Terminal C and cargo zone developed during Phase 0. CPE gateways as pre-approved device class (5-day notification). | **Significantly revised** — Accepted CRO CapEx credit model, accepted pre-authorized construction framework, accepted CPE device class |
| **H3** | Carrier Offload Revenue Share: AT&T 45% / DFW 55% Years 1-5, AT&T 30% / DFW 70% Years 6-10. Tracked separately from tenant access. Binding revenue floor guarantees. | **Accepted CRO's proposed split** — which is more favorable to AT&T than our own Round 2 ask |
| **H4** | Operational Staff Access: 24/7/365 physical access, 12 SIDA badges (including 2 DPA-trained RF engineers), <5ms remote management plane, 45-minute on-site response capability | **Maintained** |
| **H5** | Competitive Evaluation Protection: 180-day notice before carrier-operations RFP. AT&T participates on equal terms. 30% minimum weight for incumbent track record. Cargo zone competitive evaluation within 60 days. | **Revised** — Accepted competition for cargo and Terminal F. Dropped insistence on Betacom test-zone-only restriction for competitive evaluations. |
| **H6** | Brand Presence: "DFW 5G — Powered by AT&T" on passenger captive portal. AT&T referenced in tenant service agreements and DFW technology marketing. No branding on analytics products, advertising platform, or tenant-facing dashboards. No branding in secured operational zones. | **Revised to CRO's HARD scope** — narrower than Round 2 but sufficient |

### SOFT Constraints (Final)

| ID | Constraint | Status |
|----|-----------|--------|
| **S1** | Data Monetization: 0% AT&T share. DFW owns 100%. AT&T retains operational telemetry for network management and anonymized benchmarking. | **Conceded fully** — traded for carrier offload share |
| **S2** | Exclusivity: 60 months with Month 36 performance review. Terminal F competitive evaluation accepted. | **Revised down from 84 months** |
| **S3** | Technology Refresh: AT&T funds carrier-layer 5G-Advanced upgrade at Year 5 as part of binding renegotiation. | **Accepted as commitment** |
| **S4** | Terminal F: Competitive evaluation accepted with 30% track record weight and 18-month advance document access. | **Revised — accepted competition** |
| **S5** | Priority Hierarchy: AT&T implements DFW's policy. Technical advisory authority with written override transfers SLA liability when AT&T recommendation overridden. | **Maintained** |
| **S6** | Concession milestone bonus: $150K if POS general availability achieved by Month 24. | **New** |

---

## 5. UPDATED OFFERS

AT&T's comprehensive value package for this deal:

| Offer | Commitment | Value to DFW |
|-------|-----------|-------------|
| **Carrier offload revenue guarantee** | $500K Y1, $750K Y2, $1M Y3-5 — binding with shortfall payments | $4M+ committed revenue over 5 years |
| **CBRS 2.0 migration** | Full transition at zero DFW cost | 15-25% more usable capacity → directly increases monetizable spectrum |
| **Enterprise SLA framework** | 99.99% safety-critical, 99.95% operational, 99.9% commercial, 100% DPA compliance | Carrier-grade reliability no neutral-host startup can match |
| **CWP performance bond** | $250K forfeited if CWP degrades during buildout | Financial backstop for transition risk |
| **Federal compliance partnership** | 4-hour emergency directive response, SIEM integration, NIST SP 800-187 | Technical compliance partner for TSA/DHS/CISA |
| **Warm-standby transition documentation** | Quarterly-updated escrow repository, 180-day transition assistance | Eliminates vendor lock-in concern |
| **Parallel CWP operation** | 4 FTE dedicated transition team, weekly RF assessments, real-time monitoring | Zero-disruption buildout guarantee |
| **Cargo zone proposal** | Full proposal within 30 days of RFP, including indoor warehouse design and automation-grade SLAs | Competitive offering for $15M co-investment opportunity |
| **2 FTE onboarding resources** | 18-month dedicated tenant onboarding support | Accelerates adoption velocity — the CRO's biggest variable |
| **Marketing/reference rights exchange** | Case studies, conferences, site visits — mutual value | Justifies AT&T internal investment approval; gives DFW industry visibility |

---

## 6. WHAT I NEED FROM EACH PEER TO CLOSE

**From the CIO:**
1. Confirmation that the CWP investment credit mechanism (asset purchase at FMV + balance credited against co-investment obligation) satisfies the infrastructure ownership requirement
2. Decision on Betacom competitive process vs. sole-source for cargo zone — this is the last open architectural question
3. Phase 0 authorization for cargo passive infrastructure within 45 days

**From the CRO:**
1. Confirmation that AT&T's CWP investment credit satisfies the $5M co-investment soft constraint
2. Acceptance of the revenue share structure as proposed (CRO's own soft constraint numbers — we're accepting them)
3. Inclusion of AT&T's binding carrier offload commitments as "committed revenue" for board approval package

**From the Airline VP:**
1. Confirmation that the pre-authorized construction framework resolves the Terminal C timeline concern
2. Your Terminal F application-level performance specs (sub-20ms, 50 Mbps/gate, zero-loss handoff) as measurable acceptance criteria — AT&T will include these in our competitive evaluation proposal
3. The 5-year anchor tenant commitment executed as a binding term sheet element

**From the Cargo Director:**
1. Acceptance of the competitive cargo zone carrier-operations evaluation (AT&T, Betacom, others)
2. Confirmation that Phase 0 passive infrastructure proceeds immediately regardless of carrier-operations vendor selection
3. Your general contractors' contact information within 10 business days for RF design coordination

**From the Concessions Consortium:**
1. Acceptance of Tier 4 priority with guaranteed 5/2 Mbps floor (normal) and 1 Mbps floor (degraded) — this delivers the "never dark" guarantee at the bandwidth levels POS transactions actually require
2. Confirmation of the 200+ location migration commitment as a binding term sheet element
3. Star Concessions pilot timeline for Phase 1 integration

---

## Closing

Three rounds in, and here is where AT&T stands:
