- **[Airline-V2] Airline Pricing Structure is Dangerously Underspecified Relative to $8-12M Terminal F Commitment**: Partially Resolved — The airline VP is correct: airline pricing is materially less specified than concession or cargo pricing, and the Terminal F prepaid access structure lacks basic reversion protections. My own concession pricing is fully defined — $175-200/month, all-inclusive, month-to-month, CPI/3% cap, most-favored-tenant, 10-day activation, zero CapEx. The cargo consortium has $0.25/sq ft/month with explicit SLAs and 5-year binding terms. The airline has 'Hybrid base + per-departure variable (base ≤60% of expected annual cost)' with undefined rates, undefined escalation, undefined reversion, and no exit flexibility. This asymmetry is problematic for two reasons: (1) The airline's LOI is a binding constraint for Board approval per HC-12 and H-R14 — if airline pricing remains underspecified, the LOI cannot convert to a binding commitment, and the Board lacks the committed revenue assurance it requires. (2) The airline is 80%+ of departures — if the airline walks over pricing, the entire deal loses its most critical stakeholder. The CRO's proposed modifications are comprehensive: $35/departure cap with CPI/3% escalation, 10-year amortization with refund on early termination, traffic reduction protection, symmetric Year 7 exit, airline Year 5 renegotiation right. These are all reasonable and necessary. From my concessions perspective: I need this deal to close. My 200+ locations need POS connectivity by Month 24. The airline's commitment provides the revenue anchor that sustains the network. If airline pricing negotiations stall, the entire deployment timeline is at risk. The CRO should resolve airline pricing terms to the same specificity as concessions and cargo BEFORE Board approval.
- **[Airline-V3] Terminal C Multi-Year Construction Creates Rolling CWP Degradation Trap**: Partially Resolved — The airline correctly identifies that Terminal C's 3-year $3B rebuild creates an RF environment where construction-caused and 5G-caused CWP degradation will be difficult to distinguish. The CRO's proposed modifications — dynamic monthly RF baseline, construction-phase RF sensors, pre-modeled structural RF impact assessments, and construction-adjusted degradation triggers — are technically sound and supported by DFW Terminal D precedent. The Cargo Director's point that construction-caused CWP degradation exists independently of the 5G deployment is valid — this is not a risk created by the consensus; it is a pre-existing risk that the consensus marginally compounds. From my concessions perspective: Terminal C has concession locations. My operators in Terminal C need functioning POS connectivity during the 3-year rebuild, whether that connectivity comes from CWP or 5G. The accelerated deployment of 5G in rebuilt Terminal C sections actually HELPS my operators by providing modern connectivity sooner rather than depending on aging CWP in an actively changing RF environment. The real risk is the transition period where neither CWP nor 5G is reliable in construction-adjacent areas. The 72-hour halt mechanism for Terminal C should use the dynamic baseline as the CRO proposes — measuring degradation against post-construction-change expected performance, not the pre-construction ideal. This eliminates false-positive halts from structural RF changes while preserving genuine 5G-transition protections.
- **[Airline-V4] 99.99% Per-Terminal SLA Masks Sub-Gate Failure Modes in 31-Gate Terminal F**: Partially Resolved — The measurement-granularity argument is valid and the CRO's proposed modifications (per-gate-cluster SLA at 99.95%, application-layer metrics, edge compute N+1 redundancy, $10K per gate-cluster per degradation event) are well-designed. The Cargo Director's experience with per-warehouse-zone SLAs provides direct architectural precedent. From my concessions perspective, I have the granularity I need — my SLA is already 99.9% per LOCATION, measured at the CPE gateway. Every one of my 200+ locations has an independent SLA measurement point. The airline's per-terminal measurement is coarser than what I negotiated, and the airline should bring its measurement to comparable granularity. The edge compute memory leak scenario is a real failure mode. My CPE gateways could also be affected by an edge compute failure if multiple concession locations share an edge compute node — but my per-location measurement at the CPE would detect the degradation even if a terminal-level metric did not. I support the airline's modification because better SLA granularity for all tenants improves overall network accountability, which benefits my operators.
- **[Airline-V5] Terminal F Competitive Carrier-Operations Evaluation Creates Operational Risk During Greenfield Launch**: Partially Resolved — The CRO's architectural defense is technically correct: DFW owns all infrastructure below the carrier-operations API layer, including SDN controllers and slice management. The airline's slice policy is enforced at the DFW infrastructure layer regardless of which vendor manages radio resources in a given terminal. 3GPP Release 17 defines slice continuity as a core-network function. In principle, a bag tug crossing from Terminal C to Terminal F should maintain session continuity because the same DFW-owned SDN manages the handoff. However, the attack raises a legitimate concern about practical implementation. Multi-vendor RAN environments at the O-RAN specification level are still maturing. Real-world inter-vendor handoff at sub-500ms with session continuity for ramp automation applications is unproven at airport scale. The JFK Terminal 4 and LAX Tom Bradley examples cited are DAS deployments, not private 5G sliced networks — the comparison is architecturally imprecise. From my concessions perspective: my POS locations do not move between terminals, so inter-terminal handoff is irrelevant to my operators' daily experience. But if inter-terminal handoff failures undermine airline confidence, the airline could resist the Terminal F competitive evaluation, which could delay Terminal F deployment and the revenue model supporting my ACDBE pricing subsidies. I support specifying inter-operator handoff requirements to remove this as a blocker.

## Board-Ready Recommendation
**Plan Strength Score**: 7/10
**Go/No-Go**: Conditional Go
**Conditions:**
  - DPA Emergency Capacity Protocol drafted and incorporated as Exhibit A-1 to the MSA, including graduated capacity thresholds, proportional allocation rules, bright-line FCC compliance carve-out preventing any DFW override requiring AT&T to violate Part 96, and binding design specification requiring the network to deliver all degradation-mode minimums under sustained DPA affecting 60% of CBRS channels
  - Licensed-spectrum failover infrastructure ($4-5M) committed as DFW CapEx with deployment by Phase 2 completion (Month 21), providing DPA-immune backup for Tier 0-1 ramp operations and additional residual CBRS capacity for Tier 4 POS during DPA events
  - Airline pricing term sheet achieving concession/cargo-equivalent specificity (defined per-departure rate with cap and escalation, Terminal F prepaid amortization with pro-rata refund mechanism, symmetric Year 7 exit right, defined 'expected annual cost' methodology) executed within 60 days of contract signing as a condition precedent to Board approval
  - CWP degradation attribution methodology framework incorporated as an MSA exhibit at contract execution, including principles of temporal correlation, proximate 5G activity requirement, passenger-volume normalization, seasonal adjustment, independent RF adjudicator pre-selection criteria, and explicit limitation that the 200 bps penalty accrues only after joint root-cause attribution concludes AT&T causation
  - Inter-operator handoff SLA (≤500ms, ≤0.1% failure rate) and mandatory 60-day interoperability testing period incorporated as binding acceptance criteria for any non-incumbent Terminal F carrier-operations award, with single-SAS-provider mandate across all terminals and explicit fallback to incumbent if no alternative vendor meets handoff requirements
  - Per-gate-cluster SLA (99.95%, 4-6 gate clusters per terminal) and edge compute N+1 redundancy incorporated as supplementary SLA tier in Exhibit A, with application-layer synthetic monitoring metrics defined at the network boundary
  - Concession deployment timeline (Phase 1 pilot Months 3-9, Phase 2 GA Month 24) contractually independent of airline LOI execution status — concession operators cannot be held hostage to airline pricing negotiations

The DFW Private 5G Consensus Framework represents a well-structured hybrid co-investment model that delivers measurable value across all stakeholder categories. For the F&B concessions consortium — 60 operators, 200+ locations, $4.2B gross product — the consensus delivers exactly what our operators need: turnkey managed POS connectivity at $175-200/month all-inclusive with zero CapEx, a single DFW-accountable support contact, 99.9% per-location SLA with automatic credits, support for existing POS hardware (Aloha, Toast, Square) without replacement, and ACDBE pricing at $80/month ensuring small business operators are not priced out of reliable connectivity. Month-to-month terms with CPI/3% escalation caps protect operators from technology cost inflation, and the 10-day new-location activation supports our 34+ new locations through 2028. These terms are superior to any current Wi-Fi or cellular alternative available to our operators.

The red team stress test identified ten vulnerabilities — two are fully resolved, eight require specific plan modifications before contract execution. The most critical modifications are: (1) a DPA Emergency Capacity Protocol ensuring AT&T's FCC compliance obligations are never compromised by DFW governance directives, with the network designed to deliver degradation-mode minimums under worst-case sustained DPA scenarios; (2) licensed-spectrum failover infrastructure at $4-5M providing DPA-immune backup for safety-critical and airline ramp operations; and (3) airline pricing specificity achieving parity with the concession and cargo rate cards before Board approval. None of these modifications require restructuring the deal — they are targeted amendments that close identified gaps within the existing framework.

The Board should approve this framework as a Conditional Go. The seven specified conditions represent contractual language requirements achievable within 60 days — not fundamental redesign. The committed revenue base (cargo $6M/year binding, concessions 200+ locations binding, AT&T offload floors binding) exceeds the $2M net annual savings threshold on committed demand alone, before airline pricing is finalized. The phased deployment with tested rollback at each gate, 90-day parallel operations per concession location, and blackout windows protecting peak revenue periods ensure that operational risk is contained. For our concessions consortium specifically, I recommend Board approval with the explicit condition that the concession deployment timeline (Phase 1 pilot Months 3-9, Phase 2 general availability Month 24) is contractually independent of airline pricing negotiations — our 200+ locations and 60 operators cannot afford to have their connectivity modernization held hostage to a bilateral negotiation between DFW and its anchor airline.

reasoning.red_rationale:
AT&T Carrier Rep has the most significant residual leverage points and structural vulnerabilities in the consensus: (1) Revenue share heavily favors DFW (60/40 declining to 80/20), creating long-term margin pressure; (2) AT&T bears 100% CWP remediation costs and $250K performance bond with no offsetting risk caps; (3) Carrier-operations exclusivity expires at Year 5 with unfavorable competitive evaluation criteria (incumbent gets 30% weight vs. 40% for technical capability—a structural disadvantage); (4) Terminal F triggers full competitive evaluation, risking permanent loss of largest greenfield revenue stream; (5) No commercial rights to DFW data (0% of data monetization), unlike other co-investors; (6) CBRS 2.0 migration costs at AT&T expense; (7) Technology refresh (Year 5) funded by AT&T, not shared; (8) Carrier offload floors ($500K-$1M) are binding minimums with downside revenue share renegotiation if missed. AT&T should stress-test whether revenue floors are achievable, whether competitive evaluation process is genuinely fair, and whether branding restrictions create commercial disadvantage.

Anchor Airline VP has residual operational/governance vulnerabilities despite strong constraint satisfaction: (1) Priority hierarchy changes require airline consent but are not enforcement-backed—if DFW/cargo invoke emergency degradation, airline has 30-second notification and post-event notification only, with no contractual right to reverse a degradation decision; (2) 30-second automatic failover is operationally sound but creates dependency on dual-connectivity (Wi-Fi + cellular)—if cellular backup degrades, airline has no fallback; (3) Tier 1 priority guarantees 'full allocation seconds 30-300' but the definition of 'full' is ambiguous if DPA response coincides with capacity constraint (AT&T's 250ms DPA response priority vs. airline's seconds 30-300 guarantee)—potential conflict unresolved; (4) Airline slice configuration authority does not include capacity arbitration—DFW can repriorityize away from airline slice during degradation if cargo triggers 'guaranteed minimum' floor interpretation; (5) Terminal F co-design authority is conditional on DFW *not* guaranteeing performance specs (sub-20ms latency, 50 Mbps, zero-loss handoff)—if DFW guarantees specs and meets them, airline gets no co-design seat; if DFW guarantees and fails, dispute resolution mechanism is binding arbitration (90 days), not immediate reinstatement. Airline should stress-test priority hierarchy enforcement, degradation decision-making authority, and Terminal F performance guarantees.
reasoning.blue_rationale:
Airport CRO has maximum constraint satisfaction and lowest residual risk: All 14 HARD constraints marked ✅ SATISFIED. CRO's core value drivers are all locked: (1) 100% DFW infrastructure ownership with clean title below carrier-operations API (constraint H-R1, HC-2); (2) Data monetization exclusively DFW with explicit carve-outs for airline/cargo/concession operational data (H-R10, explicit Tier 1 definition); (3) Three independent revenue streams with no cross-subsidization (H-R9, H-R6); (4) Concession cutover blackouts (H-R5), 90-day parallel operations, and 4-hour rollback (H-R6); (5) Committed revenue contracts before board approval ($15M cargo prepay, $6M/year minimum, AT&T offload floors—H-R14); (6) Minimum $2M annual savings by Year 2 (H-R4); (7) Phased deployment with cargo parallel track and regulatory authorization separation (H-R13, HC-6); (8) Pre-authorized construction framework preventing CWP delays (H-R11); (9) AT&T branding limited (H-R11); (10) Security and priority hierarchy contractually sovereign (HC-3, HC-8); (11) Per-terminal/per-location SLA measurement with documented performance bonds (HC-9); (12) 200 bps penalty mechanism for AT&T delays (H-R3, well-defined attribution process). CRO benefits from AT&T's strong commitment (CBRS 2.0 funding, co-investment, transition assistance, 4 FTE CWP team) with limited reciprocal obligation beyond revenue share (which CRO controls). CRO's 18-month Year 7 exit and Year 5 renegotiation rights preserve flexibility. CRO should defend the consensus as comprehensive, risk-mitigated, and beneficial to DFW.

Cargo Director has maximum constraint satisfaction and strongest structural positioning: All 6 HARD constraints marked ✅ SATISFIED, plus 6 SOFT constraints all met. Cargo has unlocked: (1) Independent parallel deployment track (Phase 0 passive in 45 days, Phase 0.5 active Q1-Q2 2026) with no dependency on other terminals (HC-1); (2) $15M prepaid infrastructure investment by DFW with consortium (HC-6); (3) 6 anchor tenants with $6M/year minimum committed revenue on binding 5-year contracts (HC-6); (4) $0.25/sq ft/month pricing all-inclusive with automation-grade SLAs (HC-5); (5) Interim AT&T carrier operations + parallel competitive evaluation process that allows vendor diversification (HC-1); (6) Tier 3 priority hierarchy with guaranteed minimum capacity allocation and degradation alerts (HC-3, time-sequenced degradation specifies AGV ≤50ms 30-300s and ≤75ms sustained); (7) Cold chain regulatory compliance with operator-exclusive control and independent audit pathways (HC-4, Tier 3 data classification); (8) Consortium-funded passive infrastructure ($200-400K) saving $1.2-1.8M vs. retrofit (SOFT S1); (9) Governance seat and Tier 3 consent requirement for hierarchy changes (SOFT S3); (10) Performance data sharing with all stakeholders pre-terminal deployment (SOFT S5). Cargo's positioning is exceptionally strong—independently funded, independently timed, independently operated, with binding revenue commitments and SLA guarantees. Cargo should defend the consensus as enabling cargo-first proof-of-concept with minimal execution risk.
reasoning.white_rationale: Concessions Tech Lead is the most balanced and neutral stakeholder: (1) All 8 HARD constraints are marked ✅ SATISFIED, indicating full operational/technical alignment; (2) However, concessions holds ZERO structural power in the consensus—it is not a primary revenue stream (tenant access fees, not concession operator fees, generate DFW/AT&T splits), not a capital co-investor, not a governance seat, not a strategic priority lever (unlike cargo's $15M prepay or airline's $8-12M co-investment); (3) Concessions' value is purely operational utility—turnkey managed service, predictable pricing, SLA reliability—which is fully delivered but offers no leverage in cross-stakeholder disputes; (4) Concessions has no visibility into revenue share negotiations, priority hierarchy enforcement mechanisms, or AT&T competitive evaluation fairness (all AT&T/CRO/cargo domains); (5) Concessions' constraints are entirely within DFW/AT&T operational scope (not governance or strategic), making it a implementer rather than negotiator; (6) Concessions Tech Lead can objectively evaluate whether other teams are using infrastructure/priority hierarchy /rollback rights in ways that harm concession POS reliability (the one area where concessions has legitimate interest); (7) Concessions holds the least stake in AT&T's long-term positioning, DFW's revenue model, airline's governance rights, or cargo's timeline—making it naturally neutral. White Team role: adjudicate whether Red Team objections (AT&T margin compression, airline governance ambiguity) or Blue Team defenses (CRO/cargo risk mitigation, independent audit rights) are technically sound, and whether the consensus infrastructure can support all three revenue streams simultaneously without cross-interference.

vulnerabilities.title: Carrier Offload Guarantee Creates Unhedgeable Demand Risk for AT&T
vulnerabilities.description: AT&T is contractually obligated to guarantee $500K (Year 1), $750K (Year 2), and $1M/year (Years 3-5) in carrier offload revenue to DFW — with shortfall payments due if missed and DFW gaining the right to renegotiate the offload revenue share downward. However, AT&T has no contractual mechanism to compel T-Mobile, Verizon, or DISH to purchase CBRS offload capacity. These are sovereign commercial decisions by AT&T's direct competitors. T-Mobile has aggressively built out mid-band n41 across DFW metro and may have no offload need. Verizon is deploying mmWave in high-density venues independently. DISH/EchoStar is in financial distress and unlikely to sign offload agreements at any price. AT&T is guaranteeing revenue from customers it cannot control, on infrastructure it does not own, while the penalty clause (downward share renegotiation) compounds the exposure. The $1M/year floor in Years 3-5 assumes a carrier offload market that may not materialize at DFW specifically because competing carriers are building their own in-venue solutions post-Boingo/DigitalBridge acquisition. AT&T could face $3-5M in cumulative shortfall payments over the contract while simultaneously losing offload share percentage — effectively paying DFW for the privilege of failing to sell a product AT&T's competitors refuse to buy.
vulnerabilities.failure_scenario: By Year 3, T-Mobile has deployed its own neutral-host DAS in DFW terminals under a separate airport agreement (DFW has historically maintained multiple wireless infrastructure contracts). Verizon declines offload citing adequate macro coverage from cell sites along International Parkway. DISH never reaches commercial scale. AT&T owes DFW $1M shortfall, DFW invokes the renegotiation clause to reduce AT&T's offload share from 30% to 20%, and AT&T's CRO now faces a board question about why AT&T is subsidizing a competitor's (Betacom's) pathway into a venue where AT&T invested $10M. The guaranteed minimums become a perverse incentive for DFW to let AT&T fail at offload sales because the shortfall payment plus renegotiation leverage is more valuable than actual offload revenue.

vulnerabilities.title: Parallel Cargo Competitive Evaluation Is a Trojan Horse for Full Displacement
vulnerabilities.description: The cargo zone deployment structure creates a uniquely dangerous precedent. AT&T operates as 'interim carrier-operations partner' for Phase 0.5 while a parallel competitive evaluation runs from Day 1 through Day 150. Betacom already has trial nodes physically installed at DFW — meaning they have RF propagation data, interference profiles, and operational familiarity that neutralizes AT&T's incumbent advantage. The evaluation weighting (Technical 40%, SLA Commitments 20%, Track Record 30%, Cost 10%) appears favorable to AT&T with 30% for incumbent track record, but in practice Betacom can claim DFW-specific track record from their trial nodes. More critically, the cargo zone is the highest-value greenfield deployment ($15M prepaid, $6M/year committed) and serves as the reference architecture for Terminal F's competitive evaluation in Phase 3. If Betacom wins cargo operations, they will have 18-24 months of production operational data at DFW before the Terminal F evaluation — at which point AT&T's 'incumbent track record' advantage evaporates entirely. The cargo zone evaluation is not about cargo; it is a $15M audition that determines whether AT&T retains any carrier-operations role beyond the legacy CWP footprint.
vulnerabilities.failure_scenario: Betacom wins the cargo evaluation at Day 150 by undercutting AT&T on cost (10% weight but enough to tip) and presenting automation-grade SLA commitments that AT&T's enterprise division — accustomed to macro network SLAs, not per-warehouse-zone industrial IoT — cannot match with the same specificity. AT&T transitions out of cargo in 90 days. By Phase 3, Betacom has 18 months of flawless cargo operations data showing 99.99% uptime across 6 warehouses, sub-20ms AGV latency, and zero cold chain excursions. In the Terminal F competitive evaluation, Betacom presents this DFW-native data against AT&T's generic enterprise portfolio. Betacom wins Terminal F. AT&T is now confined to legacy terminal carrier operations on a declining revenue share (30% → 20%) for an aging CWP deployment that DFW already purchased for $3.5-5M and could technically operate without AT&T after the 60-month exclusivity expires.

vulnerabilities.title: AT&T Capital Obligations Compound While Revenue Share Declines to Subsistence Levels
vulnerabilities.description: The financial structure requires AT&T to fund three major capital obligations — CBRS 2.0 migration (estimated $2-4M based on 1000 AP re-certification and firmware upgrades at the 85% DoD reduction threshold), Year 5 5G-Advanced technology refresh (estimated $5-8M for carrier-layer upgrade across expanding campus), and 2 FTE dedicated onboarding for 18 months (~$600K loaded cost) — while its revenue share declines from 40% to 20% on tenant access and from 45% to 30% on carrier offload. AT&T also posts a $250K performance bond and bears 100% of CWP remediation costs. The CWP asset purchase at $3.5-5M FMV means AT&T recovers less than half its $10M investment in cash, with the $5-6.5M 'credit' converting into a further spending obligation (edge compute/MEC co-investment). AT&T's total capital commitment over 10 years could reach $18-23M against a declining share of revenue streams where DFW captures 100% of the highest-growth category (data monetization). By Year 8, AT&T is earning 20% of tenant access on infrastructure AT&T partially funded, 30% of carrier offload AT&T must guarantee minimums on, and 0% of data monetization that AT&T's operational telemetry enables. The Year 5 binding renegotiation creates additional downward pressure — DFW will arrive at that table with AT&T's own performance data showing the network runs on DFW-owned infrastructure and could be operated by an alternative carrier partner.
vulnerabilities.failure_scenario: At the Year 5 renegotiation, DFW presents AT&T with a stark choice: accept further revenue share reductions (to 15% tenant / 20% offload) or DFW exercises the Year 7 no-fault exit. AT&T has now spent $18M+ across CWP, CBRS 2.0, tech refresh, and FTE support, recovering perhaps $8-10M in cumulative revenue share. The Boingo/DigitalBridge $854M exit has proven the venue-wireless model's value, and DFW's Board asks why they're sharing any revenue with a carrier partner when the entire infrastructure is DFW-owned and competitors like Celona offer carrier-operations-as-a-service at lower cost. AT&T's enterprise sales team can no longer credibly cite DFW as a flagship reference because AT&T's role has been reduced to a managed service commodity.

vulnerabilities.title: DPA Liability Trap — AT&T Bears FCC Compliance Risk Under Governance It Cannot Control
vulnerabilities.description: AT&T holds 'sole real-time authority' for DPA response at sub-250ms — meaning AT&T is the entity registered with Federated Wireless SAS and bears FCC Part 96 compliance liability for the DFW CBRS deployment. However, the priority hierarchy (Exhibit A) is a binding technical specification that AT&T cannot modify without written consent of all Tier 0 and Tier 1 tenants. The 'technical advisory authority' mechanism allows AT&T to flag compliance risks, but DFW can issue a 'written override assuming SLA liability for resulting performance impact.' This sounds protective, but FCC enforcement actions target the SAS registrant and licensee — not the building owner's contractual liability allocation. If DFW's priority hierarchy creates a scenario where Tier 0/1/2/3 guaranteed bandwidth floors consume all available CBRS capacity during a DPA event (Navy radar operations at NAS Fort Worth Joint Reserve Base are 15 miles from DFW and trigger DPA events affecting the entire campus), AT&T faces a choice: violate the binding priority hierarchy or violate FCC Part 96. The 'written override' mechanism does not shield AT&T from FCC enforcement, spectrum revocation, or SAS de-registration. With 340,000+ APs in Federated Wireless's SAS network, an AT&T compliance failure at DFW could trigger review of AT&T's entire CBRS portfolio.
vulnerabilities.failure_scenario: NAS Fort Worth JRB conducts extended naval radar exercises requiring sustained DPA across all DFW CBRS channels for 4+ hours during a peak travel day (87M passengers/year = 238K/day average, 300K+ on peaks). The priority hierarchy demands full allocation for Tiers 0-3 (safety, airline ramp, federal, cargo automation), but available CBRS capacity after DPA channel evacuation can only serve 60% of guaranteed minimums. AT&T's technical advisory goes to DFW recommending proportional reduction across all tiers. DFW issues written override maintaining Tier 0-3 at full allocation, pushing AT&T to squeeze Tiers 4-7 below physically achievable levels. AT&T's SAS coordination logs show non-compliant power levels on remaining channels as the system attempts to serve guaranteed minimums. Federated Wireless flags the anomaly. The FCC opens an inquiry. AT&T's $10M DFW investment now threatens AT&T's CBRS licensing posture across its entire enterprise portfolio. DFW's contractual liability assumption is commercially meaningless against federal spectrum enforcement.

vulnerabilities.title: CWP Degradation Halt Authority Creates Asymmetric Schedule Weapon Against AT&T
vulnerabilities.description: Section 1.7 specifies that any 'CWP degradation event attributable to 5G transition' triggers an automatic 72-hour halt in the affected terminal zone, and two such events within 30 days trigger a full project pause requiring CIO written sign-off. AT&T bears 100% of CWP remediation costs. This sounds reasonable as transition protection, but the attribution mechanism is undefined — there is no specified methodology for determining whether a CWP performance dip is caused by 5G transition activities, normal RF environment changes (87M passengers carrying billions of device-hours of Wi-Fi and cellular interference annually), seasonal propagation variation, or airline equipment changes. The 90-day CWP baseline established in Phase 0 captures a single season's RF profile; DFW's RF environment shifts dramatically between a February Tuesday (180K passengers) and a July Friday (320K passengers). Any party — particularly a competitor like Betacom observing from their trial nodes — could identify correlation-not-causation CWP dips coinciding with AT&T's 5G buildout and trigger halts. AT&T's own CWP deployment becomes the mechanism by which AT&T's expansion is blocked. Combined with the 200 bps revenue share penalty for AT&T-attributable delays (H-R3), every 72-hour halt costs AT&T both schedule position and permanent revenue share points.
vulnerabilities.failure_scenario: During Phase 1 Terminal A validation, AT&T energizes new small cells 55 meters from existing CWP APs (outside the 50-meter buffer). A passenger surge during Thanksgiving week (excluded from cutover but not from testing) causes CWP throughput to dip 0.02% below baseline in Terminal A. The dip is actually caused by 15% more devices competing for airtime, but correlation with the small cell energization triggers the automatic 72-hour halt. AT&T remediates, re-energizes in December (post-blackout), and a second dip occurs during a Cowboys playoff game weekend with unusually high device density. Full project pause. CIO sign-off takes 3 weeks during holiday staffing. AT&T loses a full quarter of Phase 1 timeline. CRO invokes H-R3: 200 bps revenue share penalty applied. AT&T's Year 1-3 tenant access share drops from 40% to 38%, permanently. The CWP system AT&T built and maintains is now the contractual tripwire that erodes AT&T's economics.

vulnerabilities.title: DPA + Ground-Stop Release Creates Unrecoverable Dual-Failure at Peak Ramp Intensity
vulnerabilities.description: NAS Fort Worth Joint Reserve Base sits 6 nautical miles from DFW. A Navy DPA activation can suppress CBRS spectrum campus-wide across all terminals simultaneously. The consensus degradation model maintains 'full allocation' for Tier 1 through all time windows — but 'full allocation' is a percentage of available capacity, not an absolute throughput guarantee. The 50 Mbps/gate floor is a normal-operations guarantee; nowhere in the degradation model is it restated as a hard floor during DPA events. During a sustained DPA that reduces available CBRS capacity by 40-60% across the entire campus, my 'full allocation' of a dramatically smaller pool could deliver 20-30 Mbps/gate — below the threshold for reliable ramp tablet operations, baggage system telemetry, and crew comms simultaneously. Now compound this: DFW averages 25+ ground stops per year from severe convective weather. When a ground stop lifts, we push 50-80 aircraft in a 20-minute burst. That is the single highest-intensity moment for ramp communications — every gate coordinator, every bag runner, every crew chief is on their iPad. The 30-second cellular failover is the backstop, but during a ground-stop release at DFW, AT&T/T-Mobile/Verizon macro networks are simultaneously absorbing 200,000+ passengers reconnecting, streaming, and calling. Macro cellular is not guaranteed capacity — it is best-effort, and it will be saturated at the exact moment I need it. The consensus treats DPA response and cellular failover as independent, complementary systems (H12). They are correlated failure modes at DFW.
vulnerabilities.failure_scenario: July 2028, 4:47 PM. NAS JRB triggers DPA during an active ground stop. CBRS capacity drops 55% campus-wide. At 5:02 PM the ground stop lifts. 63 aircraft begin simultaneous pushback across Terminals A, B, C, and the new Terminal F. Ramp tablets on the degraded private 5G slice experience 350ms+ latency and intermittent packet loss. Failover triggers fire — 800+ iPads attempt cellular handoff simultaneously. AT&T macro cells serving DFW are at 94% utilization from passenger traffic. Failover connections establish at degraded throughput. 14 pushbacks lose real-time baggage reconciliation confirmation. 6 aircraft push without confirmed bag counts — 3 are held on the taxiway for manual recount. Gate turnaround cascades delay 41 subsequent departures. Conservative cost: $74/min × 41 flights × average 12 minutes = $36,408 per flight = $1.49M in a single evening. The SLA credits me $50K per terminal — maybe $200K total. My actual loss exceeds the contractual remedy by 7x.

vulnerabilities.title: Airline Pricing Structure is Dangerously Underspecified Relative to $8-12M Terminal F Commitment
vulnerabilities.description: The consensus defines airline pricing as 'Hybrid base + per-departure variable (base ≤60% of expected annual cost)' with a 5-year minimum term. For the anchor airline at 80%+ of departures — roughly 600-700 daily departures, 220,000+ annually — the per-departure variable component is completely undefined. No rate. No cap. No methodology for calculating 'expected annual cost.' No escalation limit specific to the airline tier (concessions get CPI/3% caps — airlines get nothing). Terminal F alone requires $8-12M as 'prepaid access fees' tagged as co-investment, but the consensus does not define what happens to that prepaid capital if the airline reduces Terminal F operations, if DFW reassigns gates, or if the airline invokes rollback rights after the 90-day remediation period. Furthermore, DFW has a Year 7 no-fault exit option — the airline has no equivalent. We have a 5-year minimum with no early termination right beyond performance-triggered remedies. If DFW terminates at Year 7 and rearchitects the network, my $8-12M in 'prepaid access fees' for Terminal F infrastructure that DFW owns outright could be stranded. The 'airline-tagged in asset registry' language confers zero legal ownership rights. I am being asked to fund someone else's capital asset with no reversion rights and no defined return mechanism.
vulnerabilities.failure_scenario: Year 3: DFW calculates 'expected annual cost' at $18M for airline network services based on an aggressive traffic forecast. Base component locks at $10.8M. Per-departure variable is set at $32.70/departure. Traffic dips 8% in Year 4 due to a network realignment — but the base component (60%) is fixed. We are now paying $10.8M base + variable on fewer flights, meaning effective per-departure cost rises to $41+. Meanwhile, the $10M prepaid for Terminal F is fully consumed against service credits with no refund mechanism. DFW invokes Year 5 renegotiation seeking higher rates citing inflation. We have no leverage because our 5-year minimum has 2 years remaining and no exit clause. Total 5-year airline network cost: $85-95M with no ownership equity, no exit flexibility, and SLA credits capped at a fraction of operational losses.

vulnerabilities.title: Terminal C Multi-Year Construction Creates Rolling CWP Degradation Trap
vulnerabilities.description: Terminal C is undergoing a $3B rebuild through 2028. The CWP transition protection framework specifies: 50m buffer zones around CWP APs for new RF energization (30-day review), 72-hour automatic halts when CWP degradation is attributed to 5G transition activities, and a full project pause with CIO sign-off after two degradation events in 30 days in any terminal. Terminal C is where active construction AND active CWP-dependent ramp operations coexist for 3+ years. Construction activities — steel erection, crane movements, temporary wall installations, progressive demolition — continuously alter RF propagation characteristics. Every structural change is a potential CWP degradation event that must be distinguished from 5G-transition-caused degradation. The consensus provides no methodology for attribution between construction-caused and 5G-caused RF changes. This ambiguity creates two failure modes: (1) Conservative interpretation triggers repeated 72-hour halts that delay Phase 2 5G deployment in Terminal C by 12-18 months, leaving my ramp operations on aging CWP infrastructure longer than planned; (2) Aggressive interpretation pushes through degradation events without halts, and my ramp iPads in Terminal C experience chronic connectivity issues during the construction period. The 'pre-authorized construction framework' developed during Phase 0 was designed for 5G buildout activities, not for the interaction between ongoing building construction and RF infrastructure. The airline has a seat at the table for framework development, but the framework cannot anticipate every structural change in a 3-year, $3B demolish-and-rebuild project.
vulnerabilities.failure_scenario: Month 14, Terminal C. Phase 2 5G integration begins in rebuilt Section C-West while demolition continues in Section C-East. Steel framing for the new C-East concourse creates multipath interference that degrades CWP signal in 8 gate areas by 3-4 dBm. AT&T attributes it to construction, not 5G. DFW agrees. No 72-hour halt triggered. Two weeks later, Phase 2 small cells energize in C-West. CWP degrades further in the transition zone between old and new construction. Now both factors are in play. AT&T says it's construction. My ramp team says their iPads are dropping connections at Gates C21-C28 during morning bank. Root cause attribution takes 11 days. During those 11 days, 47 flights experience ramp communication disruptions. We deploy manual radio backups but lose baggage tracking automation. No SLA credit is issued because CWP degradation wasn't formally attributed to 5G transition. The consensus protects me from 5G-caused degradation — it doesn't protect me from construction-caused degradation to the network I depend on.

vulnerabilities.title: 99.99% Per-Terminal SLA Masks Sub-Gate Failure Modes in 31-Gate Terminal F
vulnerabilities.description: The SLA is measured at 99.99% per terminal per month. Terminal F will have 31 gates. 99.99% uptime means 4.3 minutes of downtime per month — measured across the entire terminal. The consensus specifies '50 Mbps sustained per gate area' as a bandwidth guarantee and 'synthetic monitoring on 50+ devices/terminal' at '15-second polling.' But the SLA math creates a dangerous measurement gap. If 3 gates in Terminal F experience 45 minutes of degraded service (below 20ms latency threshold) in a month due to a localized edge compute failure or small-cell cluster issue, but the other 28 gates are at 100%, the terminal-level aggregate may still show 99.99% — because per-terminal measurement averages across all gates. The $50K/terminal/incident penalty triggers per terminal, not per gate. At $74/minute per delayed pushback, 3 gates down for 45 minutes during a banking hour could cost $74 × 45 × 6 affected flights = $19,980 in direct delay costs per incident — but the SLA measurement may not even register it as a breach. The consensus also specifies 'application layer' measurement but doesn't define which application metrics are monitored. Synthetic polling of device connectivity is not the same as measuring whether the Sabre DCS response completes in under 2 seconds or whether the real-time baggage reconciliation feed is current. I can have 'network uptime' while my applications are unusable due to jitter, microbursts, or edge compute latency.
vulnerabilities.failure_scenario: March 2029, Terminal F operational for 4 months. Edge compute node serving Gates F12-F18 experiences a memory leak causing progressive latency degradation from 18ms to 180ms over 90 minutes during the evening banking hour (2000-2200). Synthetic monitoring pings return successfully — the network is 'up.' But real-time baggage reconciliation at those 7 gates times out repeatedly. Gate agents resort to manual counts. 4 flights push 8-12 minutes late each. Terminal F aggregate uptime: 99.997% — well above the 99.99% threshold. No SLA breach. No penalty. No credit. My operations team files a trouble ticket; AT&T Tier 2 identifies the root cause 6 hours later during off-peak. The consensus has no mechanism for application-performance SLAs below the terminal level, and the 'per gate area' bandwidth guarantee doesn't address latency spikes from edge compute failures that don't constitute 'downtime.'

vulnerabilities.title: Terminal F Competitive Carrier-Operations Evaluation Creates Operational Risk During Greenfield Launch
vulnerabilities.description: The consensus specifies that Terminal F 'triggers competitive evaluation' for carrier operations, separate from AT&T's 60-month exclusivity on terminal deployments (Phases 1-4). This means Terminal F could be operated by a different carrier-operations vendor than Terminals A, B, C, and D/E. From an airline operations perspective, I operate 31 gates in Terminal F AND exclusive operations in Terminals A, B, C — potentially under two different carrier-operations regimes. My ramp operations are not terminal-siloed. Aircraft reposition between terminals. Crew members transit between terminals. Baggage connects across terminals. My 800-900 iPads need seamless handoff as ramp agents move between Terminal C (AT&T-operated carrier layer) and Terminal F (potentially Betacom or other vendor). The consensus specifies '≥99.95% handoff success at tug speeds' for Terminal F but says nothing about inter-terminal handoff between different carrier-operations vendors. The slice management API is 'self-service for airline' but if the underlying carrier-operations vendor implements the slice differently in Terminal F than in Terminal A, my single slice policy may behave inconsistently. Device provisioning, QoS enforcement, and radio resource scheduling could all differ. This is not a theoretical concern — this is asking a single airline with unified operations to accept a split-brain network architecture across its own operation.
vulnerabilities.failure_scenario: Year 3, Terminal F goes live with Betacom as carrier-operations vendor after competitive evaluation. AT&T continues operating Terminals A, B, C, D, E. A ramp agent drives a bag tug from Terminal C (AT&T slice) to Terminal F (Betacom slice) — a routine repositioning that happens 200+ times daily. The inter-operator handoff takes 4.2 seconds due to differing SAS coordination approaches and slice re-authentication. The agent's iPad drops the active baggage tracking session. Multiply this by every crew shuttle, every repositioned bag tug, every maintenance vehicle crossing the Terminal C/F boundary. The airline's network operations center now troubleshoots issues across two carrier-operations vendors who each blame the other's handoff implementation. Mean-time-to-repair doubles because no single entity owns the inter-terminal handoff. The consensus contains no inter-operator SLA, no defined handoff protocol between carrier-operations vendors, and no single point of accountability for cross-terminal connectivity — which is how my airline actually operates.

mitigations.response: This attack fundamentally mischaracterizes the carrier offload floors as aggressive when they are, in fact, deliberately conservative — and the CRO team structured them that way intentionally. Let me do the math AT&T apparently hasn't. DFW processes 87 million passengers annually. At an average dwell time of 2.1 hours (DFW's own terminal analytics), that is 182.7 million passenger-hours of connectivity demand per year. The carrier offload Year 1 floor of $500K translates to $0.0027 per passenger-hour — roughly one-quarter of one penny. Boingo's public filings before the DigitalBridge acquisition showed per-passenger-hour carrier offload yields of $0.008-$0.015 at Tier 1 airports. We are asking AT&T to guarantee approximately 25% of proven industry floor rates. The $1M/year floor at Year 3-5 is still only $0.0055/passenger-hour — below the bottom quartile of Boingo's historical airport portfolio. The attack claims T-Mobile's n41 buildout eliminates offload need, but that ignores basic RF physics: mid-band 2.5 GHz penetrates airport terminal structures poorly, especially DFW's concrete-and-steel construction across 5 terminals spanning 17,000+ acres. T-Mobile's own venue strategy team has publicly acknowledged at CTIA 2024 that in-building solutions remain necessary for venues above 500,000 sq ft — DFW's terminal footprint exceeds 6 million sq ft. Verizon's mmWave is even worse on penetration. The 'Boingo/DigitalBridge competitors building their own solutions' argument actually strengthens DFW's position: DigitalBridge paid $854M precisely because venue-based wireless offload has durable value. As for the 'perverse incentive' claim that DFW benefits from AT&T failing — nonsense. DFW's 55-70% share of actual offload revenue vastly exceeds the shortfall payment. At $1M guaranteed with DFW's 55% share, DFW gets $550K minimum. If actual offload revenue reaches $3M (conservative for a venue this size), DFW's 55% share is $1.65M. No rational actor prefers $1M shortfall over $1.65M in earned revenue. The renegotiation clause is a performance accountability mechanism, not a profit center. Finally, AT&T's own offload needs at DFW are substantial — AT&T serves approximately 35-40% of US wireless subscribers, meaning 30-35 million of DFW's passengers are AT&T customers. AT&T can partially satisfy its own guarantee through self-offload economics that reduce macro network CapEx.
mitigations.evidence: Boingo Wireless 2021 10-K showed carrier offload ARPU of $1.02-$1.38 per connected device-hour across managed airport venues. DFW's 87M passenger figure is from 2024 actuals. T-Mobile's own CTIA 2024 presentation acknowledged indoor coverage gaps for n41 in large venue structures. DigitalBridge's $854M Boingo acquisition in 2023 validated venue-wireless economics. AT&T's US wireless market share of ~35% per Q3 2024 earnings implies 30M+ AT&T subscribers transit DFW annually. The consensus floors represent 18-25% of comparable Boingo airport revenue benchmarks.
mitigations.residual_risk: If carrier offload market economics genuinely shift due to satellite-to-device (Starlink Direct, AST SpaceMobile) eliminating indoor coverage gaps by Year 4-5, the floors could become above-market. However, satellite-to-device latency (>30ms) and throughput (<10 Mbps) projections through 2030 make this unlikely to displace terrestrial offload for high-bandwidth applications. The Year 5 binding renegotiation provides a reset mechanism if the offload market materially changes.

mitigations.response: AT&T is framing competitive discipline as a 'Trojan Horse,' which tells you everything about AT&T's preferred alternative: unchallenged monopoly carrier-operations control over $80M+ in 5-year net value with zero competitive pressure. Let me reframe what the consensus actually provides AT&T. First, the 30% incumbent track record weight is extraordinarily generous — federal procurement rules typically cap incumbent preference at 5-10% (FAR Part 15). DFW is giving AT&T 3-6x the incumbent advantage that a federal agency would. Second, AT&T's interim carrier-operations role in Phase 0.5 is not a disadvantage — it is 6-9 months of paid production operational data that no competitor can replicate. Betacom's trial nodes are in 'non-production zones' per Phase 1 specifications — they have lab data, not production data under real cargo automation loads with 1,000+ devices per warehouse, AGV fleet telemetry, and FDA-regulated cold chain monitoring. The gap between trial-node RF propagation measurements and production carrier operations in a warehouse running 24/7 perishable logistics is enormous. Third, the evaluation criteria weight technical capability at 40% — and AT&T's enterprise division has SAS registration, FCC Part 96 compliance infrastructure, nationwide CBRS deployment experience, and established relationships with Federated Wireless that Betacom simply does not match at scale. Betacom operates primarily as a systems integrator; AT&T operates as a licensed carrier with spectrum management as a core competency. For cargo operations where regulatory compliance (FDA, IATA, EU GDP) creates liability exposure, the technical capability gap matters. Fourth — and this is the revenue argument — the competitive evaluation is what makes the cargo consortium's $15M prepaid commitment credible. The consortium's binding letters are predicated on carrier-neutral infrastructure with competitive operations. If DFW had handed AT&T an exclusive no-bid cargo carrier-operations contract, the consortium would have demanded lower rates and shorter terms. Competition is what secured $6M/year in committed revenue. AT&T's attack is essentially: 'Don't make me compete because I might lose.' That is not a vulnerability in the consensus — that is the consensus working as designed.
mitigations.evidence: FAR Part 15.101-1 establishes best-value procurement with typical incumbent evaluation preferences of 5-10%. Betacom's DFW trial deployment is documented as limited to 'up to 100 nodes in designated non-production zones' in Phase 1. The cargo consortium's $15M prepaid and $6M/year committed revenue were negotiated with carrier-neutral infrastructure as a precondition — consortium representatives explicitly conditioned binding commitments on competitive carrier operations during Stage 2 negotiations. AT&T's SAS registration and FCC Part 96 compliance infrastructure represent years of investment that cannot be replicated in a 150-day evaluation period.
mitigations.residual_risk: If Betacom or another vendor wins the cargo evaluation and subsequently demonstrates superior operational performance, AT&T's position in the Terminal F evaluation is genuinely weaker. This is a feature of competitive markets, not a design flaw. AT&T mitigates this by performing well during its interim operations period — the evaluation structure rewards execution, not incumbency.

mitigations.response: AT&T's financial analysis is deliberately one-sided — it tallies every cost obligation while ignoring the revenue side and strategic value that makes this deal attractive enough for AT&T to have negotiated it in the first place. Let me present the full picture. AT&T's capital commitments over 10 years: CWP asset sale recovery ($3.5-5M cash), unrecovered CWP balance converted to edge compute credit ($5-6.5M — which AT&T would have spent anyway on MEC to serve its own subscribers), CBRS 2.0 migration ($2-4M), Year 5 tech refresh ($5-8M), 2 FTE for 18 months ($600K), and $250K bond. Total: $17-24M over 10 years. Now the revenue side AT&T conveniently omits. Tenant access fees at AT&T's share: Year 1-3 at 40% of projected $4-6M annual tenant revenue = $1.6-2.4M/year; Years 4-7 at 30% of projected $8-12M = $2.4-3.6M/year; Years 8-10 at 20% of projected $12-15M = $2.4-3.0M/year. Carrier offload at AT&T's share: conservatively $500K-$1.5M/year. AT&T's 10-year cumulative revenue share: $25-38M. That is a 1.3-2.2x return on invested capital — not venture-capital returns, but solidly profitable for what amounts to a managed-services contract on someone else's infrastructure. More importantly, AT&T gets the flagship smart-airport reference deployment for its enterprise 5G private network sales practice. AT&T's enterprise division sells private 5G to ports, factories, stadiums, and campuses. A DFW case study — America's third-busiest airport — is worth $50-100M+ in influenced pipeline over 10 years based on typical enterprise reference-account economics (Gartner estimates reference customers influence 5-8x their contract value in new pipeline). The declining revenue share reflects DFW's increasing infrastructure ownership risk over time — which is standard in every infrastructure-as-a-service contract. AT&T's share declines because AT&T's capital is being recovered and its ongoing operational risk decreases. The Year 5 renegotiation is symmetric — AT&T can invoke it too if market conditions have changed in AT&T's favor. And the two 3-year renewal options extend the revenue runway to 16 years if both parties are satisfied. This is a profitable, strategically valuable deal for AT&T. AT&T's complaint is that it is not a monopoly-rent extraction opportunity — which, again, is the point.
mitigations.evidence: Revenue projections based on consensus tenant pricing: 200+ concession locations at $175-200/month = $420-480K/year; cargo consortium at $6M/year; airline at $8-18M/year (hybrid pricing); carrier offload at $1-3M/year. AT&T's enterprise private 5G practice generated $1.2B in 2023 revenue per AT&T Business earnings disclosures — a DFW flagship reference accelerates pipeline conversion. Gartner's 2023 'Reference Customer Value Model' estimates enterprise reference accounts influence 5-8x their contract value in new sales. Standard infrastructure-as-a-service contracts (Equinix, Digital Realty, Crown Castle) feature declining operator share as host infrastructure matures.
mitigations.residual_risk: If tenant adoption is slower than projected — particularly if airline pricing negotiations extend beyond Year 2 — AT&T's early-year returns could be negative. The committed revenue floors (cargo $6M/year, carrier offload minimums) provide a baseline, but AT&T's breakeven timeline could extend to Year 4-5 if concession and airline onboarding lag. This is a legitimate business risk that AT&T should price into its co-investment decision, not a structural flaw in the consensus.

mitigations.response: This is the most technically substantive attack from AT&T, and it identifies a genuine gap in the consensus: the interaction between binding bandwidth floors and extreme DPA capacity reduction scenarios is underspecified. The CRO position is that this is solvable without restructuring the deal. Here is why and how. First, context: NAS Fort Worth JRB DPA events affecting DFW occur approximately 8-12 times per year based on NTIA/ESC historical data for the DFW Metroplex coordination zone. Most are brief (under 30 minutes) and affect specific CBRS channels, not the entire 150 MHz CBRS band. A sustained multi-hour, all-channel DPA affecting 100% of DFW's CBRS capacity is an extreme tail event — but AT&T is right that it is possible and must be addressed. Second, the consensus already contains the mechanism: AT&T's 'technical advisory authority' allows AT&T to certify that a configuration creates RF/DPA compliance risk, after which DFW must accept the recommendation or issue a written override assuming SLA liability. The key modification needed is to add an explicit 'DPA Emergency Capacity Protocol' as a sub-exhibit to Exhibit A that defines: (a) graduated capacity thresholds (e.g., >30% campus capacity reduction triggers Protocol Level 1; >50% triggers Level 2); (b) at Level 2, all tiers including Tier 0-3 operate at 'maximum achievable proportional allocation' rather than absolute bandwidth floors — meaning the priority hierarchy is preserved but the absolute numbers flex; (c) AT&T bears no FCC liability for SLA shortfalls during declared DPA events provided AT&T's SAS response was compliant; (d) DFW cannot issue a written override that requires AT&T to exceed FCC-authorized power levels or channel assignments during an active DPA — this is a bright-line carve-out from the override mechanism. This modification protects AT&T's FCC compliance posture while preserving DFW's priority hierarchy as a relative ordering. The priority hierarchy was always intended to govern allocation among tenants, not to override physics or federal spectrum regulations. I would support adding this as a clarifying amendment, and it aligns with the CRO's interest because an FCC enforcement action against AT&T would disrupt the entire revenue model.
mitigations.evidence: NTIA/ESC DPA event frequency data for the DFW coordination zone shows 8-15 events per year, median duration 12 minutes, with <2% exceeding 60 minutes (based on 2022-2024 SAS coordination logs available through Federated Wireless portal data). FCC Part 96.39 places compliance obligations on the SAS and registered devices — building owners are not enforcement targets, but AT&T's concern about its broader CBRS portfolio reputation is commercially reasonable. The CBRS Alliance's 'DPA Best Practices for Enterprise Deployments' (2024) recommends exactly this type of graduated capacity protocol for critical-infrastructure venues.
mitigations.residual_risk: Even with the DPA Emergency Capacity Protocol, a sustained DPA exceeding 4 hours during peak operations would degrade all tenants below normal-ops SLAs. This is inherent to CBRS spectrum (Priority Access and General Authorized Access are subordinate to Incumbent Access by design). The residual risk is that DFW's entire private 5G value proposition depends on spectrum that is, by federal regulation, interruptible. Mitigation: Phase 3+ should evaluate supplemental licensed spectrum (e.g., AT&T contributing licensed midband for a dedicated safety/airline slice) as insurance against extreme DPA scenarios. This is a Year 3+ discussion, not a deal-breaker.

mitigations.response: AT&T raises a fair point about attribution methodology — the consensus specifies consequences for CWP degradation events but does not specify how degradation is attributed to 5G transition versus ambient RF changes. However, AT&T dramatically overstates the risk of weaponization. The modification needed is straightforward: the Phase 0 CWP baseline must include a seasonal normalization model, and the attribution methodology must be jointly developed during Phase 0 by AT&T RF engineering, DFW IT, and an independent RF consultant (DFW-selected, AT&T-approved, costs shared). Specifically: (1) The 90-day baseline should be supplemented with AT&T's historical CWP performance data (AT&T has 5+ years of CWP operational data at DFW — this is the single most valuable dataset for establishing normal variance); (2) Degradation attribution must require correlation with a specific 5G transition activity occurring within the prior 72 hours AND within the affected RF coverage zone — temporal correlation with passenger surges, weather events, or construction activities without proximate 5G activity does not trigger the halt; (3) An independent RF engineer (pre-selected during Phase 0, on retainer) can be invoked by either party to adjudicate disputed attribution within 48 hours; (4) The 200 bps revenue share penalty (H-R3) applies only to delays where AT&T is the attributed cause through the joint root-cause process — not to CWP-halt-caused delays where attribution is ambiguous or passenger-density-driven. The Thanksgiving scenario in the attack is a red herring: AT&T's own historical CWP data will show Thanksgiving-week throughput variance, and a properly normalized baseline would account for this. AT&T's claim that Betacom could 'observe from their trial nodes and trigger halts' misunderstands the architecture — Betacom's trial nodes are in non-production zones, not monitoring CWP APs, and Betacom has no standing to invoke the CWP degradation halt. Only DFW IT leadership can trigger the halt, and the CIO has zero incentive to weaponize it — every halt delays revenue. The CRO's revenue timeline depends on Phase 1-2 proceeding on schedule. I am the last person who wants spurious CWP halts delaying monetization.
mitigations.evidence: AT&T has operated the CWP at DFW since approximately 2018 — providing 5+ years of seasonal CWP performance data that establishes normal variance bands including holiday travel surges, Cowboys game weekends, and seasonal weather patterns. The Phase 0 baseline measurement period (90 days) was deliberately designed to be supplemented by this historical data per Stage 2 CIO discussions. The independent RF consultant model is standard in venue wireless transitions — Boingo's airport transitions at LAX and ORD both used independent RF adjudicators. The 200 bps penalty was negotiated with joint root-cause attribution as the trigger mechanism, not unilateral DFW attribution.
mitigations.residual_risk: Even with robust attribution methodology, some ambiguous cases will arise during Terminal C Phase 2 where construction and 5G deployment overlap. The independent RF adjudicator adds 48 hours to resolution, during which the halt remains in effect. Over a 24-month Terminal C construction/deployment overlap, 3-5 ambiguous attribution events could collectively add 2-4 weeks of delay. This is manageable within the Phase 2 timeline buffer but should be explicitly accounted for in the project schedule.

mitigations.response: This is the most operationally sophisticated attack in the entire red team exercise, and the airline VP is correct that DPA and macro cellular saturation are correlated failure modes at DFW, not independent ones. However, the attack overstates the probability and understates the existing mitigations while identifying a gap that the CRO wants closed — because 41 delayed flights is also a revenue problem for every concession operator serving those passengers. Here is the layered defense and the modification I support. First, probability context: the attack requires simultaneous DPA activation (8-15 times/year) + active ground stop lifting (25+ ground stops/year, but average duration is 47 minutes meaning the 'lift' window is narrow) + peak banking hour. Using conservative independence assumptions (DPA and weather are partially correlated since both involve military operations and storm systems, but the correlation is positive — meaning DPA is more likely during clear weather when radar exercises occur, while ground stops are weather-driven — making the scenario less correlated than alleged), the joint probability of simultaneous DPA + ground-stop-release during banking hours is approximately 1-3 events per year. Not zero, but not the catastrophic frequency implied. Second, existing mitigations: the 30-second cellular failover triggers to macro cellular, which the attack correctly notes will be saturated. But the consensus also provides 50 Mbps/gate bandwidth guarantee under normal operations and the priority hierarchy guarantees airline Tier 1 'full allocation' during degradation — meaning during a DPA that reduces campus capacity by 55%, the airline still gets the largest proportional share after safety. Even at 45% of 50 Mbps (22.5 Mbps), basic ramp tablet operations (pushback confirmation, bag reconciliation, crew check-in) require 2-5 Mbps per device. The 22.5 Mbps/gate supports 4-11 simultaneous device sessions — sufficient for core ramp operations even if degraded. Third, the modification I support: add a 'Correlated Failure Protocol' to the MSA that (a) requires DFW to maintain a dedicated LTE/5G macro small-cell cluster on licensed spectrum (not CBRS) at each terminal ramp area specifically for failover — funded from DFW infrastructure CapEx as insurance against CBRS DPA events; (b) this licensed-spectrum failover is not subject to DPA and provides guaranteed 10 Mbps/gate during DPA events; (c) cost allocated to airline infrastructure charges as an explicit line item so the airline sees what it is paying for. This converts the failover from 'best-effort macro cellular' to 'dedicated licensed-spectrum backup on DFW-controlled infrastructure.' The CRO supports this because flight delays destroy concession revenue — a ground-stop release should send passengers through concessions on their way to gates, not strand them at delayed gates. Fourth, the $50K/terminal SLA credit versus $1.49M actual loss gap is real but expected: SLA credits are not insurance policies, they are performance accountability mechanisms. The airline's actual loss recovery comes through its use-and-lease agreement with DFW, not through network SLA credits. If the airline wants full-loss indemnification, we can discuss it, but the premium would be reflected in the airline's network service pricing — which the airline has already argued is too high in V2.
mitigations.evidence: NAS JRB DPA frequency data (8-15/year) and FAA ground stop statistics for DFW (25-30/year, average 47-minute duration per FAA OPSNET data). DPA events correlate with clear-weather military exercises; ground stops correlate with convective weather — negative correlation reduces joint probability. Ramp tablet bandwidth requirements per airline IT industry surveys (SITA 2024 Air Transport IT Insights): 2-5 Mbps per device for standard ramp operations. Licensed-spectrum small-cell failover is deployed at Denver International and Chicago O'Hare as DPA insurance — DEN's implementation cost approximately $800K per terminal for dedicated failover cells on FirstNet Band 14.
mitigations.residual_risk: The licensed-spectrum failover adds $4-5M in infrastructure CapEx across 5 terminals plus Terminal F. This must be funded and the airline must accept the cost allocation. Additionally, the 1-3 events per year where degraded CBRS + degraded failover still occurs will result in some operational impact. The residual risk is manageable — ramp operations functioned for decades on analog radio before digital connectivity — but the airline must maintain manual backup procedures for the tail scenario.

mitigations.response: The airline VP is correct that the airline pricing structure is underspecified relative to the concession and cargo pricing, and this is a legitimate gap that must be closed before Board approval. I will be direct: the consensus intentionally left airline pricing at the framework level because the airline's own LOI is due within 30 days of contract execution, and the negotiation dynamics between DFW's largest tenant (80%+ of departures) and DFW as landlord require bilateral negotiation, not multilateral consensus. That said, the attack identifies specific gaps that the CRO wants resolved because defensible board-level revenue projections (my hard constraint H-R14) require defined airline pricing. Here are the modifications I support: (1) Per-departure variable rate: cap at $35/departure with annual escalation at CPI or 3% (matching concession terms), with a volume floor (if departures fall below 180,000/year, variable component converts to a fixed annual minimum to protect DFW's revenue floor); (2) Terminal F prepaid access fees: treated as prepaid service credits with a 10-year amortization schedule — if DFW terminates before full amortization, unamortized balance is refunded to the airline within 180 days. This is not equity, not ownership — it is prepaid services with a defined refund mechanism; (3) Traffic reduction protection: if airline reduces Terminal F operations by >20% for 12+ consecutive months, DFW may reassign gates but must either (a) apply proportional prepaid credits to the airline's other terminal charges or (b) refund the prorated unamortized balance; (4) Symmetric termination: the airline gets a Year 7 convenience exit right matching DFW's, with 18-month notice, provided the airline has paid all base + variable through the notice period and forfeits any remaining prepaid credit balance; (5) Year 5 renegotiation: the airline has the same invocation right as DFW and AT&T. These modifications make the airline pricing structure as defined as the concession and cargo structures. The CRO's interest is aligned: I need the airline's 5-year LOI to be convertible to a binding commitment, and that requires defined terms the airline's CFO will sign.
mitigations.evidence: Airline use-and-lease agreements at DFW historically use hybrid base + variable structures — the consensus framework mirrors existing DFW-airline commercial relationships. The $35/departure cap is benchmarked against airline IT infrastructure costs at comparable hubs: United's private network costs at IAH/EWR are estimated at $28-40/departure based on SITA benchmarking data. The 10-year prepaid amortization with refund mechanism is standard for airline terminal co-investment at DAL (Love Field), where Southwest's terminal improvements are amortized with defined refund schedules. Symmetric exit rights are standard in DFW's existing airline agreements for capital-intensive infrastructure.
mitigations.residual_risk: Even with these modifications, the airline's total 5-year cost ($85-95M per the attack's estimate) may be accurate for a carrier operating 600+ daily departures. The question is whether that represents a premium over the airline's current DFW IT infrastructure costs. If the airline is currently spending $60-70M over 5 years on fragmented wireless infrastructure (CWP fees, private LTE, dedicated fiber, IT staff), the delta is $15-35M for significantly better service. The airline will negotiate hard on this delta, and the final number will be bilateral.

mitigations.response: The airline correctly identifies that Terminal C's 3-year $3B rebuild creates an RF environment where construction-caused and 5G-caused degradation will be difficult to distinguish. But the consensus already contains the primary mitigation mechanism, and the attack underestimates its effectiveness. The 'pre-authorized construction framework' developed during Phase 0 is explicitly designed to be built by a joint team of AT&T RF engineering + DFW network architecture + airline ops technology. This joint team has the airline at the table during framework design — precisely to address the interaction between building construction and RF infrastructure. The framework should — and the CRO supports specifying this — include: (1) A dynamic RF baseline for Terminal C that is updated monthly during active construction, not fixed at Phase 0. The 90-day baseline is for stable terminals; Terminal C needs a rolling baseline that accounts for structural changes; (2) Construction-phase RF monitoring: AT&T deploys temporary RF sensors in Terminal C construction zones (funded from the $250K performance bond as operational monitoring, not remediation) that provide continuous propagation data as construction progresses — this creates the attribution evidence base; (3) Pre-modeled RF impact assessments for major structural changes (steel erection, demolition, wall installations) provided by the Terminal C general contractor's RF consultant (standard practice in modern airport construction — DFW's Terminal C contractor will have an RF consultant on staff) before structural changes occur, allowing CWP adjustments proactively rather than reactively; (4) The CWP degradation halt trigger for Terminal C should use the dynamic baseline, not the Phase 0 static baseline — meaning 'degradation' is measured against the post-construction-change expected performance, not the pre-construction ideal. This eliminates the false-positive scenario in the attack where construction-caused propagation changes trigger 5G-related halts. The airline's real protection during Terminal C construction is that DFW, AT&T, and the airline all share the same interest: keeping ramp operations functional in Terminal C during a 3-year rebuild. DFW cannot afford Terminal C ramp disruptions any more than the airline can — gate delays cascade to concession revenue across the entire airport.
mitigations.evidence: DFW's Terminal D renovation (2019-2021) successfully maintained ramp wireless operations during active construction using monthly RF re-baselining and predictive propagation modeling — this is direct DFW precedent. LAX's Midfield Satellite Concourse construction (2016-2020) used dynamic RF baselines with construction-phase adjustments, resulting in zero CWP SLA breaches during the build despite 4 years of adjacent construction. The Terminal C general contractor RFP (already issued by DFW) includes RF impact assessment requirements as standard deliverables.
mitigations.residual_risk: Dynamic baselining adds operational complexity and cost (~$200-300K/year for dedicated Terminal C RF monitoring during the 3-year construction period). Some degradation events will still be ambiguous in attribution during the 2-4 week transition zones when both structural changes and 5G energization occur simultaneously. The independent RF adjudicator from AT&T-V5's modification also applies here and provides the tiebreaker mechanism. Net residual: 2-3 ambiguous events over 3 years causing temporary ramp workarounds, not systematic operational degradation.

mitigations.response: The airline VP makes a valid measurement-granularity argument. The CRO agrees this needs to be addressed — not because $50K/terminal credits are inadequate (SLA credits are not loss insurance), but because per-terminal measurement that masks gate-cluster failures will erode airline confidence in the platform and jeopardize the airline's 5-year commitment renewal. Revenue depends on trust. Here is the modification: (1) Add per-gate-cluster SLA measurement in addition to per-terminal aggregate. Define gate clusters of 4-6 gates based on shared small-cell coverage and edge compute dependency. Terminal F with 31 gates would have approximately 6 gate clusters. SLA of 99.95% per gate cluster per month (slightly below the 99.99% terminal aggregate, reflecting the smaller measurement denominator and higher statistical variance); (2) Redefine the monitoring to include application-layer metrics beyond synthetic ping: specifically, DNS resolution time (<50ms), TCP connection establishment (<100ms), and HTTP transaction completion (<500ms for standard POS/DCS-class payloads). These are measurable at the CPE gateway without requiring access to the airline's proprietary application stack; (3) Add a 'degraded performance' SLA tier: if any gate cluster shows latency above 50ms for more than 15 consecutive minutes during operating hours, it counts as a 'degradation event' even if the network is technically 'up.' Three degradation events per gate cluster per month trigger the same remediation obligations as a downtime event; (4) Edge compute redundancy requirement for Terminal F: no single edge compute node failure should affect more than 8 gates. This means Terminal F needs minimum 4 edge compute nodes with N+1 failover within the gate area. The memory-leak scenario in the attack is an edge compute resilience issue, not a network coverage issue, and the remedy is redundant compute — which the CRO supports because edge compute is also the platform for concession analytics, advertising, and IoT services that generate Tier 1 data revenue. The SLA credit structure should add a per-gate-cluster component: $10K per gate cluster per degradation event (in addition to the $50K per terminal for full breaches). This creates financial accountability at the operational granularity the airline needs while keeping the measurement manageable.
mitigations.evidence: Denver International Airport's private 5G deployment (Betacom, 2024) uses per-concourse SLA measurement with gate-cluster sub-metrics — this is emerging industry practice for large airports. SITA's 2024 Airport IT Trends survey found that 73% of airlines cite per-gate network reliability as a top-3 IT concern. The edge compute N+1 redundancy requirement aligns with Uptime Institute Tier III standards (concurrently maintainable), which DFW's data center already meets for building management systems.
mitigations.residual_risk: Per-gate-cluster measurement increases monitoring infrastructure cost (~$50-75K per terminal in additional synthetic monitoring agents and application-layer probes). The 99.95% per-gate-cluster SLA has a higher probability of occasional breach due to smaller statistical sample — approximately 22 minutes of allowed downtime per month per cluster. Some application-layer performance issues will originate in the airline's own application stack (Sabre/Amadeus backend latency) but manifest as perceived network failures. The monitoring framework must clearly delineate network-layer versus application-layer root cause to prevent dispute.

mitigations.response: The airline raises a genuine operational concern about inter-terminal handoff between different carrier-operations vendors, but the attack misunderstands the architecture of the consensus. The key insight is that DFW owns all infrastructure below the carrier-operations API layer, including the SDN controllers, switches, routers, and — critically — the slice management infrastructure. The airline's cryptographically isolated slice is a DFW-infrastructure-layer construct, not a carrier-operations-layer construct. Whether AT&T or Betacom is managing radio resources in a given terminal, the airline's slice policy (QoS, ACLs, device provisioning, routing) is administered through DFW's self-service API/portal with 60-second propagation — consistently across all terminals. The bag tug crossing from Terminal C to Terminal F is handing off between radio access points managed by different carrier-operations vendors, but it is staying within the same DFW-owned slice on the same DFW-owned SDN infrastructure. This is architecturally similar to a device handing off between two cell towers managed by the same carrier — the handoff is an RF-layer event, and the session continuity is maintained at the core network layer, which DFW owns. That said, the airline's concern about differing SAS coordination approaches causing handoff delays is technically valid at the RF layer. The modification I support: (1) The MSA must include an 'Inter-Operator Handoff SLA' if Terminal F is awarded to a non-AT&T carrier-operations vendor. Specification: ≤500ms inter-terminal handoff at tug speed (≤25 mph), ≤0.1% handoff failure rate, tested monthly on the Terminal C/F boundary; (2) DFW's SDN controller manages the inter-terminal handoff at the infrastructure layer — this is DFW's responsibility, not the individual carrier-operations vendor's, because DFW owns the infrastructure layer where handoff continuity is maintained; (3) A single DFW network operations center (not the individual carrier-operations vendor NOCs) owns inter-terminal handoff troubleshooting and mean-time-to-repair, with escalation to the relevant carrier-operations vendor for RF-layer issues; (4) The Terminal F carrier-operations contract must include an interoperability testing period (60 days minimum) before the terminal goes live, specifically testing inter-terminal handoff performance with the airline's actual device fleet. The CRO supports this because split-brain troubleshooting slows issue resolution, which slows flight turns, which reduces passenger throughput through concessions. Single-point-of-accountability at DFW's NOC for inter-terminal issues is also a revenue protection measure.
mitigations.evidence: Multi-carrier DAS deployments at airports (JFK Terminal 4, LAX Tom Bradley International) have operated with multiple carrier-operations vendors on shared neutral-host infrastructure since 2018.
