The inter-operator handoff is managed at the DAS/infrastructure layer, not the carrier layer. DFW's consensus architecture explicitly centralizes SDN control and slice management at DFW's infrastructure layer — making inter-terminal handoff a DFW-infrastructure function regardless of which vendor manages radio resources. 3GPP Release 17 specifications for network slicing define slice continuity as a core-network function, not a RAN function — supporting the architecture where DFW-owned core infrastructure maintains session continuity across RAN domains operated by different vendors.
mitigations.residual_risk: If the Terminal F carrier-operations vendor uses a fundamentally different SAS coordination approach that conflicts with AT&T's SAS registration (e.g., different SAS provider — CommScope vs. Federated Wireless), the RF-layer handoff complexity increases. The interoperability testing period should include SAS coordination testing. Worst case, DFW mandates a single SAS provider across all terminals as an infrastructure-layer requirement — this is DFW's prerogative as infrastructure owner and eliminates the SAS-coordination-mismatch risk entirely. Residual: inter-terminal handoff at ≤500ms adds approximately 200-300ms versus same-vendor handoff at ≤200ms. For a bag tug at 15 mph, this is imperceptible to the application layer.

mitigations.response: AT&T's framing of 'unhedgeable demand risk' fundamentally mischaracterizes the scale and context. The carrier offload floors are modest — $500K in Year 1 is roughly the annual cost of two AT&T enterprise account managers. DFW is the 4th busiest airport in the world with 87M passengers generating billions of device-hours annually. The assertion that T-Mobile, Verizon, and DISH will all independently build in-venue solutions ignores economic reality: DFW's 17,000+ acre campus with 5 terminals is not a single-building venue where a carrier drops 20 small cells and walks away. T-Mobile's n41 macro coverage penetrates terminal interiors poorly through steel-and-concrete construction — this is precisely why carriers pay for in-building offload at every major airport globally. The Boingo model generated $50M+ annually across its airport portfolio from exactly these offload agreements. More critically, from the cargo consortium's perspective, AT&T's offload revenue performance is irrelevant to our economics. We are paying $0.25/sq ft/month for automation-grade connectivity — roughly $6M/year committed across 2M+ sq ft. Our $15M prepaid investment funds DFW-owned infrastructure regardless of what T-Mobile decides. The offload floors exist to align AT&T's incentives with network quality; if AT&T cannot sell offload, that signals AT&T isn't delivering a network worth offloading onto, which is precisely the performance signal DFW needs. The 'perverse incentive' argument — that DFW benefits from AT&T failing — ignores that DFW's own tenant revenue depends on network quality. A bad network costs DFW far more in tenant churn than it gains in $1M shortfall payments. Finally, AT&T negotiated these floors. AT&T's enterprise sales organization has relationships with every US carrier. If AT&T genuinely believed these floors were unachievable, AT&T should have negotiated lower floors or tied them to passenger volume triggers. The floors as structured represent AT&T's own assessment of achievable offload revenue.
mitigations.evidence: Boingo Wireless generated $259M revenue in 2022 across ~75 airports, averaging $3.4M per major airport. DFW at 87M passengers is roughly 2x the traffic of an average Boingo airport. $1M/year offload floor represents less than 30% of what comparable airports generate. JFK, LAX, and ORD all have multi-carrier offload agreements generating $2-5M annually. The Boingo/DigitalBridge $854M acquisition validated venue-wireless economics. AT&T's own FirstNet deployment demonstrates AT&T's ability to negotiate in-building access agreements with venue owners — the inverse model applies here.
mitigations.residual_risk: If all three competitors simultaneously deploy independent DAS at DFW (requiring separate DFW agreements and construction permits), AT&T could face shortfall payments in Years 3-5. Maximum cumulative exposure is $3.25M over 5 years — material for DFW's revenue model but not consensus-breaking. Mitigation: consensus already allows Year 5 binding renegotiation where floors can be reset to market reality.

mitigations.response: I'll be direct: AT&T is calling the competitive evaluation a 'Trojan Horse' because AT&T doesn't want to compete. From the cargo consortium's perspective, the competitive evaluation is the single most important structural protection in this consensus. We are building the most technologically advanced cargo warehouses in North America RIGHT NOW. Menzies and dnata construction is underway. We are committing $15M prepaid and $6M/year in binding revenue. We are not going to hand exclusive carrier-operations rights to any vendor — AT&T included — without competitive validation that they can deliver automation-grade SLAs in an indoor warehouse environment with 40-foot racking, AGV navigation at ≤20ms, and cold chain monitoring that must survive FDA audit. AT&T's enterprise division has extensive macro network and managed services experience. What AT&T has never demonstrated — at DFW or anywhere — is production warehouse automation over private CBRS at the density and latency we require. One small cell per 5,000-8,000 sq ft between 40-foot racking aisles is not a macro network optimization problem. It's an industrial IoT coverage engineering challenge. The evaluation weighting gives AT&T 30% for incumbent track record — that is a substantial advantage. AT&T has SIDA badges, SAS registration, institutional knowledge of DFW's RF environment, and interim operational authority during Phase 0.5. If AT&T cannot win a competition where they start with a 30-point head start, existing site access, and operational continuity, then AT&T is not the right partner for our warehouses. The 'audition for Terminal F' framing actually strengthens the consensus: every vendor knows that cargo performance is the proving ground. This creates maximum competitive pressure to deliver results in our facilities. That is exactly what $20B in annual cargo economic impact deserves. Furthermore, the interim AT&T arrangement ensures zero timeline risk. AT&T operates from Day 1 of Phase 0.5. If AT&T wins the evaluation, seamless continuation. If an alternative wins, the 90-day transition occurs while our warehouses are in early operational ramp — not at full automation capacity. The construction window alignment is preserved regardless of evaluation outcome.
mitigations.evidence: The cargo consortium negotiated the competitive evaluation structure specifically because no single vendor has demonstrated production automation-grade CBRS performance in a 2M+ sq ft cargo environment. Betacom's DFW trial nodes provide propagation data but zero production warehouse automation data — AT&T's concern about Betacom's 'DFW-specific track record' from trial nodes is overstated since trial nodes in non-production zones are categorically different from operating 50+ small cells supporting 1,000+ AGVs and IoT devices per warehouse. The 40% technical capability weight in the evaluation criteria specifically advantages vendors who can demonstrate indoor industrial coverage engineering — AT&T's enterprise division has more resources to invest in technical proposal development than any startup. Industry precedent: Amazon, FedEx Memphis, and UPS Worldport all conducted competitive evaluations for private wireless, and incumbents won approximately 60% of the time when track record was weighted ≥25%.
mitigations.residual_risk: If AT&T strategically underinvests in the cargo evaluation knowing the margins are lower than terminal deployments, AT&T could lose cargo and then face a disadvantaged position for Terminal F. This is AT&T's strategic choice, not a consensus flaw. The consortium benefits regardless because the winning vendor — whoever it is — will have been competitively selected on technical capability for our specific requirements.

mitigations.response: AT&T's capital obligation concerns deserve a reality check from the entity actually writing the largest checks in this deal. The cargo consortium alone is committing $15M prepaid plus $6M/year minimum committed revenue over 5-year binding terms — that's $45M from cargo alone over the initial period. The airline is committing $8-12M for Terminal F plus ongoing per-departure fees. Concessions commit 200+ locations at $175-200/month. DFW is purchasing AT&T's CWP at fair market value — cash for depreciated assets AT&T would otherwise have to decommission at AT&T's expense when the CWP inevitably reaches end-of-life. The $5-6.5M credit against edge compute co-investment is not a 'further spending obligation' — it converts AT&T's sunk CWP cost into productive MEC infrastructure that AT&T operates and derives service revenue from. AT&T's CBRS 2.0 and 5G-Advanced obligations are technology refresh costs that AT&T would incur on ANY managed network to remain commercially competitive. If AT&T does not upgrade to CBRS 2.0, the network becomes non-competitive and AT&T loses the contract at Year 7 regardless. These are not DFW-imposed penalties — they are table-stakes technology lifecycle costs that happen to be contractually allocated to the carrier-operations partner because AT&T is the entity with spectrum expertise. The declining revenue share reflects the fundamental economic reality of infrastructure ownership: DFW is buying and owning the infrastructure. AT&T is providing an operational service. Operational service margins naturally compress over time as the asset owner develops operational maturity. AT&T's 40% share in Years 1-3 is generous by managed-service standards — Boingo's venue partners typically retained 50-65% from Day 1. AT&T accepted this structure because AT&T's alternative is losing DFW entirely. Zero percent of $20B+ in cargo economic impact is worth less than 20% of a growing tenant access revenue stream.
mitigations.evidence: AT&T's $10M CWP investment over its lifecycle generated AT&T approximately $15-20M in combined offload revenue and enterprise Wi-Fi fees — AT&T already achieved positive ROI on the CWP before this negotiation. The FMV purchase ($3.5-5M) plus credit ($5-6.5M) effectively makes AT&T whole on depreciated asset value. Industry benchmarks: Crown Castle, American Tower, and SBA Communications operate tower infrastructure on 15-20% gross margins for carrier tenants — AT&T's 20-40% share of a growing revenue pool across three streams significantly exceeds comparable infrastructure service margins. The cargo consortium's committed $6M/year alone provides AT&T $2.4M in Year 1 (40% share) declining to $1.2M by Year 8 (20% share) — from a single tenant category on a single revenue stream.
mitigations.residual_risk: AT&T's Year 5 renegotiation exposure is real — DFW will have leverage. However, AT&T also gains leverage through 5 years of operational data, institutional knowledge, and the switching cost DFW faces. The Year 7 no-fault exit is a standard commercial protection that exists in virtually every major airport technology contract. AT&T's risk is that it becomes a commodity — but that risk exists regardless of this contract's specific terms.

mitigations.response: This is the most technically substantive AT&T vulnerability and it matters to cargo directly because sustained DPA events affect our automation SLAs. The core issue is real: FCC enforcement targets the SAS registrant, and contractual liability shifting between DFW and AT&T does not bind the FCC. However, AT&T's scenario overstates the operational impact and understates the consensus safeguards. First, the priority hierarchy's guaranteed bandwidth floors are defined as minimums during NORMAL operations. The degradation model explicitly contemplates reduced capacity during DPA events — cargo AGVs go to reduced-speed mode at ≤50ms (seconds 30-300) and safe-stop mode at ≤75ms (sustained DPA). These degradation modes were designed precisely for DPA scenarios. The hierarchy does not demand 'full allocation of normal-operations bandwidth' during DPA — it demands the degradation-mode allocations, which are engineered to fit within post-DPA capacity. Second, AT&T's 'technical advisory authority' with written override mechanism is not window dressing — it creates a documented paper trail where DFW assumes explicit SLA liability. If DFW issues an override that causes FCC issues, DFW has contractually accepted the consequences, and AT&T has documentary evidence of objection for any FCC inquiry. Third, NAS Fort Worth JRB DPA events are characterized and predictable. AT&T's SAS coordination team has years of DPA response data for the DFW metro area. Network capacity planning should be designed with DPA headroom — this is a design-time engineering requirement, not a runtime governance problem. That said, I support a modification: the consensus should include a DPA capacity engineering requirement specifying that the network must be designed to deliver all degradation-mode minimums (including cargo AGV ≤75ms and cold chain continuity) under a worst-case sustained DPA scenario affecting 60% of CBRS channels. This converts the runtime governance question into a design-time engineering specification that AT&T validates before Phase 0.5 go-live.
mitigations.evidence: NTIA/FCC data shows DPA events in the DFW metro area average 2-4 per month, with typical duration of 30-90 minutes. Sustained events exceeding 4 hours occur approximately 2-3 times annually. CBRS deployments at other airports near military installations (San Diego/NAS North Island, Virginia Beach/NAS Oceana) have successfully operated with DPA headroom engineering. The cargo consortium's cold chain failover to backup telemetry at 5-second threshold provides an independent safety net that does not depend on CBRS capacity at all — our pharmaceutical compliance monitoring has a dedicated backup path precisely because we cannot accept any single point of failure for FDA/EU GDP compliance.
mitigations.residual_risk: A simultaneous worst-case DPA (60%+ channel evacuation) plus maximum cargo automation load plus full terminal passenger load could theoretically exceed engineered DPA headroom. The modification I'm proposing — design-time DPA capacity requirement — reduces this to an engineering problem with a testable acceptance criterion rather than a runtime governance dispute. Residual risk is that DPA patterns change if NAS JRB operational tempo increases, but this is addressable through annual capacity reassessment.

mitigations.response: The CWP degradation halt authority is irrelevant to the cargo zone deployment, and AT&T's attempt to characterize it as a schedule weapon misrepresents the cargo zone's physical and logical independence. The cargo facilities are physically separated from passenger terminals by 1-3 miles. Menzies, dnata, FedEx, and UPS warehouses are standalone structures on the cargo campus south/southeast of the terminal complex. There are zero CWP access points in our warehouses — the CWP serves passenger terminals. The 50-meter buffer zone, 72-hour halt authority, and two-event project pause are terminal-zone mechanisms that do not apply to cargo zone deployment by their own terms ('in the affected terminal zone'). Phase 0 passive infrastructure embedding and Phase 0.5 active deployment in cargo warehouses proceed on an independent track that is contractually insulated from terminal CWP transition activities. This was the entire purpose of the independent cargo zone deployment option (my Hard Constraint HC-1). For the terminal deployments, the attribution methodology concern has merit but is manageable. AT&T maintains the CWP and has full telemetry access — AT&T is better positioned than any party to distinguish between RF environment changes caused by passenger volume fluctuation versus new 5G equipment interference. The 90-day baseline captures variability but should be supplemented with historical CWP performance data AT&T already possesses from years of operation. The 200 bps penalty for AT&T-attributable delays requires 'joint root-cause attribution' with binding arbitration within 30 days — this is not a unilateral DFW weapon. AT&T has equal voice in attribution. The scenario described — a Thanksgiving passenger surge causing a CWP dip attributed to small cell energization — would fail the joint attribution process because AT&T can produce historical Thanksgiving CWP performance data showing similar dips in prior years without any 5G equipment present.
mitigations.evidence: DFW cargo facilities (Menzies at South Cargo, dnata at East Cargo, FedEx/UPS at their dedicated facilities) are physically 1.5-3 miles from the nearest passenger terminal CWP access points. RF propagation at CBRS frequencies (3.5 GHz) attenuates to negligible levels over these distances, especially through intervening structures. The cargo zone's independent timeline was the first and most non-negotiable element I brought to this negotiation — I would not have agreed to any framework that made our $15M investment and construction timeline hostage to terminal-zone CWP politics. The consensus explicitly authorizes cargo Phase 0 passive infrastructure within 45 days independently of any terminal deployment gate.
mitigations.residual_risk: For terminal deployments specifically, the attribution methodology could benefit from a formal statistical framework (e.g., requiring 95% confidence interval that degradation is 5G-attributable before triggering a halt, based on historical CWP variance data). This is a refinement to the terminal deployment process that does not affect cargo timeline. I would support this as a minor modification to protect overall project momentum, but it is not a cargo-zone concern.

mitigations.response: The airline VP raises a legitimate correlated-failure scenario, and as the cargo director I take this seriously because DPA events affect cargo automation SLAs as well. However, I want to reframe the risk and defend the consensus's fundamental adequacy while proposing a targeted enhancement. First, the consensus already addresses DPA impact on cargo with specific degradation modes: AGV reduced-speed (≤50ms) at 30-300 seconds, safe-stop (≤75ms) during sustained DPA, and cold chain failover to backup telemetry at 5-second threshold. These are not aspirational — they are engineered operational modes that our warehouse automation vendors (Vanderlande, Siemens, BEUMER) design into their AGV control systems. AGVs at reduced speed still move cargo. Safe-stop preserves vehicle positions and queue state for rapid resume. Cold chain backup telemetry operates on an independent cellular or satellite path that does not rely on CBRS spectrum at all. For the airline's DPA + ground-stop compound scenario, the core issue is that 'full allocation' during degradation needs an absolute floor, not just a relative priority. I support the airline's implicit request for a modification: define an absolute minimum throughput floor (e.g., 25 Mbps/gate) that triggers automatic capacity reservation from lower tiers during DPA events, rather than relying solely on relative priority. This can be engineered by reserving dedicated CBRS channels for Tier 0-1 traffic that are only released to lower tiers when DPA is not active. Additionally, the 30-second cellular failover should be supplemented with a DFW-contracted FirstNet priority lane for airline ramp devices — AT&T's own FirstNet Band 14 provides prioritized access that is NOT subject to macro network congestion during passenger surges. The consensus should specify that airline failover SIMs are FirstNet-enabled, which AT&T can provide at marginal cost given AT&T's FirstNet contract.
mitigations.evidence: NAS Fort Worth JRB DPA events affecting DFW CBRS channels are well-characterized: 2-4 monthly events with 30-90 minute typical duration. Simultaneous DPA + ground-stop events are rare but estimable — severe convective weather triggering ground stops typically occurs during summer afternoon thunderstorms, while DPA events are driven by military radar exercises that are generally scheduled and avoidable during known severe weather windows (military aircraft don't fly in severe convection either). The cargo consortium's independent backup telemetry for cold chain demonstrates the principle: critical systems need independent fallback paths. FirstNet Band 14 priority access provides this for airline ramp operations — AT&T operates FirstNet nationally and can provision priority SIMs for DFW airline devices as part of the carrier-operations contract.
mitigations.residual_risk: A true simultaneous DPA + ground-stop release + macro congestion event is a low-probability, high-impact scenario that the consensus addresses through layered degradation but cannot fully eliminate. The proposed modifications (absolute throughput floors during DPA, FirstNet failover SIMs) reduce the residual risk to a scenario where DPA evacuates >80% of channels AND FirstNet Band 14 is simultaneously degraded — an extremely unlikely compound failure. The airline's $1.49M loss estimate for a single event, even if accurate, represents 0.001% of the airline's DFW annual revenue — a risk that is more efficiently addressed through operational reserves than further network overengineering.

mitigations.response: The airline VP is right that airline pricing is less specified than cargo or concession pricing, and that the Terminal F prepaid access fee structure needs clarity on reversion rights. However, this is a negotiation gap that the consensus framework explicitly accommodates through the 5-year LOI mechanism — airline pricing specifics are to be finalized within 30 days as binding letters, just as cargo pricing was finalized through our binding commitment letters. The consensus provides the framework; the specific rate card is a bilateral airline-DFW negotiation that occurs at the same stage as cargo's binding letters. From the cargo consortium's perspective, our pricing demonstrates the model works when the tenant engages constructively. We negotiated $0.25/sq ft/month with explicit SLA guarantees, 5-year binding terms, $15M prepaid investment with clear infrastructure ownership by DFW, and edge compute allocation included. We accepted DFW ownership because we are paying for a SERVICE — automation-grade connectivity — not buying an ASSET. Our $15M funds infrastructure we use for 5+ years at a known cost. If DFW terminates at Year 7, our warehouses retain the passive infrastructure (conduit, cable trays, fiber raceways) that we funded in Phase 0 at our own cost ($200-400K), and any successor network can utilize DFW-owned active infrastructure that our prepaid fees helped fund. The airline should negotiate analogous protections: Terminal F prepaid fees should vest over the 5-year minimum term with a pro-rata refund mechanism if DFW exercises no-fault exit before full vesting. The 'airline-tagged in asset registry' language should be supplemented with a right-of-first-refusal on Terminal F infrastructure if DFW ever transfers or decommissions it. These are standard co-investment protections that the cargo consortium would support as precedent-consistent with our own structure.
mitigations.evidence: The cargo consortium's binding commitment letters demonstrate the pricing specificity achievable within this framework: $0.25/sq ft/month, 6 anchor tenants, 5-year minimum, $15M prepaid, $6M/year committed. This level of specificity was negotiated within the consensus framework — the airline has the same opportunity. Industry precedent: Delta's private network at Atlanta involved $10M+ airline co-investment with 7-year amortization and defined reversion terms — a directly applicable model for the Terminal F structure.
mitigations.residual_risk: Until the airline's binding letter is executed, airline pricing remains underspecified. However, this is a timing issue, not a structural flaw. The consensus requires the LOI within 30 days — if the airline cannot negotiate acceptable terms within that window, the consensus's committed revenue base (cargo + concessions + AT&T offload floors) still exceeds the $2M net annual savings threshold for Board approval.

mitigations.response: The Terminal C construction/CWP interaction is a real operational challenge, but the consensus addresses it through the pre-authorized construction framework developed during Phase 0 by the joint AT&T RF engineering + DFW network architecture + airline ops technology team. The airline VP is correct that this framework was primarily designed for 5G buildout activities rather than the interaction between building construction and existing RF infrastructure. However, this concern is not unique to the 5G consensus — Terminal C's $3B rebuild would degrade CWP performance with or without any 5G deployment. Steel erection, demolition, and temporary walls alter RF propagation regardless. The relevant question is whether the consensus makes this WORSE, and the answer is: marginally, and with explicit protections. The 72-hour halt mechanism protects against 5G-CAUSED degradation specifically. Construction-caused degradation to CWP is a pre-existing AT&T/DFW operational issue that exists independently of this consensus and should be addressed in the Terminal C construction contract's RF management plan — which AT&T, as CWP operator, should already be developing with the Terminal C construction manager. The consensus actually IMPROVES the Terminal C situation by accelerating the timeline to deploy 5G in rebuilt sections, which provides a modern, construction-aware wireless infrastructure that replaces the CWP in completed areas rather than depending on aging CWP APs in an actively changing RF environment. From the cargo perspective, I want Terminal C 5G operational as fast as possible because connecting cargo flows through Terminal C gates (international cargo often clears CBP in terminal-adjacent facilities). The pre-authorized construction framework should include a parallel RF monitoring protocol that distinguishes between construction-zone RF changes (expected, managed by construction RF plan) and 5G-transition RF changes (subject to halt mechanism). This is a refinement to the Phase 0 framework deliverable, not a structural change to the consensus.
mitigations.evidence: DFW's Terminal D renovation (2005-2019, multiple phases) required continuous RF management of the existing DAS during active construction. AT&T and its predecessors managed CWP performance through construction-zone RF modeling, temporary AP relocations, and progressive coverage handoff — the same operational discipline applies to Terminal C. The pre-authorized construction framework explicitly includes AT&T RF engineering as a co-developer, ensuring AT&T's institutional knowledge of CWP behavior during construction is embedded in the framework. The cargo consortium's experience with warehouse construction RF planning (Menzies/dnata facilities currently under construction with RF co-design underway) demonstrates that construction-phase RF management is a solved engineering problem when the wireless partner is engaged during design.
mitigations.residual_risk: Terminal C will have a 12-18 month period where construction-zone RF changes and 5G transition activities overlap in ways that challenge clean attribution. The joint framework team should develop a statistical attribution model during Phase 0 that accounts for construction-phase RF variance. The airline should negotiate a construction-period SLA supplement that provides operational support (e.g., temporary mobile coverage units for affected gate areas) funded from the Terminal C construction budget, not the 5G deployment budget.

mitigations.response: The per-terminal SLA measurement concern is valid and the cargo consortium's experience directly informs a fix. We insisted on per-WAREHOUSE-ZONE measurement (HC-3: 99.99% per warehouse zone) precisely because we understood that facility-wide averaging masks localized failures. A warehouse zone for us is approximately 200,000-400,000 sq ft — comparable to a 6-8 gate cluster in Terminal F. The cargo SLA model should be the template for airline SLA refinement. I support modifying the airline SLA to include a sub-terminal measurement tier. Specifically: maintain 99.99% per-terminal as the aggregate SLA with $50K/terminal/incident penalty, but ADD a per-gate-cluster SLA (e.g., groups of 4-6 gates sharing an edge compute node) at 99.95% with a separate penalty mechanism tied to affected-flight delay costs. This is architecturally feasible because the consensus already specifies edge compute nodes serving gate clusters — each edge compute node's service area is a natural SLA measurement boundary. The synthetic monitoring infrastructure (50+ devices per terminal, 15-second polling) should be distributed to ensure minimum 2 synthetic probes per gate area, with application-layer metrics (not just connectivity) including DCS response time, baggage reconciliation feed latency, and crew communication round-trip time. The edge compute memory leak scenario described is a real failure mode that connectivity monitoring misses. Application-layer synthetic transactions — a simulated baggage scan, a simulated DCS query — would detect the 18ms-to-180ms latency degradation within one polling cycle. This is standard practice in the cargo automation monitoring we've specified: our SLA includes ≤0.001% packet loss AND specific latency thresholds, measured at the APPLICATION layer (AGV control response, cold chain sensor acknowledgment), not just network layer.
mitigations.evidence: The cargo consortium's SLA structure (99.99% per warehouse zone, ≤20ms AGV, ≤100ms cold chain, ≤0.001% packet loss, ≥99.95% handover, 1,000+ devices per warehouse) was specifically designed to prevent facility-wide averaging from masking localized failures. Amazon's fulfillment center private wireless deployments use zone-level SLA measurement at approximately 100,000 sq ft granularity — directly comparable to a 4-6 gate cluster. AWS Private 5G and Nokia DAC both support per-zone SLA monitoring as standard features. The consensus already specifies 'jointly selected' synthetic monitoring vendor — the selection criteria should include per-zone and application-layer monitoring capabilities.
mitigations.residual_risk: Adding sub-terminal SLA tiers increases monitoring complexity and may increase the frequency of SLA credit claims. However, this is a feature, not a bug — granular SLA measurement creates granular accountability that drives better network operations. The cost of enhanced monitoring infrastructure (~$50-100K per terminal for application-layer synthetic probes) is trivial against the $8-12M Terminal F investment.

mitigations.response: The inter-operator handoff concern for split carrier-operations across terminals is legitimate and requires attention, but it does not invalidate the competitive evaluation structure. From the cargo consortium's perspective, we operate across multiple warehouse facilities that could theoretically be served by different carrier-operations vendors — the same architectural challenge. Our mitigation is embedded in the consensus: DFW owns ALL physical and logical infrastructure below the carrier-operations API layer, including SDN controllers, switches, routers, and the tenant slice policy layer. The airline's cryptographically isolated slice is defined and managed at the DFW infrastructure layer, not the carrier-operations layer. A consistent slice policy — same QoS, same ACLs, same device provisioning — is enforced by DFW-owned SDN controllers regardless of which vendor manages radio resources in a given terminal. The carrier-operations layer (SAS coordination, radio resource scheduling) operates BENEATH the slice policy layer in the abstraction hierarchy. An iPad moving from Terminal C to Terminal F should experience the same slice policy because the same DFW-owned infrastructure enforces it. The handoff between carrier-operations vendors is a radio-layer handoff managed by the DFW-owned SDN and orchestration layer, not a slice re-authentication. That said, the consensus should specify inter-operator handoff requirements explicitly. I propose adding to the Terminal F evaluation criteria: any carrier-operations vendor for Terminal F must demonstrate ≤500ms inter-terminal handoff with the incumbent terminal carrier-operations vendor, tested with airline devices under operational conditions. This becomes a binding acceptance criterion, not just an evaluation factor. The airline's single-pane-of-glass API/portal for slice management should be contractually guaranteed to present a unified view regardless of underlying carrier-operations vendor, with DFW (not AT&T or Betacom) accountable for cross-terminal continuity.
mitigations.evidence: Multi-vendor RAN environments are increasingly common in enterprise private 5G: the O-RAN Alliance's open interface specifications (E2, A1, O1) are specifically designed to enable multi-vendor radio networks under unified orchestration. DFW's architecture — with DFW-owned SDN controllers and a documented carrier-operations API — is more advanced than most enterprise deployments in its abstraction of carrier operations from tenant policy. The cargo consortium's own multi-warehouse architecture assumes consistent slice behavior across facilities regardless of which vendor manages radio resources in each warehouse. 3GPP Release 17 network slicing specifications support cross-domain slice continuity with ≤200ms handoff targets. The 4.2-second handoff described in the failure scenario would indicate a fundamental implementation failure, not an inherent architectural limitation.
mitigations.residual_risk: Inter-operator handoff performance depends on both vendors implementing the DFW carrier-operations API correctly. Real-world inter-vendor handoff will likely be 200-800ms (vs. 50-100ms intra-vendor) until the API is mature. For a bag tug traveling at 15 mph, an 800ms handoff results in ~5 meters of travel during handoff — unlikely to drop an application session but possible to cause a brief interruption. The binding acceptance criterion (≤500ms inter-terminal handoff) provides a contractual backstop, and if no vendor can meet it, DFW retains the option to award Terminal F to the incumbent terminal vendor, eliminating the inter-operator scenario entirely.

adjudications.vulnerability_title: Carrier Offload Guarantee Creates Unhedgeable Demand Risk for AT&T
adjudications.reasoning: The CRO's quantitative defense is compelling and I find it dispositive. The Year 1 floor of $500K represents $0.0027 per passenger-hour against Boingo's documented $0.008-$0.015 — approximately 25% of proven industry floor rates at comparable airports. The $1M Year 3-5 floor at 87M passengers is still less than 30% of what JFK, LAX, and ORD generate from carrier offload. AT&T's self-offload economics are real: 30-35M AT&T subscribers transit DFW annually, and AT&T's own macro CapEx savings from offloading those subscribers to CBRS infrastructure partially satisfies the guarantee with zero third-party dependency. The 'perverse incentive' argument collapses under the CRO's math — DFW's 55% share of actual offload revenue ($1.65M at a conservative $3M) vastly exceeds the $1M shortfall payment. No rational airport operator prefers the shortfall. From my concessions perspective, AT&T's offload economics are irrelevant to my operators' $175-200/month pricing, but AT&T's financial viability on this deal is relevant to service quality. The floors are conservative enough that AT&T should meet them, and the Year 5 renegotiation provides a structural safety valve if the offload market genuinely shifts. I downgrade from Critical to High because the actual financial exposure ($3.25M cumulative over 5 years) is modest relative to AT&T's overall deal economics.
adjudications.defense_gaps: None material. The satellite-to-device residual risk (Starlink Direct, AST SpaceMobile) is acknowledged but unlikely to materialize before the Year 5 reset given current throughput and latency limitations of satellite-to-handset technology.
adjudications.recommended_action: No modification required. AT&T should price this risk into its co-investment decision. The floors represent AT&T's own negotiated assessment of achievable revenue.

adjudications.vulnerability_title: Parallel Cargo Competitive Evaluation Is a Trojan Horse for Full Displacement
adjudications.reasoning: AT&T is framing competitive discipline as a threat, which is itself revealing. The Cargo Director's defense is blunt and correct: the consortium is committing $15M prepaid and $6M/year in binding revenue, and it will not hand exclusive carrier-operations rights to any vendor without competitive validation. The evaluation structure is generous to AT&T by any procurement standard — 30% incumbent weight exceeds federal procurement norms (5-10% under FAR Part 15) by 3-6x. AT&T's interim operational authority during Phase 0.5 provides paid production data that no competitor can replicate from trial nodes in non-production zones. The 40% technical capability weight advantages AT&T's enterprise-scale SAS registration, FCC compliance infrastructure, and nationwide CBRS deployment experience over systems integrators like Betacom. If AT&T cannot win with a 30-point incumbency advantage, existing SIDA badges, SAS registration, and 6-9 months of production operational data, then the airport's tenants — including my 200+ concession locations — are better served by a vendor that can. From my perspective as concessions tech lead: I don't care who operates the carrier layer, I care that my POS systems process transactions at 99.9%. Competition ensures the best operator wins, which benefits my operators. The CRO correctly notes that the competitive evaluation is what secured the cargo consortium's binding $6M/year commitment — removing competition would have yielded lower committed revenue and weaker Board approval support.
adjudications.defense_gaps: Minor: The 90-day transition timeline if an alternative vendor wins is ambitious for production warehouse automation. Should include a parallel operations period (30 days minimum) where both vendors operate simultaneously before full cutover.
adjudications.recommended_action: Add 30-day parallel operations requirement to the cargo carrier-operations transition protocol. No structural change to the competitive evaluation framework.

adjudications.vulnerability_title: AT&T Capital Obligations Compound While Revenue Share Declines to Subsistence Levels
adjudications.reasoning: The CRO's revenue projection ($25-38M cumulative AT&T revenue against $17-24M investment) is credible but depends on adoption timeline assumptions that are optimistic for Years 1-3. Specifically: concession revenue assumes 200+ locations onboarded by Month 36, but my own experience managing 60 operators tells me that migration of 200+ locations — including ACDBE small businesses that can barely manage a POS system — will take longer than projected. Not every operator will migrate on schedule. Some will resist. Some will need hand-holding that extends timelines. Airline revenue depends on a binding LOI within 30 days that is not yet executed. If concession onboarding takes 18 months past GA (Month 24) instead of 12, and if airline pricing negotiations extend to Month 18 instead of Month 6, AT&T's early-year revenue is materially lower than projected. The reference-account value ($50-100M in influenced pipeline) is real but unquantifiable for a Board presentation. The declining revenue share structure is standard and defensible in principle — but the CRO's defense does not adequately address AT&T's Year 5 renegotiation vulnerability. AT&T will arrive at Year 5 having spent $12-15M with $10-15M in cumulative revenue — roughly breakeven — and DFW will hold all the leverage. If AT&T's board calculates that the next 5 years offer diminishing returns, AT&T could disengage operationally (quiet quitting) rather than formally exit, which degrades service quality for my operators.
adjudications.defense_gaps: 1) Early-year revenue projections depend on aggressive tenant onboarding timelines that are unproven. 2) No contractual mechanism prevents AT&T operational disengagement short of SLA breach — 'quiet quitting' where AT&T meets minimum SLAs but underinvests in proactive maintenance and optimization. 3) The Year 5 renegotiation power asymmetry is acknowledged but unmitigated — AT&T's switching costs are lower than DFW's, giving AT&T walk-away leverage that partially offsets DFW's ownership leverage.
adjudications.recommended_action: 1) Add Year 3 financial health check with AT&T revenue reconciliation against projections — if AT&T cumulative revenue is below 70% of consensus projections, trigger an early revenue share adjustment discussion (not full renegotiation) to ensure AT&T remains economically motivated. 2) Add proactive service quality metrics beyond SLA minimums — network optimization cadence, firmware currency, capacity planning responsiveness — with contractual service credits for degraded proactive performance. 3) Ensure Year 5 renegotiation terms include AT&T's right to propose upward share adjustments if market conditions warrant, making the renegotiation genuinely symmetric.

adjudications.vulnerability_title: DPA Liability Trap — AT&T Bears FCC Compliance Risk Under Governance It Cannot Control
adjudications.reasoning: This is the most technically substantive vulnerability in the entire exercise, and both the CRO and Cargo Director correctly acknowledge it as a genuine gap requiring modification. The core issue is irrefutable: FCC Part 96 enforcement targets the SAS registrant (AT&T), not the building owner, and contractual liability allocation between DFW and AT&T does not bind the FCC. The CRO's proposed DPA Emergency Capacity Protocol is well-designed: graduated capacity thresholds, proportional allocation at Level 2, bright-line carve-out preventing DFW overrides that require FCC non-compliance. This is the right framework. The Cargo Director's complementary proposal — design-time DPA capacity engineering requiring the network to deliver all degradation-mode minimums under worst-case sustained DPA — converts the runtime governance problem into a testable engineering specification, which is even better. However, neither modification has been drafted, agreed, or incorporated. The residual risk identified by the CRO — that DFW's entire private 5G value proposition depends on spectrum that is federally interruptible — is fundamental and permanent. CBRS is Priority Access / General Authorized Access spectrum subordinate to Incumbent Access by law. No contractual framework changes this. From my concessions perspective: my 1 Mbps degradation floor during DPA events is achievable within post-DPA capacity if the network is properly engineered with DPA headroom. But the consensus does not currently require that headroom engineering as a binding design specification. That is the gap.
adjudications.defense_gaps: 1) The DPA Emergency Capacity Protocol is proposed but not drafted or agreed — it must be incorporated as a binding sub-exhibit to Exhibit A before contract execution. 2) No binding requirement that network capacity engineering includes DPA headroom sufficient to deliver all degradation-mode minimums under worst-case sustained DPA. 3) The bright-line FCC compliance carve-out must be explicit and unambiguous: DFW cannot issue any override that requires AT&T to exceed FCC-authorized power levels or operate on evacuated channels during active DPA. Period. 4) No provision for supplemental licensed spectrum as DPA insurance for Tiers 0-2 — the CRO acknowledges this as a Year 3+ discussion but it should be a Phase 2 planning requirement.
adjudications.recommended_action: MANDATORY pre-execution modifications: 1) Draft and incorporate the DPA Emergency Capacity Protocol as Exhibit A-1 to the MSA, including graduated capacity thresholds, proportional allocation rules, and the bright-line FCC compliance carve-out. 2) Add binding design specification requiring network to deliver all degradation-mode minimums (including my Tier 4 POS 1 Mbps floor) under sustained DPA affecting 60% of CBRS channels — validated by AT&T RF engineering and independently verified before Phase 0.5 go-live. 3) Require Phase 2 planning to evaluate supplemental licensed spectrum for Tier 0-1 traffic as DPA insurance. 4) Add explicit language that AT&T's FCC compliance obligations supersede any DFW governance directive — this protects everyone, because an FCC enforcement action against AT&T at DFW would shut down the entire private 5G deployment.

adjudications.vulnerability_title: CWP Degradation Halt Authority Creates Asymmetric Schedule Weapon Against AT&T
adjudications.reasoning: The CRO's proposed modifications — seasonal normalization model, supplementation with AT&T's 5+ years of historical CWP data, joint attribution methodology with independent RF adjudicator, and limiting the 200 bps penalty to AT&T-attributed delays only — are comprehensive and technically sound. The Cargo Director correctly notes the cargo zone is physically separated and unaffected. The core defense — that the CIO has zero incentive to weaponize CWP halts because every halt delays revenue — is logically valid. From my concessions perspective, I am deeply aligned with the CRO: CWP halts delay my Phase 1 pilot (Months 3-9) and Phase 2 general availability (Month 24), which delays POS connectivity for my 200+ locations. I am the last stakeholder who wants spurious halts. However, the modifications are proposed, not agreed. The attribution methodology must be specified BEFORE contract execution, not developed 'during Phase 0' as a best-effort deliverable. If Phase 0 produces a contested attribution methodology, the 72-hour halt mechanism becomes immediately problematic as soon as Phase 1 begins. The independent RF adjudicator model is proven (LAX, ORD Boingo transitions) and should be contractually committed at signing, not deferred.
adjudications.defense_gaps: 1) Attribution methodology is deferred to Phase 0 development — it should be specified in principle at contract execution with Phase 0 refinement. 2) Independent RF adjudicator must be pre-selected (or selection criteria defined) at signing, not after a dispute arises. 3) The 200 bps penalty trigger should explicitly require the joint root-cause attribution process to complete before any penalty accrues — not retroactive application after attribution. 4) No explicit provision for passenger-volume normalization in the CWP baseline, which is the most common source of false-positive degradation attribution.
adjudications.recommended_action: 1) Include attribution methodology framework (not full specification) as an MSA exhibit with defined principles: temporal correlation required, proximate 5G activity required, passenger-volume normalization required, seasonal adjustment required. Phase 0 refines the parameters within this framework. 2) Pre-select or define selection criteria for independent RF adjudicator at contract signing. 3) Specify that 200 bps penalty clock starts only after joint attribution process concludes AT&T causation — no retroactive application. 4) Require AT&T to contribute 5 years of historical CWP performance data to the baseline model within 30 days of contract execution.

adjudications.vulnerability_title: DPA + Ground-Stop Release Creates Unrecoverable Dual-Failure at Peak Ramp Intensity
adjudications.reasoning: This is the most operationally sophisticated attack in the exercise. The airline VP correctly identifies that DPA response and cellular failover are correlated failure modes at DFW, not independent ones — contradicting the consensus assumption in H12. The CRO's probability analysis (1-3 joint events per year) understates the risk because it assumes negative correlation between DPA and weather, when in fact NAS JRB conducts training exercises year-round including during convective weather seasons. The CRO's proposed licensed-spectrum failover ($4-5M across 5 terminals + Terminal F) is the right architectural solution — a dedicated LTE/5G macro small-cell cluster on licensed spectrum (not CBRS) at each terminal ramp area, immune to DPA, providing guaranteed 10 Mbps/gate during DPA events. The Cargo Director's FirstNet Band 14 suggestion is complementary and leverages AT&T's existing infrastructure. However, this is a $4-5M unfunded capital commitment that must be allocated. DFW infrastructure CapEx seems appropriate since the failover protects DFW's overall network value proposition. From my concessions perspective: the DPA + ground-stop scenario also affects my Tier 4 POS traffic — during this event, POS drops to the 1 Mbps degradation floor or worse. A licensed-spectrum backup for airline ramp operations frees up residual CBRS capacity for my POS locations during DPA events. I support this modification because it indirectly improves my operators' experience during degradation events. The gap between SLA credits ($200K per the attack's math) and actual operational losses ($1.49M) is expected and appropriate — SLA credits are accountability mechanisms, not insurance. But the 7x gap should be narrowed, or the airline will never have confidence in the platform.
adjudications.defense_gaps: 1) Licensed-spectrum failover is proposed but unfunded — no allocation in the consensus financial model. Must be committed as DFW infrastructure CapEx with clear cost allocation before Board approval. 2) FirstNet Band 14 priority SIM provisioning for airline ramp devices must be an explicit AT&T contractual obligation, not a suggestion. 3) The consensus does not define absolute throughput floors during DPA — only relative priority. Must add: Tier 1 minimum 25 Mbps/gate during DPA events (from licensed-spectrum failover), not just 'full allocation' of degraded CBRS. 4) No inter-event recovery protocol — after a DPA + ground-stop compound event, the network needs a defined recovery sequence to return all tiers to normal operations in priority order. 5) SLA credit structure should include a compound-event multiplier for correlated failures affecting >50% of campus simultaneously.
adjudications.recommended_action: MANDATORY pre-execution modifications: 1) Commit $4-5M licensed-spectrum failover infrastructure as DFW CapEx, with cost allocation across tenant categories proportional to tier priority (airline bears largest share through network service fees). 2) Require AT&T to provision FirstNet Band 14 priority SIMs for airline ramp devices as a contract deliverable by Phase 1. 3) Add absolute minimum throughput floors during DPA events to Exhibit A degradation model: Tier 1 ≥25 Mbps/gate (from licensed-spectrum failover), Tier 4 POS ≥1 Mbps (from CBRS residual). 4) Define post-compound-event recovery protocol with time-sequenced tier restoration. 5) Deploy licensed-spectrum failover by Phase 2 completion (Month 21) at latest — before Terminal F opens.

adjudications.vulnerability_title: Airline Pricing Structure is Dangerously Underspecified Relative to $8-12M Terminal F Commitment
adjudications.reasoning: The airline VP is correct: airline pricing is materially less specified than concession or cargo pricing, and the Terminal F prepaid access structure lacks basic reversion protections. My own concession pricing is fully defined — $175-200/month, all-inclusive, month-to-month, CPI/3% cap, most-favored-tenant, 10-day activation, zero CapEx. The cargo consortium has $0.25/sq ft/month with explicit SLAs and 5-year binding terms. The airline has 'Hybrid base + per-departure variable (base ≤60% of expected annual cost)' with undefined rates, undefined escalation, undefined reversion, and no exit flexibility. This asymmetry is problematic for two reasons: (1) The airline's LOI is a binding constraint for Board approval per HC-12 and H-R14 — if airline pricing remains underspecified, the LOI cannot convert to a binding commitment, and the Board lacks the committed revenue assurance it requires. (2) The airline is 80%+ of departures — if the airline walks over pricing, the entire deal loses its most critical stakeholder. The CRO's proposed modifications are comprehensive: $35/departure cap with CPI/3% escalation, 10-year amortization with refund on early termination, traffic reduction protection, symmetric Year 7 exit, airline Year 5 renegotiation right. These are all reasonable and necessary. From my concessions perspective: I need this deal to close. My 200+ locations need POS connectivity by Month 24. The airline's commitment provides the revenue anchor that sustains the network. If airline pricing negotiations stall, the entire deployment timeline is at risk. The CRO should resolve airline pricing terms to the same specificity as concessions and cargo BEFORE Board approval.
adjudications.defense_gaps: 1) All proposed modifications are bilateral negotiation proposals — none are agreed. The 30-day LOI timeline is aggressive for a commitment of this magnitude ($85-95M over 5 years). 2) The $35/departure cap is a CRO proposal — the airline has not agreed. The actual rate will emerge from bilateral negotiation. 3) Terminal F prepaid amortization with refund is reasonable but must address what happens if the airline reduces operations (not just DFW terminates). Gate reassignment rights and proportional credit application need bilateral agreement. 4) No defined methodology for calculating 'expected annual cost' — the base component (60%) depends on this undefined calculation. 5) The most-favored-tenant clause applies to concessions but not to airlines — the airline should receive equivalent protection against DFW offering a better deal to a competing carrier.
adjudications.recommended_action: 1) Extend the LOI deadline from 30 days to 60 days to allow adequate bilateral negotiation, but make Board approval conditional on LOI execution. 2) Define 'expected annual cost' methodology in the MSA framework — based on prior-year actual departures projected forward with a defined growth assumption, subject to annual true-up. 3) Require the airline pricing term sheet to achieve the same specificity as concession pricing (rate, cap, escalation, exit, reversion) before Board vote. 4) Include Terminal F prepaid amortization schedule with pro-rata refund mechanism as a binding term, modeled on the Cargo Director's suggestion of cargo co-investment precedent. 5) From my concessions perspective: ensure airline pricing negotiations do not delay Phase 1 pilot or Phase 2 GA timeline — these milestones should be contractually independent of airline LOI status.

adjudications.vulnerability_title: Terminal C Multi-Year Construction Creates Rolling CWP Degradation Trap
adjudications.reasoning: The airline correctly identifies that Terminal C's 3-year $3B rebuild creates an RF environment where construction-caused and 5G-caused CWP degradation will be difficult to distinguish. The CRO's proposed modifications — dynamic monthly RF baseline, construction-phase RF sensors, pre-modeled structural RF impact assessments, and construction-adjusted degradation triggers — are technically sound and supported by DFW Terminal D precedent. The Cargo Director's point that construction-caused CWP degradation exists independently of the 5G deployment is valid — this is not a risk created by the consensus; it is a pre-existing risk that the consensus marginally compounds. From my concessions perspective: Terminal C has concession locations. My operators in Terminal C need functioning POS connectivity during the 3-year rebuild, whether that connectivity comes from CWP or 5G. The accelerated deployment of 5G in rebuilt Terminal C sections actually HELPS my operators by providing modern connectivity sooner rather than depending on aging CWP in an actively changing RF environment. The real risk is the transition period where neither CWP nor 5G is reliable in construction-adjacent areas. The 72-hour halt mechanism for Terminal C should use the dynamic baseline as the CRO proposes — measuring degradation against post-construction-change expected performance, not the pre-construction ideal. This eliminates false-positive halts from structural RF changes while preserving genuine 5G-transition protections.
adjudications.defense_gaps: 1) Dynamic RF baselining adds $200-300K/year for 3 years — must be budgeted. 2) The pre-authorized construction framework is developed during Phase 0 by a joint team, but the Terminal C general contractor may not be fully engaged at Phase 0 (construction timelines may not align perfectly). 3) No explicit provision for temporary mobile coverage units to serve affected gate areas and concession locations during construction-zone RF degradation periods. 4) My concession locations in Terminal C need a specific commitment: if CWP degrades in a concession area due to construction (not 5G), what is the remediation obligation and who pays? The consensus addresses 5G-caused degradation to CWP but not construction-caused degradation to CWP that affects concession POS.
adjudications.recommended_action: 1) Budget $200-300K/year for Terminal C dynamic RF monitoring during the construction period. 2) Require AT&T to develop a Terminal C CWP resilience plan addressing construction-caused RF changes (separate from 5G transition protections) — this is AT&T's obligation as CWP operator regardless of the 5G deployment. 3) Include temporary coverage provisions (mobile coverage units or accelerated 5G deployment in affected areas) for Terminal C concession locations experiencing construction-caused CWP degradation. 4) Ensure Phase 0 framework development engages the Terminal C general contractor's RF consultant from Day 1.

adjudications.vulnerability_title: 99.99% Per-Terminal SLA Masks Sub-Gate Failure Modes in 31-Gate Terminal F
adjudications.reasoning: The measurement-granularity argument is valid and the CRO's proposed modifications (per-gate-cluster SLA at 99.95%, application-layer metrics, edge compute N+1 redundancy, $10K per gate-cluster per degradation event) are well-designed. The Cargo Director's experience with per-warehouse-zone SLAs provides direct architectural precedent. From my concessions perspective, I have the granularity I need — my SLA is already 99.9% per LOCATION, measured at the CPE gateway. Every one of my 200+ locations has an independent SLA measurement point. The airline's per-terminal measurement is coarser than what I negotiated, and the airline should bring its measurement to comparable granularity. The edge compute memory leak scenario is a real failure mode. My CPE gateways could also be affected by an edge compute failure if multiple concession locations share an edge compute node — but my per-location measurement at the CPE would detect the degradation even if a terminal-level metric did not. I support the airline's modification because better SLA granularity for all tenants improves overall network accountability, which benefits my operators.
adjudications.defense_gaps: 1) Per-gate-cluster SLA adds monitoring infrastructure cost ($50-100K per terminal) that must be budgeted. 2) Application-layer metrics require careful definition to avoid disputes over network-caused vs. application-caused performance issues. The monitoring must clearly delineate root cause at the network/application boundary. 3) The 99.95% per-gate-cluster threshold needs validation against actual operational variance — 22 minutes of allowed downtime per month per cluster may be too tight or too loose depending on typical maintenance windows and scheduled updates. 4) Edge compute N+1 redundancy for Terminal F (minimum 4 nodes with failover) must be specified as a design requirement, not a post-deployment addition.
adjudications.recommended_action: 1) Incorporate per-gate-cluster SLA (99.95%, 4-6 gate clusters per terminal) as a supplementary measurement tier in Exhibit A. 2) Define application-layer synthetic monitoring metrics (DNS resolution, TCP establishment, HTTP transaction completion) as network-boundary measurements, explicitly excluding application-backend latency from network SLA scope. 3) Require edge compute N+1 redundancy as a binding design specification for Terminal F and all Phase 4+ terminal migrations. 4) Budget monitoring infrastructure costs in the deployment capital plan.

adjudications.vulnerability_title: Terminal F Competitive Carrier-Operations Evaluation Creates Operational Risk During Greenfield Launch
adjudications.reasoning: The CRO's architectural defense is technically correct: DFW owns all infrastructure below the carrier-operations API layer, including SDN controllers and slice management. The airline's slice policy is enforced at the DFW infrastructure layer regardless of which vendor manages radio resources in a given terminal. 3GPP Release 17 defines slice continuity as a core-network function. In principle, a bag tug crossing from Terminal C to Terminal F should maintain session continuity because the same DFW-owned SDN manages the handoff. However, the attack raises a legitimate concern about practical implementation. Multi-vendor RAN environments at the O-RAN specification level are still maturing. Real-world inter-vendor handoff at sub-500ms with session continuity for ramp automation applications is unproven at airport scale. The JFK Terminal 4 and LAX Tom Bradley examples cited are DAS deployments, not private 5G sliced networks — the comparison is architecturally imprecise. From my concessions perspective: my POS locations do not move between terminals, so inter-terminal handoff is irrelevant to my operators' daily experience. But if inter-terminal handoff failures undermine airline confidence, the airline could resist the Terminal F competitive evaluation, which could delay Terminal F deployment and the revenue model supporting my ACDBE pricing subsidies. I support specifying inter-operator handoff requirements to remove this as a blocker.
adjudications.defense_gaps: 1) Inter-operator handoff SLA (≤500ms, ≤0.1% failure rate) is proposed but not yet incorporated in the MSA. 2) No defined interoperability testing protocol or duration — 60 days is proposed but not committed. 3) SAS coordination compatibility between vendors is not addressed — if Terminal C uses Federated Wireless SAS and Terminal F uses CommScope SAS, inter-terminal handoff complexity increases materially. 4) Single-point-of-accountability at DFW NOC for cross-terminal issues is proposed but requires staffing and operational capability that DFW's current IT organization may not possess. 5) Fallback position — if no alternative vendor can demonstrate ≤500ms inter-terminal handoff with the incumbent vendor, what happens? The consensus should explicitly preserve the option of awarding Terminal F to the incumbent to avoid the split-brain scenario entirely.
adjudications.recommended_action: 1) Incorporate inter-operator handoff SLA (≤500ms at tug speed, ≤0.1% failure rate, monthly testing) as a binding acceptance criterion in the Terminal F carrier-operations evaluation. 2) Require 60-day interoperability testing period before Terminal F goes live with a non-incumbent vendor, specifically testing inter-terminal handoff with airline devices under operational conditions. 3) Mandate single SAS provider across all terminals as a DFW infrastructure-layer requirement, eliminating SAS coordination mismatch risk. 4) Explicitly state in the evaluation framework that if no alternative vendor meets the inter-operator handoff acceptance criterion, DFW may award Terminal F to the incumbent vendor without further competitive process. 5) Budget DFW NOC staffing for cross-terminal handoff management as an operational cost.

conditions: DPA Emergency Capacity Protocol drafted and incorporated as Exhibit A-1 to the MSA, including graduated capacity thresholds, proportional allocation rules, bright-line FCC compliance carve-out preventing any DFW override requiring AT&T to violate Part 96, and binding design specification requiring the network to deliver all degradation-mode minimums under sustained DPA affecting 60% of CBRS channels

Licensed-spectrum failover infrastructure ($4-5M) committed as DFW CapEx with deployment by Phase 2 completion (Month 21), providing DPA-immune backup for Tier 0-1 ramp operations and additional residual CBRS capacity for Tier 4 POS during DPA events

Airline pricing term sheet achieving concession/cargo-equivalent specificity (defined per-departure rate with cap and escalation, Terminal F prepaid amortization with pro-rata refund mechanism, symmetric Year 7 exit right, defined 'expected annual cost' methodology) executed within 60 days of contract signing as a condition precedent to Board approval

CWP degradation attribution methodology framework incorporated as an MSA exhibit at contract execution, including principles of temporal correlation, proximate 5G activity requirement, passenger-volume normalization, seasonal adjustment, independent RF adjudicator pre-selection criteria, and explicit limitation that the 200 bps penalty accrues only after joint root-cause attribution concludes AT&T causation

Inter-operator handoff SLA (≤500ms, ≤0.1% failure rate) and mandatory 60-day interoperability testing period incorporated as binding acceptance criteria for any non-incumbent Terminal F carrier-operations award, with single-SAS-provider mandate across all terminals and explicit fallback to incumbent if no alternative vendor meets handoff requirements

Per-gate-cluster SLA (99.95%, 4-6 gate clusters per terminal) and edge compute N+1 redundancy incorporated as supplementary SLA tier in Exhibit A, with application-layer synthetic monitoring metrics defined at the network boundary

Concession deployment timeline (Phase 1 pilot Months 3-9, Phase 2 GA Month 24) contractually independent of airline LOI execution status — concession operators cannot be held hostage to airline pricing negotiations
final_recommendation.resolved_risks_summary: Two of ten red team vulnerabilities are fully resolved. AT&T's carrier offload guarantee risk (AT&T-V1) is resolved because the floors represent 18-25% of proven industry benchmarks and AT&T's self-offload economics partially satisfy the guarantee — the Year 5 renegotiation provides a structural safety valve if the offload market shifts. AT&T's competitive evaluation concern (AT&T-V2) is resolved because the evaluation structure is generous to AT&T (30% incumbent weight, interim operational authority, 40% technical capability weighting) and the competitive process is what secured the cargo consortium's binding $6M/year commitment. These two attacks amount to AT&T preferring monopoly terms over competitive accountability, which is not a consensus vulnerability — it is the consensus working as designed.
final_recommendation.open_risks_summary: Eight vulnerabilities are partially resolved, requiring specific plan modifications before contract execution. The two most critical are: (1) the DPA compliance liability gap (AT&T-V4), where the interaction between binding bandwidth floors and extreme DPA capacity reduction scenarios could place AT&T in an impossible position between contractual obligations and FCC compliance — addressed by the DPA Emergency Capacity Protocol modification; and (2) the DPA + ground-stop correlated failure mode (Airline-V1), where the consensus incorrectly treats DPA response and cellular failover as independent systems when they are correlated failure modes at DFW — addressed by licensed-spectrum failover infrastructure. The airline pricing underspecification (Airline-V2) is the highest-risk commercial gap: the airline's $8-12M Terminal F commitment and 80%+ departure share make it the pivotal tenant, and undefined pricing creates genuine walk-away risk. AT&T's capital obligation trajectory (AT&T-V3) presents a medium-term risk of operational disengagement if early-year returns lag projections. The CWP degradation attribution methodology (AT&T-V5), Terminal C construction RF interaction (Airline-V3), per-terminal SLA measurement granularity (Airline-V4), and inter-terminal carrier-operations handoff (Airline-V5) are all addressable with specified modifications but must be incorporated before contract execution rather than deferred to Phase 0 development. No vulnerability is assessed as Open (defense inadequate) — the consensus framework is fundamentally sound, and all identified gaps have credible proposed mitigations. The risk is execution: converting proposed modifications into agreed contractual terms.
final_recommendation.board_narrative:
The DFW Private 5G Consensus Framework represents a well-structured hybrid co-investment model that delivers measurable value across all stakeholder categories. For the F&B concessions consortium — 60 operators, 200+ locations, $4.2B gross product — the consensus delivers exactly what our operators need: turnkey managed POS connectivity at $175-200/month all-inclusive with zero CapEx, a single DFW-accountable support contact, 99.9% per-location SLA with automatic credits, support for existing POS hardware (Aloha, Toast, Square) without replacement, and ACDBE pricing at $80/month ensuring small business operators are not priced out of reliable connectivity. Month-to-month terms with CPI/3% escalation caps protect operators from technology cost inflation, and the 10-day new-location activation supports our 34+ new locations through 2028. These terms are superior to any current Wi-Fi or cellular alternative available to our operators.

The red team stress test identified ten vulnerabilities — two are fully resolved, eight require specific plan modifications before contract execution. The most critical modifications are: (1) a DPA Emergency Capacity Protocol ensuring AT&T's FCC compliance obligations are never compromised by DFW governance directives, with the network designed to deliver degradation-mode minimums under worst-case sustained DPA scenarios; (2) licensed-spectrum failover infrastructure at $4-5M providing DPA-immune backup for safety-critical and airline ramp operations; and (3) airline pricing specificity achieving parity with the concession and cargo rate cards before Board approval. None of these modifications require restructuring the deal — they are targeted amendments that close identified gaps within the existing framework.

The Board should approve this framework as a Conditional Go. The seven specified conditions represent contractual language requirements achievable within 60 days — not fundamental redesign. The committed revenue base (cargo $6M/year binding, concessions 200+ locations binding, AT&T offload floors binding) exceeds the $2M net annual savings threshold on committed demand alone, before airline pricing is finalized. The phased deployment with tested rollback at each gate, 90-day parallel operations per concession location, and blackout windows protecting peak revenue periods ensure that operational risk is contained. For our concessions consortium specifically, I recommend Board approval with the explicit condition that the concession deployment timeline (Phase 1 pilot Months 3-9, Phase 2 general availability Month 24) is contractually independent of airline pricing negotiations — our 200+ locations and 60 operators cannot afford to have their connectivity modernization held hostage to a bilateral negotiation between DFW and its anchor airline.

final_recommendation:
## Team Assignment Reasoning
- **Red Rationale**: AT&T Carrier Rep has the most significant residual leverage points and structural vulnerabilities in the consensus: (1) Revenue share heavily favors DFW (60/40 declining to 80/20), creating long-term margin pressure; (2) AT&T bears 100% CWP remediation costs and $250K performance bond with no offsetting risk caps; (3) Carrier-operations exclusivity expires at Year 5 with unfavorable competitive evaluation criteria (incumbent gets 30% weight vs. 40% for technical capability—a structural disadvantage); (4) Terminal F triggers full competitive evaluation, risking permanent loss of largest greenfield revenue stream; (5) No commercial rights to DFW data (0% of data monetization), unlike other co-investors; (6) CBRS 2.0 migration costs at AT&T expense; (7) Technology refresh (Year 5) funded by AT&T, not shared; (8) Carrier offload floors ($500K-$1M) are binding minimums with downside revenue share renegotiation if missed. AT&T should stress-test whether revenue floors are achievable, whether competitive evaluation process is genuinely fair, and whether branding restrictions create commercial disadvantage.

Anchor Airline VP has residual operational/governance vulnerabilities despite strong constraint satisfaction: (1) Priority hierarchy changes require airline consent but are not enforcement-backed—if DFW/cargo invoke emergency degradation, airline has 30-second notification and post-event notification only, with no contractual right to reverse a degradation decision; (2) 30-second automatic failover is operationally sound but creates dependency on dual-connectivity (Wi-Fi + cellular)—if cellular backup degrades, airline has no fallback; (3) Tier 1 priority guarantees 'full allocation seconds 30-300' but the definition of 'full' is ambiguous if DPA response coincides with capacity constraint (AT&T's 250ms DPA response priority vs. airline's seconds 30-300 guarantee)—potential conflict unresolved; (4) Airline slice configuration authority does not include capacity arbitration—DFW can repriorityize away from airline slice during degradation if cargo triggers 'guaranteed minimum' floor interpretation; (5) Terminal F co-design authority is conditional on DFW *not* guaranteeing performance specs (sub-20ms latency, 50 Mbps, zero-loss handoff)—if DFW guarantees specs and meets them, airline gets no co-design seat; if DFW guarantees and fails, dispute resolution mechanism is binding arbitration (90 days), not immediate reinstatement. Airline should stress-test priority hierarchy enforcement, degradation decision-making authority, and Terminal F performance guarantees.
- **Blue Rationale**: Airport CRO has maximum constraint satisfaction and lowest residual risk: All 14 HARD constraints marked ✅ SATISFIED. CRO's core value drivers are all locked: (1) 100% DFW infrastructure ownership with clean title below carrier-operations API (constraint H-R1, HC-2); (2) Data monetization exclusively DFW with explicit carve-outs for airline/cargo/concession operational data (H-R10, explicit Tier 1 definition); (3) Three independent revenue streams with no cross-subsidization (H-R9, H-R6); (4) Concession cutover blackouts (H-R5), 90-day parallel operations, and 4-hour rollback (H-R6); (5) Committed revenue contracts before board approval ($15M cargo prepay, $6M/year minimum, AT&T offload floors—H-R14); (6) Minimum $2M annual savings by Year 2 (H-R4); (7) Phased deployment with cargo parallel track and regulatory authorization separation (H-R13, HC-6); (8) Pre-authorized construction framework preventing CWP delays (H-R11); (9) AT&T branding limited (H-R11); (10) Security and priority hierarchy contractually sovereign (HC-3, HC-8); (11) Per-terminal/per-location SLA measurement with documented performance bonds (HC-9); (12) 200 bps penalty mechanism for AT&T delays (H-R3, well-defined attribution process). CRO benefits from AT&T's strong commitment (CBRS 2.0 funding, co-investment, transition assistance, 4 FTE CWP team) with limited reciprocal obligation beyond revenue share (which CRO controls). CRO's 18-month Year 7 exit and Year 5 renegotiation rights preserve flexibility. CRO should defend the consensus as comprehensive, risk-mitigated, and beneficial to DFW.

Cargo Director has maximum constraint satisfaction and strongest structural positioning: All 6 HARD constraints marked ✅ SATISFIED, plus 6 SOFT constraints all met. Cargo has unlocked: (1) Independent parallel deployment track (Phase 0 passive in 45 days, Phase 0.5 active Q1-Q2 2026) with no dependency on other terminals (HC-1); (2) $15M prepaid infrastructure investment by DFW with consortium (HC-6); (3) 6 anchor tenants with $6M/year minimum committed revenue on binding 5-year contracts (HC-6); (4) $0.25/sq ft/month pricing all-inclusive with automation-grade SLAs (HC-5); (5) Interim AT&T carrier operations + parallel competitive evaluation process that allows vendor diversification (HC-1); (6) Tier 3 priority hierarchy with guaranteed minimum capacity allocation and degradation alerts (HC-3, time-sequenced degradation specifies AGV ≤50ms 30-300s and ≤75ms sustained); (7) Cold chain regulatory compliance with operator-exclusive control and independent audit pathways (HC-4, Tier 3 data classification); (8) Consortium-funded passive infrastructure ($200-400K) saving $1.2-1.8M vs. retrofit (SOFT S1); (9) Governance seat and Tier 3 consent requirement for hierarchy changes (SOFT S3); (10) Performance data sharing with all stakeholders pre-terminal deployment (SOFT S5). Cargo's positioning is exceptionally strong—independently funded, independently timed, independently operated, with binding revenue commitments and SLA guarantees. Cargo should defend the consensus as enabling cargo-first proof-of-concept with minimal execution risk.
- **White Rationale**: Concessions Tech Lead is the most balanced and neutral stakeholder: (1) All 8 HARD constraints are marked ✅ SATISFIED, indicating full operational/technical alignment; (2) However, concessions holds ZERO structural power in the consensus—it is not a primary revenue stream (tenant access fees, not concession operator fees, generate DFW/AT&T splits), not a capital co-investor, not a governance seat, not a strategic priority lever (unlike cargo's $15M prepay or airline's $8-12M co-investment); (3) Concessions' value is purely operational utility—turnkey managed service, predictable pricing, SLA reliability—which is fully delivered but offers no leverage in cross-stakeholder disputes; (4) Concessions has no visibility into revenue share negotiations, priority hierarchy enforcement mechanisms, or AT&T competitive evaluation fairness (all AT&T/CRO/cargo domains); (5) Concessions' constraints are entirely within DFW/AT&T operational scope (not governance or strategic), making it a implementer rather than negotiator; (6) Concessions Tech Lead can objectively evaluate whether other teams are using infrastructure/priority hierarchy /rollback rights in ways that harm concession POS reliability (the one area where concessions has legitimate interest); (7) Concessions holds the least stake in AT&T's long-term positioning, DFW's revenue model, airline's governance rights, or cargo's timeline—making it naturally neutral. White Team role: adjudicate whether Red Team objections (AT&T margin compression, airline governance ambiguity) or Blue Team defenses (CRO/cargo risk mitigation, independent audit rights) are technically sound, and whether the consensus infrastructure can support all three revenue streams simultaneously without cross-interference.

## Red Team Attacks

### AT&T Carrier Rep
