- **[V1] Carrier Offload Guarantee Creates Unhedgeable Demand Risk for AT&T** (Critical): AT&T is contractually obligated to guarantee $500K (Year 1), $750K (Year 2), and $1M/year (Years 3-5) in carrier offload revenue to DFW — with shortfall payments due if missed and DFW gaining the right to renegotiate the offload revenue share downward. However, AT&T has no contractual mechanism to compel T-Mobile, Verizon, or DISH to purchase CBRS offload capacity. These are sovereign commercial decisions by AT&T's direct competitors. T-Mobile has aggressively built out mid-band n41 across DFW metro and may have no offload need. Verizon is deploying mmWave in high-density venues independently. DISH/EchoStar is in financial distress and unlikely to sign offload agreements at any price. AT&T is guaranteeing revenue from customers it cannot control, on infrastructure it does not own, while the penalty clause (downward share renegotiation) compounds the exposure. The $1M/year floor in Years 3-5 assumes a carrier offload market that may not materialize at DFW specifically because competing carriers are building their own in-venue solutions post-Boingo/DigitalBridge acquisition. AT&T could face $3-5M in cumulative shortfall payments over the contract while simultaneously losing offload share percentage — effectively paying DFW for the privilege of failing to sell a product AT&T's competitors refuse to buy.
- **[V2] Parallel Cargo Competitive Evaluation Is a Trojan Horse for Full Displacement** (Critical): The cargo zone deployment structure creates a uniquely dangerous precedent. AT&T operates as 'interim carrier-operations partner' for Phase 0.5 while a parallel competitive evaluation runs from Day 1 through Day 150. Betacom already has trial nodes physically installed at DFW — meaning they have RF propagation data, interference profiles, and operational familiarity that neutralizes AT&T's incumbent advantage. The evaluation weighting (Technical 40%, SLA Commitments 20%, Track Record 30%, Cost 10%) appears favorable to AT&T with 30% for incumbent track record, but in practice Betacom can claim DFW-specific track record from their trial nodes. More critically, the cargo zone is the highest-value greenfield deployment ($15M prepaid, $6M/year committed) and serves as the reference architecture for Terminal F's competitive evaluation in Phase 3. If Betacom wins cargo operations, they will have 18-24 months of production operational data at DFW before the Terminal F evaluation — at which point AT&T's 'incumbent track record' advantage evaporates entirely. The cargo zone evaluation is not about cargo; it is a $15M audition that determines whether AT&T retains any carrier-operations role beyond the legacy CWP footprint.
- **[V3] AT&T Capital Obligations Compound While Revenue Share Declines to Subsistence Levels** (High): The financial structure requires AT&T to fund three major capital obligations — CBRS 2.0 migration (estimated $2-4M based on 1000 AP re-certification and firmware upgrades at the 85% DoD reduction threshold), Year 5 5G-Advanced technology refresh (estimated $5-8M for carrier-layer upgrade across expanding campus), and 2 FTE dedicated onboarding for 18 months (~$600K loaded cost) — while its revenue share declines from 40% to 20% on tenant access and from 45% to 30% on carrier offload. AT&T also posts a $250K performance bond and bears 100% of CWP remediation costs. The CWP asset purchase at $3.5-5M FMV means AT&T recovers less than half its $10M investment in cash, with the $5-6.5M 'credit' converting into a further spending obligation (edge compute/MEC co-investment). AT&T's total capital commitment over 10 years could reach $18-23M against a declining share of revenue streams where DFW captures 100% of the highest-growth category (data monetization). By Year 8, AT&T is earning 20% of tenant access on infrastructure AT&T partially funded, 30% of carrier offload AT&T must guarantee minimums on, and 0% of data monetization that AT&T's operational telemetry enables. The Year 5 binding renegotiation creates additional downward pressure — DFW will arrive at that table with AT&T's own performance data showing the network runs on DFW-owned infrastructure and could be operated by an alternative carrier partner.
- **[V4] DPA Liability Trap — AT&T Bears FCC Compliance Risk Under Governance It Cannot Control** (High): AT&T holds 'sole real-time authority' for DPA response at sub-250ms — meaning AT&T is the entity registered with Federated Wireless SAS and bears FCC Part 96 compliance liability for the DFW CBRS deployment. However, the priority hierarchy (Exhibit A) is a binding technical specification that AT&T cannot modify without written consent of all Tier 0 and Tier 1 tenants. The 'technical advisory authority' mechanism allows AT&T to flag compliance risks, but DFW can issue a 'written override assuming SLA liability for resulting performance impact.' This sounds protective, but FCC enforcement actions target the SAS registrant and licensee — not the building owner's contractual liability allocation. If DFW's priority hierarchy creates a scenario where Tier 0/1/2/3 guaranteed bandwidth floors consume all available CBRS capacity during a DPA event (Navy radar operations at NAS Fort Worth Joint Reserve Base are 15 miles from DFW and trigger DPA events affecting the entire campus), AT&T faces a choice: violate the binding priority hierarchy or violate FCC Part 96. The 'written override' mechanism does not shield AT&T from FCC enforcement, spectrum revocation, or SAS de-registration. With 340,000+ APs in Federated Wireless's SAS network, an AT&T compliance failure at DFW could trigger review of AT&T's entire CBRS portfolio.
- **[V5] CWP Degradation Halt Authority Creates Asymmetric Schedule Weapon Against AT&T** (High): Section 1.7 specifies that any 'CWP degradation event attributable to 5G transition' triggers an automatic 72-hour halt in the affected terminal zone, and two such events within 30 days trigger a full project pause requiring CIO written sign-off. AT&T bears 100% of CWP remediation costs. This sounds reasonable as transition protection, but the attribution mechanism is undefined — there is no specified methodology for determining whether a CWP performance dip is caused by 5G transition activities, normal RF environment changes (87M passengers carrying billions of device-hours of Wi-Fi and cellular interference annually), seasonal propagation variation, or airline equipment changes. The 90-day CWP baseline established in Phase 0 captures a single season's RF profile; DFW's RF environment shifts dramatically between a February Tuesday (180K passengers) and a July Friday (320K passengers). Any party — particularly a competitor like Betacom observing from their trial nodes — could identify correlation-not-causation CWP dips coinciding with AT&T's 5G buildout and trigger halts. AT&T's own CWP deployment becomes the mechanism by which AT&T's expansion is blocked. Combined with the 200 bps revenue share penalty for AT&T-attributable delays (H-R3), every 72-hour halt costs AT&T both schedule position and permanent revenue share points.

### Anchor Airline VP
- **[V1] DPA + Ground-Stop Release Creates Unrecoverable Dual-Failure at Peak Ramp Intensity** (Critical): NAS Fort Worth Joint Reserve Base sits 6 nautical miles from DFW. A Navy DPA activation can suppress CBRS spectrum campus-wide across all terminals simultaneously. The consensus degradation model maintains 'full allocation' for Tier 1 through all time windows — but 'full allocation' is a percentage of available capacity, not an absolute throughput guarantee. The 50 Mbps/gate floor is a normal-operations guarantee; nowhere in the degradation model is it restated as a hard floor during DPA events. During a sustained DPA that reduces available CBRS capacity by 40-60% across the entire campus, my 'full allocation' of a dramatically smaller pool could deliver 20-30 Mbps/gate — below the threshold for reliable ramp tablet operations, baggage system telemetry, and crew comms simultaneously. Now compound this: DFW averages 25+ ground stops per year from severe convective weather. When a ground stop lifts, we push 50-80 aircraft in a 20-minute burst. That is the single highest-intensity moment for ramp communications — every gate coordinator, every bag runner, every crew chief is on their iPad. The 30-second cellular failover is the backstop, but during a ground-stop release at DFW, AT&T/T-Mobile/Verizon macro networks are simultaneously absorbing 200,000+ passengers reconnecting, streaming, and calling. Macro cellular is not guaranteed capacity — it is best-effort, and it will be saturated at the exact moment I need it. The consensus treats DPA response and cellular failover as independent, complementary systems (H12). They are correlated failure modes at DFW.
- **[V2] Airline Pricing Structure is Dangerously Underspecified Relative to $8-12M Terminal F Commitment** (Critical): The consensus defines airline pricing as 'Hybrid base + per-departure variable (base ≤60% of expected annual cost)' with a 5-year minimum term. For the anchor airline at 80%+ of departures — roughly 600-700 daily departures, 220,000+ annually — the per-departure variable component is completely undefined. No rate. No cap. No methodology for calculating 'expected annual cost.' No escalation limit specific to the airline tier (concessions get CPI/3% caps — airlines get nothing). Terminal F alone requires $8-12M as 'prepaid access fees' tagged as co-investment, but the consensus does not define what happens to that prepaid capital if the airline reduces Terminal F operations, if DFW reassigns gates, or if the airline invokes rollback rights after the 90-day remediation period. Furthermore, DFW has a Year 7 no-fault exit option — the airline has no equivalent. We have a 5-year minimum with no early termination right beyond performance-triggered remedies. If DFW terminates at Year 7 and rearchitects the network, my $8-12M in 'prepaid access fees' for Terminal F infrastructure that DFW owns outright could be stranded. The 'airline-tagged in asset registry' language confers zero legal ownership rights. I am being asked to fund someone else's capital asset with no reversion rights and no defined return mechanism.
- **[V3] Terminal C Multi-Year Construction Creates Rolling CWP Degradation Trap** (High): Terminal C is undergoing a $3B rebuild through 2028. The CWP transition protection framework specifies: 50m buffer zones around CWP APs for new RF energization (30-day review), 72-hour automatic halts when CWP degradation is attributed to 5G transition activities, and a full project pause with CIO sign-off after two degradation events in 30 days in any terminal. Terminal C is where active construction AND active CWP-dependent ramp operations coexist for 3+ years. Construction activities — steel erection, crane movements, temporary wall installations, progressive demolition — continuously alter RF propagation characteristics. Every structural change is a potential CWP degradation event that must be distinguished from 5G-transition-caused degradation. The consensus provides no methodology for attribution between construction-caused and 5G-caused RF changes. This ambiguity creates two failure modes: (1) Conservative interpretation triggers repeated 72-hour halts that delay Phase 2 5G deployment in Terminal C by 12-18 months, leaving my ramp operations on aging CWP infrastructure longer than planned; (2) Aggressive interpretation pushes through degradation events without halts, and my ramp iPads in Terminal C experience chronic connectivity issues during the construction period. The 'pre-authorized construction framework' developed during Phase 0 was designed for 5G buildout activities, not for the interaction between ongoing building construction and RF infrastructure. The airline has a seat at the table for framework development, but the framework cannot anticipate every structural change in a 3-year, $3B demolish-and-rebuild project.
- **[V4] 99.99% Per-Terminal SLA Masks Sub-Gate Failure Modes in 31-Gate Terminal F** (High): The SLA is measured at 99.99% per terminal per month. Terminal F will have 31 gates. 99.99% uptime means 4.3 minutes of downtime per month — measured across the entire terminal. The consensus specifies '50 Mbps sustained per gate area' as a bandwidth guarantee and 'synthetic monitoring on 50+ devices/terminal' at '15-second polling.' But the SLA math creates a dangerous measurement gap. If 3 gates in Terminal F experience 45 minutes of degraded service (below 20ms latency threshold) in a month due to a localized edge compute failure or small-cell cluster issue, but the other 28 gates are at 100%, the terminal-level aggregate may still show 99.99% — because per-terminal measurement averages across all gates. The $50K/terminal/incident penalty triggers per terminal, not per gate. At $74/minute per delayed pushback, 3 gates down for 45 minutes during a banking hour could cost $74 × 45 × 6 affected flights = $19,980 in direct delay costs per incident — but the SLA measurement may not even register it as a breach. The consensus also specifies 'application layer' measurement but doesn't define which application metrics are monitored. Synthetic polling of device connectivity is not the same as measuring whether the Sabre DCS response completes in under 2 seconds or whether the real-time baggage reconciliation feed is current. I can have 'network uptime' while my applications are unusable due to jitter, microbursts, or edge compute latency.
- **[V5] Terminal F Competitive Carrier-Operations Evaluation Creates Operational Risk During Greenfield Launch** (High): The consensus specifies that Terminal F 'triggers competitive evaluation' for carrier operations, separate from AT&T's 60-month exclusivity on terminal deployments (Phases 1-4). This means Terminal F could be operated by a different carrier-operations vendor than Terminals A, B, C, and D/E. From an airline operations perspective, I operate 31 gates in Terminal F AND exclusive operations in Terminals A, B, C — potentially under two different carrier-operations regimes. My ramp operations are not terminal-siloed. Aircraft reposition between terminals. Crew members transit between terminals. Baggage connects across terminals. My 800-900 iPads need seamless handoff as ramp agents move between Terminal C (AT&T-operated carrier layer) and Terminal F (potentially Betacom or other vendor). The consensus specifies '≥99.95% handoff success at tug speeds' for Terminal F but says nothing about inter-terminal handoff between different carrier-operations vendors. The slice management API is 'self-service for airline' but if the underlying carrier-operations vendor implements the slice differently in Terminal F than in Terminal A, my single slice policy may behave inconsistently. Device provisioning, QoS enforcement, and radio resource scheduling could all differ. This is not a theoretical concern — this is asking a single airline with unified operations to accept a split-brain network architecture across its own operation.

## Blue Team Defenses

### Airport CRO
- **AT&T-V1** (Counterargument): This attack fundamentally mischaracterizes the carrier offload floors as aggressive when they are, in fact, deliberately conservative — and the CRO team structured them that way intentionally. Let me do the math AT&T apparently hasn't. DFW processes 87 million passengers annually. At an average dwell time of 2.1 hours (DFW's own terminal analytics), that is 182.7 million passenger-hours of connectivity demand per year. The carrier offload Year 1 floor of $500K translates to $0.0027 per passenger-hour — roughly one-quarter of one penny. Boingo's public filings before the DigitalBridge acquisition showed per-passenger-hour carrier offload yields of $0.008-$0.015 at Tier 1 airports. We are asking AT&T to guarantee approximately 25% of proven industry floor rates. The $1M/year floor at Year 3-5 is still only $0.0055/passenger-hour — below the bottom quartile of Boingo's historical airport portfolio. The attack claims T-Mobile's n41 buildout eliminates offload need, but that ignores basic RF physics: mid-band 2.5 GHz penetrates airport terminal structures poorly, especially DFW's concrete-and-steel construction across 5 terminals spanning 17,000+ acres. T-Mobile's own venue strategy team has publicly acknowledged at CTIA 2024 that in-building solutions remain necessary for venues above 500,000 sq ft — DFW's terminal footprint exceeds 6 million sq ft. Verizon's mmWave is even worse on penetration. The 'Boingo/DigitalBridge competitors building their own solutions' argument actually strengthens DFW's position: DigitalBridge paid $854M precisely because venue-based wireless offload has durable value. As for the 'perverse incentive' claim that DFW benefits from AT&T failing — nonsense. DFW's 55-70% share of actual offload revenue vastly exceeds the shortfall payment. At $1M guaranteed with DFW's 55% share, DFW gets $550K minimum. If actual offload revenue reaches $3M (conservative for a venue this size), DFW's 55% share is $1.65M. No rational actor prefers $1M shortfall over $1.65M in earned revenue. The renegotiation clause is a performance accountability mechanism, not a profit center. Finally, AT&T's own offload needs at DFW are substantial — AT&T serves approximately 35-40% of US wireless subscribers, meaning 30-35 million of DFW's passengers are AT&T customers. AT&T can partially satisfy its own guarantee through self-offload economics that reduce macro network CapEx.
- **AT&T-V2** (Counterargument): AT&T is framing competitive discipline as a 'Trojan Horse,' which tells you everything about AT&T's preferred alternative: unchallenged monopoly carrier-operations control over $80M+ in 5-year net value with zero competitive pressure. Let me reframe what the consensus actually provides AT&T. First, the 30% incumbent track record weight is extraordinarily generous — federal procurement rules typically cap incumbent preference at 5-10% (FAR Part 15). DFW is giving AT&T 3-6x the incumbent advantage that a federal agency would. Second, AT&T's interim carrier-operations role in Phase 0.5 is not a disadvantage — it is 6-9 months of paid production operational data that no competitor can replicate. Betacom's trial nodes are in 'non-production zones' per Phase 1 specifications — they have lab data, not production data under real cargo automation loads with 1,000+ devices per warehouse, AGV fleet telemetry, and FDA-regulated cold chain monitoring. The gap between trial-node RF propagation measurements and production carrier operations in a warehouse running 24/7 perishable logistics is enormous. Third, the evaluation criteria weight technical capability at 40% — and AT&T's enterprise division has SAS registration, FCC Part 96 compliance infrastructure, nationwide CBRS deployment experience, and established relationships with Federated Wireless that Betacom simply does not match at scale. Betacom operates primarily as a systems integrator; AT&T operates as a licensed carrier with spectrum management as a core competency. For cargo operations where regulatory compliance (FDA, IATA, EU GDP) creates liability exposure, the technical capability gap matters. Fourth — and this is the revenue argument — the competitive evaluation is what makes the cargo consortium's $15M prepaid commitment credible. The consortium's binding letters are predicated on carrier-neutral infrastructure with competitive operations. If DFW had handed AT&T an exclusive no-bid cargo carrier-operations contract, the consortium would have demanded lower rates and shorter terms. Competition is what secured $6M/year in committed revenue. AT&T's attack is essentially: 'Don't make me compete because I might lose.' That is not a vulnerability in the consensus — that is the consensus working as designed.
- **AT&T-V3** (Counterargument): AT&T's financial analysis is deliberately one-sided — it tallies every cost obligation while ignoring the revenue side and strategic value that makes this deal attractive enough for AT&T to have negotiated it in the first place. Let me present the full picture. AT&T's capital commitments over 10 years: CWP asset sale recovery ($3.5-5M cash), unrecovered CWP balance converted to edge compute credit ($5-6.5M — which AT&T would have spent anyway on MEC to serve its own subscribers), CBRS 2.0 migration ($2-4M), Year 5 tech refresh ($5-8M), 2 FTE for 18 months ($600K), and $250K bond. Total: $17-24M over 10 years. Now the revenue side AT&T conveniently omits. Tenant access fees at AT&T's share: Year 1-3 at 40% of projected $4-6M annual tenant revenue = $1.6-2.4M/year; Years 4-7 at 30% of projected $8-12M = $2.4-3.6M/year; Years 8-10 at 20% of projected $12-15M = $2.4-3.0M/year. Carrier offload at AT&T's share: conservatively $500K-$1.5M/year. AT&T's 10-year cumulative revenue share: $25-38M. That is a 1.3-2.2x return on invested capital — not venture-capital returns, but solidly profitable for what amounts to a managed-services contract on someone else's infrastructure. More importantly, AT&T gets the flagship smart-airport reference deployment for its enterprise 5G private network sales practice. AT&T's enterprise division sells private 5G to ports, factories, stadiums, and campuses. A DFW case study — America's third-busiest airport — is worth $50-100M+ in influenced pipeline over 10 years based on typical enterprise reference-account economics (Gartner estimates reference customers influence 5-8x their contract value in new pipeline). The declining revenue share reflects DFW's increasing infrastructure ownership risk over time — which is standard in every infrastructure-as-a-service contract. AT&T's share declines because AT&T's capital is being recovered and its ongoing operational risk decreases. The Year 5 renegotiation is symmetric — AT&T can invoke it too if market conditions have changed in AT&T's favor. And the two 3-year renewal options extend the revenue runway to 16 years if both parties are satisfied. This is a profitable, strategically valuable deal for AT&T. AT&T's complaint is that it is not a monopoly-rent extraction opportunity — which, again, is the point.
- **AT&T-V4** (Plan Modification): This is the most technically substantive attack from AT&T, and it identifies a genuine gap in the consensus: the interaction between binding bandwidth floors and extreme DPA capacity reduction scenarios is underspecified. The CRO position is that this is solvable without restructuring the deal. Here is why and how. First, context: NAS Fort Worth JRB DPA events affecting DFW occur approximately 8-12 times per year based on NTIA/ESC historical data for the DFW Metroplex coordination zone. Most are brief (under 30 minutes) and affect specific CBRS channels, not the entire 150 MHz CBRS band. A sustained multi-hour, all-channel DPA affecting 100% of DFW's CBRS capacity is an extreme tail event — but AT&T is right that it is possible and must be addressed. Second, the consensus already contains the mechanism: AT&T's 'technical advisory authority' allows AT&T to certify that a configuration creates RF/DPA compliance risk, after which DFW must accept the recommendation or issue a written override assuming SLA liability. The key modification needed is to add an explicit 'DPA Emergency Capacity Protocol' as a sub-exhibit to Exhibit A that defines: (a) graduated capacity thresholds (e.g., >30% campus capacity reduction triggers Protocol Level 1; >50% triggers Level 2); (b) at Level 2, all tiers including Tier 0-3 operate at 'maximum achievable proportional allocation' rather than absolute bandwidth floors — meaning the priority hierarchy is preserved but the absolute numbers flex; (c) AT&T bears no FCC liability for SLA shortfalls during declared DPA events provided AT&T's SAS response was compliant; (d) DFW cannot issue a written override that requires AT&T to exceed FCC-authorized power levels or channel assignments during an active DPA — this is a bright-line carve-out from the override mechanism. This modification protects AT&T's FCC compliance posture while preserving DFW's priority hierarchy as a relative ordering. The priority hierarchy was always intended to govern allocation among tenants, not to override physics or federal spectrum regulations. I would support adding this as a clarifying amendment, and it aligns with the CRO's interest because an FCC enforcement action against AT&T would disrupt the entire revenue model.
- **AT&T-V5** (Plan Modification): AT&T raises a fair point about attribution methodology — the consensus specifies consequences for CWP degradation events but does not specify how degradation is attributed to 5G transition versus ambient RF changes. However, AT&T dramatically overstates the risk of weaponization. The modification needed is straightforward: the Phase 0 CWP baseline must include a seasonal normalization model, and the attribution methodology must be jointly developed during Phase 0 by AT&T RF engineering, DFW IT, and an independent RF consultant (DFW-selected, AT&T-approved, costs shared). Specifically: (1) The 90-day baseline should be supplemented with AT&T's historical CWP performance data (AT&T has 5+ years of CWP operational data at DFW — this is the single most valuable dataset for establishing normal variance); (2) Degradation attribution must require correlation with a specific 5G transition activity occurring within the prior 72 hours AND within the affected RF coverage zone — temporal correlation with passenger surges, weather events, or construction activities without proximate 5G activity does not trigger the halt; (3) An independent RF engineer (pre-selected during Phase 0, on retainer) can be invoked by either party to adjudicate disputed attribution within 48 hours; (4) The 200 bps revenue share penalty (H-R3) applies only to delays where AT&T is the attributed cause through the joint root-cause process — not to CWP-halt-caused delays where attribution is ambiguous or passenger-density-driven. The Thanksgiving scenario in the attack is a red herring: AT&T's own historical CWP data will show Thanksgiving-week throughput variance, and a properly normalized baseline would account for this. AT&T's claim that Betacom could 'observe from their trial nodes and trigger halts' misunderstands the architecture — Betacom's trial nodes are in non-production zones, not monitoring CWP APs, and Betacom has no standing to invoke the CWP degradation halt. Only DFW IT leadership can trigger the halt, and the CIO has zero incentive to weaponize it — every halt delays revenue. The CRO's revenue timeline depends on Phase 1-2 proceeding on schedule. I am the last person who wants spurious CWP halts delaying monetization.
- **Airline-V1** (Plan Modification): This is the most operationally sophisticated attack in the entire red team exercise, and the airline VP is correct that DPA and macro cellular saturation are correlated failure modes at DFW, not independent ones. However, the attack overstates the probability and understates the existing mitigations while identifying a gap that the CRO wants closed — because 41 delayed flights is also a revenue problem for every concession operator serving those passengers. Here is the layered defense and the modification I support. First, probability context: the attack requires simultaneous DPA activation (8-15 times/year) + active ground stop lifting (25+ ground stops/year, but average duration is 47 minutes meaning the 'lift' window is narrow) + peak banking hour. Using conservative independence assumptions (DPA and weather are partially correlated since both involve military operations and storm systems, but the correlation is positive — meaning DPA is more likely during clear weather when radar exercises occur, while ground stops are weather-driven — making the scenario less correlated than alleged), the joint probability of simultaneous DPA + ground-stop-release during banking hours is approximately 1-3 events per year. Not zero, but not the catastrophic frequency implied. Second, existing mitigations: the 30-second cellular failover triggers to macro cellular, which the attack correctly notes will be saturated. But the consensus also provides 50 Mbps/gate bandwidth guarantee under normal operations and the priority hierarchy guarantees airline Tier 1 'full allocation' during degradation — meaning during a DPA that reduces campus capacity by 55%, the airline still gets the largest proportional share after safety. Even at 45% of 50 Mbps (22.5 Mbps), basic ramp tablet operations (pushback confirmation, bag reconciliation, crew check-in) require 2-5 Mbps per device. The 22.5 Mbps/gate supports 4-11 simultaneous device sessions — sufficient for core ramp operations even if degraded. Third, the modification I support: add a 'Correlated Failure Protocol' to the MSA that (a) requires DFW to maintain a dedicated LTE/5G macro small-cell cluster on licensed spectrum (not CBRS) at each terminal ramp area specifically for failover — funded from DFW infrastructure CapEx as insurance against CBRS DPA events; (b) this licensed-spectrum failover is not subject to DPA and provides guaranteed 10 Mbps/gate during DPA events; (c) cost allocated to airline infrastructure charges as an explicit line item so the airline sees what it is paying for. This converts the failover from 'best-effort macro cellular' to 'dedicated licensed-spectrum backup on DFW-controlled infrastructure.' The CRO supports this because flight delays destroy concession revenue — a ground-stop release should send passengers through concessions on their way to gates, not strand them at delayed gates. Fourth, the $50K/terminal SLA credit versus $1.49M actual loss gap is real but expected: SLA credits are not insurance policies, they are performance accountability mechanisms. The airline's actual loss recovery comes through its use-and-lease agreement with DFW, not through network SLA credits. If the airline wants full-loss indemnification, we can discuss it, but the premium would be reflected in the airline's network service pricing — which the airline has already argued is too high in V2.
- **Airline-V2** (Plan Modification): The airline VP is correct that the airline pricing structure is underspecified relative to the concession and cargo pricing, and this is a legitimate gap that must be closed before Board approval. I will be direct: the consensus intentionally left airline pricing at the framework level because the airline's own LOI is due within 30 days of contract execution, and the negotiation dynamics between DFW's largest tenant (80%+ of departures) and DFW as landlord require bilateral negotiation, not multilateral consensus. That said, the attack identifies specific gaps that the CRO wants resolved because defensible board-level revenue projections (my hard constraint H-R14) require defined airline pricing. Here are the modifications I support: (1) Per-departure variable rate: cap at $35/departure with annual escalation at CPI or 3% (matching concession terms), with a volume floor (if departures fall below 180,000/year, variable component converts to a fixed annual minimum to protect DFW's revenue floor); (2) Terminal F prepaid access fees: treated as prepaid service credits with a 10-year amortization schedule — if DFW terminates before full amortization, unamortized balance is refunded to the airline within 180 days. This is not equity, not ownership — it is prepaid services with a defined refund mechanism; (3) Traffic reduction protection: if airline reduces Terminal F operations by >20% for 12+ consecutive months, DFW may reassign gates but must either (a) apply proportional prepaid credits to the airline's other terminal charges or (b) refund the prorated unamortized balance; (4) Symmetric termination: the airline gets a Year 7 convenience exit right matching DFW's, with 18-month notice, provided the airline has paid all base + variable through the notice period and forfeits any remaining prepaid credit balance; (5) Year 5 renegotiation: the airline has the same invocation right as DFW and AT&T. These modifications make the airline pricing structure as defined as the concession and cargo structures. The CRO's interest is aligned: I need the airline's 5-year LOI to be convertible to a binding commitment, and that requires defined terms the airline's CFO will sign.
- **Airline-V3** (Mitigation): The airline correctly identifies that Terminal C's 3-year $3B rebuild creates an RF environment where construction-caused and 5G-caused degradation will be difficult to distinguish. But the consensus already contains the primary mitigation mechanism, and the attack underestimates its effectiveness. The 'pre-authorized construction framework' developed during Phase 0 is explicitly designed to be built by a joint team of AT&T RF engineering + DFW network architecture + airline ops technology. This joint team has the airline at the table during framework design — precisely to address the interaction between building construction and RF infrastructure. The framework should — and the CRO supports specifying this — include: (1) A dynamic RF baseline for Terminal C that is updated monthly during active construction, not fixed at Phase 0. The 90-day baseline is for stable terminals; Terminal C needs a rolling baseline that accounts for structural changes; (2) Construction-phase RF monitoring: AT&T deploys temporary RF sensors in Terminal C construction zones (funded from the $250K performance bond as operational monitoring, not remediation) that provide continuous propagation data as construction progresses — this creates the attribution evidence base; (3) Pre-modeled RF impact assessments for major structural changes (steel erection, demolition, wall installations) provided by the Terminal C general contractor's RF consultant (standard practice in modern airport construction — DFW's Terminal C contractor will have an RF consultant on staff) before structural changes occur, allowing CWP adjustments proactively rather than reactively; (4) The CWP degradation halt trigger for Terminal C should use the dynamic baseline, not the Phase 0 static baseline — meaning 'degradation' is measured against the post-construction-change expected performance, not the pre-construction ideal. This eliminates the false-positive scenario in the attack where construction-caused propagation changes trigger 5G-related halts. The airline's real protection during Terminal C construction is that DFW, AT&T, and the airline all share the same interest: keeping ramp operations functional in Terminal C during a 3-year rebuild. DFW cannot afford Terminal C ramp disruptions any more than the airline can — gate delays cascade to concession revenue across the entire airport.
- **Airline-V4** (Plan Modification): The airline VP makes a valid measurement-granularity argument. The CRO agrees this needs to be addressed — not because $50K/terminal credits are inadequate (SLA credits are not loss insurance), but because per-terminal measurement that masks gate-cluster failures will erode airline confidence in the platform and jeopardize the airline's 5-year commitment renewal. Revenue depends on trust. Here is the modification: (1) Add per-gate-cluster SLA measurement in addition to per-terminal aggregate. Define gate clusters of 4-6 gates based on shared small-cell coverage and edge compute dependency. Terminal F with 31 gates would have approximately 6 gate clusters. SLA of 99.95% per gate cluster per month (slightly below the 99.99% terminal aggregate, reflecting the smaller measurement denominator and higher statistical variance); (2) Redefine the monitoring to include application-layer metrics beyond synthetic ping: specifically, DNS resolution time (<50ms), TCP connection establishment (<100ms), and HTTP transaction completion (<500ms for standard POS/DCS-class payloads). These are measurable at the CPE gateway without requiring access to the airline's proprietary application stack; (3) Add a 'degraded performance' SLA tier: if any gate cluster shows latency above 50ms for more than 15 consecutive minutes during operating hours, it counts as a 'degradation event' even if the network is technically 'up.' Three degradation events per gate cluster per month trigger the same remediation obligations as a downtime event; (4) Edge compute redundancy requirement for Terminal F: no single edge compute node failure should affect more than 8 gates. This means Terminal F needs minimum 4 edge compute nodes with N+1 failover within the gate area. The memory-leak scenario in the attack is an edge compute resilience issue, not a network coverage issue, and the remedy is redundant compute — which the CRO supports because edge compute is also the platform for concession analytics, advertising, and IoT services that generate Tier 1 data revenue. The SLA credit structure should add a per-gate-cluster component: $10K per gate cluster per degradation event (in addition to the $50K per terminal for full breaches). This creates financial accountability at the operational granularity the airline needs while keeping the measurement manageable.
- **Airline-V5** (Mitigation): The airline raises a genuine operational concern about inter-terminal handoff between different carrier-operations vendors, but the attack misunderstands the architecture of the consensus. The key insight is that DFW owns all infrastructure below the carrier-operations API layer, including the SDN controllers, switches, routers, and — critically — the slice management infrastructure. The airline's cryptographically isolated slice is a DFW-infrastructure-layer construct, not a carrier-operations-layer construct. Whether AT&T or Betacom is managing radio resources in a given terminal, the airline's slice policy (QoS, ACLs, device provisioning, routing) is administered through DFW's self-service API/portal with 60-second propagation — consistently across all terminals. The bag tug crossing from Terminal C to Terminal F is handing off between radio access points managed by different carrier-operations vendors, but it is staying within the same DFW-owned slice on the same DFW-owned SDN infrastructure. This is architecturally similar to a device handing off between two cell towers managed by the same carrier — the handoff is an RF-layer event, and the session continuity is maintained at the core network layer, which DFW owns. That said, the airline's concern about differing SAS coordination approaches causing handoff delays is technically valid at the RF layer. The modification I support: (1) The MSA must include an 'Inter-Operator Handoff SLA' if Terminal F is awarded to a non-AT&T carrier-operations vendor. Specification: ≤500ms inter-terminal handoff at tug speed (≤25 mph), ≤0.1% handoff failure rate, tested monthly on the Terminal C/F boundary; (2) DFW's SDN controller manages the inter-terminal handoff at the infrastructure layer — this is DFW's responsibility, not the individual carrier-operations vendor's, because DFW owns the infrastructure layer where handoff continuity is maintained; (3) A single DFW network operations center (not the individual carrier-operations vendor NOCs) owns inter-terminal handoff troubleshooting and mean-time-to-repair, with escalation to the relevant carrier-operations vendor for RF-layer issues; (4) The Terminal F carrier-operations contract must include an interoperability testing period (60 days minimum) before the terminal goes live, specifically testing inter-terminal handoff performance with the airline's actual device fleet. The CRO supports this because split-brain troubleshooting slows issue resolution, which slows flight turns, which reduces passenger throughput through concessions. Single-point-of-accountability at DFW's NOC for inter-terminal issues is also a revenue protection measure.

### Cargo Director
- **AT&T-V1** (Counterargument): AT&T's framing of 'unhedgeable demand risk' fundamentally mischaracterizes the scale and context. The carrier offload floors are modest — $500K in Year 1 is roughly the annual cost of two AT&T enterprise account managers. DFW is the 4th busiest airport in the world with 87M passengers generating billions of device-hours annually. The assertion that T-Mobile, Verizon, and DISH will all independently build in-venue solutions ignores economic reality: DFW's 17,000+ acre campus with 5 terminals is not a single-building venue where a carrier drops 20 small cells and walks away. T-Mobile's n41 macro coverage penetrates terminal interiors poorly through steel-and-concrete construction — this is precisely why carriers pay for in-building offload at every major airport globally. The Boingo model generated $50M+ annually across its airport portfolio from exactly these offload agreements. More critically, from the cargo consortium's perspective, AT&T's offload revenue performance is irrelevant to our economics. We are paying $0.25/sq ft/month for automation-grade connectivity — roughly $6M/year committed across 2M+ sq ft. Our $15M prepaid investment funds DFW-owned infrastructure regardless of what T-Mobile decides. The offload floors exist to align AT&T's incentives with network quality; if AT&T cannot sell offload, that signals AT&T isn't delivering a network worth offloading onto, which is precisely the performance signal DFW needs. The 'perverse incentive' argument — that DFW benefits from AT&T failing — ignores that DFW's own tenant revenue depends on network quality. A bad network costs DFW far more in tenant churn than it gains in $1M shortfall payments. Finally, AT&T negotiated these floors. AT&T's enterprise sales organization has relationships with every US carrier. If AT&T genuinely believed these floors were unachievable, AT&T should have negotiated lower floors or tied them to passenger volume triggers. The floors as structured represent AT&T's own assessment of achievable offload revenue.
- **AT&T-V2** (Counterargument): I'll be direct: AT&T is calling the competitive evaluation a 'Trojan Horse' because AT&T doesn't want to compete. From the cargo consortium's perspective, the competitive evaluation is the single most important structural protection in this consensus. We are building the most technologically advanced cargo warehouses in North America RIGHT NOW. Menzies and dnata construction is underway. We are committing $15M prepaid and $6M/year in binding revenue. We are not going to hand exclusive carrier-operations rights to any vendor — AT&T included — without competitive validation that they can deliver automation-grade SLAs in an indoor warehouse environment with 40-foot racking, AGV navigation at ≤20ms, and cold chain monitoring that must survive FDA audit. AT&T's enterprise division has extensive macro network and managed services experience. What AT&T has never demonstrated — at DFW or anywhere — is production warehouse automation over private CBRS at the density and latency we require. One small cell per 5,000-8,000 sq ft between 40-foot racking aisles is not a macro network optimization problem. It's an industrial IoT coverage engineering challenge. The evaluation weighting gives AT&T 30% for incumbent track record — that is a substantial advantage. AT&T has SIDA badges, SAS registration, institutional knowledge of DFW's RF environment, and interim operational authority during Phase 0.5. If AT&T cannot win a competition where they start with a 30-point head start, existing site access, and operational continuity, then AT&T is not the right partner for our warehouses. The 'audition for Terminal F' framing actually strengthens the consensus: every vendor knows that cargo performance is the proving ground. This creates maximum competitive pressure to deliver results in our facilities. That is exactly what $20B in annual cargo economic impact deserves. Furthermore, the interim AT&T arrangement ensures zero timeline risk. AT&T operates from Day 1 of Phase 0.5. If AT&T wins the evaluation, seamless continuation. If an alternative wins, the 90-day transition occurs while our warehouses are in early operational ramp — not at full automation capacity. The construction window alignment is preserved regardless of evaluation outcome.
- **AT&T-V3** (Counterargument): AT&T's capital obligation concerns deserve a reality check from the entity actually writing the largest checks in this deal. The cargo consortium alone is committing $15M prepaid plus $6M/year minimum committed revenue over 5-year binding terms — that's $45M from cargo alone over the initial period. The airline is committing $8-12M for Terminal F plus ongoing per-departure fees. Concessions commit 200+ locations at $175-200/month. DFW is purchasing AT&T's CWP at fair market value — cash for depreciated assets AT&T would otherwise have to decommission at AT&T's expense when the CWP inevitably reaches end-of-life. The $5-6.5M credit against edge compute co-investment is not a 'further spending obligation' — it converts AT&T's sunk CWP cost into productive MEC infrastructure that AT&T operates and derives service revenue from. AT&T's CBRS 2.0 and 5G-Advanced obligations are technology refresh costs that AT&T would incur on ANY managed network to remain commercially competitive. If AT&T does not upgrade to CBRS 2.0, the network becomes non-competitive and AT&T loses the contract at Year 7 regardless. These are not DFW-imposed penalties — they are table-stakes technology lifecycle costs that happen to be contractually allocated to the carrier-operations partner because AT&T is the entity with spectrum expertise. The declining revenue share reflects the fundamental economic reality of infrastructure ownership: DFW is buying and owning the infrastructure. AT&T is providing an operational service. Operational service margins naturally compress over time as the asset owner develops operational maturity. AT&T's 40% share in Years 1-3 is generous by managed-service standards — Boingo's venue partners typically retained 50-65% from Day 1. AT&T accepted this structure because AT&T's alternative is losing DFW entirely. Zero percent of $20B+ in cargo economic impact is worth less than 20% of a growing tenant access revenue stream.
- **AT&T-V4** (Plan Modification): This is the most technically substantive AT&T vulnerability and it matters to cargo directly because sustained DPA events affect our automation SLAs. The core issue is real: FCC enforcement targets the SAS registrant, and contractual liability shifting between DFW and AT&T does not bind the FCC. However, AT&T's scenario overstates the operational impact and understates the consensus safeguards. First, the priority hierarchy's guaranteed bandwidth floors are defined as minimums during NORMAL operations. The degradation model explicitly contemplates reduced capacity during DPA events — cargo AGVs go to reduced-speed mode at ≤50ms (seconds 30-300) and safe-stop mode at ≤75ms (sustained DPA). These degradation modes were designed precisely for DPA scenarios. The hierarchy does not demand 'full allocation of normal-operations bandwidth' during DPA — it demands the degradation-mode allocations, which are engineered to fit within post-DPA capacity. Second, AT&T's 'technical advisory authority' with written override mechanism is not window dressing — it creates a documented paper trail where DFW assumes explicit SLA liability. If DFW issues an override that causes FCC issues, DFW has contractually accepted the consequences, and AT&T has documentary evidence of objection for any FCC inquiry. Third, NAS Fort Worth JRB DPA events are characterized and predictable. AT&T's SAS coordination team has years of DPA response data for the DFW metro area. Network capacity planning should be designed with DPA headroom — this is a design-time engineering requirement, not a runtime governance problem. That said, I support a modification: the consensus should include a DPA capacity engineering requirement specifying that the network must be designed to deliver all degradation-mode minimums (including cargo AGV ≤75ms and cold chain continuity) under a worst-case sustained DPA scenario affecting 60% of CBRS channels. This converts the runtime governance question into a design-time engineering specification that AT&T validates before Phase 0.5 go-live.
- **AT&T-V5** (Counterargument): The CWP degradation halt authority is irrelevant to the cargo zone deployment, and AT&T's attempt to characterize it as a schedule weapon misrepresents the cargo zone's physical and logical independence. The cargo facilities are physically separated from passenger terminals by 1-3 miles. Menzies, dnata, FedEx, and UPS warehouses are standalone structures on the cargo campus south/southeast of the terminal complex. There are zero CWP access points in our warehouses — the CWP serves passenger terminals. The 50-meter buffer zone, 72-hour halt authority, and two-event project pause are terminal-zone mechanisms that do not apply to cargo zone deployment by their own terms ('in the affected terminal zone'). Phase 0 passive infrastructure embedding and Phase 0.5 active deployment in cargo warehouses proceed on an independent track that is contractually insulated from terminal CWP transition activities. This was the entire purpose of the independent cargo zone deployment option (my Hard Constraint HC-1). For the terminal deployments, the attribution methodology concern has merit but is manageable. AT&T maintains the CWP and has full telemetry access — AT&T is better positioned than any party to distinguish between RF environment changes caused by passenger volume fluctuation versus new 5G equipment interference. The 90-day baseline captures variability but should be supplemented with historical CWP performance data AT&T already possesses from years of operation. The 200 bps penalty for AT&T-attributable delays requires 'joint root-cause attribution' with binding arbitration within 30 days — this is not a unilateral DFW weapon. AT&T has equal voice in attribution. The scenario described — a Thanksgiving passenger surge causing a CWP dip attributed to small cell energization — would fail the joint attribution process because AT&T can produce historical Thanksgiving CWP performance data showing similar dips in prior years without any 5G equipment present.
- **Airline-V1** (Plan Modification): The airline VP raises a legitimate correlated-failure scenario, and as the cargo director I take this seriously because DPA events affect cargo automation SLAs as well. However, I want to reframe the risk and defend the consensus's fundamental adequacy while proposing a targeted enhancement. First, the consensus already addresses DPA impact on cargo with specific degradation modes: AGV reduced-speed (≤50ms) at 30-300 seconds, safe-stop (≤75ms) during sustained DPA, and cold chain failover to backup telemetry at 5-second threshold. These are not aspirational — they are engineered operational modes that our warehouse automation vendors (Vanderlande, Siemens, BEUMER) design into their AGV control systems. AGVs at reduced speed still move cargo. Safe-stop preserves vehicle positions and queue state for rapid resume. Cold chain backup telemetry operates on an independent cellular or satellite path that does not rely on CBRS spectrum at all. For the airline's DPA + ground-stop compound scenario, the core issue is that 'full allocation' during degradation needs an absolute floor, not just a relative priority. I support the airline's implicit request for a modification: define an absolute minimum throughput floor (e.g., 25 Mbps/gate) that triggers automatic capacity reservation from lower tiers during DPA events, rather than relying solely on relative priority. This can be engineered by reserving dedicated CBRS channels for Tier 0-1 traffic that are only released to lower tiers when DPA is not active. Additionally, the 30-second cellular failover should be supplemented with a DFW-contracted FirstNet priority lane for airline ramp devices — AT&T's own FirstNet Band 14 provides prioritized access that is NOT subject to macro network congestion during passenger surges. The consensus should specify that airline failover SIMs are FirstNet-enabled, which AT&T can provide at marginal cost given AT&T's FirstNet contract.
- **Airline-V2** (Mitigation): The airline VP is right that airline pricing is less specified than cargo or concession pricing, and that the Terminal F prepaid access fee structure needs clarity on reversion rights. However, this is a negotiation gap that the consensus framework explicitly accommodates through the 5-year LOI mechanism — airline pricing specifics are to be finalized within 30 days as binding letters, just as cargo pricing was finalized through our binding commitment letters. The consensus provides the framework; the specific rate card is a bilateral airline-DFW negotiation that occurs at the same stage as cargo's binding letters. From the cargo consortium's perspective, our pricing demonstrates the model works when the tenant engages constructively. We negotiated $0.25/sq ft/month with explicit SLA guarantees, 5-year binding terms, $15M prepaid investment with clear infrastructure ownership by DFW, and edge compute allocation included. We accepted DFW ownership because we are paying for a SERVICE — automation-grade connectivity — not buying an ASSET. Our $15M funds infrastructure we use for 5+ years at a known cost. If DFW terminates at Year 7, our warehouses retain the passive infrastructure (conduit, cable trays, fiber raceways) that we funded in Phase 0 at our own cost ($200-400K), and any successor network can utilize DFW-owned active infrastructure that our prepaid fees helped fund. The airline should negotiate analogous protections: Terminal F prepaid fees should vest over the 5-year minimum term with a pro-rata refund mechanism if DFW exercises no-fault exit before full vesting. The 'airline-tagged in asset registry' language should be supplemented with a right-of-first-refusal on Terminal F infrastructure if DFW ever transfers or decommissions it. These are standard co-investment protections that the cargo consortium would support as precedent-consistent with our own structure.
- **Airline-V3** (Mitigation): The Terminal C construction/CWP interaction is a real operational challenge, but the consensus addresses it through the pre-authorized construction framework developed during Phase 0 by the joint AT&T RF engineering + DFW network architecture + airline ops technology team. The airline VP is correct that this framework was primarily designed for 5G buildout activities rather than the interaction between building construction and existing RF infrastructure. However, this concern is not unique to the 5G consensus — Terminal C's $3B rebuild would degrade CWP performance with or without any 5G deployment. Steel erection, demolition, and temporary walls alter RF propagation regardless. The relevant question is whether the consensus makes this WORSE, and the answer is: marginally, and with explicit protections. The 72-hour halt mechanism protects against 5G-CAUSED degradation specifically. Construction-caused degradation to CWP is a pre-existing AT&T/DFW operational issue that exists independently of this consensus and should be addressed in the Terminal C construction contract's RF management plan — which AT&T, as CWP operator, should already be developing with the Terminal C construction manager. The consensus actually IMPROVES the Terminal C situation by accelerating the timeline to deploy 5G in rebuilt sections, which provides a modern, construction-aware wireless infrastructure that replaces the CWP in completed areas rather than depending on aging CWP APs in an actively changing RF environment. From the cargo perspective, I want Terminal C 5G operational as fast as possible because connecting cargo flows through Terminal C gates (international cargo often clears CBP in terminal-adjacent facilities). The pre-authorized construction framework should include a parallel RF monitoring protocol that distinguishes between construction-zone RF changes (expected, managed by construction RF plan) and 5G-transition RF changes (subject to halt mechanism). This is a refinement to the Phase 0 framework deliverable, not a structural change to the consensus.
- **Airline-V4** (Plan Modification): The per-terminal SLA measurement concern is valid and the cargo consortium's experience directly informs a fix. We insisted on per-WAREHOUSE-ZONE measurement (HC-3: 99.99% per warehouse zone) precisely because we understood that facility-wide averaging masks localized failures. A warehouse zone for us is approximately 200,000-400,000 sq ft — comparable to a 6-8 gate cluster in Terminal F. The cargo SLA model should be the template for airline SLA refinement. I support modifying the airline SLA to include a sub-terminal measurement tier. Specifically: maintain 99.99% per-terminal as the aggregate SLA with $50K/terminal/incident penalty, but ADD a per-gate-cluster SLA (e.g., groups of 4-6 gates sharing an edge compute node) at 99.95% with a separate penalty mechanism tied to affected-flight delay costs. This is architecturally feasible because the consensus already specifies edge compute nodes serving gate clusters — each edge compute node's service area is a natural SLA measurement boundary. The synthetic monitoring infrastructure (50+ devices per terminal, 15-second polling) should be distributed to ensure minimum 2 synthetic probes per gate area, with application-layer metrics (not just connectivity) including DCS response time, baggage reconciliation feed latency, and crew communication round-trip time. The edge compute memory leak scenario described is a real failure mode that connectivity monitoring misses. Application-layer synthetic transactions — a simulated baggage scan, a simulated DCS query — would detect the 18ms-to-180ms latency degradation within one polling cycle. This is standard practice in the cargo automation monitoring we've specified: our SLA includes ≤0.001% packet loss AND specific latency thresholds, measured at the APPLICATION layer (AGV control response, cold chain sensor acknowledgment), not just network layer.
- **Airline-V5** (Mitigation): The inter-operator handoff concern for split carrier-operations across terminals is legitimate and requires attention, but it does not invalidate the competitive evaluation structure. From the cargo consortium's perspective, we operate across multiple warehouse facilities that could theoretically be served by different carrier-operations vendors — the same architectural challenge. Our mitigation is embedded in the consensus: DFW owns ALL physical and logical infrastructure below the carrier-operations API layer, including SDN controllers, switches, routers, and the tenant slice policy layer. The airline's cryptographically isolated slice is defined and managed at the DFW infrastructure layer, not the carrier-operations layer. A consistent slice policy — same QoS, same ACLs, same device provisioning — is enforced by DFW-owned SDN controllers regardless of which vendor manages radio resources in a given terminal. The carrier-operations layer (SAS coordination, radio resource scheduling) operates BENEATH the slice policy layer in the abstraction hierarchy. An iPad moving from Terminal C to Terminal F should experience the same slice policy because the same DFW-owned infrastructure enforces it. The handoff between carrier-operations vendors is a radio-layer handoff managed by the DFW-owned SDN and orchestration layer, not a slice re-authentication. That said, the consensus should specify inter-operator handoff requirements explicitly. I propose adding to the Terminal F evaluation criteria: any carrier-operations vendor for Terminal F must demonstrate ≤500ms inter-terminal handoff with the incumbent terminal carrier-operations vendor, tested with airline devices under operational conditions. This becomes a binding acceptance criterion, not just an evaluation factor. The airline's single-pane-of-glass API/portal for slice management should be contractually guaranteed to present a unified view regardless of underlying carrier-operations vendor, with DFW (not AT&T or Betacom) accountable for cross-terminal continuity.

## White Team Adjudication
- **[AT&T-V1] Carrier Offload Guarantee Creates Unhedgeable Demand Risk for AT&T**: Resolved — The CRO's quantitative defense is compelling and I find it dispositive. The Year 1 floor of $500K represents $0.0027 per passenger-hour against Boingo's documented $0.008-$0.015 — approximately 25% of proven industry floor rates at comparable airports. The $1M Year 3-5 floor at 87M passengers is still less than 30% of what JFK, LAX, and ORD generate from carrier offload. AT&T's self-offload economics are real: 30-35M AT&T subscribers transit DFW annually, and AT&T's own macro CapEx savings from offloading those subscribers to CBRS infrastructure partially satisfies the guarantee with zero third-party dependency. The 'perverse incentive' argument collapses under the CRO's math — DFW's 55% share of actual offload revenue ($1.65M at a conservative $3M) vastly exceeds the $1M shortfall payment. No rational airport operator prefers the shortfall. From my concessions perspective, AT&T's offload economics are irrelevant to my operators' $175-200/month pricing, but AT&T's financial viability on this deal is relevant to service quality. The floors are conservative enough that AT&T should meet them, and the Year 5 renegotiation provides a structural safety valve if the offload market genuinely shifts. I downgrade from Critical to High because the actual financial exposure ($3.25M cumulative over 5 years) is modest relative to AT&T's overall deal economics.
- **[AT&T-V2] Parallel Cargo Competitive Evaluation Is a Trojan Horse for Full Displacement**: Resolved — AT&T is framing competitive discipline as a threat, which is itself revealing. The Cargo Director's defense is blunt and correct: the consortium is committing $15M prepaid and $6M/year in binding revenue, and it will not hand exclusive carrier-operations rights to any vendor without competitive validation. The evaluation structure is generous to AT&T by any procurement standard — 30% incumbent weight exceeds federal procurement norms (5-10% under FAR Part 15) by 3-6x. AT&T's interim operational authority during Phase 0.5 provides paid production data that no competitor can replicate from trial nodes in non-production zones. The 40% technical capability weight advantages AT&T's enterprise-scale SAS registration, FCC compliance infrastructure, and nationwide CBRS deployment experience over systems integrators like Betacom. If AT&T cannot win with a 30-point incumbency advantage, existing SIDA badges, SAS registration, and 6-9 months of production operational data, then the airport's tenants — including my 200+ concession locations — are better served by a vendor that can. From my perspective as concessions tech lead: I don't care who operates the carrier layer, I care that my POS systems process transactions at 99.9%. Competition ensures the best operator wins, which benefits my operators. The CRO correctly notes that the competitive evaluation is what secured the cargo consortium's binding $6M/year commitment — removing competition would have yielded lower committed revenue and weaker Board approval support.
- **[AT&T-V3] AT&T Capital Obligations Compound While Revenue Share Declines to Subsistence Levels**: Partially Resolved — The CRO's revenue projection ($25-38M cumulative AT&T revenue against $17-24M investment) is credible but depends on adoption timeline assumptions that are optimistic for Years 1-3. Specifically: concession revenue assumes 200+ locations onboarded by Month 36, but my own experience managing 60 operators tells me that migration of 200+ locations — including ACDBE small businesses that can barely manage a POS system — will take longer than projected. Not every operator will migrate on schedule. Some will resist. Some will need hand-holding that extends timelines. Airline revenue depends on a binding LOI within 30 days that is not yet executed. If concession onboarding takes 18 months past GA (Month 24) instead of 12, and if airline pricing negotiations extend to Month 18 instead of Month 6, AT&T's early-year revenue is materially lower than projected. The reference-account value ($50-100M in influenced pipeline) is real but unquantifiable for a Board presentation. The declining revenue share structure is standard and defensible in principle — but the CRO's defense does not adequately address AT&T's Year 5 renegotiation vulnerability. AT&T will arrive at Year 5 having spent $12-15M with $10-15M in cumulative revenue — roughly breakeven — and DFW will hold all the leverage. If AT&T's board calculates that the next 5 years offer diminishing returns, AT&T could disengage operationally (quiet quitting) rather than formally exit, which degrades service quality for my operators.
- **[AT&T-V4] DPA Liability Trap — AT&T Bears FCC Compliance Risk Under Governance It Cannot Control**: Partially Resolved — This is the most technically substantive vulnerability in the entire exercise, and both the CRO and Cargo Director correctly acknowledge it as a genuine gap requiring modification. The core issue is irrefutable: FCC Part 96 enforcement targets the SAS registrant (AT&T), not the building owner, and contractual liability allocation between DFW and AT&T does not bind the FCC. The CRO's proposed DPA Emergency Capacity Protocol is well-designed: graduated capacity thresholds, proportional allocation at Level 2, bright-line carve-out preventing DFW overrides that require FCC non-compliance. This is the right framework. The Cargo Director's complementary proposal — design-time DPA capacity engineering requiring the network to deliver all degradation-mode minimums under worst-case sustained DPA — converts the runtime governance problem into a testable engineering specification, which is even better. However, neither modification has been drafted, agreed, or incorporated. The residual risk identified by the CRO — that DFW's entire private 5G value proposition depends on spectrum that is federally interruptible — is fundamental and permanent. CBRS is Priority Access / General Authorized Access spectrum subordinate to Incumbent Access by law. No contractual framework changes this. From my concessions perspective: my 1 Mbps degradation floor during DPA events is achievable within post-DPA capacity if the network is properly engineered with DPA headroom. But the consensus does not currently require that headroom engineering as a binding design specification. That is the gap.
- **[AT&T-V5] CWP Degradation Halt Authority Creates Asymmetric Schedule Weapon Against AT&T**: Partially Resolved — The CRO's proposed modifications — seasonal normalization model, supplementation with AT&T's 5+ years of historical CWP data, joint attribution methodology with independent RF adjudicator, and limiting the 200 bps penalty to AT&T-attributed delays only — are comprehensive and technically sound. The Cargo Director correctly notes the cargo zone is physically separated and unaffected. The core defense — that the CIO has zero incentive to weaponize CWP halts because every halt delays revenue — is logically valid. From my concessions perspective, I am deeply aligned with the CRO: CWP halts delay my Phase 1 pilot (Months 3-9) and Phase 2 general availability (Month 24), which delays POS connectivity for my 200+ locations. I am the last stakeholder who wants spurious halts. However, the modifications are proposed, not agreed. The attribution methodology must be specified BEFORE contract execution, not developed 'during Phase 0' as a best-effort deliverable. If Phase 0 produces a contested attribution methodology, the 72-hour halt mechanism becomes immediately problematic as soon as Phase 1 begins. The independent RF adjudicator model is proven (LAX, ORD Boingo transitions) and should be contractually committed at signing, not deferred.
- **[Airline-V1] DPA + Ground-Stop Release Creates Unrecoverable Dual-Failure at Peak Ramp Intensity**: Partially Resolved — This is the most operationally sophisticated attack in the exercise. The airline VP correctly identifies that DPA response and cellular failover are correlated failure modes at DFW, not independent ones — contradicting the consensus assumption in H12. The CRO's probability analysis (1-3 joint events per year) understates the risk because it assumes negative correlation between DPA and weather, when in fact NAS JRB conducts training exercises year-round including during convective weather seasons. The CRO's proposed licensed-spectrum failover ($4-5M across 5 terminals + Terminal F) is the right architectural solution — a dedicated LTE/5G macro small-cell cluster on licensed spectrum (not CBRS) at each terminal ramp area, immune to DPA, providing guaranteed 10 Mbps/gate during DPA events. The Cargo Director's FirstNet Band 14 suggestion is complementary and leverages AT&T's existing infrastructure. However, this is a $4-5M unfunded capital commitment that must be allocated. DFW infrastructure CapEx seems appropriate since the failover protects DFW's overall network value proposition. From my concessions perspective: the DPA + ground-stop scenario also affects my Tier 4 POS traffic — during this event, POS drops to the 1 Mbps degradation floor or worse. A licensed-spectrum backup for airline ramp operations frees up residual CBRS capacity for my POS locations during DPA events. I support this modification because it indirectly improves my operators' experience during degradation events. The gap between SLA credits ($200K per the attack's math) and actual operational losses ($1.49M) is expected and appropriate — SLA credits are accountability mechanisms, not insurance. But the 7x gap should be narrowed, or the airline will never have confidence in the platform.
- **[Airline-V2] Airline Pricing Structure is Dangerously Underspecified Relative to $8-12M Terminal F Commitment**: Partially Resolved — The airline VP is correct: airline pricing is materially less specified than concession or cargo pricing, and the Terminal F prepaid access structure lacks basic reversion protections. My own concession pricing is fully defined — $175-200/month, all-inclusive, month-to-month, CPI/3% cap, most-favored-tenant, 10-day activation, zero CapEx. The cargo consortium has $0.25/sq ft/month with explicit SLAs and 5-year binding terms. The airline has 'Hybrid base + per-departure variable (base ≤60% of expected annual cost)' with undefined rates, undefined escalation, undefined reversion, and no exit flexibility. This asymmetry is problematic for two reasons: (1) The airline's LOI is a binding constraint for Board approval per HC-12 and H-R14 — if airline pricing remains underspecified, the LOI cannot convert to a binding commitment, and the Board lacks the committed revenue assurance it requires. (2) The airline is 80%+ of departures — if the airline walks over pricing, the entire deal loses its most critical stakeholder. The CRO's proposed modifications are comprehensive: $35/departure cap with CPI/3% escalation, 10-year amortization with refund on early termination, traffic reduction protection, symmetric Year 7 exit, airline Year 5 renegotiation right. These are all reasonable and necessary. From my concessions perspective: I need this deal to close. My 200+ locations need POS connectivity by Month 24. The airline's commitment provides the revenue anchor that sustains the network. If airline pricing negotiations stall, the entire deployment timeline is at risk. The CRO should resolve airline pricing terms to the same specificity as concessions and cargo BEFORE Board approval.
- **[Airline-V3] Terminal C Multi-Year Construction Creates Rolling CWP Degradation Trap**: Partially Resolved — The airline correctly identifies that Terminal C's 3-year $3B rebuild creates an RF environment where construction-caused and 5G-caused CWP degradation will be difficult to distinguish. The CRO's proposed modifications — dynamic monthly RF baseline, construction-phase RF sensors, pre-modeled structural RF impact assessments, and construction-adjusted degradation triggers — are technically sound and supported by DFW Terminal D precedent. The Cargo Director's point that construction-caused CWP degradation exists independently of the 5G deployment is valid — this is not a risk created by the consensus; it is a pre-existing risk that the consensus marginally compounds. From my concessions perspective: Terminal C has concession locations. My operators in Terminal C need functioning POS connectivity during the 3-year rebuild, whether that connectivity comes from CWP or 5G. The accelerated deployment of 5G in rebuilt Terminal C sections actually HELPS my operators by providing modern connectivity sooner rather than depending on aging CWP in an actively changing RF environment. The real risk is the transition period where neither CWP nor 5G is reliable in construction-adjacent areas. The 72-hour halt mechanism for Terminal C should use the dynamic baseline as the CRO proposes — measuring degradation against post-construction-change expected performance, not the pre-construction ideal. This eliminates false-positive halts from structural RF changes while preserving genuine 5G-transition protections.
- **[Airline-V4] 99.99% Per-Terminal SLA Masks Sub-Gate Failure Modes in 31-Gate Terminal F**: Partially Resolved — The measurement-granularity argument is valid and the CRO's proposed modifications (per-gate-cluster SLA at 99.95%, application-layer metrics, edge compute N+1 redundancy, $10K per gate-cluster per degradation event) are well-designed. The Cargo Director's experience with per-warehouse-zone SLAs provides direct architectural precedent. From my concessions perspective, I have the granularity I need — my SLA is already 99.9% per LOCATION, measured at the CPE gateway. Every one of my 200+ locations has an independent SLA measurement point. The airline's per-terminal measurement is coarser than what I negotiated, and the airline should bring its measurement to comparable granularity. The edge compute memory leak scenario is a real failure mode. My CPE gateways could also be affected by an edge compute failure if multiple concession locations share an edge compute node — but my per-location measurement at the CPE would detect the degradation even if a terminal-level metric did not. I support the airline's modification because better SLA granularity for all tenants improves overall network accountability, which benefits my operators.
- **[Airline-V5] Terminal F Competitive Carrier-Operations Evaluation Creates Operational Risk During Greenfield Launch**: Partially Resolved — The CRO's architectural defense is technically correct: DFW owns all infrastructure below the carrier-operations API layer, including SDN controllers and slice management. The airline's slice policy is enforced at the DFW infrastructure layer regardless of which vendor manages radio resources in a given terminal. 3GPP Release 17 defines slice continuity as a core-network function. In principle, a bag tug crossing from Terminal C to Terminal F should maintain session continuity because the same DFW-owned SDN manages the handoff. However, the attack raises a legitimate concern about practical implementation. Multi-vendor RAN environments at the O-RAN specification level are still maturing. Real-world inter-vendor handoff at sub-500ms with session continuity for ramp automation applications is unproven at airport scale. The JFK Terminal 4 and LAX Tom Bradley examples cited are DAS deployments, not private 5G sliced networks — the comparison is architecturally imprecise. From my concessions perspective: my POS locations do not move between terminals, so inter-terminal handoff is irrelevant to my operators' daily experience. But if inter-terminal handoff failures undermine airline confidence, the airline could resist the Terminal F competitive evaluation, which could delay Terminal F deployment and the revenue model supporting my ACDBE pricing subsidies. I support specifying inter-operator handoff requirements to remove this as a blocker.

## Board-Ready Recommendation
**Plan Strength Score**: 7/10
**Go/No-Go**: Conditional Go
**Conditions:**
  - DPA Emergency Capacity Protocol drafted and incorporated as Exhibit A-1 to the MSA, including graduated capacity thresholds, proportional allocation rules, bright-line FCC compliance carve-out preventing any DFW override requiring AT&T to violate Part 96, and binding design specification requiring the network to deliver all degradation-mode minimums under sustained DPA affecting 60% of CBRS channels
  - Licensed-spectrum failover infrastructure ($4-5M) committed as DFW CapEx with deployment by Phase 2 completion (Month 21), providing DPA-immune backup for Tier 0-1 ramp operations and additional residual CBRS capacity for Tier 4 POS during DPA events
  - Airline pricing term sheet achieving concession/cargo-equivalent specificity (defined per-departure rate with cap and escalation, Terminal F prepaid amortization with pro-rata refund mechanism, symmetric Year 7 exit right, defined 'expected annual cost' methodology) executed within 60 days of contract signing as a condition precedent to Board approval
  - CWP degradation attribution methodology framework incorporated as an MSA exhibit at contract execution, including principles of temporal correlation, proximate 5G activity requirement, passenger-volume normalization, seasonal adjustment, independent RF adjudicator pre-selection criteria, and explicit limitation that the 200 bps penalty accrues only after joint root-cause attribution concludes AT&T causation
  - Inter-operator handoff SLA (≤500ms, ≤0.1% failure rate) and mandatory 60-day interoperability testing period incorporated as binding acceptance criteria for any non-incumbent Terminal F carrier-operations award, with single-SAS-provider mandate across all terminals and explicit fallback to incumbent if no alternative vendor meets handoff requirements
  - Per-gate-cluster SLA (99.95%, 4-6 gate clusters per terminal) and edge compute N+1 redundancy incorporated as supplementary SLA tier in Exhibit A, with application-layer synthetic monitoring metrics defined at the network boundary
  - Concession deployment timeline (Phase 1 pilot Months 3-9, Phase 2 GA Month 24) contractually independent of airline LOI execution status — concession operators cannot be held hostage to airline pricing negotiations

The DFW Private 5G Consensus Framework represents a well-structured hybrid co-investment model that delivers measurable value across all stakeholder categories. For the F&B concessions consortium — 60 operators, 200+ locations, $4.2B gross product — the consensus delivers exactly what our operators need: turnkey managed POS connectivity at $175-200/month all-inclusive with zero CapEx, a single DFW-accountable support contact, 99.9% per-location SLA with automatic credits, support for existing POS hardware (Aloha, Toast, Square) without replacement, and ACDBE pricing at $80/month ensuring small business operators are not priced out of reliable connectivity. Month-to-month terms with CPI/3% escalation caps protect operators from technology cost inflation, and the 10-day new-location activation supports our 34+ new locations through 2028. These terms are superior to any current Wi-Fi or cellular alternative available to our operators.

The red team stress test identified ten vulnerabilities — two are fully resolved, eight require specific plan modifications before contract execution. The most critical modifications are: (1) a DPA Emergency Capacity Protocol ensuring AT&T's FCC compliance obligations are never compromised by DFW governance directives, with the network designed to deliver degradation-mode minimums under worst-case sustained DPA scenarios; (2) licensed-spectrum failover infrastructure at $4-5M providing DPA-immune backup for safety-critical and airline ramp operations; and (3) airline pricing specificity achieving parity with the concession and cargo rate cards before Board approval. None of these modifications require restructuring the deal — they are targeted amendments that close identified gaps within the existing framework.

The Board should approve this framework as a Conditional Go. The seven specified conditions represent contractual language requirements achievable within 60 days — not fundamental redesign. The committed revenue base (cargo $6M/year binding, concessions 200+ locations binding, AT&T offload floors binding) exceeds the $2M net annual savings threshold on committed demand alone, before airline pricing is finalized. The phased deployment with tested rollback at each gate, 90-day parallel operations per concession location, and blackout windows protecting peak revenue periods ensure that operational risk is contained. For our concessions consortium specifically, I recommend Board approval with the explicit condition that the concession deployment timeline (Phase 1 pilot Months 3-9, Phase 2 general availability Month 24) is contractually independent of airline pricing negotiations — our 200+ locations and 60 operators cannot afford to have their connectivity modernization held hostage to a bilateral negotiation between DFW and its anchor airline.
